column2: Fast track digital journey for licensing to support Businesses out of Covid-19
column4: The Council will be required by the Planning and Business Bill to deliver a fast track pavement licensing regime for local businesses from the end of July, when the proposal receives Royal Assent. The project will enable all businesses to access a licensing portal that will post directly into back-office software, not only for the pavement licensing regime, but for all licensing applications needed to be made by business to enable them to operate. The client journey will be simplified and much faster than the current facility much of which is provided through the Gov.uk website and some of which depends upon paper based systems.  The proposal will enable better and more effective public and statutory authority consultation, improved availability of data to interested parties and an improve client journey and licensing process
column7:
The project has been discussed and a ground0 development document has been produced.  There are proposals to develop a specification for the software needed for the project including the digital application process, enabling upload of digital supporting documents and information, enabling online fee payments where required, plus delivering public registers and awareness of licensing applications, registers and systems that support community participation. The CP&L Service has a dedicated Business Partner supporting and delivering our digital transformation needs.

The project will need to deliver an interim facility to enable the fast-track pavement trading applications now required by Government and a medium longer term plan to deliver all licensing applications through a digital facility.
column8:
The Ground0 document for the project has been produced.  An IT Business Partner and Transformation Officer are in place and with scope to progress and deliver the initiative

The interim digital facility to enable Pavement Licensing applications will be required and in place by end of July 2020

The project and scoping document to identify all needs of the digital licensing journey and to enable tenders to be submitted for delivery of the product will be ready in 8 weeks.

Clients to benefit will be all businesses in the borough with the need for a licence to enable them to operate successfully.  The Service recognises the needs of business to have fast track licensing decisions and to enable the businesses to operate and key staff to be licensed in accordance with legislation.  Communities and community engagement will be supported by the introduction of the proposals fro the project.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Ability to deliver of the declared objectives

A fast track access to licensing services for businesses in the borough

An improved and simplified client journey through the licensing process

Ability of business to access licensing without recourse to costly and unnecessary legal services and/or licensing support agencies.

A comprehensive digital package to deliver the complete range of statutory licensing requirements, including the new fast-track pavement licensing required for businesses emerging from Covid - 19 restrictions.

Improved ability for community consultation, engagement and influence on the licensing processes necessary to keep the communities safe, informed and provided with amenity.
column13:
At the end of the project we would anticipate having a comprehensive digital package and the means to acquire it.  This could be shared with other local authorities, all of whom have the same needs to deliver statutory licensing for their businesses and their communities.

Licensing services across local authorities are widely networked, particularly within the London boroughs and the sharing of best practice is encouraged through such networks

column2: Developing a crisis response model to support Neighbourhood Team delivery
column4: During the Covid-19 pandemic Manchester, like other places, had to rapidly design solutions that supported the immediate response. Some of these solutions may have been built in haste and focused on a specific job, but what they represent are new approaches that should be taken into new ways of working. This application focuses on how we get intelligence into the hands of our 'Team Around a Neighbourhood (TANs)' functions, taking data from processes developed during Covid (i.e. data on vulnerable and those in need of support) and providing this as actionable intelligence for TANs to resource plan and deliver direct support.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Manchester City Council has developed internal a number of approaches to delivery, monitor and exchange data about Covid activities and responses. These approaches were initially designed to simple process requests and responses, however as time has progressed these same solutions needed to adapt to support wider recovery efforts.
Solutions were developed to log and process calls to the Council’s Covid Emergency Response line and Food Response Service, and supporting approaches were developed to make it possible to deliver joined up responses (i.e. checking administrate datasets). These were all developed using basic and in-house available technical solutions (i.e. forms and spreadsheets).

During our shift from response to recovery we’ve been working with our ‘Teams Around a Neighbourhoods (TANs)’ to look at how data and intelligence can support a shift from just making sure that our residents have the crisis support they needed (i.e. food parcel) to supporting our residents to engage with the appropriate support (i.e. public and local groups/services).

We have developed approaches to bring intelligence together (some manual and some digital) to support this work and have been piloting/testing an approach with two of our thirteen TANs for the last month. We want to do more detailed engagement with our TANs on the requirements and establish how a digital solution can support them.
column8: We have already developed approaches to access data from our core systems, including: Shielded Groups; MCC Emergency Hub; MCC Food Response; Housing Benefit and/or Council Tax Support; Social Care; Private Rented; Adult Social Care Safe and Well checks; Health Contacts via Integrated Teams. We have also developed approaches to match and bring these datasets together to understand the overlaps, opportunities and gaps. We have been engaging with our ‘Teams Around a Neighbourhood (TANs)’ for about 4-6 weeks already and piloting/testing in two TANs on how these approaches fit against their requirements. Stage 1 - Requirements and Engagement (2-3 weeks) Overall there are 13 TANs in Manchester so we would like to firstly drill deeper in to the two pilot areas to capture the specific requirements and opportunities, followed secondly by a wider engagement of the other 11 TANs to ascertain a consistent set of central requirements. This would involve engagement through established ‘Neighbourhood Team Leads’ group and ongoing support from internal project team currently managing the pilot work. Stage 2 – Solution Design and Testing (5-6 weeks) We would work with internal data owners, analysis, and system owners to develop efficient and clear specifications for a tool to bring data together resulting in intelligence that is actionable by our TANs. Piloting to date uses simple forms and spreadsheet functions with the councils ICT suite (namely Google), however we would be looking here to develop an approach which is efficient, secure, low cost and open or transferable to any range of platforms. The output from this stage may not be a complete product, but would be a complete design specification that has had some testing to support it. This would involve taking learning from Stage 1 and working with internal the ‘Performance, Research and Intelligence’ service to test options. May also involve collaboration with a third party if deemed required.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: As our 'Teams Around a Neighbourhood (TANs)' are operational and our data flows are real, then our success will be clearly visible by the fact that the solutions will have to meet the TANs requirements in order to be useful. Therefore, by the end of the project we would expect to have an specification which all Manchester TANs can agree with and that also represents the core functions within Neighbourhood Teams so is therefore transferable to other areas. If the stage 2 work delivers a tested and usable tool then this would be a further demonstration of success, however if not a finalised tool it would still represent clearly articulated and tested specification for how emergency response lessons from Covid could live on in wider Neighbourhood working.
column13: Neighbourhood Team working is already a joined up reality with Greater Manchester and through ongoing collaboration with the Greater Manchester Combined Authority and the other nine GM authorities we will be able to test that 'what works in Manchester' is aligned to the neighbourhood working principles agreed across the whole of GM. We will also develop in the open and encourage other areas to help us test and shape our thinking (i.e. Core City groups, Social Media, etc).
column14: The tool will be predominately used for internal services rather than residents themselves, however the design specification produced will ensure that requirements of users are incorporated.

column2: Digital Engagement
column4: The proposed project is an investigation into the feasibility of developing solutions to address the challenge of Community Engagement in a post Covid-19 environment. This Discovery is aimed at identifying and identifying key barriers to community engagement generated by Covid-19, and explore the scope of Digital Solutions that applied to overcome these barriers. A range of technical, logistical and design features associated with potential digital models will be addressed as part of the discovery project.
column7:
The Resident Involvement team and the Digital Infrastructure Programme team have been proactively cultivating Digital Literacy through the development of a local network of Digital Champions in the community and the ongoing partnership work with Community Fibre and Hyperoptic to install high speed fibre broadband in digitally excluded areas of the borough.

We have improved the digital infrastructure and installed free broadband connections to all housing estates with a target audience that includes vulnerable and elderly residents that are traditionally digitally excluded.

https://www.southwark.gov.uk/housing/housing-getting-involved/better-broadband-for-southwark?chapter=3

https://www.southwark.gov.uk/news/2018/mar/thousands-of-homes-across-southwark-set-for-better-broadband

https://www.hyperoptic.com/press/posts/southwark-chosen-for-launch-of-first-hyperoptic-digital-skills-academy/
column8: Our users are the Resident Engagement and Digital Infrastructure teams internally. We plan to engage a supplier who has strong user research credentials and will be able to engage with our digitally excluded residents to inform focused outcomes. We plan to  to use digital unite champions to further advance our community engagement
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: Success to be measured by uptake in our digital engagement directly related to how the Digital Champions can engage with residents.
column13: The outputs will be freely available and all outputs will be shared on Slack, we plan to share our progress and work in the open. Where wireframes or prototypes are produced these will be made available on Github.
column14:
The focus for this work is to identify how these barriers can be overcome and allow these segments of the population to be engaged.

This represents a big challenge. However, we are already undertaking work in terms of bridging the digital gap in Southwark, and will build upon this with our partners from the private, public and third sectors. Specifically, we will work with community-based organisations to seek their views on how best to make the project meaningful to those currently digitally excluded. We will also look at ways of recycling redundant IT equipment, with a view to providing a bank of resources for those unable to afford to buy their own equipment. Furthermore, we will develop our network of volunteer digital champions, with the aim of their promoting digital literacy in the community.

column2: Infrastructure and Covid Insights
column4: Existing infrastructure sensors provide us with data that shows how often pedestrian crossings are being demanded as well as the waiting times until they can cross. We will bring together these existing dynamic datasets using a publicly available visualisation tool. This will enable insights to be drawn into how different areas of the city are performing to enable support to be focused on areas of the city that are recovering more slowly from the pandemic. It can also provide a measure of the impact of active travel measures.
column7:
No specific work has been done but the relevant systems that will supply the data are already in place. The Urban Traffic Management Control (UTMC) system that is used to control the pedestrian crossings already stores data regarding frequency of demand and the time until each demand is served.  This data will also be collected by other local authorities should they wish to replicate this project. New traffic sensors have been recently installed at specific locations in Leeds city centre. The sensors record classified volumetric data across multiple classes including pedestrians and cycles. The data is readily available via an API from the sensor supplier. It is proposed that the data will also used as part of the project for comparison between fine granularity data in specific locations to the more widespread pedestrian crossing data. In addition to the traffic sensors, footfall counters also provide weekly data for the main shopping street, Briggate. It is expected that a further 16 counters will be deployed across the city and, subject to the outcome of that funding bid, data from those sensors will also be incorporated. We will reuse and extend existing assets and data visualisation developed by ODI Leeds in delivering this project, including:
  • Traffic Growth visualizations https://odileeds.github.io/traffic-growth
  • The Data Mapper https://mapper.odileeds.org/
column8:
Key milestones:
  1. Relevant footfall and traffic density data will be identified from the UTMC system. Week 1
  1. Development of a data feed or API to openly publish relevant transport data to drive visualisations. This work will be carried out by BR Hallworth Ltd working with Underdog Software. Week 1 – 3
  1. Research sources of business recovery data from existing published datasets on Data Mill North, or potential new datasets. Weeks 2 - 3
  1. Development of a visualisation tool that will provide useful insight into captured pedestrian footfall across the district and, where feasible, tie this into other measures of business recovery. This work will be carried out by ODI Leeds. Weeks 4 - 6
  1. Obtain feedback following user research with key service areas in the council (e.g. Visit Leeds, Economic Development, Highways) and through ODI Leeds and their regular ‘Covid-19: Open Data Save Lives’ webinars. Weeks 7 - 9
  1. Amendments made to the solution following user feedback. Week 10
  2. Promotion of the tool and report to MHCLG will details of how other local authorities could replicate for their areas. Week 11
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The popular success of this project can be measured directly by the volume of users making use of the visualisation tool. This is, perhaps, the easiest metric for project success and will prove the worth of visualising data and thus making it accessible to more people.

However, this in itself does not demonstrate success with regard to assisting in the recovery from the pandemic. The true success of the project may well be demonstrated in the following months where insights from the data will allow resources to be focused on areas of the city that may take longer to recover.

The impact of the project will ultimately be assisting in achieving a more inclusive economic recovery. This can be encouraged through investment in active travel measures in a city where one third of households do not own a car. The project can help to measure the success of active travel measures and enable the Highways service to focus investment in locations where demands are, for example, suppressed in relation to location and economic activity.
column13:
We will publish information about the project on a dedicated page on the ODI Leeds website. In addition we will publish three ‘deliverables’:
  1. A Story post describing the context and benefits of the work
  2. A Technical post describing how we achieve the benefits and identify reusable approaches
  3. The code, so that anyone else can pick it up and do the same
We will take opportunities to discuss and share our work during regular data-oriented forums including the Open Data Working Group and ODI Leeds Open Data Saves Lives-Covid19 activities. The UTMC system that we will use for this project is already used by various councils and can be integrated into the systems that other LAs use. Similar data may also be able to be produced from other UTMC systems already, thus making this an easily scalable project.

column2: Unaccompanied Asylum Seeking Children Pledge and Placement Tracking
column4: Covid-19 has led to even greater numbers of people making the difficult decision to travel to the UK/Kent to seek refuge/asylum, including Unccompanied Asylum Seeking Children. For this reason, we want to create a digital tool to support the journey of UASC. This would start at first encounter, capturing information about the child, their story, wants and needs so that information is captured once, early in the process. Data captured would be shared across supporting services, tracking a child/young person's journey, allowing better decisions to be made about their pathway, creating efficiencies for the partners supporting them.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
In response to the steep spike in children/young people seeking refugee/asylum status since Covid-19, we conducted a short review of the process and journey for children arriving in Kent, identifying opportunities to improve the experience for children, young people and staff from multiple agencies involved in supporting children and young people to arrive at a permanent home, by providing better recording and tracking tools.

Our research also identified open source products, which have applicable functionality to meet our requirements. Specifically, the Beacon solution developed by FutureGov/Camden/Hackney councils (https://beacon.support/) which enables councils to record the needs of their citizens, and keep track of need fulfilment through council, voluntary and community services. Conceptually similar to a CRM/case management tool, its data model has contacts (referred to as people in need), and contacts can have needs associated with them (referred to as support actions but are treated a bit like tasks), which are assigned to users, who can mark them as completed and leave notes on the needs. Beacon is an open soure solution and can be modified to meet our local needs, allowing us to deploy it quickly as a solution for UASC.
column8: We will work with FutureGov as our digital partner to refine our understanding of user needs and customise an implementation of Beacon to deploy as a minimum viable product (MVP). We will work in an agile way as a blended team: collaborating closely with service users; this includes UASC as well as service providers within the council, other councils and stakeholder organisations including charitable and voluntary groups. We will work iteratively and flexibly, so that we can respond to our changing understanding of user needs. The project will run for eight weeks, broken into four sprints: Sprint 1: 3 Aug - 14 Aug We will build on work done to-date and refine our hypotheses which we can then validate through research with users in future sprints. This will help us understand whether our initial proposed approach will help solve the problem. We will also start engaging service users and groups as participants for our research. Sprint 2: 17 Aug - 28 Aug We will start to customise the Beacon software and tailor it based on our understanding of user needs in Kent and other councils, building on the pathway model. Sprint 3 and 4: 31 Aug - 10 Sept We will start quick weekly iterative rounds of usability testing with all our engaged research participants, improving the software with each round. We will also start planning an open beta to begin at the end of sprint four, which will include discussion of how to host the digital service and support the ongoing development of it. We will map how users should be signposted toward the new service we are building. By the end of the project we will have a well-tested implementation that is ready to be made more generally available to test with other councils and partners.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The project focusses on iteration of a product and as such will capture regular feedback which will assist the team in understanding the users experience. Success in the short term will be demonstrated through satisfaction of users of the product and adoption of its use. Lead indicators for success will be measured for the 2 quarters following development to include;
  • A % improvement in pace of placements
  • Effective use of resources increased
  • A % reduction in time young people spend in reception centers
  • An improvement in young people accessing the most appropriate placement to meet their needs in a timely way
  • Age appropriateness is measured quickly the right people remain in the system
  • Age discrepancies are dealt with quicker
column13: Engagement with other councils would begin immediately, given the ambitions of the project. We would seek to leverage our own network as well as that of FutureGov and MHCLG to do so. We will work transparently, sharing our experiences during the 8-week project through published weeknotes. We would also leverage the support of FutureGov to develop case studies and blogs on the work, with the hope that these would also be shared positively by the MHCLG. Most importantly, any product developed will be open source and open to reuse. The foundation of the solution is Beacon, the code of which is already openly published: https://github.com/wearefuturegov/beacon. We would append all work done to the Beacon solution via github whilst also making it available through government portals (Knowledge Hub) and other local government knowledge sharing resources, both non-Covid and Covid specific such as https://www.coronaviruslocalresponse.co.uk/.
column14: The solution will meet government accessibility requirements including Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. The Beacon solution assumes service provider digital access, but the approach supports digitally excluded/vulnerable populations. The implementation of any product development will be supported by a detailed change and adoption training programme.
column16: We expect the solution will have relevance for UASC services across the country and would seek to engage other councils early in the project. Therefore, we would welcome support from MHCLG in supporting communication to other councils as well as government departments. We would also look to MHCLG to support us in validating our approach to evaluation, ensuring we are capturing the right metrics to demonstrate value in embedding the solution within Kent/Medway and the case for other councils looking to adopt similar solutions
column17: FutureGov

column2: Reducing Household Waste Recycling Centre congestion
column4:
Household waste recycling centre’s (HWRC) are overcrowded due to reduced capacity, social distancing and increased demand during the lockdown.
Queue times are running into 3 hours in some instances- we want to conduct further user research in order to:

Reduce queue wait times
- Encouraging users to plan their visit for quieter times
- Encourage users to check bin availability before setting off
- Encourage users to check temporary servicing before setting off

Increase traffic flow through the HWRC’s
- Publicise bin layout to help users quickly dispose of waste in the correct bins
- Ensure parking spaces are utilised as much as possible to maximise capacity
column7:
Initial user research has identified issues with the household waste recycling centre’s (HWRC)- the key themes were identified:
  • Long queue times
    • Currently up to 3 hours in some instances
  • Unavailable bins
    • Users unable to dispose of their waste due to the bin being unavailable
  • Closures during opening times due to temporary servicing
    • Users arriving at the HWRC while it is closed for temporary servicing
Improvements made so far: Long queue times- encouraging users to check the website to plan their visit
  • Queue cams
  • Peak time graphs
  • Manually updated queue times
Unavailable bins
  • Digital solution to manually record bin counts for haulier
  • Updating website to display bin availability
Closures during opening times due to temporary servicing
  • Digital solution to manually record temporary servicing
  • Updating the website to display temporary servicing message
This project aims to iteratively further build on these developments to automate and improve real-time data to manage customer expectations and improve traffic flow. On reopening day, our HWRC web pages made up 5 of the top 10 pages on our hertfordshire.gov.uk website. Traffic to those pages was up more than 400% compared to a typical Monday. This funding would enable us to better meet the needs of our users in a post-Covid world. Please see the user research library for further information.
column8:
Users: Public Private sector partners: Amey plc- operating HWRC’s                                          Serco applications- system development                                          Zengenti- website development Local authorities: Seeking other organisations to work in collaboration Initial user research has identified the issues to resolve and while we have an idea of what we would like to improve using automation and real-time data, further user research is required to ensure improvements meet user needs: Encourage users to check the website
  • Improve queue wait time
  • Improve bin availability information and redirection to next nearest available bin if unavailable
Improve the flow of traffic through the HWRC
  • Ease congestion by improving the flow of traffic through the HWRC’s
  • Improve signage/maps of bin layout
  • Free up operative time to assist users to dispose of their waste quickly
Improve bin availability
  • Improve bin availability information displayed on the website
  • Incorporate communication with bin haulier to replace bins
    • Further improving bin availability
  • Prevent temporary servicing during operating hours
Manage customer expectations- clearly promote the following:
  • Current queue times
  • Bin availability
  • If bin unavailable next nearest available HWRC
  • Map of bin layout
  • Temporary servicing messages
  • HWRC opening times
Project management
  • Agile methodology- expanding on the iterative approach
  • User research- evidence based decision making
  • Beta testing
  • Please see Pipeline for further information
Timeframes
  • Discovery
    • User research- 1 week
    • Identify solutions and procurement process- 2 weeks
 
  • Beta
    • Development- 2.5 weeks
    • Testing and deployment- 2 days
    • Implement pilot at 3 HWRC’s- 2 weeks
Total- 8 weeks
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success would be measured by:
  • A reduction in queue wait times
    • Currently up to three hours in some instances
  • Reallocation of waste among the HWRC’s
    • Bin availability information and redirection to next nearest HWRC should help spread out waste among HWRC’s
    • Cost saving for haulier bin collection and replacement
  • Reduction in fly tipping
    • Fly tipping has increased recently likely due to rouge traders and reduced HWRC capacity
    • Cost savings for fly tipping clear up
  • Improvements in user satisfaction with the HWRC and website
    • Longer queue times, reduced HWRC capacity and social distancing have likely all lead to reduced user satisfaction with the HWRC’s
  • Automation of manual processes
    • Cost savings for bin counts, queue times and traffic management
    • Improved accuracy and real time data

column2: Adult Social Care Market Insight Tool (ASCMIT)
column4:
We are refining the ASCMIT to enable it be become a tool to help commissioners make strategic and operational decisions to improve care and manage the market.

There are two key parts to the tool: 
A data repository / lake that includes the following data sets 
London ADASS data
London minimum quality standards
Currently Covid SITREP but will be Capacity Tracker 
Publicly available data 
NHS data 

A reporting tool, underpinned by Power BI
column7:
Pre-Covid we were working on these areas:
  • Annual price analysis of prices paid by LAs for bed based care and home care services
  • Developed a minimum set of quality standards for older people bed based care in collaboration with NHS, CQC and NICE colleagues
  • Supporting Quality Safeguarding and Information Panels through developing a ASC market insight dashboard
  • Developed a cost modelling tool for home care
During Covid we worked on the following areas:
  • Developed a daily Covid SITREP which was a response to a specific situation, had an excellent response rate, built data quality infrastructure, worked with HAS Technology and London School of Economics (LSE) to deliver the Market Intellegence Report (MIR) which included London wide analysis. We recognised the importance of data quality; the importance of the Single Points of Contacts (commissioners) rather than the data collation tool; the importance of analysis (MIR), but also reporting (MIT reports) and the added value LSE bring in terms of analysis.
  • Demand and capacity modelling. We actively Active concontributed to the NHS led model, contributing to the assumption underpinning demand and providing actuals to map against that demand – fully utilising the links to COVID SITREP and LSE
As we move into the recovery phase, we would like to evolve the ASCMIT so that:
  • We embed an evidence based approach to commissioning through hypothesis testing
  • Develop a partnership to do this better across LondonADASS, Care Quality Commission, LSE, NICE, NHS and providers
column8:
LondonADASS works with the 32 Boroughs and City of London on a number of workstreams. We would use this funding to specifically focus on the following elements:
  • Impact of Covid on BAME communities (particularly bed based care) - to be collected first two weeks in August 2020. We are keen to capture within the ASCMIT the characteristics of care home residents in light of emerging evidence on the disproportionate impact of the disease on BAME communities. The key factors we would like to collect data on (through a one off survey) include:
    • Age
    • Gender
    • Ethnicity
    • Diagnosis
  • Monitoring the performance of social care systems, and assessing whether they allocate resources and support equitable and efficiently requires information about the characteristics of the people with social care needs. This is particularly relevant in the context of the COVID-19 epidemic, which is showing significantly worse health outcomes for older people and people with greater health and social care needs. The resources requested will help us design and implement processes for extracting from local administrative systems information about the need-characteristics of social care service users, and match this data with the rest of evidence collected in the ASC MI analytical system, in order to develop need adjusted indicators of risk. This information will be fed to local commissioners in order to inform their service provider support and market oversights activity. This would be completed by the end of September
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success factors include:
  • All Adult Social Care commisisoners will be using the ASCMIT on a regular basis to support operational decision making
  • Senior Adult Social Care leaders will be using the ASCMIT on a regular basis to support strategic decision making
  • Minimum of 80% of Adult Social Care providers will be completing the regular returns and report that the approach in supporting them transition to the recovery phase

column2: Universal Credit data: Real-time insights into COVID recovery
column4:
Luton is among the councils worst affected by Coronavirus. 

This proposal would provide LAs with timely and fuller data on low-income residents, enabling councils to better support those affected by the economic fallout from COVID-19. 

Luton currently uses benefits data to this end (https://bit.ly/2OoWv5N). However, councils are losing Housing Benefit data and are dependent on benefits system providers to work with Universal Credit data. This proposal would develop a secure approach to working with UC data, shared by DWPwith councils daily. This real-time picture of employment, income and debt will promote timely support interventions and a faster recovery.
column7: Lower tier and unitary councils routinely work with benefits data to process claims. 50+ councils, including Luton, also use benefits data to pro-actively deliver targeted support on employment, debt, benefits and housing support. Councils are losing visibility over their low-income population. HB data is being lost as UC rolls out and only limited data is held for CTR claims. The DWP shares UC data with councils on a daily basis. This proposal will enable councils to work with this richer UC data six weeks earlier than if they waited for CTR claim processing at a time when targeting support is crucial. Using data to drive delivery to low-income households is established, and the value to councils proven. PIP has experience of processing benefits data to deliver analytical tools. It has DPAs in place with each participating council. DWPs Housing Delivery Division supports the project and can confirm demand for timely and relevant data analytics to inform service delivery. Councils have already taken steps to access further data, having signed a letter calling on DWP to share data on all Universal Credit claims (https://bit.ly/2CuT7Um). This provides a solution to access timely and richer data in a secure and scalable way.
column8: Luton uses benefits data to target housing, employment and financial support working with preventative support teams within customer services. Six councils are actively supporting this project. Policy in Practice has developed a shared data infrastructure for 50+ councils across the UK and crucially understand the governance and information security implications of using benefits data.  Week 1: Kick-off meeting with six local authorities led by Luton. To reestablish project objectives; review data transfer and processing agreements and governance arrangements; develop metrics for project evaluation. Week 2: Understand XML data content and limitations from the five daily UC data files shared with local authorities. Establish necessary Extract Transform Load (ETL) processes, e.g. redacting PII for analytics purposes while retaining a secure identifier so the council can match output data with household records. Week 3: Build SFTP Portal and ETL script to clean the data ready for processing. Ensure the script is scalable. Week 4: Adapt existing analytics to work with new UC data and develop maximum indicators. E.g.by determining household characteristics from benefit levels. Second stakeholder meeting to validate approach and develop use cases. Week 5: Repeat and QA process with data from all six stakeholder councils; resolve issues and produce blog/report on possibilities of new data, and the use to power a local response to COVID-19 Week 6: Complete programming and iterate to meet use cases; adapt LSI visualisations and benchmarking for public view. Week 7: Feedback to include independent evaluation by CIOs, feedback on visualisations from managers and frontline advisors. Week 8: Deliver use cases by providing real-time amalgamated data to Luton for targeted support, interventions and evaluation to stop people spiralling further into debt, mitigate the five week wait and support the 18,000 people currently furloughed in Luton. Track the recovery from COVID and track the effectiveness of interventions.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We know that data can be used to better target employment support, prevent homelessness and track the impact of interventions. The metrics for evaluation will be set at the initial steering group meeting. These will fall into the following categories:
    • Timeliness: Universal Credit claimants would be accessible to Luton’s frontline teams six weeks earlier than now to identify and engage vulnerable residents.
    • Access to broader data: Information on deductions from benefits and the five week wait mitigates against collections or enforcement activity.
    • Adoption: All six participating councils are able to use the platform. Clear documentation enables subsequent adoption by others.
    • Public dashboard: Participating councils see COVID-19 recovery data presented in aggregated via an online ‘living standards’ dashboard (https://bit.ly/3ernXus).
    • Admin efficiencies: e.g. reduction in CTR administration,targeted and timely intervention re housing costs, debt advice, and risk of homelessness.
    • Impact on residents: identification of those in need of support and measuring of impact.
 
  A related InnovateUK backed project to securely automate the monthly transfer of benefits data has been successfully delivered within six weeks. A similar approach to securely processing standardised Universal Credit data on a daily basis can be implemented in a similar timeframe
column13:
The steering group of six councils will use the data to:
  • Proactively engage and track outcomes for people impacted by COVID-19 (unemployment, arrears, vulnerability, housing).
  • Analyse trends in caseload changes, and its implications for local services to inform strategic planning.
  • Evaluation will ensure learnings are shared. A similar pan-London project is now self-sustaining through council contributions. Luton will share the project objectives via blogs, and final outcomes via reports and webinars, disseminated through partners.
  • Policy in Practice regularly works with DWP data and will ensure data handling meets central government governance and information security requirements. External assurance will be provided by an independent partner. Councils will have the choice of running a similar cloud-based setup themselves, or licensing the infrastructure and analytics capabilities from PIP or others.
  • CIOs from participating councils will oversee the production of technical documentation and ensure the platform can be scaled to other councils.
column14: Alongside the need for real-time data to track the recovery from the pandemic, we know that benefits data can tackle digital and social exclusion. Widening access to benefits is a major issue, as is engaging people in employment or debt support. While nine in ten applications to a DHP are successful, analysis of Luton’s benefits data shows that only one in five of households most in need apply. Analytics can proactively identify households often excluded from reactive support mechanisms. A pro-active targeted approach can assist households whose income has been affected by COVID, households missing out on benefits, and households struggling with debt. Councils can offer these household holistic and preventative support, ranging from employment, training and debt support, with engagement over the telephone. Benefits Data is already used to detect fraud and chase arrears. This project will allow for it to be used to help citizens.
column16:
We believe that with Luton, and our local council and private sector partners that we have the capability to deliver this project within the timeframe.

MHCLG can raise awareness of the potential for Universal credit data to drive efficiencies for councils. We estimate that using DWP data we estimate potential administrative savings of £400m to councils and DWP by automating the administration of council tax support.
column17:
MHCLGs support for the project and wider central government interest in the potential for benefits data to be used across government for prescribed purposes.

DWPs active engagement, particular the Housing Delivery Division responsible for the data share with councils.

Administrative Data Research Network (ADR-UK) and LOTI have agreed to back the project and support dissemination. Their equivalents of LOTI in Scotland, Wales, and outside of London would be helpful.

The Institute or Revenues, Ratings and Valuation to engage the local authority data controllers.

column2: Digital Futures
column4:
This project aims to: 

 

Develop systems that enable a better understanding of digital exclusion to inform the allocation of IT resources to residents following a major investment in devices and connectivity. 

Map and communicate the significant training and support offer that is available and being developed to support all members of our communities to enable digital inclusion. 

Develop resources to support and assist volunteers who are motivated to help vulnerable members of their community to access IT equipment and the internet.  

Develop accessible materials and guides to support residents who have previously been digitally excluded. 

Introduce digital jobs in emerging industries for young Westminster residents, future proofing the City’s workforce by introducing cutting edge technology
column7:
• A key priority of the Council’s post COVID-19 Renewal programme is “Digital Connectedness”. A major project has been initiated to raise funds for and distribute up to 5000 devices and provide internet access those who are digitally excluded.
• Young Westminster Foundation, has been engaged to lead on the allocation and distribution of devices and an effective system needs to be set up at pace to develop criteria, consult with partners and school and invite nominations
• Existing courses and sources of support for those who wish to learn or develop digital skills are available but not communicated in a coordinated way. This offer will grow through developing links with Microsoft regarding their training offer. We will also expect training and support to be an element of the service provided by the supplier awarded with the contract to supply devices.
• A review of existing “Digital Champion” services has identified a need for a layer of support for the most vulnerable at the very earliest stage of access to digital. A potential source of such support is from the 3,100 volunteers registered to support wider community needs during the pandemic, many of whom are keen to continue to help.
• Identified delivery partners for taster events for young people promoting emerging technologies, e.g. virtual reality and 3D printing for career pathways led by industry partners including Lendlease, Hobs Academy, Multiplex, Microsoft and others.
column8:
1. Resources • Access to shared expertise, particularly from Young Westminster Foundation, Westminster Adult Education Service and community Library Services (from 1/8/20) • Project management on two workstreams of the project to: a. consult with stakeholders and develop shared criteria, referral mechanisms, multi-agency allocation systems for the distribution of IT equipment and internet access (Drafted by 31/8/20, tested and finalised by 30/9/20) b. Research, consult and develop key products in a short space of time to include:  - Mapping and presenting the current sources of support available regarding digital skills (Draft by 15/9/20)  - Active linking with major partners such as Microsoft to share learning on effective approaches to supporting use of digital technology.  Accessible, entry-level support and training content (30/9/20)  Plain English written documentation on using devices and the internet for the first time (30/9/20)  Identification of opportunities for use of corporate or partner infrastructure to host, publicise and, where appropriate, deliver training and support (15/9/20)  Communications plan to engage community of potential trainers (30/9/20) 2. Involvement from local government sector • We are liaising closely with the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea to identify joint needs and opportunities in relation to this and their proposed Challenge application (Ongoing). 3. Data requirements • Analysis of key community languages spoken by those likely to be digitally excluded (15/8/20) 4. Involvement from the broader population • Consultation with the Westminster Connects volunteer workforce (Ongoing) • Communications to other providers of potential training for trainer trainees (e.g. carers, local businesses and employers) (15/9/20) • Consultation with target population likely to be offered digital support (15/9/20)
  • For those with the aptitude and interest, the City Council and  industry partners will provide 30 paid work placements in a digital role with one of our partners
  • Training partner delivering the VR academy has digitised the offer to run the course virtually
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
• A documented and tested system is in place which manages the identification of need for and allocation of IT resources to vulnerable residents.
• Existing and planned training and support for new users, more advanced users and training for trainers is mapped and communicated.
• Entry level training content and supporting information are produced and published resulting from consultation with residents, potential volunteers and subject matter experts.
• A plan is conformed to provide training and information across a range of appropriate channels (including eLearning and other online methods) to meet identified need.
• We will work with up to 100 young people as part of the taster sessions to be run during the summer. This will showcase the growing number of career options and roles which require Virtual Reality now and in the future e.g. in construction, consultation, health care and psychological fitness
column13:
• We will share ideas and learning with neighbouring boroughs, particularly the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea, during the project.
• The proposed project partner, Young Westminster Foundation, will work closely with its partner Young People’s Foundations across 6 London boroughs as well as Manchester, to share practice and learning.
• Tested process to manage the identification of demand for and allocation of IT resources will be shared..
• Training content and supporting materials (e.g. eLearning content, short written guides) will be transferable to other councils who wish to use it, with scope to provide this in other relevant community languages.
• We will gather and analyse data to evaluate the impact of developing the materials in terms of use by volunteers and the degree to which they are used to engage and support vulnerable members of the community.
• We will share the programme details and findings with colleagues part of Central London Forward
column14:
• Learning from existing local programmes, we are aware that vulnerable and/or isolated residents are wary of engaging with unknown people away from their homes to receive support with digital skills. This project therefore aims to provide resources to build on existing informal relationships to impart knowledge, learning and support.
• A partner has shared existing MHCLG resources (“Volunteers, English language learners and conversation clubs”) which establish good practice in terms of engaging trainers and service users who have English as an additional language.
• Focus groups and testing of materials will take place with residents who are volunteers and those who are digitally excluded to ensure needs are understood.
• Provision of training materials in key community languages.
• Map and highlight opportunities for progression onto structured digital courses to enhance employability skills amongst new users.
• The programme will engage 100 young people from the most deprived wards to encourage and promote digital inclusion
column16: Sharing of relevant learning from previous Digital Challenge rounds and connection with local authorities developing similar projects through the COVID-19 challenge.
column17: Westminster are part of the Greater London Authority and London Office of Technology and Innovation (LOTI) digital collaboration portal with at least 10 boroughs involved. This provides additional opportunities for sharing learning and accessing ideas and advice from other authorities. The intention is that LOTI will be a pan-London collaboration.

column2: Digital Enhancements for Covid19 Community Helpline Project - Sheffield
column4:
Folloing the implementation of the Local Community Helpline we now seek to improve our service offer by:

•	An increased use of technology to improve the customer experience - such as via Artificial intelligence (AI) and Natural Language voice recognition.
•	Improving accessibility for customers who currently struggle to contact the council , especially in the BAME communities by employing multi-language service offers by the use of new technologies as above. These customers are the ones mostly affected by Covid19. This will include improvements our video platform which will enable improved video C19 messages
•	Increase choice of access by implementation of a of webchat service and  ‘Chatbot’ facility during OOHs - to provide key C19 messages 24 hrs a day/7 days a week
- Fully utilise our Storm technical capabilities to provide outbound call campaigns for C19 customers shielding or in isolation
column7: We have recently upgraded our CRM and telephony (Storm) platforms . We now wish to fully exploit these systems to improve access and choice for the C19 customer and thereby improve the service offer for C19 customers.
column8: To follow
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We have already proven that we are able to complete the 4 stages of a project within demanding timescales (72hrs to implementation). Discovery - Define, Develop- Deliver. This has been evidenced in our successful implementation of the C19 Community support helpline which provides excellent support to our community. We have:
  • Delivered over 1000 emergency food parcels
  • Telephoned over 16,000 shielded customers
  • Answered over 11,000 calls
column13:
We will complete a benefits realisation document and also provide feedback via a Lesson learned document.

We will engage a customer panel to ensure all objectives are customer centric and will ensure these are engaged throughout the project. The customer panel will reflect the diverse community of Sheffield especially those in the deaf, and autistic spectrum who will greatly benefit by the addition of these technologies

We will also utilise the  knowledge and support of our current partners and suppliers - Verint and Content Guru who we have good relationships with. To ensure the most effective and efficient solutions are used
column16: None
column17: VAS, Sheffield ACP, CAB, LCRF - existing partners who we will work with for customer feedback and engagement

column2: Exploring ways to bring meaningful connections to the relationship between people who live in Camden and work in Camden providing essential services through tested interventions, with particular attention upon the experiences of BAME workers and residents and to help the area recover from the Covid-19 pandemic and lockdown.
column4:
Our participatory design project will find new ways to build meaningful connections in the interactions between our essential workers and our residents that increase social-connectivity and cohesion, and help Camden to recover more quickly from the Covid-19 pandemic and lockdown.  Our work has a particular focus on the experiences of BAME essential workers and residents. 
It will support officers who both live and work in the borough to pool their experiences to grow and test new ideas. It will generate a digital playbook of activities and ideas on how to bring a more human interface to both physical and digital experiences.
column7: Camden Council is due to complete a rapid six week learning phase working with services to present evidence on inequalities in health, employment, workforce, education, adult social care/shielding and housing. The output will help shape the activities of this project that is focused on the human interface of essential work in the neighbourhood. In July, we launched an active listening exercise across the borough. Working with our VCS, community organisers and front-line staff, we are gathering citizens' lived experiences of Covid-19 and exploring their hopes and fears for the future in a creative and innovative way (words, but also photo and video). The platform is open to all and will allow us to look more deeply into the impact Covid-19 is directly having on BAME communities across the borough.  We have designed the project scope such as what the phase of work will be, the key activities within each phase and the learning and skills development that will be realised as a result of this 8 week process. (View details here) If successful, we will begin mobilising a network of council staff through sharing of lived experiences in preparation to begin the project cycle.
column8: This project will be ready to begin in the week of August 03 and be completed in eight weeks.    Week 1:  Confirm a group of 20 Camden Council employees who are residents in Camden ensuring the group is diverse and reflects Camden’s diversity.  Begin training them on how to engage with each other, residents and other essential workers about lockdown experiences. Week 2 - 4:  Support the group of 20 Camden Council employees to reach out to residents and essential workers (approximately 20) to research a wide range of experiences around the essential worker interface. Week 5:  Support the group to collectively generate ideas that enrich and humanise the interactions between residents and essential workers in Camden. Week 6 - 7:  Support the group to prototype, run and evaluate 3 live pilot interventions & evaluate their impact & efficacy.  Week 8 (and beyond): Share learnings via digital playbook with MHCLG, LOTI and stakeholder organisations. The users for this project are essential-workers and borough-residents, with a particular focus on people who fulfil both of those roles.  This project involves them in the co-design of solutions and builds lasting skills and confidence around user-research and co-design.  Working with our internal HR processes, we will ensure a diverse group of staff (from different teams) as well as encourage a greater number of BAME participation. Similarly working with our participation team, VCS partnership teams and the Camden Safety network, we will select residents from a diverse background from all parts of the borough who are traditionally less involved in these conversations. We are likely to engage with private-sector partners, especially firms that run outsourced services for Councils (eg Veolia, Serco).    In terms of involvement from the local-government sector: We will work closely with partner boroughs and leverage the LOTI and MHCLG networks.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success for this project will be demonstrated in the following ways:
  • Participation from Council staff across different departments in the whole project cycle.
  • Completion of their training in how to do it user research and co-design generally 
  • A bank of community generated ideas that could help people of Camden to have more meaningful connections in real life and potentially enhance the design of physical and digital experiences. 
  • 3 trials in field, completed and evaluated part of this project.
  • Tangible approach to enabling collaborative work with residents via a shared digital playbook.
Impact for this project will be demonstrated in the following ways: 
  • Feedback and recommendations on the trials carried out.
  • Connective-infrastructure ideas to potentially deliver to scale.
  • Increase in cross-service collaboration within the Council - with new connections established and opportunities for potential collaboration surfaced
  • Upskilled staff to tackle issues and share skills learned with others and create a sense of agency.
  • Refreshed attitude among staff: confident, energised that fuels new work in their service teams.
  • Innovative ways surfaced to further drive forward Camden Council’s objectives around inequality and disproportionality.
  • Present a digital playbook of common, validated approaches and solutions that can be reused and built upon by multiple councils.
column13: Both the approach and outputs are reusable The participatory design approach that brings together resident-officers and residents to lead, through co-design activities, will be facilitated & documented in a digital environment. This will form an experiential playbook of activities and learning that can be used by other councils to plan and deliver such projects at speed.  The outputs such as ideas generated and tested will also be captured in this digital playbook that can be reused across different councils and also over time grow through the input of other councils into a common bank of workable ideas, methods and communities.
column14: As part of the selection process, we will work with our participation, VCS partnership and Camden Safety teams who have developed strong relationships and active networks with, for example, Somali and Bangladeshi residents.  After working out a profile of Camden’s diverse population, we will be able to deploy our team of Community Researchers, a team of residents and members of the community that we have trained to carry out social research and engagement – to recruit residents, including digitally excluded segments of the population. Through the user research and co-design phase we will support our staff in running activities in their local neighbourhoods to ensure we are listening and pooling experiences of digitally excluded segments of the population. We will consider the use of postcards, journals, paper prototypes and stories to facilitate easy exchange of experiences, ideas and serve as a record to play back the learnings amongst the group.
column16: We would like to tap into MHCLG’s network to enrol other interested councils and stakeholders; We would like MHCLG to enable connections with other projects being funded related to this challenge.
column17: We will partner with Studio Dhesi and Boyle & Perks to gather shared lived experiences and support with the build of the digital places and channels to support the collaborative work.  We will partner with the Neighbourly Lab, who will train and model the ethnographic engagement, co-design sprint and trialling of the ideas. They will support in the development of the playbook as well as evaluating impact of the different ideas and process as a whole.  We will partner with our community researchers to identify the residents who will participate in this project.

column2: Positive Behavioural Support platform
column4: This project is a discovery piece to inform the creation of an online platform to support parents when they have a recently diagnosed child with ASD. As a result of Covid-19 families with children with ASD have been faced with challenges in their children accessing school. The project will explore the benefits of having an online platform where families can access PBS training, in their own time and space. It will supplement the current face-to-face offer and will widen the reach of KCC services as we are not limited to the number of families the platform will support. The data collected from the platform will also inform the design of our future services.
column7:
KCC has already developed training resources for staff and families around the PBS approach. These resources currently support the delivery of intensive support in the family home. This existing content would be reviewed and updated so that it is appropriate for a virtual learning platform.

Due to the Covid-19 situation KCC has already started to develop a virtual offer for our service-users and this includes some online content. Feedback from our service-users is being collected now and this will inform the design of the platform as well.
column8:
We will follow the agile philosophy of testing out ideas early and often with service-users under realistic conditions to learn how they can be improved and proactively manage risks. We will use low fidelity prototyping – be it through paper or quick digital mock-ups – to enable us to test concepts rapidly and inexpensively. Our work will be divided in eight one-week sprints, and the work will be delivered by a blended team:
  • 2 people from the Autism team (1 day a week)
  • 1 person from our Digital team (1 day a week)
  • 1 person from our Innovation Unit (1 day a week)
  • 4 consultants from FutureGov (1 day a week)
Aimed users:
  • Parents who have a child who is recently diagnosed with ASD
Time line
  • First two sprints will be focused on understanding the problem from a service-users’ perspective.
  • The user insights we get from the first two sprints will help identify opportunities, prioritise concepts and ideas that deliver a better service experience.
  • The last six sprints will be focused on testing our hypotheses, and testing basic prototypes constantly with the service-users;
  • At the end of the 8 weeks we will have a clear understanding of what the need is and we will have tested our (paper-based) prototypes with the service-users to ensure that we have a tested solution that is responding to the needs of our service-user.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Short term
  • Clear understanding of where the need is for parents around this subject, and how they would prefer to engage with our services
  • Feedback from our service-users around the tested content, ways of engaging, design, format of the online platform etc. which will provide us with a clear way forward
  • Tested proof of concept, ready to be developed
Longer term:
  • Wider offer for our families who have a child who received the ASD diagnosis, measurement;
  • Tapping into a new service-user group that might not need the high intensity offer but would benefit from on online offer, measurement: amount of log-ins on our platform;
  • Engagement with the service-users on the platform will provide us with data that can inform the future design of the Autism service, measurement: evaluations that families can do on the platform anonymously, will provide us with more data around the problems they encounter.
column13:
We would seek to leverage our own networks as well as that of FutureGov and MHCLG to disseminate the outputs/outcomes of our work to be used by other councils.

We will work transparently, sharing our experiences during the 8-week project through published weeknotes. We would also leverage the support of FutureGov to develop case studies and blogs on the work, with the hope that these would also be shared positively by the MHCLG.

Most importantly, any product developed will be open source and open to reuse. All training content will be made available to all councils.

Data collected on the platform through “check-ins” and evaluation, trends and patterns will inform future design and will be shared with other councils.
column14:
The platform will meet government accessibility requirements including Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. It will not replace but will operate next to our face-to-face offer.

It will be developed to be mobile first, this means that the platform is designed to be used on smartphones but will also be available for PC’s and laptops. Research has shown that while some people might not have a laptop or desktop, most people do have access to a smartphone. This will increase accessibility because people are able to access it on their phones.

Another project within Kent County Council and Medway is looking at the current state of Digital Poverty within out council and how best to support digitally excluded segments of our population. This project will inform the design of the PBS platform, to ensure that we are not digitally excluding service-users.
column16:
We expect the solution will have relevance for disabled children’s services across the country both during Co-vid episodes and during business as usual. Therefore, we would welcome support from MHCLG in supporting communication to other councils as well as government departments who may feel the platform has relevance for their Covid response efforts, such as the Department for Health and Social Care.

We would also look to MHCLG to support us in validating our approach to evaluation, ensuring we are capturing the right metrics to demonstrate value and impact.
column17: FutureGov, The Tisard Centre, The Challenging Behavior Foundation, National Autism Society

column2: Vulnerable people check in and monitoring system
column4:
To develop a tool to securely log contacts with vulnerable residents and share information between teams and partner agencies. It will
o	Automate referrals to partners
o	Simplify reporting and enable the reopening of services to be targeted for those most in need
o	Enable staff working remotely to securely and instantly share data on vulnerable residents, supporting our multi team / agency approach 
o	Support the reopening of community centers with a “check in” feature to aid track and trace
o	Ensure accurate information is held centrally, critical for the response should we find ourselves dealing with a localised outbreak or lockdown
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
• Community Support Services (CSS) are the welfare leads for shielded work and supporting vulnerable residents. They have supported 3,800 shielded residents and 4,500 vulnerable people with initial and follow up calls.
  • The teams within Community Support Services hold separate records on residents, which have had to be manually combined with data from central government and county. Multiple teams can provide support to one person, so their information will be held in multiple places that are not linked together.
  • Updates and referrals are made manually by email or phone.
  • Management reporting and reports to Surrey County Council are also produced manually.
  • ICT and CSS have developed a basic proof of concept system using a trial of Power Apps to hold customer records and are currently at the testing and data cleaning stage. This funding would be used to cover the development of extra features, supporting links with partner agencies and track and trace components.
column8:
Week 1 – 2
  • Beginning with a basic system that logs a vulnerable customer record, and text notes and the date of a check up call
  • ICT staff undertake technical training
  • Work with Community Support Services staff to map out the calling and internal referral process between teams – to capture questions asked and the information passed between teams
  • Scope out Elmbridge management reporting requirements and work with Surrey CC to scope out their data and reporting requirements, and what we need to build to integrate with their shielding lists.
  • Work with Community Support Services staff
Week 3 – 4
  • Development to enable call flagging by issue and automated reporting internally
  • Development to automate a call schedule for each team
  • Testing and training with Community Centre staff
  • Further development based on testing feedback
  • Work with partner organizations (e.g. Central Surrey Voluntary Action) to map out their referral process and how our system can complement it
  • Development of check in feature in test
  • Work with Records Management officer to ensure GDPR compliance
Week 5 – 6
  • Feedback from partners and further improvements on calling referrals
  • Further development of check in / track and trace logging features and begin phased use at one or two centres for feedback.
  • Training for staff and development of some promotional materials explaining why we are collecting this information and how it will be used
  • Look to incorporate prompts for check in calls to gather feedback from vulnerable residents on how the system has worked so far and areas we can add or improve on
  • Also look to gather feedback from centre users on the check in system, and any further developments we can look into to support them during the recovery process
Week 7 – 8
  • Roll out check in features to other centres
  • Further development work on management reporting and scoping out how we can use the data collected and integrate with other council data to aid recovery forward planning
  Note – the project’s main themes are the check in / track and trace logging features and the automating of referrals and highlighting of trends based on keywords. As the Covid recovery situation develops we may switch the order in which these features are focused on to meet the need at the time.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
• Looking at the number of automated referrals being made
  • Time saved by CSS staff able to be rediverted to other recovery work
  • Feedback from users on how they are finding the check in call / support referral process and if the introduction of the system has given them more confidence in attending the centres again
  • Feedback from partner organisations and county council on the referrals we are making
  • Successful "dummy run" of a test and trace incident - showing that we can provide the required data and mobilise support quickly using the system
  • Actions identified and taken forward following analysis of data gathered to target recovery
column13:
• Power Apps has the ability to export template apps which we could share with other councils
  • Discovery work on process mapping will be made public
  • Development staff will write blog updates on progress, including technical learnings and code that could be reused in similar projects by other councils
  • We’ll document our data analysis finding on trends and how this is informing our recovery plans
column14:
• Software developed will confirm to accessibly requirements
  • Development work will be carried out in partnership with Community Support Services staff, so the design and training can take be tailored to all users needs
  • Solution will be developed and iterated throughout the process based on feedback from users
column16: Helping us link up with other councils to share skills and learning from similar projects
column17: A member of our ICT team has just started a Surrey Peer Group channel in the Local Digital Slack group, so we’ll promote our work there and see if colleagues in other Surrey district ICT departments can input or use our work

column2: Wellbeing Hub
column4: The Wellbeing Hub enables local residents to easily find information about what’s available in their area for their health and wellbeing. It streamlines the search and booking of sessions and the way local organisations interact with residents to improve accessibility to services. Creating a simple way for residents to interact with providers will increase participation, enabling providers to further understand health and social inequalities in the area & inform gaps in provision and where further work is needed. Helpful tools for both the user and provider will make local service use less complicated, more interactive and informative and enhance the local offer.
column7:
So far we have completed a number of tasks get to this stage, we have
  • Worked with our ICT department as well as our GDPR and procurement officers in regards to the use of this as a local authority
  • Created a conception of our design based on our own and others  requirements
  • Looked at what available locally and national for digital offers
  • Researched if any councils already have a digital offer

column8:
During our two month delivery period we’ll tender for the best supplier, create a bespoke digital offer and deliver this to test groups which include other councils, organisations and residents. This enables us to gain valuable insight around usability/ versatility, changing the design as we go. Choosing a designer/developer with a pre-existing platform and services reduces time to market and provides a library of existing features. This product will tackle the issue of community engagement, and also help us address data and reporting through developing a new working practice for councils. By the end of the project we will be ready to launch our Wellbeing Hub and other councils can start to look into acquiring this for their area.

column10: By working with other Local Councils that govern different demographics and populations, it will ensure versatility in the Hubs design through making it suitable for a number of differing requirements and areas of work. Through working closely with our chosen developers keeping to set deadlines and key milestones, we’ll be able to make the most of feedback received from supporting councils, organisations and residents to allow for alterations in design and to document discoveries. Testing will be done through focussing on carefully selected groups which will show a diverse cohort of people in order to gain as wider perspective as possible on the Hub. Simple feedback survey forms will make giving and receiving feedback easy and clear for everyone involved. The results found from those using the Hub in this test period will be collated into a well-presented impact document which will also make comparisons to the slow existing paper practices currently used to achieve the same results,  maximising the impact of our findings.
column13:
By gaining valuable feedback from key supporting Councils during the testing stages, we’ll ensure that the outputs are extremely versatile. Using a varied cohort of councils and organisations will ensure the hub’s suitability for all demographics.

Creating both an app and website, and making them equally adaptable, other local authorities can choose aspects of the design they desire to fit with their demographics, projects, inequalities or strategy focuses. We’re aware that although not every council has a community or wellbeing department, all council’s have an element of work involving engagement with their local community- an area that digitally needs improving in most Local Authorities. The Hub will streamline communication process’ and keep information in one place- making it a more time and cost effective initiative. Cost is a big consideration of ours; making the hub cost-effective, easy to use and modify is crucial to ensure it’s well received and therefore used by other councils.
column14:
The use of an app will mean anyone with a smart phone will have access to the Hub. Fenland District Council has a large number of deprived communities that we work closely with, information gathered suggests these communities access the internet mainly through the use of smart phones.

For the older demographic who may not have smart phones but do have access to a computer, the Website will ensure they’re included. For further help, a simple “how to” user guide can be offered.

Technology training groups which take place at community venues nationally have computers and tablets available for community use meaning all residents will have the ability to access the Hub. We’ll approach technology hubs such as job centres, community groups, schools and colleges locally to allow further reach and advertisement.

Finally, there’ll be the option to book on someone’s behalf, this function will be popular for those with young children or older family members who’re not able to use technology. This will enable their engagement in the Hub with bookings registered by someone else.

column2: Automated Verified Arrangement Project
column4:
Developing an automated process for the assessment / processing of arrangements, ensuring that they are:

•	Quickly verified
•	Accurate
•	Fair
•	Sustainable


Customers can engage with the Council electronically to apply for Council Tax arrangements. Through set parameters, the Council will be able to quickly and automatically assess offers and verify income/expenditure information to assess suitability and affordability. This will be uniquely achieved through the consented Open Banking system.

Increasing the volume of customers with whom the Council engages and avoids progressive recovery and enforcement action where resolution can be achieved by alternative means and so reducing unnecessary face to face contact.
