column2: Self Service - Digital Evidence and Verification
column4: As we move to greater self-service then we are at risk of introducing more barriers to those who lack the technology, skills, and confidence to use technology. Being able to upload digital evidence to support applications for Council Services is essential and any barriers in place to stop citizens doing this may lead to social exclusion and greater service delivery costs further down the line. This project is aimed at giving access to the Digitally excluded a facility to upload documentation to support applications in secure and supported way.
column7: During the pandemic restrictions, Bolton Council customer contact centre staff have been working remotely, serving customers by digital (online) channels or by phone. The access points have been closed for face-to-face visits and are now only partially reopening.    To prepare for opening to more customers, we have developed plans to reconfigure the existing one stop shop so that greater level of self-service can be delivered. Improving and speeding up service delivery whilst ensuring the safety of staff and citizens.    We now wish to enhance this recovery position by giving the facility (initially in the One Stop Shop) for Citizens to scan the relevant evidence to support their applications. This will extend the current self-service offer from the main Town Hall access point. We also wish to investigate a mobile solution so that the verification service can be delivered from other access points within different localities (such as libraries and partner organisations)    We are working with the Revs and Benefits services to look at the evidence they require to support claims, and this will form the basis of this project.  It will also look at how confidence can be given in our approach to address any security concerns that citizens may have in using such a service.    This service will also be extended to other services requiring verification of documents, such as licensing.    The project will reduce the ‘touch-points’ through the workflow to reduce the risk of infection and to increase productivity.    This new approach will need Marketing and Communication support to encourage changes in behaviour. The area will need to maintain Covid-19 appropriate cleaning and support social distancing.

column2: Developing Tools Together for Understanding Demand for Children's Services During COVID Recovery
column4:
During the C19 recovery we expect greater need for children's services.  This is evidenced through increased concerns about young people's mental health, alongside increases in alcohol abuse, domestic violence and mental ill-health among adults which all typicaly rise during economic stress.
But protecting spend on services requires hard data not anecdote, particulalry for early help services.
This project will help Directors of Children's Services to evidence the changes in demand which have already occurred, model their impact on key services, and keep updating that picture month by month as more data on the impact of C19 emerges.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
We are a group of Children's Services data analysts building shareable tools as part of sector led improvement through the Data to Insight project. We identify common needs using our network of 550+ Children's Services analysts in 150 LAs. We built the most used analytical tool in the sector (the ChAT) and host a library of LA-built tools.

In April we identified a common need for demand modelling.

So, we surveyed analysts to better understand DCSs needs and how analysts were meeting them. 71% had been asked to build demand models. 76% said that they would value a common model. The needs driving DCS interest were identified as 'deciding staff allocation ahead of the September return to school', and 'informing October's budget-setting conversations'.

We have distributed an Excel-based prototype to test how it is used, and what changes in functionality DCSs prioritise. This saw 50+ downloads in three weeks, eight LAs have given us feedback, and four have contributed improvements. We have a list of developments and know which will have the most significant impact to make the tool more valuable to DCSs.

We need help to move forward, our community can't deliver the necessary features fast enough to meet C19-Recovery needs, and we are missing skills necessary to deliver more complex features.
column8: The project will have three key milestones: 1. Release After One Sprint (est. day 12). This will be a development of the Excel-based tool containing no personally identifiable information so we can build and distribute it quickly. Support required will be procured separately to that for release 2.0 and will cost under £15,000 so we can procure in 24h. Despite its simplicity 1.0 will deliver critical elements of the features required to make the tool usable in decision-making: a. Transparency about the variables and how they are used in calculations, and explanations of the assumptions in the baseline scenario and our confidence in them. b. The ability for users to configure alternative variables to test scenarios using their own assumptions. 2a. Right to Process Data Confirmed (est. day 12) We have experience of delivering DPIAs in under a week, we have a standard contract and data processing agreement ready to use with the supplier, and the project sponsors are authorised to sign the contract for third party processing. 2b. Data Extract Prepared (est. day 12) As performance and information managers we oversee the teams who will be responsible for providing the data and assess this as low risk. 2c. Supplier for Release 2.0 in Place (est. day 15) Contracts under £100,000 are handled by me rather than the procurement team, removing usual dependency. I have identified the basis for evaluation, and notified potential suppliers that we may need a quick RFP and immediate start. We can deliver this in 14 days. 3. Version 2.0 release (est. day 56) We expect to release 2.0 after three sprints with features enabling LAs to: 1. Identify changes in volume/severity/type of demand from different groups/neighbourhoods 2. Incorporate this intelligence into modelling of demand for key services (Early Help, Child Protection). This is possible in three sprints due to extensive re-use of existing open source components.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success to us is three things:
1. The tools are used by other analysts supporting their DCSs to plan for C19-Recovery, and influence decisions such as resource allocation (to be made in Oct 2020).
2. The experience of developing these tools together from identifying needs, through defining them in the survey and prototype, to being engaged in user research and bug-spotting - supports the CS analyst community's development of an open source culture.
3. The experience raises interest among our community in learning more advancing programming and modelling skills - by providing an example of how they can solve salient problems which Excel cannot.

We will demonstrate utilisation of the tool through tracking downloads (Excel) or clicks (browser-based) from our library - tracking what proportion of LAs are using it. Our ChAT tool has been downloaded by 151/151 LAs, and updates often prompt 100 downloads in 48h.

We will follow this up with a survey to Performance and Information Managers to confirm use (not just download) and to understand how they used it with their DCSs and what impact these analyses made - e.g. how many £m of preventative services protected/re-targeted.
column13:
The five key challenges to re-use in our space are:
1. Doesn't meet a common need
2. Poor marketing
3. Can't produce data extract required for input
4. Can't get permission for novel processing of personally identifiable information (PIN)
5. Can't get permission to install tool

How They Are Overcome
1. We know that the need exists due to our user research
2. Marketing is easy for us as we run the sector's library of tools, have a mailing list of over 570, and run regular regional and national meetings.
3. Both releases will use only data fields present in statutory returns (so captured universally)

Release 1.0 overcomes challenges 4 and 5 by being built in excel and using no personally identifiable information.

Release 2.0 overcomes challenges 4 and 5 by re-using the browser-side calculation method from our LAC Data Alpha.

We will apply a suitable open source licence to all code

We would value support from MHCLG in two areas - both optional but adding value to the project.
1. Delivering a GDS Service Review following release 2. This would help us to develop a backlog of work to be continued by the community which is focussed on delivering a great service. It would also help with adoption of GDS standards in our community.
2. Hosting  small event for senior managers in Children's Services to publicise the tool itself, and to highlight MHCLG's support for the development of open source tools by and for the community.
column17:
The CS-NPIMG is the national network of Performance and Information Managers in Children's Services. Alistair is its chair, and would use the mailing list of 570 analysts and the network of regional chairs to engage the community in user research and bug spotting. From 2021 our apprenticeship programme will provide a workforce of user/contributors who can help to maintain and extend software in Python beyond this project.
ADCS - particularly it's network of regional ADs and DCSs meeting quarterly is also key for dissemination. As chair of the CS-NPIMG on the ADCS Standards, Performance and Inspection Committee, Alistair can request for items to be included on the agenda.

column2: Citizen Welfare Profile Modelling Playbook
column4:
To assess how the data available internally, publicly, via partner agencies or the private sector can support building a greater understanding of our communities and individuals. This understanding could support (these are the deliverables of the project):

•	Data model(s) that identify groups and individuals that  require outreach support for advice, services, or financial intervention. 
•	Insight of debtors that will inform an alternative collection model that  supports  a reduction in un-necessary debt recovery that has with no prospect of recovery.
•	By taking action earlier, preventing vulnerable individuals falling into crisis
•	A playbook to support other Councils sharing and using their data, whether this is in house or if they choose to outsource.
•	A data sharing principles / agreement for use in multi – agency data–sets and settings
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Covid – 19 has been a catalyst for local government to join up services, across partner agencies both in terms of people working together and by sharing data that aids the welfare of citizens. In many cases systems of triage for support have been put in place locally and Councils are already looking at how these integrated services can continue to operate and add value over the recovery period of Covid – 19 and beyond.

 

We know that many services that provide direct welfare support to communities will take some time to return to normal. In addition services that collect the income Councils rely on to deliver these services are also considering how they can re–commence statutory recovery processes (given that access to magistrates courts is limited), whilst also protecting the most financially vulnerable. The proactive use of data sets is a further enabler to support the current environment and prepare effectively for the period ahead.

Authorities named on this bid, have done some initial discovery work on the data sets that could be brought together and have some learning on the data protection principles and alternative collection policies that would need to be put in place
column8:
• Week1 – Recruitment of a delivery team to produce the playbook
  • Week1-3 – Work with partners and providers to refine and test user needs, i.e. an understanding of which data sets reveal which characteristics. Also develop an understanding of what sign offs are needed on each data set to share it.
  • Week3 – Deliver a report that identifies the data sets / sources available and which elements will support the creation of a model.
  • Week4 – Assessment of the data protection impact of the use of this data and production of advice on how to overcome these hurdles.
  • Week4 –Outline agreement for the secure sharing / transfer of data sets for future enablement of the data model and outline data sharing document / DPA protocol – In readiness for any later enablement (a template for use locally, unlikely to have this fully agreed at this stage). For initial testing purposes the use of anonymized data could be used to bridge the gap between development of follow on products and relevant agreements
  • Week 5-8 An outline model that maps the data feeding into it, its purpose and the categorisations that the model will support and how these will support proactive, targeted support or intervention / actions. See specific elements the model aims to address in section 5.5
  • Week 8– Publish playbook so that other councils can engage with the market or their own in house digital and data teams to replicate success.

The playbook will also refer to existing resources available both publically and via market providers.

Many of the elements included in this proposal will already be in consideration by local Councils and in places; early development – This work will supplement and support or enhance these initiatives and also collect data on these existing capabilities, ensuring that work is not repeated across the country.
column14:
As services become increasingly digitalised, insight into individuals and groups who may have less access to digital services or the ability to utilise them, will rely on agencies increasingly using data to identify those with a propensity for services or intervention.

Future provision that aims to minimise the need for users to actively make multiple contact to multiple agencies for services with the new data available can just be awarded supporting a reduction in un-necessary demand or activity by agencies.

This project would actively support those who are digitally excluded by identifying them and supporting them where they need it and in a way that works for them.

Any legal support or expertise on Data sharing agreements / protocols for use of personal sensitive data / referral of any known expertise within the scope of this project. Critical analysis of expected output model and its perceived value to wider agencies.

Access to the provider market, we believe some engagement from specialists here may help them provide better targeted solutions for local government from the playbook.

Our named partners on this bid are actively exploring this area of work and have expertise and practical scenarios where we can test and apply our thinking.

column2: PPE digital ordering process and PowerBi reporting dashboards
column4: NCC has recently developed an end to end digital solution to enable local businesses and organisations to request PPE. The process has an initial order form with triage, escalation, packing and collection stage, keeping customers updated throughout the process. A stock control database has also been created. Powerful dashboards in PowerBi are using the data from the process to create government returns, stock needs, facilitate product recalls, stock trend analysis and much more, all of these reports were previously done manually in Excel. The process completely removes the need for paper, scanning, printing and spreadsheets and the need to create manual reports
column7: Our digital PPE process has been live since 12 June 2020 replacing an existing manual paper process that was time consuming.  Designed around the customer, it provides real-time updates on orders placed, outcome of triage and notifies the customer when the order has been packed and is ready for collection. It also removes the need for customers to sign paperwork or provide identification when collecting PPE. Data from the process is captured to provide real-time dashboards for a range of needs. These provide the council with a true understanding of our PPE provision and the local demand for products.   Since go live additional iterations have been made to further enhance the system and reporting dashboards. A webinar on Nottingham City Council’s digital response to Covid19 (which features PPE) can be seen at https://go.granicus.com/GC-WBN-2020-06-03UKNottinghamCityCOVIDResponse_Recording.html. The Local Government Association  has also created a case study on the process that can be seen at https://www.local.gov.uk/covid-19-good-council-practice  under the heading Personal Protective Equipment (PPE). This resulted in nearly a dozen councils contacting us about the process and asking if we could share it. The full process is designed using Granicus platform and PowerBi for reporting dashboards.
column8: Milestone 1 - Package up the govService process so it can be deployed to other councils. A prerequisite paper would need to created that outlines the requirements (from a council) before the process could be applied e.g. SQL databases created, specified data tables included, connection strings required for integrations and access to PowerBi/govService solution for NCC to assist in configuring the process and dashboards (if required). NCC resource required 3 x FTE for 5 days. Milestone 2 - Setup and configuration – Documentation would be created to allow councils to implement the digital process and replicate the reporting Dashboards (3 x FTE for 4 days). We would expect councils with experienced developers and data analysts to be able to configure the system themselves with minimal support from NCC. We estimate that councils adopting the process may require 2-3 days support each from NCC. We estimate the councils would need to identify 8 -12 days resource to fully implement the process and re-create PowerBi dashboards. Milestone 3 - Communication to Granicus customers (145 councils) to make them aware of the offer. This will provide details (prerequisites) for implementing the PPE process and reporting dashboards. This would be facilitated by Granicus. Milestone 4 – NCC will facilitate several webinars with councils that show an interest in adopting the process. This would demonstrate the full end to end process and dashboards that have been developed. Milestone 5 – NCC would aim to provide the process and support 10-15 councils in implementing the PPE process and dashboards. Any council’s that feel they would be able to implement the process themselves without any NCC support would also be provided with the process and setup/configuration documentation.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: We would aim to support 10-15 councils implement the new PPE process and Dashboards on their platforms.
column13: The bid is focused on sharing our PPE process which is built on the govService platform so other councils can adopt it and re-use it.
column14: We have processed about 400 PPE orders since go live. We have had 100% online take up. There is the option to quickly add the process to our contact center so orders can be processed by a Customer Services Agent over the phone, but at present there is no demand for this

column17: Granicus who provide the govService technology have an extensive user community (145 UK Councils) and commitment to help share learning and good practice across the entire community.  They aim to empower public service organisations to maximise the benefits of digital technologies, reducing the costs and risks of developing solutions in isolation.  Their Learning Centre features blogs, case studies and webinars and operates on Discover, Learn and Share principles.  They have already started to promote the PPE package as good practice.

column2: Community engagement playbook
column4:
The pandemic has placed significant focus on the inequalities that local authorities need to address in order to support communities in a fair and equitable way. It has required councils to engage and consult in different and creative ways due to social distancing.

In response to this, we will conduct research into how authorities have been digitally engaging with residents and other stakeholders during the pandemic. We will engage directly with these users to understand their needs and how well they have been met.

We will explore how effective and equitable that engagement has been, including whether it has generated genuine conversation with diverse communities, and how engagement approaches could be improved.

Our output will be a playbook of digital tools and approaches to guide other councils in best practice and how to apply particular tools to particular circumstances.
column7:
Within our organisations, and then in conversation between organisations, we have discussed the challenges around community engagement during, and emerging from, the pandemic. The key questions we identified were:
  • How can we meaningfully engage and consult with residents when meeting face-to-face is not an option?
  • How do we ensure the people that we engage with represent a broad range of voices?
  • How do we engage with the parts of society that are typically hardest to reach?
  • How do we increase engagement overall?
We have also explored existing research. In Digital Democracy: The tools transforming political engagement, Nesta says: 'Digital democracy can achieve deeper and broader participation; it can contribute to a richer public sphere for argument and debate than was ever possible with traditional mass media'. The State of Digital Engagement adds: ‘local authorities are [currently] only increasing capacity for one-way and directed participation, rather than meaningful citizen participation and engagement’. A Select Committee on Democracy and Digital Technologies reported: ‘...local government must acquire and develop greater digital capacity and skills to facilitate digital democratic engagement’. These sources - and our experiences from our own organisations - align with an opportunity the pandemic has created to work differently and increase engagement. We need a sharable model for effective and equitable engagement, which is what this project sets out to achieve.

column9: MHCLG runs the procurement and project
column10:
This project will give us the chance to take learnings from the Covid-19 response and remodel council engagement with communities with significant longer term benefits. As described, we will produce an evidence-based playbook of effective practice for digital engagement. This will clearly describe user needs around resident engagement and how accessible and readily available tools and approaches can meet these needs. The discovery work will give us insight into what creative and digital tools and methods councils have been using during the pandemic and provide us with data and insight into what has worked well, what hasn’t and what councils have learned. This kind of evidence will provide us with the material to create a helpful, tried & tested playbook that all councils can use to help them effectively engage and consult with their residents. We will promote and share our outputs widely through a range of local government communications channels. We will conduct show-and-tells or live-streams, and make video recordings available to encourage a wider understanding of the work. Success and impact will be demonstrated by:
  • Being able to evidence the content of the playbook based on research and data
  • Increased numbers of residents engaging with their authorities
  • A more diverse range of residents engaged in conversations with their authorities
  • Positive feedback from residents who have been able to engage with their council and democracy in a way that suits them and fits in with their lives
  • From a human centred design perspective, many councils are rightly championing designing services for user need, and this work will continue to grow this capability and provide a playbook which will help councils understand how to practically and successfully engage with residents.
  • Identifying a mechanism for users of the playbook to contribute to future iterations
If there are similar bids submitted we would be happy to collaborate to ensure broad and representative user needs are incorporated and that our outputs have the maximum reach.
column13:
The research we have already conducted suggests strongly that there is a common need across councils for an enhanced digital community engagement model. By conducting further research as part of this project we will ensure that the recommendations in our output meet both the needs of external users (i.e. residents, partner organisations such as parish council, and other community groups) and those of local authorities.

All councils are facing the same challenges and some councils are further ahead in terms of using digital tools to engage with residents than others. This playbook, based on research with numerous authorities, will provide a practical and usable guide for all councils grappling with this same challenge and offer them tried and tested solutions which they can pick from and use depending on their needs.
column14:
All recommended tools and approaches included in the playbook will meet WCAG 2.1 accessibility standards. Our research will also include people with additional access needs.

Part of our research will be to identify digitally excluded groups to ensure they are included in consultation and engagement approaches. For instance, at the beginning of the pandemic several local authorities attempted engagement through printed materials, which proved difficult to produce and circulate in a timely manner given the restrictions and continually changing situation.

column2: Learning Ecosystems
column4: Learning Ecosystems is a discovery piece to inform the creation of an online platform to support schools with developing their virtual offer in the event of further lockdown. The understanding captured will shape the content and format of this resource. The online platform will offer practical guides around all aspects of a virtual school experience, including developing virtual teaching resources, options for delivering virtual lessons, online assessments and feedback, online engagement with parents to support home learning, and all the safeguarding considerations of delivering a virtual school offer. It will support schools to assess their hardware and software requirements.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Initial capture of learning and feedback from Kent schools to KCC CoVid Children’s Services Recovery cell has highlighted wide discrepancies between virtual offers provided by schools. Some schools were able to mobilise swiftly and deliver a robust and varied digital offer to their pupils. However, for other schools, particularly smaller primaries, this has been more of a challenge and work has been set using more traditional approaches. Digital poverty of some pupils has contributed to the challenge for some schools.

A survey of Kent schools to assess confidence around virtual delivery, enthusiasm for an online platform, and key areas of support need, content ideas and suggestions has been completed. The results reinforce the feedback heard by the Recovery cell around variation in confidence, skills and ability to deliver a virtual offer. The average confidence level is 5 on a scale of 1 to 10, where 1 is not at all confident and 10 is very confident. Almost one quarter of respondents scored themselves at 1 or 2.

83% of respondents said that they would benefit from an online platform to share resources and best practices, and 41% reported that accessing support in delivering virtual lessons would be their top priority.
column8: We will adopt an agile approach, working in a bended team, with input from an external consultancy (FutreGov). The work will be collaborative, working closely with service users (schools) and other stakeholders, so that we can be iterative and flexible in our planning, delivery and implementation of the project. We will prototype what an online platform might look like, the content, whether primary/secondary focus, open source or register to access. Throughout we will be seeking service-user feedback so we can best understand and meet schools’ needs and wants. The project will be for 8 weeks, broken into short sprints: Sprint 1- Project kick off Understand the aim and objectives of the project, collate existing knowledge and research around best practice, stakeholder identification and mapping, develop a broad project plan Sprint 2/ Sprint 3/ Sprint 4 – user engagement, prototyping Engage with users to identify and test hypotheses, gain insight, prioritise concepts and start designing platform, content and format, constant review and testing with service users and other stakeholders. By the end of sprint 4 this will include an exploration of where the platform can be hosted, who would develop and maintain it, and how to access it, where to signpost to additional support and guidance (including role of The Education People, and KCC in-hose teams such as Digital services and the Education Programme). By the end of the project we will have a tested solution that meets the needs identified by our research and service user feedback for developing into an alpha model.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Short term:
  • Clear understanding of schools’ needs and wants with regard to delivering education virtually
  • Feedback from stakeholders around the suggested design, format and content of the online platform
  • Understanding around possible flexible and hybrid delivery mechanisms (so that schools are able to switch to or deliver a hybrid of education in the event of a local lockdown)
Longer term:
  • Increase in schools’ confidence to deliver virtually
  • More pupils able to access education virtually/have their needs met
  • Delivery of best-practice virtual lessons
  • Reduced digital exclusion for pupils
  • Engagement through evaluations and feedback, data-capture and platform analytics to understand more about what schools use and the impact the platform has on delivery, school ability and confidence, and ultimately quality of teaching and learning.
column13:
We would seek to leverage our own network as well as that of FutureGov and MHCLG to disseminate the outputs/outcomes of our work to be used by other councils.

We will work transparently, sharing our experiences during the 8-week project through published week-notes. We would also leverage the support of FutureGov to develop case studies and blogs on the project, with the hope that these would also be shared positively by the MHCLG.

Most importantly any product developed will be open source for any school or establishment to access and reuse. Data captured from the platform can be collated and shared to inform wider projects, research and service design/delivery around delivering virtual education.
column14:
The solution will meet government accessibility requirements including Web Content Accessibility Guidelines.

KCC Children’s Services Recovery Cell has highlighted wide discrepancies in the digital offer made by schools to support delivering a virtual education. Some schools have really embraced this approach and use a wide variety of resources and software, other schools have really struggled and have barely been able to deliver a virtual curriculum. This is because of their own limitations in terms of hardware, access to devices, IT skills and knowledge, as well as those of the pupils and families they are supporting.

This project proposal aims to tackle this issue - how to support schools to better deliver virtual education to all pupils, including those who are digitally excluded/subject to digital poverty.

This discovery proof of concept project will explore and support schools to best deliver a virtual curriculum to digitally excluded segments of population.
column16:
We expect the solution will have relevance for schools and education services across the country. We would also look to MHCLG to support us in validating our approach to evaluation, ensuring we are capturing the right metrics to demonstrate value and impact for schools and their pupils.

column2: Reducing Food Poverty and Waste through Digital Community Engagement
column4:
Havering, Camden and Enfield Councils are partnering with OLIO to develop and implement a digitally enabled Food Waste Reduction Campaign that will tackle food waste and food poverty issues in the boroughs. Using the existing networks and OLIO’s digital platform we will be able to quickly and accurately assess where and how we are making an impact.

Through community networks, food banks and local business partnerships we can take the step away from dependence towards self-reliance. From these newly established networks we can provide access to more resources and connect residents to opportunities to enable communities to flourish.
column7:
Work has been carried out as part of the ‘Havering Love Food, Hate Waste Strategy’, Camden and Enfield’s Reduction and Recycling Plans aiming to tackle Food Poverty through food banks, community engagement through local area Community Hubs and Waste reduction through recycling and sharing initiatives.

This work has included establishing the Havering Digital Community Hub, as well as working in collaboration with North London Waste Authority to provide community fridges across North London for food sharing. An online free food sharing app moves the concept of food sharing online, making it highly scalable and accessible.

OLIO - www.olioex.com

OLIO connects neighbours with each other so that surplus food can be given away rather than thrown away.

The OLIO digital platform will promote food sharing within neighbourhoods and has been shown to:

-Reduce waste costs and food poverty through directly reducing the amount of food thrown out unnecessarily reducing waste and CO2 while increasing food availability to a community.

-Track and measure food poverty through a world-first thematic map, developed in collaboration with the University of Nottingham.

- Reduce social isolation and strengthen community cohesion through developing networks and creating positive change, building community resilience and self-reliance.
column8:
Food waste and food poverty in Camden (C), Enfield (E) and Havering (H) will be addressed by promoting digitally enabled food sharing practices. The local authorities will gain the ability to measure food waste and food poverty within their constituency while offering a solution that all households can engage with. The private sector partner is OLIO, the digital tool to support food sharing and data capture. C and E campaigns will be amplified on the North London Waste Authorities (NLWA) social media channels with £2000 of their own budget. Schedule:
  • Week (W) 1:
○ C, E, H, OLIO and NLWA execute digital marketing via social media channels ○ E and H prepare outreach campaign ○ C, E, H promote food sharing in recycling newsletter
  • W 2:
○ E and H schedule leaflet campaign ○ E and H prepare billboards and bus stops campaign ○ C, E, H promote food sharing in local magazine ○ NLWA amplification (Until week 8) ○ H to promote campaign on digital community hub (Until week 8)
  • W 3:
○ E and H leaflet & outreach campaign execution ○ E and H order assets for billboards and bus stops
  • W 4:
○ E and H launch billboards and bus stop campaign (Until week 8) ○ C, E, H feature food sharing and good news stories on websites
  • W 5:
○ C, E, H feature food sharing in Covid newsletter and in Waste and Recycling newsletter
  • W 6:
○ E inserts flyer in bin liner distribution to all households ○ C, E, H feature food sharing in local magazine
  • W 7:
○ C, E, H feature food sharing in Covid newsletter
  • W 8:
○ C, E, H feature promote food sharing in local magazine ○ C, E, H feature food sharing in websites

column10: Success will be determined by an objective assessment of the impact that has been made through the campaign in the Boroughs of Havering, Camden and Enfield. This impact will be measured using analytics data from the OLIO Platform which will give an indication of the level of community engagement, and act as an early indicator of the level of community action toward food waste reduction. Data points that will be captured are the number of new users, the number of meals shared, the monetary value of food given away on the platform, the number of door step exchanges in local communities, the equivalent number of water litres saved and weight of diverted CO2. On the University of Nottingham food poverty map we will increase granularity in geo-based data over time signalling the presence or risk of food poverty in each constituency at a street level. A comparison will also be drawn between the physical and digital approach used for Havering and Enfield and the digital only approach used for Camden.
column13: This project is supported by East London Waste Authority and endorsed by NLWA, University of Nottingham and Advance London SME support programme of the London Waste and Recycling Board (LWARB), the partnership of the Mayor of London and the 33 London boroughs to improve waste and resource management. Funding to trial the promotion and use of the OLIO online food sharing app. supports our aims to reduce the amount of avoidable food waste that is thrown away each year; we believe it could also have a direct positive impact on food poverty. Advance London will write a case study to share the key outcomes of the project and lessons learned of the collaboration between an innovative digital start-up and local authorities. The intent is to create a blueprint to scale the project to other London boroughs and will also be promoted through LWARB communication channels.

Although over 95% of the population of the UK has access to a smartphone, by having a wider reaching cultural impact within communities, the project will have an overarching benefit to even those without digital access.

column2: Low contact people management
column4:
The project seeks to implement a digital rota and rostering solution to enable service managers to work at arms-length while still ensuring continuity of service.

Managing staff with reduced day to day contact and on site presence has been identified as a critical path towards ensuring operational needs are met.

The project is identified as a means to ensure employees are able to receive critical updates and manage their tasks and duties. For example identification of required equipment and delivery such as PPE in real time at the same time as managing client status and health & safety.
column7:
Prior to the start of COVID it was identified via a report conducted by IT, and a business case by the Service Managers that the best solution across the council was for a remotely hosted rota management solution enabling digital rostering of staff across a wide variety of roles.

A revised business case was drafted by one of the Service Managers in Contact Cares to outline the basic requirements and end goal of any project, with objectives and outcomes in April 2020 in the midst of the COVID Pandemic

A tender was arranged to run for the end of June that would identify viable suppliers in the market. While this is coupled to our telematics requirements, the underpinnings of the tender exercise are robust for identifying working solutions that are ready to deploy.

Award: 1st August
Deploymentcompletion: 21st September (week of)

While deployment of the platform is expected to be quicker, any additional configuration and setup to meet our Covid requirements pushes the solution out to 4-6 weeks, so a prospective due date of late September. Some hardware may be required to ensure that this is successfully deployed which will be procured as part of the project, but we are hoping existing devices will be sufficient.

Elimination of paper processes and historic time intensive management techniques by converting all rotas to a digital platform that can be accessed from a wide range of devices. Contribution to both the efficiency agenda and the green agedna with improved environmental impact

Efficiencies gained in the day to day management load
Improved speed and efficiency of the building of rotas, management, and deployment of staff into critical roles based around critical needs arising from changing demands. This will result in greater staff productivity, improved throughout and financial savings.

Feedback on how our customers who are amongst the most vulnerable within our society feel the system is best meeting their needs and identifying any further improvements we may be able to leverage.

column2: Oxfordshire All In
column4:
Oxfordshire All In’s (OAI) core idea is a comprehensive, accessible, easy to use, up to date Oxfordshire resource, mapping and supporting community engagement and action. 
The simple, effective and proactive work - on mapping, data collection/sharing, community engagement,
sustaining community action, support for cross-sector partnership/collaboration, openness, and
accountability - has been widely praised and remains critical for further potential waves of Covid-19 and for
supporting a strong recovery longer term. 
Our bid is two fold to sustain and improve this critical resource, and realise its huge general potential to
contribute to more effective, responsive, accountable cross-sector community infrastructure; bringing all scales of organisations together interested in community and social action to improve outcomes for residents.
column7:
OAI has been central to facilitating a more effective Covid-19 response through

(1) hosting the only locally-managed, user friendly, public map of all charities operating in
Oxfordshire,

(2) work with partnerships/forums across the county to share best practice and coordinate
between them, and help them connect with people and communities,

(3) work on a data sharing and establishing new ways of working between key infrastructural organisations.

This work has been welcomed positively for its immediate impact, and also for demonstrating a
promising and much needed roadmap for continuing to work more effectively in the future, particularly
within and across the voluntary and public sectors . there is the potential that business to engage through corporate social responsibility policies in the sector too.
OAI is quickly realising its potential as a more general facilitator for community action and support.
An important new priority around cross-sector sharing/collaboration, effectiveness, openness, and accountability is prompting this application

Currently, OAI is unique in the work we are doing in Oxfordshire. Since March all the
resources have been targeted at supporting existing infrastructure across the county to great effect
