column2: Economic Resilience and Indicators – supporting local communities and business to thrive
column4:
Councils stood up services at pace in our Covid-19 responses with the initial urgent efforts focused on supporting the medically vulnerable. As we plan for recovery, it is crucial that our resilience plans consider resultant economic shocks, sector volatility, changing consumer behaviours and risks specific to local socio-demographic profiles.

Our project will develop an Economic Indicator Framework which includes interventions to support local economies and communities to thrive. Although initial output is focused on Barnet’s (and West London) economy, the methodology and indicators are applicable to inform other Councils’ monitoring of economic resilience and outcomes, e.g. business remobilisation, workforce re-engagement.
column7:
We have conducted several dedicated analysis looking at the financial and economic impact to our local economies, including across the West London region.

At a service level, we have analysed costs factoring in changes to supplier unit prices and modelled the impact on anticipated service levels going forward. We analysed revenue income impacts: short-term impact via lost income and medium-term impact via changing market conditions.

At a local economy level, we have modelled different situations to inform our recovery planning. We have modelled three situations by looking at changes in council tax via a cash comparison, unemployment forecasts and the possible impact on homelessness and council tax revenues, ongoing Covid-19 pressures, new homes funding and anticipated Governmental funding levels.

In addition, EY have supported the Council to undertake a financial resilience assessment in order to determine potential interventions. This is based on an economic modelling tool which draws on macro and local data to develop economic forecast scenarios and impact on the Council’s finances, in-year and over the Council’s Medium-Term Financial Period.

This identified key areas of financial risks with focused recommendations to mitigate the impact. In particular, the review has identified measures and interventions that would benefit the local economy and its recovery and resurgence from the current crisis, at the same time improving the financial resilience of the Council. Key themes and socio-economic risk factors from these findings are all relevant to other Councils, as all are faced with similar challenges albeit to varying degrees.
column8: This project will be jointly delivered between our Finance & Digital Innovation teams and an external agency (EY) who will provide an economic modelling tool and specialist Economic Modelling resources. Mobilisation - Week 1: Develop understanding of the data environment along with a summary of key economic challenges and objectives (for Barnet’s local economy and wider local government sector). Identify additional Councils who would like to jointly shape this. Phase 1 – Weeks 2 – 3: Establish a framework of economic indicators and key lines of enquiries with appropriate focus on leading and lagging economic indicators resulting from Covid-19 impacts relevant to local area/sectors. Data requirements to inform this will include publicly available socio-economic and business performance data along with locally sourced data from businesses and community forums. Phase 2 – Weeks 4 – 5: Collate interventions and recommendations that can be utilised to improve economic outcomes and objectives based on identified challenges. This will draw on Barnet’s proposed economic policy interventions, example recovery initiatives being utilised in areas across the UK, as well as drawing from EY’s global practice on lessons learnt from other governments’ interventions and business remobilisation measures to date. Phase 3 – Weeks 6 - 7: Application of the economic indicator framework to ensure interventions being implemented have a robust means of being appraised, monitored and optimised. This deployment would create a continuous flow of insight that supports an evidenced-based assessment of the effectiveness of interventions both on the local economy as well as the financial resilience of Barnet Council. Users will include the Council, as well as Local Authorities and local businesses in the West London economic region who can refine and optimise their uptake of such interventions. Show and Tell – Week 8 We will commit to presenting our project approach output and findings so other Councils can benefit from this to inform their economic recovery plans.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The ultimate success of the project is determined by the Council being empowered with a framework and an ability to monitor and refine the interventions they are deploying within the local economy. The project will deliver value on two fronts. Firstly, interventions will be appraised to the degree to which they support the local economy recover and remobilise from the current crisis. The framework will ensure the Council has an ability to identify interventions to bridge financial gaps, monitor impact on outcomes, including business remobilisation, workforce re-engagement, business resilience, investment options.

Secondly, the project would also be deemed a success if the interventions are simultaneously able to improve the financial resilience of the Council. This would be measured through re-analysing the impact on the Council’s financial standing, both with respect to the impact on revenue budgets in the current financial year and over the following four years. This will assess the degree to which the interventions support the Council’s ability to deliver services despite potential economic volatility and the impact on core funding streams and expenditure budgets.

The Economic Indicator Framework co-developed through this project will have directly applicability for other Councils facing very similar economic resilience challenges.
column13:
We want our methodology, findings and insights on economic resilience indicators to inform other Council’s economic recovery planning where there are similar challenges and socio-economic vulnerability factors, e.g. sharing how we utilise economic impact findings to inform the Council’s interventions on skills & development; business remobilisation, workforce re-engagement and investment opportunities. We have identified core networks to communicate the work:
  • Deputy CFO Network - network for local government Deputy Finance Directors
  • NSLT - network for local government Finance Directors
  • ADEPT (Association of Directors of Environment, Economy, Planning & Transport) – network for Place Directors and directors of local enterprise partnerships, sub-national transport bodies and corporate partners across key service sectors.
We commit to partnering with EY to present findings from our Economic Indicator Framework at the Deputy CFO network as part of the guest speaker program. We would also work with trade applications such as Public finance to share meaningful insights.
column14:
Barnet Council has outlined priorities for our Borough Plan, which include developing prevention and intervention models to address socio-economic vulnerabilities due to Covid-19. This includes assessing how our services are representative of wider vulnerabilities and accessibility factors. We will ensure this forms part of our Key Lines of Enquiries when assessing socio-economic impacts to local communities.

In addition, outputs of the project are proposed to be available across a range of formats. These would ensure appropriate access to all digitally excluded segments of the business population, via relevant business groups and community forums.
column16:
As our project will consider both macro-level and local economic data across multiple sources, we will work with MHCLG to identify additional policy input, research and/or statistical trends which may be useful to enrich our economic models.

We will also value support to identify relevant forums where we can collaborate with sector partners and to share our findings around an economic indicator framework for recovery – in particular during the Show and Tell phase of our project.
column17:
This will depend on what the detailed analysis says about the effective interventions for Barnet’s local economy. We anticipate working closely with the voluntary and community sector through our Barnet Communities Together Network - a 1,500 membership strong network including the public, private, faith and community sectors who can support delivering community-related initiatives at pace. Specific examples:
  • Strategic partnership with Cambridge Education to deliver skills-based interventions for the local community
  • BOOST – partnership with Barnet Homes, JobCentre Plus, colleges delivering employment, skills and wellbeing projects

column2: Learning from Howbusyistoon.com - Citizen focused data sharing to encourage economic renewal
column4: Newcastle City Council wants everyone to be confident when they come into town, and this is essential for post Covid economic recovery. We have worked alongside teams at Newcastle University and NE1 to develop www.HowBusyIsToon.com as a Beta.  The site uses real-time computer vision data from cameras that measure footfall levels in the City Centre and presents information via a website to show residents how easy it is to social distance.  We want to build on the early uptake success to understand further user needs on data that will support a return to the high street, and re-develop the site in a way which can be shared as an online service for other locations.
column7: Public, private and academic partners in Newcastle worked together to develop www.HowBusyIsToon.com as a Beta.  The site uses real-time computer vision data from cameras that measure footfall levels in the City Centre and presents information via a website to show residents how easy it is to social distance.  Business intelligence has been provided by NE1 Business Improvement District and Newcastle City Council, data has been provided by the Urban Observatory at Newcastle University, with the analytic capacity being provided by the National Innovation Centre for Data and Newcastle University’s Research Software Engineering team. HowBusyIsToon soft launched in early July to test the concept and the public’s reaction. There have been more than 25,000 visits to the site, a significant level of social media engagement, positive political and user feedback and interest in the scheme from other areas such as local public transport providers and other cities too.  We have seen that the website demonstrates how data can benefit everyday life, increase public confidence in a safe return to the high street and support economic renewal.    It also helps provides intelligence to inform local authority COVID19 responses and communications planning.
column8:
We will maintain the existing committed core capacity in place for HowBusyIsToon.com with data science and analysis capacity from Newcastle University and business intelligence coming from Newcastle Council and NE1.  We will look to procure external capacity to support user research, user experience/design, and additional software build capacity if required.  We do not anticipate delays in doing this and have framework procedures in place to do this as soon as funding is confirmed.

Milestone 1 (Week 1-2): User research on other needs which would be useful to address within HowBusyIsToon v2 concept.  What other data would people like to see within the site.  How would they like to consume it?  What are the limitations of the existing HowBusyIsToon site? Incorporating existing feedback on Beta site.   Research undertaken by external organisation to be procured as soon as funding confirmed.

Output 1: User research report on citizen focussed data requirements to support a safe return to the high street.  Clearly articulated user needs and priorities to inform prototype.

Milestone 2 (Week 2-3): Build prototype of HowBusyIsToon V2 reflecting user needs in data and design functionality.  User research and engagement on prototype.   Build and research undertaken by external organisation to be procured.

Output 2: Prototype of HowBusyIsToon v2 (show and tell)

Milestone 3 (Week 4 - 7):  Iteration of HowBusyIsToon v2 through to Beta including ongoing user research and engagement, further incorporation of data.  Build and research undertaken by external organisation to be procured.

Output 3: Beta of HowBusyIsToon v2 (show and tell)

Milestone 4 (Week 7-8):  Packaging of HowBusyIsToon as a shareable platform for other towns and cities

Output 4: Open source product for integration with local data sources

Milestone 5 (Weeks 1-8): Measuring success and impact

Output 5: Benefits report and case study on impact of project
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: We already know that there is a user appetite for citizen focused data through the high interest and usage of the HowBusyIsToon beta site.  We are also aware of research and benchmarking undertaken by Professor Colin McFarlane (Durham University) exploring perceptions of crowded places.  The survey between June 10<sup>th</sup> and 24<sup>th</sup> found that respondents were at ease with crowds in Newcastle city centre before the crisis, but the pandemic has dramatically changed their perceptions of crowded situations.  39% of respondent said they would be ‘very uncomfortable’ of crowded transport stations, and only 5% of respondents would go to crowded sites in the City Centre if social distancing was further reduced.  Yet we know that unless people do return to the high street/city centre, the financial impact through loss of income and employment will be devastating.  We need to help people feel more comfortable with returning to the city centre, using data as a means to do this.  We will engage with Professor McFarlane throughout the project to reassess perceptions of returning to the City Centre, and also undertake qualitative and quantitative user analysis to understand the direct impact of the HowBusyIsToon v2 site.
column13:
The existing system is tightly coupled to the Urban Observatory’s systems. We will reengineer the system to ensure compatibility with commercial footfall sensors such as those available from Streets Systems, CityVerve or Springboard. Not all authorities will have these sensors, but most will have an existing CCTV infrastructure; the UO developed a computer vision model for vehicle and pedestrian counting in collaboration with the Office for National Statistics over the last three months which will soon be open sourced; it was tested using CCTV feeds from 21 different authorities. HowBusyIsToon will be made compatible with the UO-ONS system.

The UO team will dedicate two days per week to supporting other local authorities in deployments and will continue to work with central government to ensure the resulting analysis is also available for public health purposes.
column14: We have already identified features to improve accessibility based on feedback from the beta site, this includes the use of traffic light colour coding which is not accessible by those with colour blindness (up to 10% of male population).  We will ensure that we incorporate accessibility and accessibility testing fully within our technical specification for re-build.  We will be able to provide access to the site through our People’s Network public access PCs and will also look to provide snapshot data from the site through other public access screens in communities where possible.
column16: We are already aware of other authorities who have shown an interest in howbusyistoon.com.  We would appreciate MHCLG support in raising interest/awareness with other locations through promotion of show and tell events/the project.
column17: We will utilise our Innovation Partnership with Urban Foresight (at no additional cost) to ensure that the project remains on track for delivery as stated.  We will also engage existing networks including the LGA, the M7 and other Business Improvement Districts to understand interest from other places.

column2: Impact of Covid and social vulnerability: Changing lives through understanding and managing risk. Using holistic life event risk scoring to target preventative activity at households most likely to be impacted by poor outcomes linked to Covid
column4: We know that certain risk factors in life increase your risk of a poor outcome. Job loss and low income, institutional background, family breakdown or bereavement. All factors that are openly known to public sector institutions, but which are seen as events in themselves rather than factors which increase the chance of a more expensive and destructive crisis in the future. This has never been truer than under Covid. Our project will confirm scoring for such events and pilot targeted engagement with high risk households to seek to prevent outcomes through the application of prevention activity rather than demand response.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column8:
Discovery phase – Weeks 1-2
  • Mapping of customer journey activity within minimum of 4 of the partner authorities, focused on cohorts who have experienced a ‘crisis’ – homelessness, arrest, mental breakdown. Root cause analysis of underlying risk factors within population.
  • Allocation of risk scores (from HDC derived from Holmes-Rahe Stress score) and cost figures from the Manchester CA CBA tool to customer journey maps
  Define phase – Weeks 2-3
  • Week 2 – Request for personal data of agencies holding data that relate to known outcomes of Covid – for pilot activity – New UC claimants, Recently bereaved, children not been in school etc.
  • Week 3 - Analysis of customer journey maps, and identification of other risk event data that is explicit in public sector data holdings, rather than abstract. Request this data.
  • This work will identify Covid impacts most likely to have significant long-term effects on residents’ lives without early intervention and support from partner agencies.
  Develop phase – Weeks 1- 3
  • Week 1 – develop agreed data architecture to define data fields for agency returns, and to agree how data will be held. Building on HDC power apps model (office 365 suite).
  • Week 1-4 – Build digital integration from Power Apps to Gov.notify to allow bespoke targeted digital comms with links to digital comms.
  • Week 1-4 – Customisation of PowerApps to handle reactive comms
  • Week 1 - 3 Develop suite of solutions and mitigation activity (to be held digitally wherever possible to meet needs of those at increased risk) e.g. employment support, befriending, mentoring, rent deferral etc. Logic being that solution structured around resident needs not delivery and organisational structure. Single front door.
  • Week 3-4 - Develop outbound scripts on back of needs and available solutions/mitigation
  • Week 3-5 Develop targeted digital comms content to run mitigation campaigns
  Deliver phase – Weeks 4 - 8
  • Run targeted campaigns, both digital and outbound personal contact, on the basis of risk which proactively signpost people to solutions and resources that they were not previously aware of. Driving up individual resourcefulness. Stress test assessment of the limits of current data sharing arrangements practically and relevant legislation (Digital Economies Act and/or other legal gateways).
  • As part of campaign capture unmet needs, and capture structured responses, to better inform risk model, and refine future comms. An assessment of the potential scale/volume of those made more socially vulnerable by Covid and therefore what this means for resource planning, future ways of working etc.
Assessment – Week Seven – What has been the impact of calls and take up. What has been the reception to contact which seeks to understand the needs of the customer, rather than the services of the agency.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Deflection rate and take up of signposted solutions.
  • Evidence provided by signposted solutions of effective engagement and impact e.g. income maximization rates, people supported into work, mentors assigned, volunteer hours donated, and future employment references assigned.
  • Quantify demand – By the identification of a control groups who do not receive engagement and define differential engagement rates with services e.g. customer services, housing, revenues and benefits, to quantify demand.
  • Quantify impact by tracking number of negative outcomes e.g instances of homelessness, mental health crisis, social care involvement and employment rates between engaged group and control group.
  Access
  • Track take up of digital solutions outside traditional operational hours, driven by outbound messaging and digital comms.
  • Track number of people who find a solution without the need to speak to a member of staff. Proving a capacity and scalability point to enable more solutions to be delivered with limited resource.
column14:
Outbound text and e-mail contact will be augmented by outbound telephone contact. Where advisors phone residents on the basis of risk and seek to understand and signpost. These conversations will also be used to refine and improve digital content. Therefore digital skills and resources are not a necessity. We would also seek to work with community groups to make the resources available within community environments for assisted self-service.

The tailored nature of the outbound contact would also allow for the distribution of messages in multiple languages.
column16: Support on data sharing discussions, and potentially supporting ‘pilot’ / ‘discovery’ activity through sharing data that is not commonly shared, particularly government data such as pertaining to specific data around people newly effected by Covid and were previously unknown to local vulnerability services. E.g. New UC claimants. To ensure that people are able to navigate the support.
column17: Potential for support from NESTA and Centre for Public Impact.

column2: Citizen Welfare Profile Modelling Playbook
column4:
To assess how the data available internally, publicly, via partner agencies or the private sector can support building a greater understanding of our communities and individuals. This understanding could support (these are the deliverables of the project):

•	Data model(s) that identify groups and individuals that  require outreach support for advice, services, or financial intervention. 
•	Insight of debtors that will inform an alternative collection model that  supports  a reduction in un-necessary debt recovery that has with no prospect of recovery.
•	By taking action earlier, preventing vulnerable individuals falling into crisis
•	A playbook to support other Councils sharing and using their data, whether this is in house or if they choose to outsource.
•	A data sharing principles / agreement for use in multi – agency data–sets and settings
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Covid – 19 has been a catalyst for local government to join up services, across partner agencies both in terms of people working together and by sharing data that aids the welfare of citizens. In many cases systems of triage for support have been put in place locally and Councils are already looking at how these integrated services can continue to operate and add value over the recovery period of Covid – 19 and beyond.

 

We know that many services that provide direct welfare support to communities will take some time to return to normal. In addition services that collect the income Councils rely on to deliver these services are also considering how they can re–commence statutory recovery processes (given that access to magistrates courts is limited), whilst also protecting the most financially vulnerable. The proactive use of data sets is a further enabler to support the current environment and prepare effectively for the period ahead.

Authorities named on this bid, have done some initial discovery work on the data sets that could be brought together and have some learning on the data protection principles and alternative collection policies that would need to be put in place
column8:
• Week1 – Recruitment of a delivery team to produce the playbook
  • Week1-3 – Work with partners and providers to refine and test user needs, i.e. an understanding of which data sets reveal which characteristics. Also develop an understanding of what sign offs are needed on each data set to share it.
  • Week3 – Deliver a report that identifies the data sets / sources available and which elements will support the creation of a model.
  • Week4 – Assessment of the data protection impact of the use of this data and production of advice on how to overcome these hurdles.
  • Week4 –Outline agreement for the secure sharing / transfer of data sets for future enablement of the data model and outline data sharing document / DPA protocol – In readiness for any later enablement (a template for use locally, unlikely to have this fully agreed at this stage). For initial testing purposes the use of anonymized data could be used to bridge the gap between development of follow on products and relevant agreements
  • Week 5-8 An outline model that maps the data feeding into it, its purpose and the categorisations that the model will support and how these will support proactive, targeted support or intervention / actions. See specific elements the model aims to address in section 5.5
  • Week 8– Publish playbook so that other councils can engage with the market or their own in house digital and data teams to replicate success.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
• A clear consolidated documented playbook (provided as open source) in support of key deliverables across Local Government in the recovery from Covid – 19.
  • A clear and targeted model(s) that supports greater understanding of individual circumstances with the development of functionliaty locally or with providers and is available as open source to all local authorities, in addition to those areas identified above.
  • A template that identified potential data sharing/DPA agreements across data providers and supports the formation of local agreements, including any national best practice on the use of data.
  • This will help all councils to share data better and provide and opportunity for the market to engage and provide better, lower cost solutions where councils do not have an in house data capability.
column13:
All of the elements of the deliverables from the model(s) created; the data sets to include in the model the underlying principles on forming data sharing agreements / agreements on the use of any personal sensitive information will be made available to providers and Local Authorities. This will enable the creation, enrichment and deployment of local data models that directly support citizens. The playbook will also refer to existing resources available both publically and via market providers.

Many of the elements included in this proposal will already be in consideration by local Councils and in places; early development – This work will supplement and support or enhance these initiatives and also collect data on these existing capabilities, ensuring that work is not repeated across the country.
column14:
As services become increasingly digitalised, insight into individuals and groups who may have less access to digital services or the ability to utilise them, will rely on agencies increasingly using data to identify those with a propensity for services or intervention.

Future provision that aims to minimise the need for users to actively make multiple contact to multiple agencies for services with the new data available can just be awarded supporting a reduction in un-necessary demand or activity by agencies.

This project would actively support those who are digitally excluded by identifying them and supporting them where they need it and in a way that works for them.
column16:
Any legal support or expertise on Data sharing agreements / protocols for use of personal sensitive data / referral of any known expertise within the scope of this project. Critical analysis of expected output model and its perceived value to wider agencies.

Access to the provider market, we believe some engagement from specialists here may help them provide better targeted solutions for local government from the playbook.
column17:
Procurement of expertise may well include targeted approach to known providers of data analytics with knowledge of Local Authorities, this could support longer – term enablement of follow on projects.

The lead authority for this is one of the shadow authorities in Northamptonshire, in effect this submission brings together all eight councils across Northamptonshire, as well as the commitment and support of our other partners named on this bid.  Good relationships exist with suppliers of data based solutions who we can work with in order to explore this playbook further.

column2: Economic Hardship and Food Poverty - Identifying indicators and working holistically with families as a system
column4:
Many families who were just about coping before COVID-19 are now struggling to buy food and essentials, which may have long-term health, stability and wellbeing effects.
We would like to use system-wide data to identify possible triggers that may alert us to families in need before they experience crisis, so that we can offer holistic and preventative support.
We will create a relevant and reusable discovery report detailing findings from matching data, identifying families experiencing economic hardship and food poverty as a result of COVID-19. The report will include the findings of the resulting holistic and relational engagement with 10 families.
column7: The Cambridgeshire and Peterborough COVID hub has been focused mainly on supporting people in the county who are shielding due to their medical vulnerability by contacting them proactively and helping them meet their needs.  We developed a CRM system that has enabled us to track contact with shielding residents and ensure that all vulnerable shielding people are receiving the support they need. Eight public sector organisations in Cambridgeshire and Peterborough created an initial shared vision and approach for building community resilience called  ‘Think Communities’. The partner organisations have begun to share data and identify areas and communities that may be vulnerable and have expanded the partnership to include Health and Voluntary & Community Sector representation.  We want to build on this work to identify families experiencing hardship and work together to provide immediate support and sustainable place-based options.  The current COVID-19 response has highlighted a significant proportion of those that have contacted us are doing so due to financial hardship as a result of COVID-19.  The DWP North & East Midlands Group June 2020 report states that there has been a 235.1% increase in Universal Credit claims from May 19 to May 20 in Wisbech, a 307.5% increase in Cambridge, a 341.9% in Huntingdon, a 257.8% increase in Ely and 133.6% increase in Peterborough. Continuing to provide food parcels is not sustainable and does not build capability within families and communities.
column8:
Week 1
  • Gathering of all of the stakeholders from the partners who will be involved with the work and kick the project off. Business Intelligence, hub coordinators, community team, Transformation, LGSS Digital, District partner specialists such as data specialists, staff from council tax, housing, benefits, customer services.  We will work with Voluntary sector partners (inc. food bank), social prescribers, health and we would also like to work with the Citizens Advice Bureau and the DWP.
  • Collate, share and understand the data work done so far to identify areas and families with potential need.
  • Idea session about what indicators / triggers we could use to make educated assumptions about need (e.g. requests to delay council tax payments, social care data, increase requests to Housing teams and / or inability to pay rent, food bank voucher requests, areas where there has been a high demand of community resources)
Week 2
  • Pull together cross-system data about 10 families living in different areas in Cambridgeshire and Peterborough and contact them to ask if they would be willing to work with us.
  • By the end of week 2 we will have 10 families identified and their consent for us to work with them and have a shared understanding of their circumstances and interactions with services.
Week 3 - 7
  • Continued data work looking at indicators and triggers for potential need.
  • Sensitively work with the identified families to understand their lives, their situation, their needs, what happened as a result of COVID, what impact that has had on their lives, wellbeing, health.  (Multi-disciplinary team made up of people from all partners, ideally working directly with each family as one voice)
Week 7 – 8 – Outputs and conclusions
  • Documented report on the triggers and indicators we used to identify families who were nearing crisis – what mix of identifiers were most useful to identify different kinds of need, how this data was presented etc.
  • Documented stories of the families we worked with during the project – a story of their lives, struggles, needs and how we worked with them – what worked and what didn’t.  Insights such as assumptions we made versus real findings.
column9: MHCLG runs the procurement and project
column10: Local Government organisations in Cambridgeshire agree that the economic impact of COVID-19 on families is an important issue to tackle.  We have learned over the last 4 months that a shared problem / shared vision and a short timescale to deliver, can produce fantastic and meaningful results. A lot of good quality data work is already happening at county and district level to identify economically vulnerable communities and families.  This project will enable us to build on that foundation to validate the triggers that have been identified and test the response and understand the potential impact with individual families. We feel that working with 10 diverse families in the timeframe is achievable if we use procured resources alongside professionals from all of the participating councils and wider system. The 8 weeks will provide us with an opportunity to bring some of this data to life and experiment with a new approach.  We will be using data and partner experience to identify the families and create multi-disciplinary teams to work with them in a relational way to promote resilience and prevent crisis.  Our learning, in both our successes and failures, will be useful to other councils who are also grappling with the same issues. Our aim is to have a good shared understanding of the indicators for a family on the brink of financial crisis and have a proven model to work with those families to avoid them falling into financial crisis.  This work is the first step, the discovery phase, to work together as a system and help us understand how we can get there.  We will demonstrate success if we gather sufficient evidence as a system to make the decision on the next step and are able to share useful findings openly.
column13: Many councils are experiencing similar challenges as COVID is impacting on families financially. This article clearly states that children are suffering from malnutrition as a result of food poverty, not just in Cambridgeshire, but nationwide.   Our outputs will be relevant and usable by authorities nationwide.  By trialling bringing together different recognisable datasets to help us identify those in need, other councils will be able to build on this in their own areas. As we intend to work across Cambridgeshire and Peterborough local authority areas, we will be working as part of a 2-tier system and a unitary.   The documentented outcome will be a useful and practical guide for both kinds of democratic situation, to help them identify which data sets can demonstrate which triggers for potential hardship, enabling them to be more proactive.  Documented accounts of our work with families will also raise awareness and understanding about what kinds of interventions work with families hitting different triggers and indicators.
column14: This project does not discriminate and does not require residents to be digitally enabled.  The data gathered and analysed by the team will involve interrogating public sector systems and data.  As this proposal is for the creation of a discovery report, we will not be investing in or requiring the use of digital solutions for the public to use at this time.  The holistic work we will be doing with families will be relational and accessible, although staff will be required to document the experience using technology, they will have the skills to do this.
column15: Maximum £70K - depending on the cost of the partner
column16:
We will need the MHCLG to help procure a partner to work with us 
Also, supporting us with any knowledge or expertise they have in this area by acting as a sounding barrier / coach would be very useful
column17:
Organisations such as Social Finance when it comes to data modelling.

Design agencies such as Modern Human for facilitation and ethnographic research with residents

Citizens Advice Bureau

Content design / report writing specialists

column2: Improving Data Exchange between Local Authorities, Partners and the VCS
column4: Our vision is to enable a federated response across LAs, Partners and the VCS to quickly and effectively support residents whose needs have changed during COVID-19. A key barrier to achieving this is the lack of easy data exchange processes between councils and VCS organisations. Building on work by the London Office of Technology and Innovation, this project will see LAs and VCS partners in Central Bedfordshire and London develop and test better ways of exchanging data that are secure, reliable and scalable, ensuring more effective ways of working and better resident support during COVID-19 and beyond.
column7: This bid will develop on work done across the partnering organisations and their partners, looking to reuse existing learning and solutions wherever possible. The pandemic has underlined the importance of knowing our residents in order to best support them. A number of obstacles (real and perceived) made it difficult to establish a rounded view of resident need based on insight from the Council, health and other partners. At the same time, local and community responses have enabled a much greater reach and more personal response to those in need. Work across some of the London boroughs has built on work in Greater Manchester to establish an approach to information sharing that is more simple and standard. This work is a key enabler to this bid. Building on the learning on hub-and-spoke data infrastructure will also be key.  Camden and Hackney’s work to develop a platform to bring together datasets and establish a single view of need (Beacon) also underpins this bid and provides a framework for better insight and closer collaboration across stakeholders. Work across Bedfordshire, involving the Councils and Local Resilience Forum partners, has worked to understand the challenges to better data sharing, including accessibility, quality, and other barriers.
column8:
We will use LOTI’s outcomes-based methodology and take a hypothesis-led approach,  ensuring focus on making a difference to vulnerable residents. All project phases will involve a multi-disciplinary team from the partnering organisations, including Information Governance leads to ensure that data sharing mechanisms are legal, ethical and secure by design. Weeks 1-3: Discovery Drawing on insights from the partnering organisations, this phase will explore the problems to be solved and define those to be addressed in the 8 week project. The problems are not merely about lack of an effective data exchange process, and the project will explore problems relating to conditions, data people, processes and technology. Weeks 4-6: Design, develop and prototype This phase will identify up to 3 challenge areas where changed ways of working or prototypes can be created. Those solutions could include:
  • identification of new collaborative ways of working (patterns and processes) across partner organisations (that are data-enabled)
  • identification of which critical datasets, need to be shared and for what purpose
  • development of data sharing processes and information governance arrangements 
Weeks 6-7: Testing Testing and evaluation of solutions created across the partnering organisations. Testing will involve a number of different approaches:
  • remote user experience testing
  • refining and iterating prototypes
  • getting feedback from users
Week 8: Lessons learned / templatising outputs The final week of the project will focus on creating reusable outputs to facilitate take-up by other Councils and organisations:
  • description of mechanisms for data exchange
  • best practice guidelines 
  • service patterns for up to 3 ways of working that are enabled by data 
  • information governance products and an overview of what can and can’t legally and ethically be shared so that any data exchange process is equitable and mutually beneficial  
We will make use of open standards and build on existing solutions.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We will demonstrate the success and impact of our work by:
  • showing that we have built on existing work, and benefitted from that head start
  • rapidly defining parts of the problem where tangible progress is possible using agile sprints across the partnering organisations… and delivering at pace
  • focusing on tangible outcomes that will make a real difference to our residents’ lives (and deliver concrete insights and learning)
  • working as wide partnership with a joint commitment to identifying common problems and working together to solve them
Discovery: the extent of the engagement across participating partners, and by the joint working to agree a common definition of the problems to be tackled.  Design, develop and prototype: the extent to which patterns, processes and prototypes are reusable. We will be working with existing sector solutions such as Beacon and LOTI’s 7-step information governance framework. Testing: involvement and feedback from colleagues on progress against the problems identified in discovery.  Lessons learned / templatising outputs: we will work in the open and share: our learning from the process and reusable ‘take-aways’ from the project. The ultimate measure of success will be the extent to which these are taken on and refined and iterated.
column12:
We will start preparatory work for the project by the week commencing 3 August. This will include procurement, putting in place delivery partners to support the work and then getting people and resources in place across the partnership organisations.

We will be building on the information governance work undertaken by LOTI (which has been agreed in principle) and which we anticipate will be imminently signed-off, and may adjust timings to align with formal agreement.
column13:
The partnering organisations will work across geographies and areas of focus to understand the challenges with sharing and exchanging data. We will do this across a range of different sized organisations with different cultures, systems and technologies to ensure what we create is reusable elsewhere.  We will:
  • Ensure that the patterns, processes and prototype solutions are underpinned by a common data exchange mechanism that is described in a technology agnostic way (based on the Technology Code of Conduct).
  • Adopt open standards wherever possible
  • Work with and benefit from the support of LOTI who are experienced in ‘templatising’ deliverables to be reused by different organisations.
  • Test patterns, processes and prototypes on multiple scenarios throughout the project.
  • The final week will be dedicated to making deliverables reusable.
  • All findings will be published in plain English, with a specific requirement in any procurement to ensure that findings remain open and accessible to all.
column14: The main objective of this work is to help councils and their VCS partners better reach groups and individuals and provide them with the support they need. A key aspect of this project is to increase access to data across a range of different organisations with different levels of digital capability, capacity and maturity. We will be starting the project with user research and a wide-spread discovery phase, as well as learning from partnering organisations’ C19 experiences; so getting a rounded view. We will look to develop patterns, processes and prototypes for data exchange that are suitable and accessible regardless of technical resource and which support our ambition to enable a federated response.
column16: None except to help share our learnings and deliverables to the wider local government sector afterwards.
column17: MHCLG for dissemination across the sector. Other LAs have also registered interest in supporting this work including Adur & Worthing, Greenwich, Hammersmith and Fulham, and Newham. Regionally, we will look to work through partners and their extended networks (including BLRF, the GLA, LGA and LOTI) to share our work and outcomes to be reused and built on by others. Academic partners, including the University of Bedfordshire will support analysis and assessment of solutions and ‘templatising’ of our outputs. We are committed to deploying the outputs and learnings from this work to address the challenges and improve outcomes as quickly as possible.

column2: Rough Sleeping Intelligence and Insights
column4:
Newham Council, working closely with MHCLG, has brought their rough sleepers off the street and into emergency accommodation. They are now working together to transition to the post-COVID period where the lessons of what has been recently achieved can be captured, understood, and shared with other authorities so that rough sleeping can be reduced and eliminated. This involves developing an innovative new system to make best use of the information, using cutting-edge IT, which will be developed and rolled out in 2 months to enhance:   

- Real-time tracking outcomes and costs  
- Wider Council and health dataset 
- Rough Sleeper felt/expressed needs
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7: Partners: LBN commissioning and data scientists, Public Health, ELFT/NHS, London School of Economics,  Centre for Homelessness Impact, Change Grow Live, GPs, Thames Reach, Single Homeless Provider, Anchor House, Stonewall Housing, Barts Health.  Private: Eclecticity Limited We have rapidly accommodated 220 chaotic and vulnerable rough sleepers into emergency accommodation, bringing our total to 305.  Eclecticity Limited (specialist in developing intelligence driven health and care insights) were brought in to ensure a robust approach to data and intelligence  An excel spreadsheet, with innovative minimum dataset (quantifying their accommodation, immigration status, demographics, and needs through the CHAOS index), was developed to case-manage and provide analysis, which is now directly informing a new agile approach to commissioning and managing services. The excel approach provides benefits, but this can be significantly improved through a database.  This multi-stakeholder data capture, access and management process is being configured into a prototype template and sql database, which enables for a reporting dashboard through Power BI allowing for real-time analysis. The database and front end have been built upon the foundations outlined within the digital declaration. It is being built using a collection of Granicus govService forms to capture and maintain key information directly into SQL database for analysis.
column8:
The aforementioned partnership is in the final stages of 2nd phase discovery but the crux relates to moving away from retrospective and evaluation to real-time analysis that leads to agile and needs appropriate commissioning and operations delivery.  It will reduce duplication across our stakeholder network and for Rough Sleepers and develop reports to exemplify actions.  Data processing agreements and service user consent is in place.  New modules will be developed and added to the database to analyse outcomes and costs over time.  Academic and specialist providers will be used to inform this process, ensuring the right questions are being asked.    We will use the database to identify service users by NHS number by working with ELFT and use this to determine service users through our North East London Local Care Record Exemplar (Discovery).  This will give us access to the patient summary care record of NHS resource use through the subscriber service.  We will extract relevant care record data for service users from Discovery by working with the relevant GP practices.  Longitudinal care record analysis will be carried out.  By utilising Covid-19 risk and care record analysis we will also be able better understand how to improve our support of this group, outcomes, Covid-19 risk and cost analysis, contract and KPI development. The Outcome Star also allows for tracking and involving rough sleepers in their path to housing, giving them agency and voice.   The information can be broken down by protected, historically marginalised and ignored groups, which in Newham have been hit hardest by COVID. Such as No Recourse to Public Funds, BAME, LGBQT+, domestic/sexual violent victims, and victims of modern slavery. Such an analysis will allow for more specific, tailored and appropriate support.  Dates and Milestones

column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Impact: 
  • User panel established in week 1 
  • Production of a beta version by week 4, involving at least 40% of the key modules which are likely to indicate the usefulness and robustness of the system in testing 
  • Creation of a QA and technical testing framework and script by week 4, with trained testers, and a continuous daily reporting and resolving of issues to the end of the project 
  • Structured two week comparison of the manual to the new system using live cases in weeks 5-7 
  • Initial consideration of the effectiveness of the systems and areas of weakness and strength during week 8 
  • Involvement of partners in a weekly email group to share experience and issues, with Zoom checkpoints and system demonstrations in weeks 3, 6, and 8 
Success: 
  • Advanced real time outcomes monitoring, reporting and analytics to the MHCLG.  Meeting the need for time intensive weekly reporting to the GLA and MHCLG  
  • Data visualisation allowing for accessibility of information for non-specialist stakeholder 
  • Longitudinal progress outcomes monitoring including qualitative assessment tools  
  • COVID health risk assessments to minimise breakdown and allowing for second wave planning 
  • Real time financial/economic analysis on which interventions are working by cohort 
  • New commissioning intentions reflecting need
column13:
Newham exists as part of a wider North East London Commissioning Network. Interest has been show from Tower Hamlets, Hackney and the City of London for various elements/modules of the project, if not adoption of the whole project. The database and reports will enhance collaboration, wider information sharing, improve collaborative arrangements, reduce duplication for rough sleepers, and enable joint commissioning with health.  There are two potential collaborative arrangements, beyond information sharing:  
  1. The technological specifications will be made available to all Councils that wish to use it. The system is using standard software, that can be adopted by other boroughs.   
  1. Newham is willing to host the database with other Councils utilising its intelligence capabilities to create a wider dataset. 
The work has been designed so various elements can be replicated in full or in part: 
  • COVID Risk Assessment Tool 
  • Connectivity to health  
  • Power Bi data visualisation and analysis
column14: The project fits into a wider program about digital exclusion and improving safe guarding issues through data sharing. The data derived and the subsequent analysis will enable stakeholders to tackle previously unknown inequalities through agile commissioning strategies.  The partnerships involved in this project are made up of a wider range of third sector, NHS and private sector organisations that all require cross-collaboration around data at various points. Allowing information to be shared enhances and speeds up usually onerous processes and reduces (lack of connectivity results in rough sleepers being asked the same questions by multiple providers, which can impact on engagement) duplication for the service user.   The database has been designed to be web-based, allowing for flexibility for front-line providers. Rough sleepers are predominantly a digitally excluded population group. Being web-based means there is the potential for rough sleepers to have a greater engagement with data collected about them.
column16: Newham works closely with the MHCLG advisors for Rough Sleeping, Homelessness and Health.  It envisions that the advisers would be involved in the development of the project. Rough Sleepers have complex needs that cross-cut government departments such as health and social services. Developing an IT solution that links is crucial to accessing sustainable funding.  The political aspirations between local and national government are aligned.  Newham aims to bring together specialist skills, including MHCLG officers, to develop a shared product that meets the collective ambition to keep people of the streets through sustainable means and promoting independence.
column17: The partnership is a blend of Council (commissioning & data scientists), Public Health, NHS, academia (LSE), specialist third sector and private consultants.  We feel that the expertise involved would allow us to meet the objectives outlined within the proposal at pace.  Private sector providers with expertise in this area have been procured for phase 1, reducing the lead in time.

column2: Council Webcasting and Community Engagement Programme
column4:
To implement an enhanced audio and IT solution within the Council Chamber to support extended/improved engagement with democratic processes though IT solutions which will live stream and support remote engagement with Council meetings and make meetings available to view whilst they were taking place, and subsequently through publication for viewing at any time.  

The new offer would facilitate community engagement into the future by providing the opportunity for members of the public to actively take part in meetings remotely and would improve accessibility for people with hearing and visual impairments by ensuring the appropriate solutions are incorporated into its development.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The initial success factor is the installation of the system will support the holding of hybrid meetings and the improvement of live-streaming and publication of the Council’s public meetings into the future.  From this solid foundation we will continue to promote community engagement through the online participation. Success factors include
  • increased attendance at such meetings by Elected Members and members of the public;
  • greater accessibility to the democratic process to a wider audience than was previously possible, with numbers watching and subscribing to online channels monitored;
  • greater engagement with groups who might otherwise have been excluded through disabilities which would have prevented them accessing Council Meetings within a Chamber environment;
  • reviewing feedback regarding public meetings as members of the public will be able to see how their representative Members are acting on their behalf which could ultimately increase democratic engagement and an increased turnout at future local elections.
  Building on this, this approach can then be utilised to increase engagement in other Council consultation programmes.
column16: The Council would welcome further support from MHCLG to learn more about reusing existing user research on engaging and encouraging local communities to participate through digital platforms. The Council would be specifically interested in understanding innovative ways in which digital innovations for public meetings that have already been tested during the COVID-19 crisis can offer lessons for how digital technology can be used to take participative democracy one step further, from involvement in public meeting, to true participation in designing services around the needs of the community.

column2: A Playbook for Remote Teams in the New Normal
column4:
Covid-19 has forced many teams to work remotely (some for the first time) on high-stakes, complex and novel challenges. Against this ‘new normal’, this project will prototype and document how teams can confidently, quickly, and transparently: 
•	Collaborate 
•	Make good and inclusive decisions
•	Embrace new tools, techniques, and mindsets
•	Support each other’s wellbeing
•	Hold each other (and the organisation) to account  
The project will enable us "grow our own" coaching capability to further roll out lessons learned
A Playbook will capture learnings, and will be shared and promoted with other councils. It will support councils to build their own happy, high performing, remote teams that can adapt and respond to customer expectations in the Covid and Internet age.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Haringey has been experimenting with new working practices for many years: • In 2016 we were the first Local Authority to run a full ‘Google Ventures’-style Design Sprint. • In our purpose-built innovation room, we have incorporated many new working practices including ‘Liberating Structures’ and ‘Agile Sprints’. Senior managers and staff have vocally supported our test and learn approach. • Since May 2018 50+ staff have received Agile training, (some via the Digital Declaration). • In late 2019 we launched a grassroots movement to challenge assumptions about how we work, we blogged about our experiments in meetings, and bureaucracy and created recruitment case studies • Haringey’s Local Area Coordinators were recruited to work in self-managing way • Haringey initiated and hosted the first Reinventing Work Local Gov meet up • When lockdown started we quickly learned new tools and techniques, for example:
  • We're using Virtual Whiteboards, for our post Covid-Recovery and Renewal workshops
  • We’ve shared new Decision-Making techniques with the Local Gov Digital community and One Team Gov
Basis have been an important part of this journey, providing Agile training to staff, and facilitating Strategy Sprints with four out of five Directorates. They have provided ad hoc coaching support to staff to allow us to plan and facilitate workshops across the Council.
