column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
One of our key objectives is to build happy, high performing, remote teams that despite any challenges to their working environment can succeed. To establish success: ·         Independent and anonymous before-and-after surveys of the teams and surrounding managers will establish changes in performance, wellbeing, and alignment on purpose. ·         A tracker will capture:
  • Meetings held (who was included, what outcomes were reached)
  • Decisions made (what they were, how they were made and communicated)
  • Actions taken
  • Feedback given (how it was given, and the impact it had)
·         Existing performance indicators will be baselined at the beginning and monitored throughout.   ·         Information, comments, and data captured from the weekly Retros will be used to “test the temperature” as the project progresses and will form part of a lessons learned document.  To establish the success of our communication and engagement we will capture:
  • Readership and engagement of blogs
  • Attendance and engagement at our online event
All of this information will be made available in the final report.
column13: Following completion, we will make all the tools, techniques and approaches used available in a Playbook “how to guide”. This will include: ·         An overview of the project and what was achieved ·         A Roadmap showing the timeline, key milestones and events to illustrate the time and effort required to achieve a success ·         Details of our approach to workshops, how we tested ideas ·         Case studies of specific challenges faced ·         A Lesson Learned section showing what went well/not so well, and why. We’ll also talk about what we continue to work on. With regards outcomes, success is that the teams continue to work in a self-reliant way, build on what they have learned and develop the new way of working as opportunities present themselves. Before the end of 2020 we will host a second Reinventing Work Local Gov event, to share our findings. We will offer one-to-one sessions with councils who wish to engage further and understand our journey better.
column14: Whilst not directly digitally excluded Team members will be introduced to and supported to embrace new and existing digital tools to improve how they work collaboratively We will consult with our Internal Comms team to ensure our Playbook is digitally accessible
column16: We would welcome MHCLG’s views on how future Council teams may work. We would also welcome comments and suggestions on how the draft Playbook can be improved prior to circulation to other LAs. MHCLG’s support in sharing and promoting the Playbook would also be very welcome. We are not aware of any other LA that is looking at this area, but would look to MHCLG to signpost us with any partners so we can explore how to collaborate and share ideas, learning and outputs.
column17: We will keep in communication with several organisations along the way, especially neighbouring LA’s to make sure that our Playbook is accessible and usable. We would be happy to share findings with other organisations including, LGA, NLGN, and Reinventing Work to get their insight and advice.

column2: Communicating with communities in a targeted way
column4:
We want to develop the capability to target communications to geographical areas or cohorts of people, responding to a local lock-down using a range of methods (letter, text, social media, email, phone).

The project would consider how we harvest and collect data using geographical ring fencing, and development of a communications utility to provide multi-channel notifications, building on our experience of gov notify.

We want a capability we can iterate, thinking about visitors to Dorset and responding to future civil emergencies, so we want to explore information governance and data protection requirements to enable us to provide ongoing safety information.
column7:
During our response to COVID we used a range of communication methods to provide information to four main cohorts:
  • Residents
  • Vulnerable/shielded individuals
  • Our workforce
  • Visitors to Dorset
We have used social media, a card postal drop to every household, and Gov Notify to send letters and emails.  We did not have a single capability to help us do this efficiently and have used ‘blanket’ approaches in most cases rather than targeting communities and communication preferences of individuals. The communications team have identified the need to improve how we distribute messages and establish a multi-channel approach based on people's needs.  We have had initial discussions around developing two strands of work:
  1. How we harvest and collect data from multiple sources to target individuals in an area that needs to ‘lock down’
  2. Development of a communications utility based on Gov Notify to distribute messages in a range of methods
We want to develop a capability that can be re-used by others and iterated.  In 2017, there were 29million day trips/overnight stays in Dorset, developing this capability to communicate with visitors is our ultimate goal. During the MHCLG session 6th July, Huntingdonshire and Stockport indicated interest.   Cornwall Council would be interested in working with us.
column8:
As detailed above we have most of the resources within the Council with the skills required to do the work without the need for procurement or external support.

To deliver at the pace we need we would run this project in 2-week sprints, equating to 4 sprints over an 8-week period.  This would break down as follows:

3<sup>rd</sup> August – Discovery phase/user research kick off

31<sup>st</sup> August – Definition & prototype planning

14<sup>th</sup> September – Basic prototype (alpha) development and testing

12<sup>th</sup> Oct - Lessons Learn from the prototype and associated materials for sharing with others

26<sup>th</sup> Oct – Closure

The main risk to resources will be undertaking this work through the summer holiday period, so contingency has been built into the plan.  The other challenge would be the impact of a second wave of COVID which would affect delivery.

We will deliver a basic prototype focused on the residents of Dorset and how we could communicate with geographical areas efficiently, our users are broader than residents and will include the communications and customer services teams.  We will invite representatives contacted through the discovery stage to be involved in the ongoing testing and development of the solution.

We have an established ‘digital approach’ in Dorset.  We use collaboration tools such as slack and MS teams, and have been completing discovery activity and delivering workshops virtually since the COVID19 lock down.  We have adopted an open way of working using our external blog, internal week notes and regular show and tells.

It would be helpful for us to embed agile development methods to draw on MHCLG resource around delivery management if available.

Cornwall Council have indicated they would like to work with us, further discussion is required around the detail of how this would work, but it could be contributing to discovery activity (providing data / analytics / insight) to understand any different context and helping with prototype testing.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
As part of the discovery phase we would establish measures of success, for example the number of subscribers to our visitor messages, communications that sent and were received successfully.  % reduction in paper-based postal methods compared to our recent approaches in COVID.  Reduced numbers of people receiving each communication because we are only targeting the people we want to reach.

We would include a feedback mechanism for people to express their views on the notification service to ensure the solution meets the needs of our users and can be adjusted based on user input.

The ultimate measures of success are associated with direct responses, behaviour change and or impact as a result of actions we are asking the public to comply with or be aware of.  These can be measured campaign by campaign to monitor effectiveness of both the messaging and the reach.
column13:
Building a capability with Gov Notify at its core will enable other councils to re-use at low cost.

Through completing the prototype, we will understand the level of technical skills that are required.

We will use open source GIS tools, QGIS, meaning others can use freely.

We will share discovery materials, prototype details and lessons learnt on the Hackney pipeline.
column14:
We follow the GDS service standards around accessibility when designing solutions and our content designers adopt a plain English style writing for age 9, using tools such as Hemingway and Readable.io to help review content that has been produced.

Use of Gov Notify enables us to send letters at a cheaper cost to those people not online so we do not exclude anyone.  We will use the data we collect about people’s preference and needs to consider whether we need to produce communications in large print for example.
column16:
Depending on whether a partner Council can provide, we would be looking for MHCLG to provide some delivery management support to help us truly embed agile development.

We would be looking for MHCLG to help us share our work with other councils, advertising and supporting show and tells for example, talking about our work.
column17: It could be helpful to have input / technical advice from the GDS notify team to develop an early prototype.

column2: Digital Health Check for Designated Centres
column4:
The project will utilise data science and geospatial analysis to create a “Digital Health Check” (DHC). This dynamic evidence base will be capable of automatic and more frequent update than traditional data collection methods, which will enable the delivery of accelerated support and more responsive future policy interventions to enable Designated Centres to cope with the various short, medium and longer term impacts of COVID-19, as well as the ongoing impact of the decline in demand for traditional high street retail.

The DHC tool will comprise a new algorithm utilising an existing digital platform and will:
-	Carry out traditional health checks, (footfall and turnover), with easy repeats at a reduced cost and time inputs, and;
-	Use new geospatial data sets to check retail frontage and property health and investment prospects.
column8:
1) your resources
We expect that approximately 75-80% of the work/resources/effort will be delivered by our private sector partner Urban Intelligence (UI). The key will be to provide a small investment of officer time at project inception, followed by a weekly catch up meetings over the delivery of the 8 week project.

The day to day delivery of the project will be with UI lead with Council oversight. “Issues” will be dealt with via Teams calls or email correspondence.

The approximate programme will run as follows;
Week 1 - Inception meeting.
Week 2 - Existing data review.
Week 3 - Workshop to define learnings from the existing data and refine the ‘exam question’ so that UI can write an algorithm to answer it.
Week 4 - First iteration of the computation, video workshop with stakeholders to discuss results and feedback.
Week 5 - Second iteration of the computation, video workshop with stakeholders to discuss results and feedback - Issue Draft Report.
Week 6 - Third iteration and ‘sense check’ sampling with wider stakeholders (citizens and businesses) - Issue Final Draft Report.
Week 7 - Review of Final Draft Report and sign-off.
Week 8 - Contingency week.

(2) involvement from the local government sector
Throughout the process, the project partners will seek to engage with internal Council experts to advise on portfolio matters. We will also maintain communications with other relevant projects that are being undertaken as part of the challenge to learn from others where possible. We will inform neighbouring local authorities about the work so that they can input if desired and benefit longer term from a solution that is easily scalable and capable of wider rollout.

(3) data requirements
In order to understand the context and solve the problem, we will work with our partners to obtain/share the following data:
● Planning Policy incl. accurate administrative/ neighbourhood/ centre boundaries
● Internally available spatial data on the Council’s COVID-19 response to-date
● Land use data
● Retail footfall data
● Real estate market data for a variety of uses to understand potential market drivers between uses as a result of shifting consumer demand.

(4) involvement from the broader population.
We will aim to engage with a sample of local citizens and businesses to check that the analysis is correct and accurately reflects the situation on the ground. We understand that the situation with COVID-19 is evolving rapidly, and so engagement with the latest government guidance will empower us to make sure that the project is fit for purpose.
column9: MHCLG runs the procurement and project
column10:
Success will be demonstrated by:
  • Designated Centre business groups being interested in the results of the DHCs and agreeing to review updates.
  • Local councillors, communities and businesses agreeing future planning policy changes as a result of analysis of DHCs.
  • Westminster/ Camden developing new and innovative strategies with councillors, businesses and communities to better support and sustain centres in the longer term beyond the impact of COVID-19.
  • Showcasing the benefits of DHCs in terms of their speed of delivery and ability to provide up to date and frequent data demonstrating the relative performance of Designated Centres to other Local Planning Authorities.
  • Cost and speed of production of HCs being reduced by the creation of DHCs.
By trialing the use of data science, computation and building on proven methods, this will enable us to rapidly deliver a novel project that could be used by multiple teams within each Council. This digital methodology proposed will increase efficiency for subsequent evidence updates by being of a common structure offering a building block for regular and wider updates. Our partner Urban Intelligence has a track record of delivering public sector projects quickly to provide detailed and accurate results backed by robust logic agreed with their clients. Such clients include the MHCLG (Digital Land Pilot) and Transport for London (Portfolio Analysis). The project should provide the Council with intelligence to address the following questions:
  • How and where COVID-19 is having the greatest impact on commercial uses within our Designated Centres?
  • Is it possible to create a dynamic evidence base that is not static and could be updated easily on a more regular basis?
column13:
In order to ensure that the results can be shared with other councils we will:
  • Ensure that we fully document the project and ask our partners to do the same in order to make sure that if necessary, the project could be expanded or replicated by other parties.
  • Utilise adopted or industry standard techniques that can be widely used again.
  • Be transparent in our methods of analysis.
  • We will also seek to use open data wherever possible, such as Energy Performance Certificates (from MHCLG) or data covered under the Public Sector Mapping Agreement (from Ordnance Survey) where possible, so the cost of undertaking this work is not prohibitive for other public sector bodies to undertake, if they are interested in repeating the study in their own areas.
  • Undertake digital ‘share and tell’ sessions with other local authorities and relevant public sector bodies (on Teams or similar platforms).
column14: We will ensure that there is a pdf version of the report available at the end of the commission, which if required, could be printed into hard copy and made available at our offices.
column16: We would welcome the opportunity to work with MHCLG’s in-house experts around the use of land and property data and property technology (or ‘Proptech’). We understand that MHCLG has invested considerably in its digital land programme, and we are keen to learn from some of the outcomes in order to optimise our own project experience. We will also seek to communicate with MHCLG’s internal planning experts to ensure that our project is as compatible as possible with the wider digital programme. We would be grateful if MHCLG could also facilitate communications with the projects running in parallel on this challenge to ensure that there is an optimal opportunity to learn from others.
column17: We aim to approach other technology or data businesses that provide building data and footfall data (data providers are currently TBC) to investigate where their data on land use or retail footfall can be included in our project. Whilst our private sector partner UI has confirmed the quote with Geomni for Building Data, they have not yet had time to confirm the quote for footfall data; albeit they are comfortable that a range of providers within budget. UI have confirmed that MHCLG’s experience in this part of the data gathering would be welcome. UI may also engage with real estate agents in order to get a good understanding of how the land economics of our city is changing as a result of COVID-19.

column2: COVID-19 Recovery Monitoring
column4: Worcestershire County Council are developing a local tool to monitor COVID-19 recovery, building on existing approaches to local outbreak monitoring (NIMROD). The tool will enable Worcestershire to monitor its COVID recovery and facilitate the reporting of our local statistics into the wider Local Resilience Forum (LRF) which covers Worcestershire, Herefordshire, Shropshire and Telford and Wrekin. With the grant, there will be potential to scale the development to meet all four local authority areas recovery reporting if agreed by the other authorities.
column7:
The Council already has existing dashboards to monitor local COVID-19 Response Activity, and throughout COVID-19 response we have been coordinating this on behalf of the county and district councils in Worcestershire - adopting a "One Worcestershire" approach. and we are currently agreeing with LRF partners a suite of recovery measures to report to senior managers across the LRF. At the moment this is largely collated manually. The proposal is to host relevant data in a Sharepoint site(s) (initially for Worcestershire but with potential to roll out to other LRF authorities and beyond) and create live dashboards to provide up to date recovery information in an efficient / automated way – through creation and controlled sharing of Power BI dashboards.

In addition – the Council’s Public Health and ICT teams have already developed a system to monitor and track local outbreak information, called NIMROD, and we already have expressions of interest from other local authorities to deploy elsewhere. This project will explore further development of NIMROD to incorporate broader recovery outcomes such as economic recover, communities not covered in the original NIMROD scope and develop an overarching recovery framework for Worcestershire.
column8:
Capture requirements and key audience for reports – Early August

Agree platform for hosting data and establishing access – Early August

Create data flows - mid / end August

First draft dashboard created – early September

Consult on dashboard contents and areas for development – mid September

Dashboard launched to agreed audience – end September / early October

Explore roll-out to LRF authorities – October onwards
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success criteria for the project are as follows..

Successful and lean delivery of LRF reporting requirements (maximise automation and live link to data where appropriate to minimise manual labour).

Opportunity to produce dynamic management information in a variety of formats – to enable fast decision making about emerging issues (e.g. local outbreak, capacity issues, geographical patterns).

Reduction in the amount of officer time spent producing reports across a range of stakeholders, including reduction in duplication of effort if applied across multiple authorities
column13: We are currently working with Business Intelligence Managers across the LRF region to agree common sets of indicators to inform Recovery planning. Once the tool is created for Worcestershire it should be relatively easy to modify to incorporate other local authority data, particularly where the source is common. The intention is for the proposed project to be scalable across the other LRF authorities.
column14: This project will initially focus on internal reporting requirements to inform LRF recovery planning and is not intended for publication at this stage. Worcestershire County Council has developed accessibility standards for digital content – called SCULPT. We will apply these standards to any published output that results from the project over time.
column16:
Advice on creation of live links to data / API to automate recovery reports - including access to appropriate national resources if appropriate. Early indication of any national reporting tools that may be emerging that could render local monitoring unnecessary. Some horizon scanning of likely recovery themes that may emerge nationally that we may need to monitor in the future (future proofing).

Advice about how to apply Agreement 8.2 below where sensitive data outputs, and the need for specific third party licenses (e.g.Power BI) may be involved.
column17:
We are currently in dialogue with our existing suppliers of dashboard solutions to seek advice about sharing across multiple agencies. We intend to explore the use of Power BI as a the potential application for the consumption of recovery reporting output.

Engagement from other public sector agencies (particularly across the LRF region) and support to create links to data sources as appropriate.

column2: Intelligence Based Decision Making: Ensuring the Recovery, Reform and Reset Programmes for Telford and Wrekin understand communities they are supporting and the impact they are having.
column4:
Telford and Wrekin Council have established a Recovery, Reform, Reset (RRR) Strategy to move the organisation from providing an emergency response to leading the borough’s recovery. This is supported by 6 workstreams. 

This proposed project will accelerate work to ensure that these programmes have a clear understanding of the communities they are working with and the impact that they are having.  This will be through the delivery of two reporting projects, both delivered through Power BI:
1: bespoke dashboards for all programmes enabling them to regularly monitor the progress of their outcome measures and the impact of the recovery work 
2:  place-based community intelligence tool and profiles to inform a local response to the recovery programme based on an understanding of communities
column7:
The Recovery, Reform and Reset Strategy and 6 programmes for Telford & Wrekin Council have been established. There is a clear commitment from the Chief Executive within the Strategy to make evidence based decisions and to make performance against the aims of the programmes transparent and available. A decision had already been made to reform performance monitoring processes, to embed clear structures to the data and to utilise the capabilities of Power BI for visualising the results. A ‘Gold’ Power BI dashboard has already been developed and used to inform the Council’s evidence-based response to the pandemic to date.

It has also been established that all 6 recovery programmes need to have readily available intelligence about the communities they are working with. We have already decided that we will utilise Power BI to build a place-based tool that enables a detailed understanding of communities and enables us to work at newly defined local geographies. Segmentation data will significantly enhance this through CACI’s Acorn segmentation at household level, plus the specific Wellbeing Acorn segmentation under pinned by evidence from the Council’s own datasets.
column8:
Key milestones: End August 2020:
  • establish clear performance frameworks, with outcome measures that efficiently and clearly monitor the impact of the 6 workstreams
  • develop a process in Power BI and Sharepoint for collecting and displaying progress against outcomes
  • collect data against outcome measures to set baselines
  • develop a first model of a set of dashboards for each of the 6 programme workstreams
End September 2020:
  • use Acorn segmentation and local data to provide a working first model of a place-based intelligence tool
October 2020:
  • Workstreams have access to regular outcomes dashboards and a presentation of place-based intelligence tool bespoke to their specific programme aims
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success would be demonstrated by the delivery of 2 clear products to the RRR programme:

1: bespoke dashboards for all RRR programmes enabling them to regularly monitor the progress of their outcome measures

2: place-based community intelligence profiles available to inform the recovery programme at a local level.

 

Success would also be measured through the RRR groups reviewing their actions plans based on the availability of new and improved intelligence. In the longer term, success would be the delivery of the programmes with demonstrable impact in the local community.
column13: The products we will deliver (an outcome monitoring dashboard for each RRR programme and a Place-based community intelligence tool) will both be developed in Power BI. This Microsoft product is utilised by many authorities. The ‘skeleton’ of all tools would be available to share, as would the learning from key officers which would be shared, as required, with others wishing to implement similar projects.
column14: n/a
column16: It is expected that we can deliver this project using in-house expertise. Support from the MHCLG in making links with other organisations undertaking similar work would be beneficial
column17: as above

column2: Cycling through COVID19
column4:
Lincolnshire County Council is proposing a digital project to measure active modes of transport around our county. Using modern cycle counting technology would enable us to monitor cycle usage, flows and pinpoint areas of high use for improvement – supporting active travel in our Covid-19 recovery.

We want to increase the amount of residents literally getting on their bikes, travelling around in a cleaner and safer way as this will support the avoidance of public transport, in line with national guidance, and contribute towards greener 'new normals' Our project will provide a superior cycling monitoring/data collection system, which supports our  goals.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Lincolnshire County Council has acknowledged the need to improve our data gathering in our emerging Cycling Strategy and through the development of our Local Cycling & Walking Investment Plans (LCWIPs).

As we recover from the effects of Covid-19 we have recently been successful in obtaining finance through Tranche I of the Emergency Sustainable Travel Fund. As we move into Tranche II and beyond towards supporting a more sustainable transport system, monitoring and analysis of key cycling routes (already identified through our LCWIPs) will be key to prioritising improvements and affecting greater modal shift from cars and public transport to bicycles.

We have explored options on mobile and fixed cycle counters that will provide us with a wide range of data including numbers, direction of travel, time of day of travel, and speed.
column8:
We expect the project to start week commencing 3<sup>rd</sup> August 2020. We will engage with our partner organisations – City of Lincoln Council and South Kesteven District Council, to finalise the locations of devices as well as local businesses, town and parish councils and voluntary sector organisations to determine levels of involvement in project activity.

Our project officer will carry out project activity as a part of their usual workload.

We would expect that equipment will be installed by the first week in September to capture valuable data as schools reopen and people return to their work environments.

We will carry out an initial monitoring of systems and data at the end of September to inform the remaining time of the project activity

During the first week in October and as we approach the end of the funded project activity, we will carry out an additional monitoring of data collected.  This data will inform the prioritisation of cycle routes in the county due to take place by mid November 2020.

The challenges identified within the project include collection and distribution of equipment – this will be carried out by Lincolnshire County Councils Highways and Sustainability teams. In addition, procurement of equipment may become a challenge. We are already having discussions with suppliers to mitigate this.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Collection of quantitative data including the numbers of cyclists will be collected at the beginning of the project and compared to those at the end and this will form a baseline from which conclusions can be gathered.

Success will be measured in the collection of quantitative data from temporary cycle routes allowing us to ascertain which are most useful and which see higher/lower footfalls. Having data for 10 routes to be able to see if they are being used, and if we can spot increases in cycling as knowledge of the routes grows, will be the major success for us.

The success isn't ultimately about improving numbers of people cycling, success will be about accurately measuring numbers of cyclists through an 'improved data collection system to monitor cycle flows in key areas in Greater Lincolnshire'.
column13:
We will ensure outputs and outcomes can be utilised by other councils, firstly by ensuring outcomes are fed back to MHCLG to share with the forum as part of wider digital funded projects.

We already work closely with all seven of our district councils across Lincolnshire, including through our emergency cell in response to the pandemic, which has representation from all our districts.

We will also ensure that success and good working practices are shared through production of a final report that will be communicated with other local authorities. We will ensure project activity is distributed to a wide range of networks and communications including local cycling groups.
column14:
Several existing and temporary cycle paths will be used. These include the already high use Tritton Road cycle path and the developing 'pop-up' cycle infrastructure in Lincoln City, especially around the emerging Wigford Way route.

The council will use existing high use paths in the first phase of this work to 'sense-check' our methodology before moving on to other areas where local communities are advocating for improved provision to measure cyclist numbers which will aid prioritisation for maximum effect.

Data is collected simply by the cyclist riding over the counter and will not require any specific digital equipment by the rider. The information will be able to be shared with anyone in the county through digital or traditional formats and through the reports issued by the council.
column16:
We are keen to explore working with MHCLG for support during, and sharing findings afterwards.

Following completion and evaluation, we will explore opportunities to implement initiatives including sourcing funding for activity to widen the reach across local districts.

We are interested in working closely with all sectors to aid improvement of services, training for communities and implementation of technology. With the expansion of broadband networks across the county there are increased opportunities for involvement. We will work collaboratively to ensure that adequate support is available to the most vulnerable, ensuring opportunities are promoted and joint approaches discussed.
column17:
As a county council we will be able to rely on our own resources to deploy the project and undertake all necessary data analysis. However, we already have a developed network of contacts with our district council colleagues who will enable better targeting of routes and data sharing across departments.

We will work closely and alongside a range of local community groups i.e. town and parish councils, and cycling groups.

We have a good working network with our town and parish councils, and we can quickly disseminate information to them and get meaningful responses and insight into our communities.

column1: Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames
column2: Consultation and Engagement in a Post-COVID Environment
column4: We are proposing a digital playbook for public engagement and consultation in light of continued social distancing and limitations in more traditional methods. There is evidence that engagement has increased due to the ease of access through digital routes and the project will explore  building on these opportunities. The participating Councils cover a wide demographic with a mixture of urban, regional town and rural communities ensuring that the playbook is applicable across local authorities.  The project will save Councils’ time in finding solutions to a common challenge and will provide ideas for increasing engagement and inclusion through digital channels.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7:
Below is an overview of work undertaken to date.
  • Council meetings are now online and accessible to the public through established channels. Evidence suggests streaming of Council meetings and easy access to these has increased the number of viewers compared to pre Covid numbers. The LoTI playbooks developed proved helpful to expedite implementation of virtual meetings.
  • There is increased need to enable more two way engagement and consultation with residents. From contributing to Council meetings, engaging in community meetings through to formal consultations.
  • In Cambridgeshire, work has started to rethink how they will do consultations that were traditionally face to face, online in a more creative way, making best use of digital tools rather than just replicating how things currently work.
  • In Sutton, they are considering the role of Local Committees and ways to increase resident participation. A survey has been commissioned to understand resident needs when engaging with the Council.  An initial desktop exercise has also been undertaken to identify methods of engagement used elsewhere.
  • Waltham Forest are looking at ways to move their Citizens Assembly to a virtual platform enabling continued delivery of actions and commitments, as well as ongoing engagement in a non-physical space.
column8:
The local authority project team will be supplemented with an external partner procured by MHCLG.  Partners could include: MySociety, Nesta, Involve and FutureGov, all of whom have expertise in the public consultation and engagement area.   The London Office of Technology and Innovation (LOTI) are also keen to support this work. Central to being able to solve the challenge relating to consultation and engagement is involvement from residents, our users. Their input and insight will be essential to help us to build up an initial understanding of contexts and perspectives; including existing barriers to consultation and preferred alternative methods going forward. Working with a broad cross-section of users throughout the process to test and iterate prototypes will ensure maximum reusability.   Drawing on a wide range of user research gained from across the sector and partners such as LOTI will be invaluable to the project to achieve outcomes.  In addition published learnings from other organisations, such as FutureGov and their recent blog discussing digitally native planning engagement. Data around current levels of engagement will also be gathered to help the project establish baseline metrics in order to measure improvement.  A picture will be built up as part of the discovery phase. Key dates and milestones will be agreed with the supplier on project kick-off, however our broad approach to solve the challenge would be as outlined below. Discovery week 1-3
  • Build up an understanding of contexts and perspectives - based on evidence and user research.
Alpha week 4-5
  • Prototype plays to meet those contexts
Beta week 6-7
  • Test those plays (if timing aligns this will include testing on a live consultation being conducted by Cambridgeshire on their Economic Recovery Strategy)
Live: Week 8 
  • Iterate on plays - using feedback and insight to make changes ensuring relevance, usability and accessibility.
  • Publish the playbook
column9: MHCLG runs the procurement and project
column10:
Success and impact will be demonstrated in the following ways:
  • At the most basic level delivering a set of reusable and transferable approaches covering a variety of methods and technologies to achieve digital democracy at a local level.  Enabling councils to meet statutory requirements and bridge the gap between traditional face to face consultation, with more digital and inclusive approaches.  
  • Through the level of take-up of the playbook across the sector, the ease at which it could be applied to different live scenarios and on the infrastructure used.  
  • The ability for the sector to apply the playbook with minimal effort and resource.
  • From a resident perspective the new approaches will take consultation and engagement to them, where they are, at a time that is convenient for them.
  • Measuring increased level of engagement against the baseline.
  • Increased engagement from different sectors of the community through the removal of physical constraints in favour of a lower barrier to entry through digital tools. 
  • Ensuring that the foundations and design of the playbook will have user experience and need at its core.
  • An opportunity to engage more Council officials and Members in user centred design and its benefits.
column12: Project timeline will start once the partner is in place.
column13: The intended output from this work is a reusable playbook that will be a tried and tested set of digital and blended approaches for councils’ to use when engaging with residents across different contexts in a post-COVID environment.  It will be based on technology and consultation processes in use today but also focus on the more practical ‘how to’ elements of resident engagement, and the opportunities that the increased time and relevance of local democracy, and the removal of ‘location’ as a constraint brings.   Between the participating Councils we have a mixture of urban, regional town and rural authorities that will ensure that the playbook is applicable to a wide range of local authorities.  The large number of authorities also ensures that a wide range of technologies and technology availability will be covered.
column14: Recognising more traditional face to face forms of engagement become less viable in a socially distanced world, it's critical that as digital innovation is increased, technology remains a positive enabler to invite input from a wider segment of the population, rather than becoming a barrier.   Defining user needs from the outset will ensure that the project is clear on the positive impacts of asynchronous ‘virtual’/ hybrid approaches, as well as the possible negative impacts of digital engagement.  The project will consider all aspects relating to digital exclusion and accessibility, as well as other barriers present such as language.     Throughout the project, tools and approaches will be shared and tested across the sector to gain insight and understanding around how to make consultation and engagement more accessible. The findings will be applied to the playbook ensuring the output enables sector partners to deliver inclusive, flexible and effective digital engagement at pace.
column15: The consortium is seeking funding for the provision of the partner organisation and the amount of funding required will depend on the results of the procurement process.  Further based on initial conversations funding may be required to backfill Council resources (e.g. subject matter experts, business analysts etc..), this would be determined based on the approach agreed with the partner however.  We would expect that the total amount would be less than the maximum £80k.
column16: Running of the procurement for the partner organisation.   Support in the distribution and testing of the output playbook to as many of the local authorities as possible. This would be to aid feedback, insight and understanding of any changes needed to increase relevance, accessibility and support for as many contexts and perspectives as possible.   Support to make available and cascade the playbook to all local authorities following completion of the project.
column17: The London Office of Technology and Innovation has already produced a playbook  associated with running Council Meetings in a post Covid environment and have confirmed that they would be willing to support this work should this bid be successful.

column1: Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea
column2: Food+
column4:
The Food+ Project is a collaboration of the Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea with 13 experienced Food Banks and Community Kitchens.

We are proposing a give and get food app that builds on the experience of support provided during the COVID-response. But, as food aid is a gateway to other services, the Food+ App will tackle food insecurity plus additional needs by:

•	Improving sustainability of outlets and collaboration across boroughs
•	Allowing targeted donator notifications
•	Directing service users to available food resources 
•	Promoting broader hardship support (benefits advice, financial support, housing, children’s services and adult social care)
column7: We are ready to develop the digital solution. Food outlets are engaged and providing insights, we have built strong relationships across the food support sector beyond our LAs. We have been interacting with service users at food outlets and through the food referral partnership. The monitoring of service delivery at each food outlet since lockdown started provides enough quantitative and qualitative data to analyse service usage and user needs, revealing challenges and opportunities: donations are erratic, users struggle to find food aid, and service staff struggle to signpost to additional support that could help users out of poverty. The Food+ app targets donors, service users, and food bank staff/case workers. Donors can follow outlets and receive notifications of critical shortages. Users can find outlets and check eligibility, choosing to get notifications on additional support. Food banks can report shortages and send push notifications, and staff at food banks or any other frontline role can easily find the right information to signpost and refer. The app will utilise existing georeferenced databases of voluntary organisations, community facilities and public services in RBKC and WCC. Initial ideas were discussed with four private app developers, this proposal is aligned with Draw and Code.
column8: The project will be developed in 4 stages. An app launch will take place on 30 October and a communications campaign will run for extra six or more weeks: Weeks 1+2: Alpha 1 – Set up multidisciplinary team, user assessment, specifications IT Progress: Base UX Flow PR Progress: Scoping the narrative, launch Food+ press office Engagement Progress: Food bank steering group set up, introductions, first inputs Weeks 3-5: Alpha 2 – Testing and adjustments IT: Prototyping, Map Integration, Filtering, User Authentication PR: Support stakeholder updates on app development, first outputs Engagement: Feedback from steering group, service users, donors and council teams Weeks 6-8: Beta – Iterations to final product IT: Notifications, Localisation, Food Requests, Apple and Play Store submissions PR: Pre-launch campaign, media alert for app launch Engagement: Feedback from steering group and council teams, reach out to London food networks 30 Oct: App Launch Week 9-12/14: Live – Marketing campaign and performance assessment IT: App performance data report, potential business partnership for continued improvements PR: Case study development (human stories), media alerts, Christmas campaign Engagement: Outreach and training with service users and service staff, sharing app case study with LAs and other stakeholders Existing Resources The app is proposed by the RBKC Community Engagement Team, involved in devising, implementing and monitoring the Council food support in partnership with the voluntary sector. The IT, Communications and Customer Services teams are providing expert support, Legal is assuring data protection. Data requirements Data will be stored locally on devices and only anonymised data will be collected. Future resources RBKC is waiting for further information following the Government announcement on 15 June that there will be £16.3m for LAs to provide food support. This fund may allow further enhancement of the App to provide integration with food retailers and a booking/messaging feature for signposting and referrals.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The launch of the app should lead to a genuine increase in support for residents through a coordinated and collaborative approach to tackling food shortages in the boroughs. Our experienced food banks will be supported by this technological solution which will reduce the strain on services to manage variation in food donations and demand. The app will provide further opportunities to monitor the data associated with food shortages and surplus, feeding into our existing databases and reporting mechanisms. The app will also provide a technological solution to make valuable information easily accessible to target audiences and raise awareness of food needs and aid. We will demonstrate success and impact by:
  • Monitoring the number of app downloads
  • Monitoring the number of donations received from push notifications
  • Monitoring the number of additional support services accessed
  • Collecting and publishing performance data
  • Monitoring the access to additional support services hosted on the app
  • Sharing the learning and technology with stakeholders
column13: The terms of the app development will allow cost effective replication in other boroughs for it will be open source code under an open license, and ownership of the intellectual property will remain with local authorities. Ideally, other boroughs would input their data into the same app. The App will be immediately available to Westminster City Council who works closely with RBKC in many bi-borough arrangements. Westminster’s food banks, community kitchens and referral information will be included in the app. We will develop a communications plan for news outlets and other trade pages to ensure other Councils learn about the project and understand how to become a partner. We will share our community engagement strategy around food needs and data gathering. We are members of the London Boroughs Food Group and will present a case study to this group to share the learning from our COVID-recovery with this digital project.
column14: RBKC and WCC are learning from the experience of working through the pandemic, adapting work practices and connecting with our communities in innovative ways. At this time, we have collected evidence from RBKC’s existing food support system that at least half of online referrals were completed by users themselves or by community organisations. We will increase access to online aid through the Food+ app, a bespoke platform informed by feedback from users to ensure it matches their skill levels. Frontline staff will also be trained to learn how to use the digital tool and how to share this knowledge with service users. We will also improve digital access through the reopening of community centres and libraries. We will look at having a Food+ simplified webpage for desktops. Food donors are less likely to be digitally excluded, nevertheless the app will be available for both Android and IOS systems.
column15: We are asking for the maximum grant available £80k.
column16: We think that MHCLG can play a strategic role to advance the project in its relationship with regional authority (GLA) to ensure the London Boroughs Food Group continues beyond the COVID emergency. The collaboration of London Food Leads has enabled valuable learning to be shared and we welcome MHCLG taking a coordination role to bring food support needs into focus and create more opportunities for lessons to be shared nationally. There are also opportunities for MHCLGs relationship with Defra to influence commercial food suppliers, so that food supplies to food banks are protected during emergencies.
column17: We believe we have identified the main organisations that are needed to mobilise the project. However, connection to a large-scale Digital Company who has the capacity to replicate this project and offer an attractive implementation package would be welcomed.

column2: Enabling Digital Conversations
column4: To create a low-contact face-to-face experience for residents who do not have access to digital alternatives. This will create digital meeting points at council access points to allow interaction with service officers via video-conferencing. These bookable appointments will let those residents who need to interact face-to-face to have a safer, reassuring experience and improve officer productivity.
column7: During the pandemic restrictions, Bolton Council customer contact centre staff have been working remotely, serving customers by digital (online) channels or by phone. The access points have been closed for face-to-face visits and are now only partially reopening.   To prepare for opening to more customers, we have developed plans to reconfigure the existing one stop shop so that greater level of self-service can be delivered. Improving and speeding up service delivery whilst ensuring the safety of staff and citizens.   We are investigating the introduction of video conferencing suites. These would allow a digitally excluded resident or business to interact with the relevant service, seek advice or make an application for service.   Initially we are working with the Revenues and Benefits services to improve interactions with customers under the new circumstances. This area is expecting a potential growth in demand due to increased unemployment impacting the local economy. Some customers need a mediated service to explain and reassure by speaking face-to-face to specialist. This video-conferencing option will allow them to speak face-to-face adviser and will support social-distancing. Assistance will be provided to support their use of technology and to ensure the space is clean, to reduce the risk of infection.   We have an existing method of booking appointments which will be adapted to support the new way of working.  Other services that require face-to-face contact include homeless welfare and money advice service. There are also aspects of Planning, building control, licensing and registrars that need a physical presence in order to fulfill processes.
column8:

  • Commence H&S audit and guidance preparation.
Week 2:

Week 7;

  • Health and Safety Checklist and compliance with latest Covid 19 guidance.
Week 8
  • Sign off operational instructions and user guidance
  • Soft launch.
Week 9-12
  • Monitoring take up
  • Engage with other partner services (such as the Citizen’s Advice Bureau, Age UK) to expand the service beyond the council to reduce customer footfall into other buildings.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: TAKE UP OF NEW SERVICE (Reaching residents identified as digitally excluded. Matching postcodes against ‘predicted users’ to ‘actual users’)  USER QUESTIONNAIRES   STAFF FEEDBACK  IDENTIFICATION OF SAVINGS OR CHANNEL SHIFT  Efficiency of the interaction (first contact resolution – reduce the need for repeat contacts)
column13: We will create some best practice guidance, technology independent, about creating the appropriate space, how to address the H&S concerns, skills and training needed for staff involved in video service delivery.    Marketing and awareness campaigns Specific lessons learned from project deployment.   We would also be happy to showcase and support other councils in replicating this project.
column14: Our project is specifically aimed at those residents who are digitally excluded and may potentially lose access to council services, as more services move online in response to the C-19 pandemic. Those digitally excluded residents are familiar with visiting the One Stop Shops or access points. This is the first step in moving them onto a digital platform in a safe and supported manner.   This will assist those citizens who are digitally excluded due to costs or lack of skills or are lacking in the confidence to interact digitally with the Council.   There will always be some need to have a mediated service delivery for some residents and this project will provide a template for that approach that may be rolled out in more local areas and through partnerships although this is not an outcome from this stage of the project due to the timescales.
column16: At this stage i cannot say, however we would look for guidance and support to ensure project delivery within timescale.
column17: This project is predicated on using internal resources to deliver. It will be speeded up if we use some outside agency to provide support especially around the Marketing and Communications. Specific assistance with identifying the customers who will be most impacted by digital exclusion, to undertake a targeted campaign explaining the new ‘video-conferencing’ offer.     We will also engage the Council’s ICT and Property Services outsourced companies.

column2: Connecting Communities
column4:
Covid-19 has made it even more difficult for people to connect. We will develop a tool that helps people find what they need based on where they are, and what they are interested in. It will be an interactive map of activity across the city, with the ability to generate alerts about nearby offers. This connectivity tool will help individuals, community groups, businesses, and professionals to connect. It will also help us to find gaps in support so that we can plan future interventions better.
Functionality includes: 
•	Geotagging/GPS functionality  
•	Personal selection of support  
•	Real time alerts
•	Access to organisation’s website
column7:
• Development of common, simple approach to addressing gaps in connectedness
  • Ongoing discussions which have highlighted the requirement for the solution via continuous feedback
  • Creation of a multi sector steering group highlighting issues in locating relevant information and accessing its services. This group is made up over 30 voluntary and community sector organisations
  • Reviewing existing artefacts holding this information, identifying its strengths and weaknesses and areas of improvement
  • Discussions around the customer journey/experience relating to the new solution.
  • High level requirements to develop the solution documented
  • Meetings with relevant stakeholders whose input will be required to develop the solution
  • Investigated Geographical Information System, to identify the ‘categories’ of support which could be used in solution, which also details, name of establishment and address
  • Feasibility studies in extracting data from existing artefacts holding local details about support services and activities that take place in the city to upload onto new solution.
column8:
The challenge of delivering this project within 8 weeks will be managed via Agile scrum, this will ensure that if there are problems during development, they’re managed and dealt with at the earliest opportunity to minimise disruption and guarantee delivery of the solution. There will also be ‘Daily Stand Ups’ involving key stakeholders to deliver transparency of any issues and resolution of them as a collaborative team.

column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success will be determined by the implementation of the solution and co-design through engagement with users. Measures of success will include:
  • App downloads
  • User engagement e.g. frequency of use
  • User feedback
  • No of representatives/ stakeholders contributing to the design
There are wider impacts on health inequalities as a result of Covid-19 and this solution will enhance networks by providing a way in which people can shape and connect with local groups. For example, there is evidence that strong social networks can reduce feelings of loneliness and improve mental resilience. Specifically, we see immediate potential for this solution to enhance social networks. The solution will allow us to map support in local areas and provide the user with the choice of support/resources in their area to access. This will enable us to gather intelligence on preferences, needs and relate to people’s circumstances such as education, income, housing, social security and social networks. This will also provide evidence on gaps in provision across the city to inform future priorities, particularly contributing to our community recovery plans and strategic priorities. The impact of the project will support people AND communties to connect to what is important to them in their places.
column13:

column2: Development of a Resident’s impact model to support targeted campaign delivery
column4:
Covid-19 requires us to think creatively about how we identify and engage residents. Councils face new challenges as new cohorts of residents need support. The impacts of Covid-19 are and will continue to be multi-faceted. Our responses need to be developed with these complexities in mind and with a recognition of the importance of households to both individuals and the community response. 
We have been working with an external partner to gain better predictive insights on households.  New toolsets and data science methods, can help us better respond to changes both before and as they occur, achieving more sustainable impacts.

column7: With partners (‘Policy in Practice’, NHS etc) we use data insights to better understand drivers of demand and develop services. For a full background, watch this short video or see here. Our Business Intelligence (BI) function, driven by demand analytics enables a Single View of the Household (SVOH). This provides a higher degree of decision confidence to plan early interventions and new service propositions during ‘usual business.’ This has also been a crucial element of our Covid-19 response.   This household view is maintained in our evolving Covid-19 response and our preparations for a ‘second wave’ in autumn/winter.   Our Data Science function is part of this shift to predicative analytics creating preventative interventions. Our data science and BI function will create integrated data models comprised of deep data interrogation enabling a holistic SVOH.   Our community-based service ‘Connected Communities’ has contacted 12,500+ residents during Covid-19. This data will be added to our standard data sets to create a richer understanding of needs and efficiency of various interventions.   A key lesson has been difficulties of data management with the use of single purpose applications or spreadsheets. Our existing data integration model was to develop a golden record for each individual/household, over a 2-3-year plan. We need to achieve this in 2-3 months.

column2: Everyone In data sharing
column4:
During the Covid-19 lockdown, local housing authorities have accommodated a huge number of individuals rough sleeping following the Everyone In directive from Luke Hall MP and Dame Louise Casey. 

To bring everyone in required collaboration for numerous partner agencies to ensure that individuals were identified, suitable accommodation secured, access to food and medicines (including substance misuse treatment) were arranged, requirements to shield or self-isolate were known, housing assessment and move on plans were completed, etc.

In order to share data to enable joint working relating to all of the above, particularly social care and health data, emergency data sharing arrangements have been put into place and manual, time intensive processes have been created to collate, share and cross reference data. As lockdown eases, the need to maintain accurate records of the health and housing status of individuals rough sleeping/at risk of rough sleeping/a history of rough sleeping is crucial, particularly as part of the tracing and management of any local outbreaks. This data can also be used in the future commissioning, design and monitoring of services to ensure needs are being met.

There are a number of systems in use by partner agencies to store the data that is relevant to the work they do, however, there is no single system that brings this data together or that allows data to flow automatically between systems to enable all agencies to have a comprehensive view of each individual. Instead, we hold lengthy multi-agency meetings to discuss cases and spend time collating data in spreadsheets that partners then have to manipulate and cross reference with their own data.

This project will scope out the data sharing needs of all partners working with individuals rough sleeping across Nottinghamshire, i.e. what data they require from others, for what purpose and the systems that are involved. It will also review the software options available to us to enable this data sharing and the most effective solution.
column8: Once awarded the funding, we would work with MHCLG to procure the required expert support to carry out the scoping exercise. The key milestones for this individual would be to engage with the partners listed as consultees in this application to confirm the data they require from others, for what purpose and the systems involved. At the same time they would review the functionality and capacity of the systems currently in use and also those available to us (including those detailed above) to identify the most effective solution to meet our data sharing needs. Finally, they would coordinate a small scale pilot of any proposed solutions (likely to be a partial solution) involving systems that are currently available to us. At the end of the project, they would provide a report detailing the findings of the project and recommendations for the next steps to operationalise the solution, including any development work to enable systems to connect, as well as technical specifications that can be used by IT teams in the partner organisations going forward.

Through the pilot, the potential impact of the fully operationalised solution would be considered and the impact on the management of the rough sleeping cases included in the pilot would be assessed.

column2: Residential Care Master Data Management– developing an integrated data product to support residential care providers across the health and social care system
column4:
Data accessibility across health and care is critical to ensure informed decisions are made to support the best health outcomes for care residents. Making sure data can be shared and embedded in local pathways is key to planning effective interventions and timely support. 

Our project will join up data across the health and social care system to help providers and commissioners better understand the impact of our health and care services and their interdependencies. It will allow us to consider innovative ways of delivering health and care together which may be blended roles, pooled budgets, and person centered outcomes.
column7: Early in the COVID response it became apparent that there was a need to have improved alignment between data held by different partners across health and social care; but also that there were opportunities if data sets were joined together in new ways to derive new insight into activity and responses. Working as a multi-agency group a series of performance data tools were developed to enable intelligence to be gathered, shared and analysed across health and social care settings. Using data collected daily on staffing levels, PPE supplies and covid infection levels of residents and services users we developed a Covid specifc OPEL type escalation framework which was applied and monitored directly with provider settings, to identify providers requiring support. As the pandemic developed and the NHS Capacity Tracker was broadened we integrated this data from into our output. These tools have been used by partners to direct and inform responses and shape how they engage with local providers. Specifically, this meant we were able to target specific, joined-up support from local authority commissioners and operational staff, local infection control, mental health trust, and community nursing to care homes most in need at the earliest opportunities.
column8:
Our Resources and local gov involvement Our experience means we’re uniquely positioned to maximise the benefits of joined-up working. Our new Integrated Commissioning Service will sponsor this project ensuring cross-system buy-in. Specifically, this project will embed the close working across the health and social care system we’ve experienced during the Covid crisis in a tool to better manage resilience across the sector.   Data requirements There is a wide range of data available relating to care homes and in order for the market to be better supported these data need to be brought together to provide collective insights from across the health and social care system enabling and encouraging collaborative actions. Pathfinder local products would explore and utilise feeds from data sources such as:  
  1. NHS Capacity tracker (NECS) – overall capacity
  2. Local authority data on care home admissions and discharges to care homes and homes in a position to accept new placements
  3. CQC provider data – inspection outcomes and provider registration
  4. Registered deaths – monitoring trends
  5. Outbreak Monitoring PHE – history of covid outbreaks + all relevant available resident/staff testing data
  6. GP data (NECS)
  7. Healthcall data – registered patient and referral data
  8. Local monitoring visit outcomes from local authority commissioners and health services
Involvement from the broader population This development would provide a route for other authorities/systems to replicate for their own local dashboards to support their local market as well as monitor their own vulnerable population health and care sector outcomes. The local users of the final provider level products will include our integrated care board partners including clinical commissioning group and foundation trusts, well as other public sector agencies. The development of this will also include provider engagement led by Durham County Council Commissioners, and updates/consultation with regional ADASS partners (ADASS).

column2: Using video to enable face to face service in the age of COVID-19
column4: A key COVID-19 challenge for Stevenage Borough Council is how to improve face to face support to our customers, who need our help more than ever, whilst at the same time minimising unnecessary journeys and in-person contact. We want to use video calling to supplement our limited capacity for safe in-person customer appointments and support high priority services like homeless assessments and debt management, where we think that being able to see each other will build trust and facilitate better outcomes on difficult and emotional subjects.
column7:
As lockdown started our homeless caseworkers reported that they found it harder and more time-consuming to fully understand someone’s situation and thoroughly assess their case over the phone rather than in person. This lead to the initial inception – could we use video to enable face to face conservations in a time of COVID-19?

Our discovery work:

Similar impacts were identified for helping our tenants with debt. We also discovered that there were other situations in which video could potentially help, but requires practical testing to find out more, such as reporting a death and assistance completing a housing benefit application

We found that people in 3 of our 9 customer segments are much more likely to contact us about Homeless and housing advice services. Two of those segments were also more likely to contact us about rent, along with a segment representing poorer older people.

Identified that some staff will require equipment, and because of the customer segments we will also need to provide safe video booths for customers to use if they don’t have their own devices. We have also found that off-the-shelf video technology will likely be suitable for our needs.

There will be a need for us to develop safeguarding and data protection procedures – especially for staff taking video calls at home. We also need to understand more about customer attitudes and what we can do to make video a successful medium.

Adoption of video calls fits in with our emerging vision for our customer interface, and aligns well Co-Operative Neighbourhood strategy of finding new ways to engage with our community.
column8:
We have developed 9 customer segments to use for understanding our customers, as follows:

Comfortable Families

Comfortable Seniors

Dependent Seniors

Metro Community

Modest heritage

Prosperous Positions

Social Renters

Striving Singles

Struggling Families

In our discovery work we have identified that users of the Homeless service are much more likely to come from the following segments: Social Renters, Striving Singles, Struggling Families.

We also identified that users of the rent service are much more likely to come from the following segments: Social Renters, Struggling Families, Dependent Seniors

Behind each of the segments sits demographic, behavioural and service use information drawn from a combination of Experian Mosaic, our own anonymised customer data, and Hertfordshire County Council’s anonymised customer data.

Because video is inherently a participative process, we identify our staff users as being caseworkers within the Housing Options team, Income Advisors and Debt Advice Support Workers within the Income team, and Customer Service Advisors within Customer Services.

Key activity milestones:

Week1

Define staff equipment requirement (needed for procure stage)

Define customer equipment requirement (needed for procure stage)

Define video software requirement (needed for procure stage)

Develop initial safeguarding procedure for video calls (needed for Pilot calls stage)

Develop initial data protection procedure for video calls (needed for Pilot calls stage)

Develop initial etiquette/how to procedure for video calls (needed for Pilot calls stage)

Week 2

Procure (needed for IT configuration stage)

Week 4

IT configuration of tablet devices (needed for Pilot Calls, and installation of equipment stage)

Week 5

Installation of customer equipment (needed for Pilot calls-Homeless)

Pilot calls commence – Homeless

Pilot calls commence– Debt

Pilot calls commence– Customer Services

Weeks 6-7

Assess, learn and iterate loops

Week 8

Collect latest procedures and learning into our shared guide

Collect latest measures for sharing

Approach

Although equipment is needed to make this project work, the heart of it is really about how to make video calling successful with real customers and real conversations; as video calling is now an off-the-shelf product we’re not expecting to focus much on the technicalities of how to make it work.

Consequently the sooner we can start pilot calls, and start the learning and re-iteration of our procedures the better. It also gives us longer to understand what’s important in practice from a customer perspective – does it matter if they can see staff sat in their own home is it better to have our logo displayed as a virtual background etc. How many customers have devices but don’t feel confident actually speaking to our staff virtually? Etc

Although the outline milestones make it look like quite a waterfall project, in practice, it appears that some of the Income team (working on debt) already have tablets that could be used for making calls to customers that have their own devices, and thus don’t have to wait for extra staff equipment or customer video booths to be set up.

Our previous experience of digital project is that it’s important to quickly identify team members who are brave enough to try new things, get them using the new way of working early, and form to then lead and encourage colleagues. We will therefore be flexible with our commencement of piloting calls, and get it started where it can get started as quickly as possible.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Our aims for this project are to time minimise unnecessary journeys and in-person contact.

To assess our success at this, and the potential of video calling in general we will measure:

  • Cost to serve over course of project and projected over lifespan
  • Number of journeys avoided
  • Number of in-person meeting avoided
  • Customer satisfaction with video calling
  • Customers reached by video that can’t attend in person (e.g. shielding)
  • Customers that couldn’t participate by video and why

And to help with understanding the outcome in human terms we also be capturing customer stories e.g. today’s video call helped me because….

We also want to understand and share how to make video calls really successful as a medium, not just technically achievable. One of the outputs of the project will therefore be a re-usable guide of what worked and didn’t worked, including procedures that we developed, and our measures of success.
column13:
We also want to understand and share how to make video calls really successful as a medium. One of the demonstrable successes of our project will therefore be a re-usable guide of what worked and didn’t worked, including procedures that we developed.

Other councils (and partner agencies) will be to use this output to help them understand whether video calling might be a good solution for them. And if it is, to make sure they start their own projects with a headstart.

We will also be happy to speak to other authorities about our experience, and share our learning.
column14:
Video calling will not be suitable for everyone, and so we will be using it in addition to the limited amount of safe in-person meetings we can offer. This extra capacity will in turn free up in-person capacity for people that are unable to use the video option for whatever reason.

To extend the video reach we will be looking for a software that provides on-the-fly captioning, and setting up video booths to enable customers that can travel to our customer service centre (it’s next to public transport hubs) to make use of devices there.

Assistance with how to craft our messages to customers so that they are as enticing as possible for trying video calling.

Advice on how safeguarding and data protection procedures, especially given that many of the staff will be operating video calls from their own home.

Assistance with making our output learning guide easy for others to use, and look professional.

column2: Future Reservists Model
column4:
In response to the pandemic, we had to quickly set up mechanisms to ensure the deployment of staff to ensure the continued delivery of critical services.

Our ‘Reservist Model’ thus evolved, enabling us to utilise the Wigan Council workforce to work flexibly, and across organisational boundaries in an integrated way, providing a whole system response.

As we ‘build back better’, our aim is to embed our ‘Reservist Model’ into our new ways of working for the future enabling us to better respond to future emergencies and business continuity planning.  To be successful, we need the digital infrastructure to support this.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We have clearly set out within Appendix 1 the outcomes to be achieved by implementation of our ‘Future Reservist Model’, and of key parts of the project in Appendix 2.   In terms of this digital project specifically, we can measure success by achievement of the following outcomes:  
  1. Staff reservist data will be held within our ITrent HR & Payroll System, replacing the ‘live’ staffing registers that we are having to manually maintain
  2. All staff will be linked in the system to a ‘neighbourhood’ to which they would be deployed
  3. All staff will be linked in the system to a ‘specialism’ which determines the work/activities they would be deployed to undertake
  4. We will have the facility to record all training and work placements undertaken by staff in respect of their Reservist role
  5. We will have the ability to record all current and future deployments
  6. We will have a suite of reports and will be able to produce comprehensive workforce dashboards enabling us to effectively manage and monitor our future emergency and business continuity response(s).

column2: Broxbourne and East Herts Employability Support Project
column4:
Supporting recently unemployed residents to improve digital skills and increase chances of getting back into work swiftly. The scheme, designed to provide digital routes to retrain of the workforce. This would include navigating the job seeking market, access to CV writing and interview skills. The scheme will address confidence and assurance of individuals to make changes to give a better chance of re-employment. The scheme seeks to train volunteers to work with this cohort to improve employability.
This work will enhance the services the DWP provide helping to reduce the number of benefit claimants and avoid council tax arrears.
