column2: Developing an App for residents to deliver services
column4: During lockdown the Council had to close critical face to face services. We have responded to this challenge by serving more residents digitally, yet for some this remains a challenge. As lockdown eases, challenges for councils remain, with higher demands for services from an increasing group of vulnerable residents, reduced budgets and continuing social distancing requirements, accessible digital tools are more important than ever. We want to deliver a digital front door that will be designed around the resident with accessibility a critical feature. We know the most vulnerable people in our society may not own a PC but do have access to a smartphone we want to deliver digital services in a way they can access simply and easily, as well as being tailored to their needs. The app we will develop will deliver standard council front door services, and, as such, will be reusable across a wide range of authorities
column7:
We have already carried out significant work to create online engagement with our residents which could be much easily deployed through the app in future.

The key examples are:

Community support form an integrated process which targets the most vulnerable in the borough to understand their needs and to help provide support to these residents and households.

Friendly check-in service case management system for volunteers to call shielding residents from NHS list and community support form. Logs calls and escalates alert to core services of residents at potential risk or requiring additional support.

Business impact form developed a form so business can reach out and let us know how they have been impacted and identify what support they may require and a discretionary grant application process for SME to apply for support.

Financial support form for residents who are suffering severe financial hardship because you have lost all or most of your income.

Library delivery form for those shielding and self-isolating to order books which are delivered to their homes.

A suite of forms / processes which looks at both sickness in relation to Covid-19 and the creation of an internal agency for reassigning staff to essential front-line work. All the information collated works together to understand what is happening with the workforce and who is available for reassignment (looking also at their skills).

An online booking form made available to funeral director / undertakers that confirms the dimensions of the deceased and replaces paper processes.

The App has been developed by the supplier Granicus and is ready to be developed for the use of local authority.  The product will be integrated with the local systems including CRM.   The key will be to develop and test the product in consultation with the internal Comms team and residents to create a platform that can be shared also by a number of other authorities who use Granicus platform.   The initial consultation will also involve members to ensure the product delivers on resident’s priorities and expectations.   The timescales will depend on the supplier availability and scope for additional requirements but below is an indicative timetable following Agile methodology:   Discovery week 1-2

Alpha week 3-6

Beta week 6-7
  • Testing, consultation and final sign off.
  Live: Week 8
  • Product ready for release.

column2: Home Work Station Assessment
column4: As more of us work from home, often in improvised office space, poorly designed workstation or working environments presents a real risk to both personal health and wellbeing, and to productivity.  Managers are limited in their ability to assist with or supervise workstation assessment, so thorough self assessment becomes key.  This project seeks to give individuals the best possible guidance on workstation self assessment at the same time as streamlining managerial intervention and the provision of appropriate equipment.

column2: Digital Resilience for Town Centres
column4:
Maybe* research have demonstrated 30% of high street businesses in the Rochford District have a social media presence, only 26% are active on a daily basis; surprisingly, Covid-19 has created a 50% drop in High Street social media output. 

We are working closely with stakeholders in our community to reverse the negative consequences of Covid-19 on our already declining High Streets and use this disruption to drive positive change to make our High Streets vibrant and attractive places of the future.

This pilot project will test new ways to increase community engagement and stimulate economic recovery across our towns.
column8:
1/ The delivery of the technology and deployment of this project will be resourced by Maybe* in collaboration with Rochford District Council.  In order to make information publicly accessible, Maybe* will use Slack, Trello and Google docs.  If funding is granted there are no resource constraints   2/ Rochford District Council is directly involved, collaboration with other government sectors will be welcomed   3/ The core data will be collected by Maybe*.  All survey and consumer spend data will be displayed within the Maybe* dashboard and is GDPR complaint.   4/ This initiative will provide a tool set and methodology to facilitate business engagement with the community.  It aligns with the necessary changes Covid-19 has imposed and drives “life after Covid” success.   Project plan is as follows:   Week 1 – intensive engagement of local community   Week 2-3 – Deployment of the Maybe* platform Including:
  • Town wide surveys using Facebook
  • A contactless loyalty programme. This will link Facebook, credit and debit card spending in a physical location.
  • A plug in for the council website / others that serve the consumer desire to communicate with retailers prior to visiting a town or to book an appointment.
  Week 2-8 – Insights work to…
  • Report on survey insights
  • Measure uptake through local engagement, spend and booking data
  • Define the most effective and efficient decision-making process and drive local place shaping based on new local data
  • Measure in-process indicators:
    • local organisations with a digital presence
    • daily businesses active on social media
    • consumers engaged in this project
    • sample of local spend (baseline established)
    • sample of appointments booked (baseline established)
Week 2-8 – Engagement and training for local businesses
  • Using strength-based approach, work with business champions to highlight and promote early adopters of the technology, inspiring others to follow suit.

column10:
The success of the project will be measured in terms of:
  1. The number of businesses that engage with the initiative
  2. Local business revenue increasing as a direct result of this approach*
  3. Footfall to the area increasing (subject to grant funding to enable measurement)
  * It is acknowledged that businesses may not wish to share their revenue, in which case qualitative measurements via interviews and surveys will be used.   The impact of the project will be demonstrated via high level KPIs which look at our local High Street revival.  Information available on request.   Maybe* has already provided data regarding how many businesses are active on social media within our area.  This project will establish a framework for measurement and reporting that will enable management-by-fact via access to real time local data. In a socially distanced world being able to understand to what is being said locally provides the Council with improved ability to react and plan.   In addition, we will consider the project a success if we make connections by sharing our work and learning from the experience of other districts.
column14:
Since lockdown businesses’ use of social media dropped by 50% in Rochford District. Our older demographic amongst residents and traders makes it even more important to support bridging the digital divide.

The output is twofold:

1)    encourage local businesses to leverage opportunities to connect with their customers through the channels that they are already in.

2)   accelerate and increase businesses’ use of digital channels by increasing digital skills.

There is a risk that our small local businesses become a digitally excluded segment, putting them at risk of not reaching the population that represents the new opportunity of “shop local.”

column2: City Centre Movement Monitoring
column4: Anonomised Tracking of vehicles, cyclists and pedestrians to monitor traffic flows, routes taken, areas of queuing, and interractions between all users and use this information to feed into designs for new schemes.
column7:
A Pilot scheme was started in February 2020, in conjunction with Vivacity Labs ltd, who after research were found to be the market leaders for this particular type of technology.

The aim of the initial pilot scheme was to collect accurate and representative traffic (motorised and non-motorised) and pedestrian data within a contained strategic network, helping to better inform future decisions around re-development within the City Centre.  While the pandemic somewhat undermined the 'representative' nature of the data, it was apparent that the system brought added benefits; we're able to monitor changes in travel behaviour (time, volume, speed and mode) over the weeks during and since lockdown, along with using the cameras to identify social distancing "hotspots" following Vivacity's development of this tool with funding assistance from Innovate UK, helping to identify if measures needed to be put in place in these locations.

It was agreed that following the completion of the 1-year initial period (March 2020 to March 2021), Sunderland Council would have the option of purchasing the assets at a discount price taking into account the value of the initial project.
column8:

column2: Enhance C19 Response; Adding AI to Existing Digital Camera Networks
column4:
Leicester City Council will combine existing CCTV/digital cameras with Artificial Intelligence to assess the impact of measures that encourage cycling and walking, manage traffic and maintain social distancing in public transport hubs and key public areas.

The use of AI/Smart CCTV will reveal traffic patterns on roads, cycling and walking routes at key points in the city. The Covid19 crisis has highlighted the need for reliable, high quality, accessible data for sustainable transport movements in the city. 

Leicester’s Smart CCTV project will greatly improve the speed and effectiveness of data collection, making it easier to identify problem ‘hotspots’ and maintain emergency measures, such as local lockdowns, without collecting or processing any personal identifiable information. We will also be able to assess and monitor the impact of the easing of lockdown restrictions.
column7:
Previous discussions around using the CCTV network to collect data have resulted in questions around reliability and value. Within recent years visual recognition algorithms have significantly improved.   We have undertaken proof of concept tests with our partner,  Spectral 360' which demonstrated the effectiveness and potential of their video analysis AI algorithm. We believe we can significantly increase the effectiveness and value of existing our digital camera  technology through the AI integrated software.

We have made enquiries with our CCTV system partner to make available additional video output points for use during this project. This will enable us to work closer with our project  partners to assess and quantify the benefits of the numerous targeted measures and initiatives which are being delivered throughout the COVID19 crisis. If this bid is successful, we will be able to target  the cameras and AI algorithm across various hotspots in the city; including sensitive traffic load management areas, pedestrian heavy areas and pop up cycleways installed as a response to the COVID19 pandemic.
column8:
Week 1 –  Procure and install additional data access points for use by the AI Digital Camera algorithm Week 2 – Procure and begin implementation of Machine learning algorithm to Tyco access platform Week 3/4 – Testing and commissioning of the AI algorithms and associated staff training. Week 5 – Establish camera/counting strategies to extract data in a report style with real-time/daily/weekly counts of pedestrians/cyclists/other vehicles. Week 7/8 – Using available video feeds evaluate the success of current and future measures  including COVID19 and local lockdown; use data to inform future resilience strategies; review and assess the potential for the AI / video analysis system and associated “City Dashboard” to deliver significant benefits across the authority in terms of operations, service delivery & community engagement. Technical strategy:
  • The intention is to have 2 wall driver workstations, each with 2 HDMI outputs.
  • These 4 video output channels will be used by operators to select archived and live footage to be fed to the AI count system. This allows the operator to select any camera or monitor available on the core Leicester City Council CCTV/Digital Camera network, comprising of Traffic, Public Space, Housing and Redepolyables (RDCs)
  • The AI software will be installed on a standalone PC  which will physically sit with the wall drivers. The integration method between the HDMI outputs and the Machine Learning workstation will be advised by software provider which has been subject to previous PoC tests.
  • The software will capture and classify anonymised data from the video feed. The data captured will be defined by the city council staff using the AI tools.
  • Transport Strategy already have a data analysis platform to pull the AI counting data into and incorporate with existing reporting structures.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
• Successful implementation will be defined as the ability to easily collect, process & report  transport data and to measure the space between/density of cyclists/vehicles/pedestrians, highlighting areas of concern based on pre-defined alarm threshold. Where available we can also analyse historic video feeds.
  • Leicester, as the first city to go under local lockdown, has a unique perspective on immediate and COVID19 data challenges. We have experienced pressures with collection of timely, high quality and accurate data in targeted areas and hotspots.
  • We will be demonstrating success via policy implementation; we will be using the platform to evaluate the effectiveness of social distancing in public transport hubs etc. We will also be using it to analyse the impacts and benefits of implementing  over 10miles of pop-up cycle lanes.
  • The success of this tool will also include the sharing of information of ‘hotspot’ locations with partner organisations across the city. The accuracy of this tool will allow the authority to release the reported data on the Leicester Open Data platform, allowing residents to have evidence during consultations or proactively via tools such as www.widenmypath.co.uk.
column13:

We would like this project to be a critical service during Leicester's local lockdown and therefore could become part of a toolkit in supporting localised lockdowns and the long-term management of the city
  • We will be sharing the experience of this project via multiple sources. We will share via our social media channels which have numerous links to other councils.

Leicester also is co-chair of the BSI Smart Cities readiness committee, with a role to advise cities with ambitions to become ‘smarter’. This project is a key reinforcer of the idea that although specialised data collection tools can be useful, being a smart city is often about making best use of existing technology and assets.

column2: Vulnerability Index
column4:
As part of our COVID-19 emergency response, the Business Intelligence team at Barnsley created a 'COVID Vulnerability Index,' using a vast range of internal and externally-sourced datasets to identify and measure vulnerability for all households in the Borough. 

This was used to make proactive contact with almost 9000 households, checking that our residents were safe and well and were able to access the support services on offer.

This project will embed the ongoing data collection process, explore analytical methods to improve the identification of vulnerable households, introduce multiple ‘dimensions’ of vulnerability to improve flexibility of use, explore data sharing opportunities with the health sector, and ultimately produce a product which can be used to more proactively support vulnerable residents in both emergency and ‘business as usual’ scenarios. 

The project will also explore potential use cases within the health sector and examine the information governance arrangements required to enable them.

The methodology and specification will be made available for re-use by other local authorities, together with any technical components which may be reusable, including ETL routines, database tables and reporting tools.
column7:
Our COVID Vulnerability Index was created during March and April in response to a strategic need to identify vulnerable people who might require support from the Council during the pandemic.

Using a combination of existing internal datasets and new data supplied by external organisations, a set of 26 indicators was built for each household within the Borough. Indicators were weighted, classified and tested before producing a composite index score indicating vulnerability against the set of input factors.

The outputs were presented as a PowerBI report which displayed a household view to staff working on the Council’s emergency response. Whilst speaking to residents, staff were able to see the household composition, contact details, vulnerability index score, markers indicating the nature of vulnerability, government shielding status, and a summary of recent contacts made.

This report was used both in a reactive scenario (where residents required emergency support) and proactively, where a team of 33 staff made calls to residents to check on their wellbeing and offer Council services to those in need. The index allowed the identified vulnerable households to be prioritised by their index score, so the most vulnerable people were contacted first.

The methodology has been documented and shared with other local authorities and via the Public Health England knowledge hub, and is available as a PowerPoint slide deck to support the bid if requested.
column8:
Solving the Challenge: On completion of the project, the data product will be available for immediate and continued use within the Local Authority, and will contribute to proactive targeting of services to residents in our COVID-19 recovery plan. It will also support the monitoring of impact of outbreaks and support the identification of vulnerable residents in the event of localised lockdowns. A concurrent Information Governance assessment and engagement workstream will explore the opportunities, risks and barriers to sharing the data products within local health services. NHS colleagues have shown considerable interest in using the outputs created so far, and this stream of the project will focus on defining and testing the feasibility of specific use cases, such as supporting the ‘discharge to assess’ process. By sharing and promoting our work for re-use within the local government sector (and with MHCLG support) we would envisage adoption of the methodology elsewhere. Deliverables:
  1. Information Governance (IG) evaluation of all input datasets to determine appropriateness of use in emergency and ‘business as usual’ scenarios
  2. Identification and IG evaluation of additional input datasets which could indicate vulnerability within identified use cases.
  3. Automation of data processing into Data Warehouse for all input datasets.
  4. Define and construct Data Warehouse structure to be flexible and re-usable for any set of indicator inputs/datasets.
  5. Develop Extraction, Translation, Load (ETL) processes to create household summary and indicator flags as database tables, using both Unique Property Reference Number (UPRN) and people-matching processes.
  6. Create configuration tables to record indicators and weightings, and enable customisation.
  7. Test, validate and improve methodology for creating composite/index score and allow for multiple ‘dimensions’ of vulnerability.
  8. Update/configure reporting tool to visualise the revised data model.
  9. Detailed exploration of IG risks, issues, barriers and opportunities for sharing vulnerability index with health sector partners.
  10. Stakeholder engagement to understand needs and attitudes to proposed data usage, including with local authority/NHS staff and residents.
  11. Publication of methodology, best practice, IG findings and re-usable technical components.
Project Plan (8 weeks)

11 Publications BMBC BI team x x
Support Required:
  1. Expert advice on IG elements via ICO/facilitated by MHCLG
  2. Consultancy support for data processing automation
  3. Consultancy support for reviewing and improving the methodology for creating the index
  4. Consultancy support for database design/configuration
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success would be demonstrated by the key outputs:
  1. A data product available for immediate use by BMBC and/or health partners to proactively identify vulnerable people and shape further support offerings.
  2. Publication of methodology and re-usable components for other local authorities to adopt.
  3. Publication of findings of IG review into input datasets and potential for sharing in NHS/health settings.
Since the timescales for the funding bid and project completion are extremely short, it is difficult to predict the impact of the project beyond the use cases we are aware of or can predict locally – and these are currently evolving. The project to date has already demonstrated a significant positive impact in the local identification of households requiring proactive support, and we anticipate this will be an ongoing requirement through further ‘waves’ of the pandemic. We would hope that by sharing best practice we would identify other local authorities who wish to adopt and benefit from the project, which would provide a measurable impact, and may lead to further opportunities for collaboration. Similarly, a detailed exploration of the IG issues surrounding data sharing with our local health partners could unlock opportunities for embedding the data product in their settings, which would have a positive impact on local health services.
column13:
• By ‘working in the open, blogging, promoting and using MHCLG networks, other councils will benefit from the existing work. We’ve already received a lot of positive feedback from others about this and shared parts of our work, including with MHCLG itself.
  • The indicators identified, weightings applied and methodology for creating a composite score will be published as a best practice guide, building upon previous guidance from LGA. All will be applicable to and reusable by other councils.
  • Several technical components will be sharable and re-usable for other councils, depending on the technology they employ. This will include ETL routines created in FME, Microsoft SQL Server database and config tables, and Microsoft PowerBI reports.
  • Findings from the Information Governance work will be shared, and hopefully provide clarity and guidance in an area which is highly complex.
  • Applications of the index and reporting tools within BMBC and the local health service will be shared as case studies, promoting understanding of how such an approach can benefit citizens.
column14:
The project has already delivered benefits to digitally excluded residents by ensuring that proactive support has been offered by letter and by telephone to residents who might otherwise have been unaware of the support services available, which were promoted primarily through digital channels.

By developing the data product as a sustainable output which can be used on an ongoing basis, this would help to ensure that those same benefits can be achieved in any future scenarios, such as a localised lockdown.
column16:
We would like to learn and benefit from MHCLG experience and networks to promote awareness of the work undertaken, in order to facilitate re-use and collaboration. Advice and support on ‘working in the open’ would be gratefully received.

The Information Governance assessment will be conducted by our own staff, but would benefit from having direct access to support from the Information Commissioners Office. If MHCLG are able to facilitate this access, this would benefit the project.

Whilst we would prefer to undertake any procurement ourselves, we would welcome direct MHCLG involvement in the project in any other way. In particular, any support which could be offered in relation to project management and documentation of outcomes would be welcome.
column17: Direct access to and/or expert advice from the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO) would be extremely beneficial in supporting the IG workstream

column2: Online Affordable Food Pantry
column4: The virus has highlighted an increased need for our residents to access free or affordable food with over 3,500 households receiving additional emergency food parcels in Carlisle alone in recent months. With a possible increase in unemployment and future loss of income for many households, more families are at risk of falling in to food poverty. This project aims to increase the access and awareness of locally affordable food projects by providing a coordinated online presence; enabling people to purchase affordable food from a website/app which could then be delivered and/or picked up from a nearby community-centred location.
column7: Cumbria County Council had been working with community groups to set up a coordinated approach to affordable food hubs before CV19 began. The increase in need for emergency food parcels in response to CV19 highlighted challenges presented by the rural geographical area of Cumbria and the ability to ensure accessibility of local hubs.  The idea of an online digital platform being developed came from this.  There has been some initial research into websites and food apps and potential approaches to develop a useable coordinated system that would make the most of the food available; ensuring it is accessible and distributed appropriately to reduce food waste.  The major food distribution voluntary sector groups have been involved in the early stages of this project (CV19 Community Help group, Longtown Food Bank, Brampton Area Action Group, Brampton Acting Together, Affordable Food Hub Steering Group, Food Carlisle).  Initial conversations have also taken place with local private sector food groups including ASDA and Tescos, as well as a local website developer.
column8: The project is ready to begin and the plan for 8 weeks would be: Week 1 – Further analysis of data.  Consultation with local community food distribution groups on the concept/model and agree clear design parameters and requirements. Formation of a digital steering group of interested partners. Initiate user research. Week 2 - 4 –Development of appropriate solution (e.g. website/app) using the parameters provided by the community organisations and initial user research. Consultation with digital steering group for feedback throughout development, using agile approach. Prototype solution for testing by end of week 4 Week 5 - Alpha testing with trusted users, steering group organisations. Week 6 and 7 - Beta testing with trusted users/Pilot areas. Introduction to local private sector businesses  Week 8 - Roll out to pilot areas for real world ‘BETA’ launch. We have current data on incoming food from varying sources (e.g. end of shelf life food from supermarkets, donated food from drop off points, purchased food from local producers, and self-grown food). We currently have information on how this is distributed, but we need to coordinate this information better in one place and develop an established process and clear affordable pricing structure for the public to access.  The primary users of this project will be people who are in need of affordable food across the local authority area, particularly those new households who have been accessing free food because of a change in circumstances. The digital project will also bring local community food distribution groups together to provide a coordinated database of food availability across the area, promoting an accurate way to measure the distribution through these wide ranging organisations/hubs. The project will have a steering group with representatives from local voluntary sector community food distribution groups.  Early research and testing will be done with members of the public who are users of local food banks. The project has the full support of the Local Authority, with resource committed from Cumbria County Council Community Development and Digital Development teams, along with Food Carlisle.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: Feedback from users and organisations will be key to developing the solution to ensure it serves the needs of the groups and people who will be using it.  The project’s success and impact will ultimately be demonstrated by people in the community accessing and using it.  By working closely with the local community food distribution groups we will be able to identify the households who have been able to move from a reliance on free food towards affordable food. These will be an important measurement in the success of the project. We will also build in mechanisms for getting ongoing feedback on the usability of the service and suggestions on how it may improve.  The development of a system will support reporting of clear and unambiguous data showing the amounts of food available, the amount that has been distributed, and the amounts of local food that has been saved from being wasted. It is hoped this project will have a long term benefit in showing the increasing amounts of local food avoiding landfill and going direct to people who need it the most. Having this developed will significantly change how this information is collated locally, reducing manual intervention.
column13: The development of any solution will use accessible mainstream software which would be available to any other local authority or organisation to use. We will be happy to share the coding/programming to anyone wishing to look at increasing the accessibility to affordable food, and measure effectively food waste across their authority. This will be built in to any agreement with the website/app developer.  One of the other key outcomes is to develop a coordinated approach between affordable food hub outlets and community food distribution groups which is something that the community development team can advise others about. We will share the user feedback and research and we are committed to working in the open throughout the project in order to share the learning throughout the 8 weeks.  We will use blogs and show and tells to communicate with others about the project and will share our experiences.
column14: We are aware that there are potential issues of digital exclusion amongst the user group that this project is targeting.  As part of the project we have asked for some funding to provide resource to support outreach work from our community teams to support people in gaining access to any online solutions.  Our libraries are key locations where users will be supported to access the website if needed, and local community centres and village halls have accessible computer terminals should people need to use these. A small number may not have access to suitable internet connectivity, but they will be able ring or drop in to their nearest community based affordable food hub to speak to someone direct and access their food needs as required.  Accessibility will be built into the solution by design, and will be part of the feedback sought throughout the project.
column16: Access to potential areas of user research already undertaken, access to datasets to support development of the project and further support in awareness raising of work being undertaken. We have already contacted some private sector organisations locally, but these are companies that have both local and national presence.  Given the potential to roll out this project in other areas it may help to have support from MHCLG to leverage support from these organisations more widely.
column17: Support from private sector partners would be beneficial, we have initiated contact with organisations who have both local and national presence and would like to continue this work on a wider basis if successful.

column2: Vulnerable Persons Register
column4:
During a major incident, one of the key priorities is to identify vulnerable people involved, utilising data held by multiple organisations.  Collating this data is currently done reactively. It is time consuming and can lead to delays in decisions being taken; directly impacting on individuals’ outcomes. 

This project seeks to facilitate the proactive collation of data in one place to support easy access, analysis and cross referencing.  It is intended that this data would be maintained on an ongoing basis using APIs where possible to ensure that should an emergency event occur vulnerable people can be quickly identified and the data shared with the appropriate agencies.
column7:
To date a piece of work has been undertaken within the authority in relation to those shielding during COVID19, which has been beneficial in providing information to support the distribution of food and medicine supplies.  This has enabled us to start to build our own database of vulnerable people, pulling in Adult Social Care data and NHS Shielding data.  Exploratory work has also been undertaken with Sellafield on mapping this data.

 

Research has been carried out on the subject area and initial discussions held with the Local Resilience Forum (LRF) Programme Board to seek input and support with progressing the project.

A project team has been established with representatives from partner organisations and the Digital Transformation team. A senior responsible officer has been identified who will sponsor the project as well as acting as an escalation point to then feed issues and update reports back into the LRF.

This project builds on lessons learned from other emergency events in the county such as flooding and extreme winter weather.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We intend to use the POC solution during the next planned emergency exercise with sample data as appropriate.  The initial measures of success during this exercise will be:
  • The ability to access accurate data with minimal notice
  • Accessibility for different agencies
  • Established appropriate data access levels to ensure compliance with General Data Protection Regulations and the Civil Contingencies Act.
  It is planned that we will complete the build of the prototype solution using ‘Liberty Create’ (a Low Code software development platform).  We have proven ability to develop very quickly with this solution with minimal investment of time; thus we are confident we can deliver the POC and associated lessons learnt quickly without a significant overhead of building resource.   If successful it is intended that there will be active program of future phases of development to ensure that the solution continues to evolve as required.
column13:
The project will directly benefit all 6 Borough Councils in Cumbria, as well as the neighbouring authorities of Lancashire and Dumfries and Galloway.  There is also the potential to engage with Northumberland County Council in the future.

 

We intend to offer benefits to non-neighbouring council’s through sharing learning about the findings of the initial POC development and associated processes.  In addition we would be able to share the POC solution for all other local authorities or third party organisations who use the Liberty Create Platform, plus the service patterns and data on-boarding processes would be sharable with the wider local government community.  Liberty Create is currently used by   a growing number of local authorities, NHS authorities and some utility providers).  The platform has an active network of development forums and a key benefit of the software is the ability to create ‘apps’ which can quickly be implemented by other authorities using the platform.  Benefits could thus quickly be realised by a wide number of other councils.

We are committed to working in the open and sharing the learning throughout the project using for example show and tells/a blog.
column14:
As the solution is intended to be used by staff at partner organisations then digital exclusion of the wider population is not relevant to the first phase of the project.

We will be working closely with the key stakeholders to understand the requirements about accessibility to data which will include any requirements for accessing data digitally where there may be technological limitations (i.e. hardware used by different organisations and network coverage given the rural geographic nature of the county).
column16: Whilst we would want to run the project ourselves, we think help from MHCLG would be beneficial in accessing user research, data sources that could be of benefit and the leverage this would provide with other organisations we would wish to work with.  Support around data sharing implications would also be beneficial.
column17: Support from private and public sector organisations locally would help to deliver the project at speed.  Initial discussions have demonstrated that there is support for the project and we would want to develop these relationships further.

column2: Being Well at Home: Co-creation of strength-based and person-centred care plans for domiciliary care
column4:
Many local authorities commission in-home care through a ‘time and task’ approach, on the basis of an agreed number of hours to meet eligible needs.

More innovative, agile and flexible commissioning approaches are required to accommodate the anticipated additional demand resulting from Covid-19 and to support shielding individuals. This includes post-infection morbidity, informal carers returning to work and new mental and physical conditions resulting from shielding. 

This project will develop new digital and data-enabled working practices to co-create personal care plans. This will support the commissioning of more holistic, self-prescribed and self-managed care that integrates existing voluntary and community support.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
The project will integrate learning and resources from existing platforms to develop new digitally enabled working practices in Adult Social Care.   Citylife Line was developed by NCC as a centralised, coordinated and targeted response to the urgent needs of citizens during the Covid-19 lockdown. This established new relationships with community and voluntary organisations and new data on food poverty, insecurity and the 10,300 shielding residents in the city.   NCC’s Adult Social Care team has developed frameworks for strength-based person-centred care plans. The project will also benefit from the resources available at the social prescribing NHS Futures collaboration platform.   Information Now is NCC’s online directory of local services, helpful information, community groups, voluntary organisations and neighbourhood events.   This project will align with other live development activities:
  • The Being Well initiative that is transforming care provision from ‘time and task’ to outcomes focused.
  • The introduction of new case management software Eclipse in Autumn 2020. This provides new opportunities for remote access to full client records and accessible, personalised assessments.
Participation in Great North Care Record regional information sharing programme that will enable patient records to be shared with health and social care professionals in the region.
column8:
The project will be delivered by NCC with support of key partners, including:
  • Newcastle Disability Forum
  • Connected Voice
  • Healthwatch Newcastle
  • Newcastle Disability Forum
  • Care providers
  Partners in neighbouring authorities will be engaged to review the overlap in voluntary and community services, support data-sharing, referrals and reuse.   Week one will consist of a preliminary scoping, project set-up and procurement. NCC will make use of existing frameworks and contracts to support rapid deployment. This will be followed by a two-week discovery with all partners to:
  • Review the way social workers can assess peoples’ needs to achieve a clearer focus on outcomes.
  • Define end-to-end care pathways and inefficiencies, such as:
  • Open-ended and unsuccessful referrals.
  • Loss of information between services.
  • Insufficient information from individuals on referral.
  • Duplication of effort in assessing individuals in their care journey.
  • Ethical and security considerations/regulations on sharing data between services.
  • Identify best practice across UK adult social care in the development of strength-based and person-centred care plans.
  • Identify innovative preventative services and wider support networks, including local and online resources.
  • Develop a clearly articulated set of user needs and priorities.
  Weeks 4 to 7 will see development of a digital toolkit and guidelines. It will focus on new working practices and integration across existing digital platforms to:
  • Make community-based and online support more accessible through digital pathways.
  • Develop care plans to support self-care and measure improvement journey.
  • Create an online triage function that matches needs to support.
  • Maintain online community directories.
  • Case management software which quantifies the impact on patients and the local voluntary and community sector.
  • Prompts and guides for quality assurance.
  Week 8 will focus on evaluation, sharing of learning, recommendations for further development and reuse.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The project will focus on the 6-week reablement service to support rapid evaluation of success and impact. It will aim to:
  • Support self-management
  • Facilitate shared decision making between professionals and the people they support
  • Create new links to voluntary and community-based support
  • Facilitate the development of personalised care and support plans
  • Increase the choice and flexibility of how and where people receive care
It will also consider the potential to:
  • Provide analytics to justify commissioning
  • Help patients better understand health risks
  • Free-up GP and care worker time through direct referrals and ‘tell us once’ patient profiling
  • Support community capacity building and impactful volunteering by sharing data on user needs
  • Provide accurate epidemiological information to facilitate greater reporting
  • Support the introduction of personal care budgets
column16: None.
column17: None.

column2: Culture, Connection and Wellbeing Project
column4: Culture, Connection and Wellbeing is a digital solution providing online access to a range of council, VCSE and community resources presented under interlinked themes. Social isolation was prevalent during lockdown. This resource was created to prevent loneliness and have a positive impact on wellbeing by connecting the public to cultural and wellbeing resources in a non-threatening, inclusive way, meeting needs that might otherwise escalate or add pressure to vital services. It is facilitated by a partnership of council, VCSE and community. Community involvement is invited, aspiring to a community-led, council-assisted model, capturing the zeitgeist of mutual aid experienced during lockdown.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7: In response to COVID-19 lockdown, Tunbridge Wells Borough Council (TWBC) executed measures to respond to community needs remotely. Social isolation was identified as a big issue for vulnerable/shielded people. The TWBC cultural team was already exploring how to tackle social isolation with a cultural and wellbeing approach, drawing from a growing body of evidence. Connection is vital to avoid mental stress. TWBC Culture team developed a community engagement digital concept, at pace, to create an online cultural platform linking connection and wellbeing, called TW Social. A multi-agency Social Isolation Group was established to provide insight, identify need, co-produce content for the website and target support through outreach projects. Members included VCSE focused on housing, homeless, young people, elderly, SEND, welfare, domestic abuse, and vulnerable families. The website is rudimental, but an evaluation has highlighted the potential as a self-help resource with over 13,000 views in its first 9 weeks. Areas to improve include site navigation, reporting with analytics more reflective of the target audience, and the addition of an App for those identified as socially isolated, to nudge, alert and gather regular information on wellbeing for impact assessment. The digital divide was identified as a key issue to address collectively.
column8:
• Tunbridge Wells Borough Council is ready to progress immediately: the partnership is established, the audience identified, and the website is live, with community engagement.
  • A Social Isolation Group sub-group will be formed immediately with representatives from culture, public health (for wellbeing) and VCSE representatives (for connection, e.g. Town and Country Housing (Peabody), Age UK, Compaid) as well as technical expertise from the digital and project management teams in TWBC.
  • Contacts will be made with any parallel projects identified from Local Digital C-19 proposals, to ensure that best practice and learning is shared with other councils throughout the project.
  • Research of well-evidenced wellbeing / social isolation impact measures will be reviewed early August to incorporate into the App e.g. UCL Umbrella of Wellbeing or the Canterbury Wellbeing Scales.
  • Specification for the App –ready for procurement process by mid-August.
  • Co-production forum, focus groups and feedback processes will be created early August with a diverse range of community members including parish and mutual aid representatives, Town and Country Housing (Peabody) residents, and other community representatives from vulnerable groups.
  • Improvements will be made to TW Social website including navigation, analytics and streamlining content to meet community needs (linked to iterative co-production process) by end August.
  • Procurement process for the App and prototyping may exceed two months depending on availability of a digital agency. The basic design and components will be agreed within the period and some prototyping will have happened.
  • Audit and distribution of community digital resources to support the digital divide for targeted audience by September.
  • A Social Return on Investment model will be established to present the benefits of this approach specific to social isolation, with intermediate indicators by October.
  • Guidance document for replication by October.
  • If further lockdown occurs, milestones will be achievable as the entire project can be conducted remotely.
column9: MHCLG runs the procurement and project
column10:
• Google analytics is already used to analyse a range of metrics on the website including page use and time spent. Early improvements will build on this, including the enablement of demographic data collection with cookie consents. A pop-up website survey will enable postcode data to be gathered and create a more definitive link between use of the site by location and vulnerable cohort.
  • Feedback and statistics on related social media (Twitter and Facebook) will indicate how well received the content is.
  • Feedback from community co-production groups will indicate how well each element of the project is received and thorough testing with this group will build on that initial feedback.
  • Linking the digital community resources from VCSE with the App will give tangible data about the level of engagement, reduction of the digital divide, and in-App needs analysis, progress, and feedback.
  • Wellbeing/social isolation check-ins on the App will provide insight to respond to need and to identify impact.
  • SROI will demonstrate how the investment will provide savings and will increase reach to deliver a positive impact on local services such as NHS, public health and key VCSE.
  • Working in partnership will avoid duplication of effort and maximise available resources.
column13: The concept of how culture impacts on isolation and wellbeing and how connection is a key protective element of wellbeing is already well-evidenced. https://www.culturehealthandwellbeing.org.uk/, https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/stress-anxiety-depression/improve-mental-wellbeing/, https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/a-connected-society-a-strategy-for-tackling-loneliness The model TWBC is proposing, ensures that it is community-led, reaches the people who it will most benefit and provides digital and remote access to a rich array of resources which can be used independently or with targeted support by VCSE. This model is replicable and will be well informed through co-production and through the cultural expertise available within TWBC. Working in partnership to achieve wider benefit is nothing new but for this project, it addresses the digital divide, bridging a solution between the provision of devices and the effective use of them to connect and improve wellbeing. Provision of a SROI will present the business case for other authorities to follow suit. The App will be open source for wider replication and development.
column14: From wellbeing and welfare calls undertaken by TWBC Public Health team and TCH Housing during lockdown; we know the digital divide is increasing. Only 53% of those contacted had full digital access, reducing to 8% for those over 70. We will focus on older people who are technology averse, digitally unskilled or have no connectivity to access the website in alternative ways e.g. by physical assistance, access to devices, access to community wi-fi or wi-fi enabled devices. A short-term focus may be disadvantaged young people requiring additional online educational support post-lockdown. These alternative access methods will be facilitated by a range of VCSE partners who have worked hard to provide devices, connectivity, and digital upskilling but who lack resources to ensure this translates to sustainable outcomes and not just quick fixes. Adding the App to each device will increase the ability to engage meaningfully and identify needs, trends, and outcomes.
column16: MHCLG guidance may be needed to present the findings to other authorities for replication, in a compelling way. Support with selection of impact measurement tools for the App and development of a model which demonstrates social return on investment will be useful together with links to key people within government who may be able to support, advise and guide around the key themes of social isolation, culture and wellbeing. Other insight gained about the social impact of COVID-19 research will also be helpful. Facilitation of best practice sharing with other Local Digital C-19 Challenge participants will be supportive.
column17:
The project will be delivered effectively, with an ecosystem of partners committed to user-centric approaches. The more users that are attracted to the site and accessing information, events and opportunities, the greater likelihood of social return on investment. It will be a multi-agency effort with all predominantly community-facing services, to get broad reach in urban and rural areas.

There will be a legacy beyond lockdown for NHS, public health, social care, and mental health services. We will create a dialogue early in the project to establish how the project outcomes impact on reducing pressure points in high-need and overwhelmed services.

column2: Unified COVID-19 Reporting of Partnership Recovery Activity
column4: Our proposed project will build upon our COVID-19 reporting systems, to unify reporting of Partnership recovery activity including shielding hub data that we will continue to collect as part of our business as usual activity.   This will provide one picture to support lessons learnt and plan for any future surges; whilst supporting our ambition to work with individuals at an early stage to understand what they feel are their strengths and challenges, to enable professionals and the community to better identify how they can develop and support resilience to reduce health inequalities and keep those we are responsible for safe.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7: WITHIN THIS TEXT BOX WE HAVE INCLUDED THE RESPONSE TO 5.1  `OTHER' AS WELL AS THE RESPONSE TO 5.2 AS THERE IS A TEXT BOX ON THE WORD'  APPLICATION VERSION FOR THIS BUT NOT ON THE `ONLINE' VERSION.  WE HAVE SEPARATED OUT THE RESPONSE FOR THIS AND 5.2 BY A PUTTING IN A DIVIDING LINE.  IF THIS INFORMATON DOES NOT COME THROUGH DUE TO THE WORD COUNT CAN YOU PLEASE LET US KNOW 5.1: Various deliverables have been identified and some are at the inception stage whilst some are working prototypes. We have implemented an initial proof of concept in the form of reporting dashboards during our response to COVID-19, using this within Plymouth City Council, Livewell Southwest and NHS Devon CCG; it has helped different organisations work seamlessly together to deliver a response to vulnerable citizens. Partner feedback is positive but work is needed to progress this project so it can become more efficient and sustainable. We also seek to take the learning from this approach into wider cohorts across the city in time, e.g. in our thinking as we redesign our Early Help offer for families. We have started to explore how we can get information from our reporting dashboards into our back-office application CareFirst to support more efficient and effective process but cannot progress without additional resource. This project attempts to provide an additional layer to CareFirst, taking the strengths of each application, using them to their maximum; including taking the data from our Plymouth Online Directory `Proof of Concept’ into the Data Warehouse reporting to aid our vision of unifying COVID-19 reporting of Partnership recovery activity using different data sources. __________________________________________________________________________________ 5.2 To support our Local Resilience Forum reporting, we have created DataMart reports that collate relevant information into a dashboard for reporting purposes. Also developed is a daily SITREP reporting tool, linked to our Plymouth Online Directory that our Community Providers can access. This allows us to view complex information easily and t make decisions quickly. Central to the project has been the philosophy of ‘create once, use many times’. We have created an open architecture where we can consume and share data openly with people who need to view it. However, although sound in principle the implementation is somewhat rough around the edges and needs to be refined. We have developed a `standalone Proof-of Concept’ for our Wellbeing Hubs in our communities, not yet launched due to COVID-19. In principle it works in a similar way – collecting and displaying data – and we would seek to utilise this to inform our further requirements scope and build for this project These data sources are not linked and a considerable amount of manual work is needed to align the information. We have started to identify spreadsheets within MS Teams and these do not help us have a single-view of our customers and providers.

column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The development of a bespoke software package would allow us to be able to track and evidence the outcomes of local service provision within a particular area of focus, initially, using the software’s reporting functionality. For example, the Caring for Plymouth Support Hubs who support extremely clinically vulnerable people and other local vulnerable people due to their age and/or disability.

By focusing on one service area to begin with, we would be able to scrutinise the software with a small number of providers to check the software’s usability and to feedback alterations to develop the software further as part of the testing phase. The learning points from the testing phase will be used to develop future guidance and training for other partners who may wish to use the software to report on their recovery activity.

Moving forward this initial reporting could then be adapted to capture a wide range of recovery activity which would allow reports to be generated across partners. The standardised software would allow the data to be recorded in the same software and would allow commissioners to see a system level view of reporting of local activity that would delivery our vision.
column13:
We are part of the regional ADASS and IT forum where we share innovative projects and should we be successful in our bid we will share this and any lessons learnt.

The project will be developed on an ‘open’ platform, which means we can make it available to other organisations without incurring exorbitant Application Programming Interface (API) or licensing costs.

Locally, the Council and our various health partners all use different IT systems. As this project will be web-based, it means that all partners need is a web browser and internet connection. This means we do not need to create an expensive IT project; we can move from conception to delivery more quickly.

As a local health system, we are all signed-up to the concept of unifying different data sources to provide one picture. Plymouth has a very good record of accomplishment of partnership working that achieves outstanding results.
column14:
To meet government accessibility requirements, this project will:
  • Meet level AA of the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG 2.1) as a minimum
  • Work on the most commonly used assistive technologies - including screen magnifiers, screen readers and speech recognition tools
  • Have an accessibility statement that explains how accessible the service is
We will ensure we engage with a diverse audience during the Discovery phase to take into account different views and opinions. Because we have been required to learn to quickly work remotely with our partners during COVID-19 we will be looking at lessons learnt to ensure we take into account any learning that can aid this project in relation to accessibility We will undertake an Equality Impact assessment and a Data Protection Impact Assessment to inform the project.
column16: We might require advice and information at key milestones but at the present moment we do not anticipate any other additional support will be required.
column17:
Caring for Plymouth, which is an alliance between Plymouth City Council, Livewell Southwest and a large number of voluntary and community sector organisations, has been established in response to the COVID-19 pandemic to ensure that shielded and vulnerable people are provided with emotional support, shopping, medicine collection and support with paying bills and accessing money.

We would invite the Caring for Plymouth partners to help to develop and test the software including volunteers recruited through the local Plymouth Good Neighbours Scheme (PGNS). These PGNS volunteers have provided practical support to vulnerable people such as shopping and collecting medications from pharmacies.

column2: Vulnerable Persons and Reusable Devices Matching Project
column4: Development of a ‘proof-of-concept’ data matching platform to enable the matching of key vulnerable residents of any age, increasingly isolated by the social impacts of COVID-19 and identified within datasets held by council and CVS partners with suitable donated ICT equipment or devices. In addition, the platform would incorporate a Databank facility that builds upon the ethical principles of foodbanks to enable local communities to donate funds (and eventually unused data) to support connectivity to the identified vulnerable residents including direct sponsorship of individuals or families. Device use and connectivity would be supported by the established CVS digital buddy network.
column7: Throughout the COVID-19 pandemic the DIZ (www.diz.org.uk) local authorities and CVS partners have worked with and refined datasets that have enabled the identification of vulnerable or shielding residents. Through these efforts an understanding has emerged of individuals that are both digitally excluded and who could continue to self-isolate far beyond the removal of strict isolation guidance further exacerbating the extent of their digitally exclusion. The CVS organisations have already developed a ‘Digital Buddy’ system, across a one year pilot programme, focused on supporting vulnerable individuals to make best use of digital equipment and make their home lives more resilient and digitally included. This network of champions will be drawn upon to support this project and deliver the digital training that will be necessary to start users off on their digital journey. The CVS organisations and Local Authorities have done much work around food banks and the idea of communities ‘paying forward’ in order to support those in their communities unable to afford what many take for granted. It is intended that this experience, learning and the supporting systems will inform a brand new Databank concept that enables the provision of data and connectivity to facilitate connection to the online world.
column8: Strand 1 – Vulnerable Persons Data (Co-ordinated by CVS partners) Wk1 Confirm CVS teams for data exercise Identification of datasets needed and locations Seek organisational permission to column10:
Identification of appropriate datasets, including design and signing of organisational data sharing agreements along with guidance for identification, collation and analysis of datasets including the key characteristics to determine eligibility for support.

Identification of digitally excluded / vulnerable people and families.

Identification of local authority sources and supply of ICT devices / equipment and creation of dataset. Guidance for approaches to councils, corporations and local businesses to be designed including principles and guidance for the reconditioning and re-purposing of equipment.

Target number of people theoretically matched.

Target number of ICT devices donated in appropriate condition.

Creation of databank payment system including secure payment system, T&Cs, banking protocols, refund processes. Identification of models of providing connectivity – SIM cards, top-up vouchers, subsidised connectivity agreement with suppliers.

Identifying cohort of digital buddies ready to support – voluntary.

Creation of prototype digital platform to support programme.

Creation of a scheme or initiative with a reusable vision, mission statement and brand for the databank / platform concept that can be used to scale nationally and allow all LAs to adopt exactly the same model
column13:
The datasets used should be locally available to all local authorities. The data sharing agreements underpinning the matching exercise will be reusable by all LAs and adaptable to reflect local organisational arrangements.

A recommended letter of approach to organisations for ICT devices to donate will be developed and shared, alongside recommendations for the condition of donated equipment that will be agreed and shared.

The concept and mechanisms used for the Databank as well as any publicity and promotional approaches adopted to generate support and take-up can be shared. The approach taken to engage with suppliers can be shared however whilst the sharing of the format of any agreements with local connectivity suppliers may be explored, these could be subject to commercial confidentiality.

The digital platform coding should be developed in open source format that can be freely reused by other authorities including opportunities to locally brand and adapt the offer.
column14:
Those most digitally excluded and vulnerable, of any age, will be identified at the outset. Matching of equipment, training and support will account for specific accessibility requirements as will the data connectivity or allowances provided.

The platform will be for all digitally excluded sectors of society supporting the socially isolated elderly, young children without access to online learning and adults needing to access job opportunities and support for situations they may not have faced before eg. COVID-19 related unemployment.

A key element of the project, the Databank, adapts the food bank and ‘Pay It Forward’ concepts to enable individuals and communities to feel they are part of the solution to a problem in their communities. In addition, through the online platform, access to the system is given in a way that does not exacerbate any embarrassment or shame felt at having to access such support, potentially for the first time.
column16:
Guidance and advice on opening up access to additional protected datasets might be helpful as well as assistance in designing appropriate data sharing agreements.

Assistance in locating access to secure, subsidised / free software, operating systems and apps for reconditioned laptops and devices.

Advice on open source coding standards etc. that would guide the appropriate procurement and commissioning of the online platform to make it as open, accessible and reusable by other authorities as possible.

Introductions to national organisations such as DevicesDotNow or national contacts at local connectivity suppliers eg. BT
column17:
DevicesDotNow  and the Vodafone - Great British Tech Appeal could help us scale the roll out of devices once proof of concept is established.

Digital Champions Network could assist with digital training support materials

BT / Gigaclear / Virgin ie. local dominant ISPs could help us to develop appropriate subsidised data packages that could tie in with the Databank concept.

Citizens Online have previously partnered with Epping Forest DC on a digital inclusion project and could assist the DIZ in identifying characteristics and locations of vulnerable persons

column2: Building on the Foundations of Council/Community Organisation partnership working through Covid 19 to develop the insfrastructure to provide assurance on the delivery of continuing Social Value
column4:
During the Covid 19 Pandemic the City Council through its Housing and Neighbourhoods Service worked in an integrated form with the voluntary sector to ensure that all vulnerable citizens were identified, contacted, and offered access to support.  As we move out of Covid 19 there is the need to ensure that the relationships forged with the Voluntary Sector to tackle the crisis are not lost as the focus moves toward savings and cuts.

The project will deploy blockchain technology to facilitate the triangulation of the relationship between the Council, its contractors, and Voluntary and Educational establishments to ensure that Social Value to Community Organisations is identified, monitored and strategically applied in a manner that delivers a win win outcome for both contractors and the Voluntary Sector.  Through the approach support to voluntary sector organisations will be strategically aligned in a manner that both supports those organisations and the Commercial organisations who will be able to see the value that auditable Social Value can create for their own marketing.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7: Hull City Council has commissioned Microsoft Cloud Navigator to transition its server infrastructure to the Azure Cloud.   Within that project Microsoft has provided to the Council the Citizen ID product which allows the authentication of citizens and organisations establishing customer/business accounts with the Council.  Microsoft directly support the Ethereum Quorum block chain ledger[1] allowing for a Blockchain account to be attached to authenticated accounts. Hull City Council’s Cabinet and the NHS Hull Clinical Commissioning Group (CCG) have established a common executive for delivering integrated projects using a Committees in Common structure.  Together the Council together with the CCG, through the CCG as lead commissioner, tendered a Social Prescribing contract within which was provided the arrangement for a sub-contractor to identify community need for the purpose of directing grant funding.  The contractor in Hull is the North Bank Forum.  At the outset of the Pandemic the  Legal Services and Partnerships directorate engaged with the Hull North Bank Forum through the Council’s Neighbourhoods Team to develop an integrated approach to co-ordination of Council and Voluntary Sector engagement in the city to combat the impact of the Pandemic. The Council has a partnership with the Two Ridings Community Foundation.  The Foundation established the Coronavirus Community Fund at the outset of the Pandemic for the region (North Yorkshire, East Riding and Hull) and raised and is distributing £2m. Engagement has taken place with Microsoft, Sonnet Advisory, and the Community Foundation and Two Ridings upon the future arrangements for ensuring that the success in delivering a cohesive approach through the pandemic is maintained. The University of Hull have been commissioned with national funding to undertake research into Social Value.  That work stalled at the outset of the Pandemic, however, the Research and Development team will be engaged in supporting dissemination of the learning.  In addition support is accessible through Sonnet Advisory who work in partnership with Sheffield Hallam University. Through this team the Authority believe that it has in place the right team to deliver the project. [1] https://www.goquorum.com/
column8:
Within the two month window for this project Hull City Council will develop a block chain account 'proof of concept' for contractors, community organisations and educational establishments through which opportunities for the delivery of social value can be both identified and subsequently monitored based on the Pillars for the identification of Social Value developed by Sonnet.

It is intended that Sonnet provide audit of the project to ensure that there is independent verification of the project approach to ensuring confirmed social value.

The project will deploy agile principles with a Proof of Concept being tested on an iterative approach through  the Community Cohesion Project Board.

The resources deployed will be internal, partner and contractor.  Support will be provided internally through Procurement, ICT Smart City Team and Neighbourhoods.

The services to develop the blockchain front end and integrations to the Citizen ID solution used for Business to Customer Services are anticipated to be commissioned through the Council's existing contract with Microsoft.  The Social Value Assurance input is anticipated to be commissioned through Sonnet Advisory.

The broader population engagement will be through the partnership with the Voluntary Sector leadership (North Bank Forum) and the Neighbourhoods Team.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: Sharing progress within the project will be monitored through the Community Cohesion Project Board.  Development of the plan for sharing learnings more widely will be progressed the nationally funded University of Hull Social Value project and the Sheffield Hallam University relationship with Sonnet Advisory. Successful, strategically co-ordinated application of Social Value has huge potential to deliver social and financial benefits.  The methodology developed by Sonnet Advisory (within  Bates Wells Braithwaite) identified the measure of real value that are achievable through strategic application of Social Value[1]. Through working with Sonnet Advisory the intention is to develop the existing model that will provide an auditable assurance for demonstrating the social and financial benefits realisable through Social Value and enabling those benefits to be applied and monitored strategically, creating a methodology for evidencing that value. [1] https://www.ceci.org.uk/the-value-of-a-social-tenancy/
column13: This bid proposes to establish the foundation for a solution which can provide value to all Councils seeking to realise Social Value based on internationally established platforms that are accessible through the Crown Commercial Services MOU[1].  The Ethereum blockchain is an open source resource supported by Microsoft [2].  The project will enable the triangulation of the relationship between the Council, its contractors, and Voluntary and Educational establishments to ensure that Social Value to Community Organisations is identified, monitored and strategically applied in a manner that delivers a win win outcome for both contractors and the Voluntary Sector.  Through the approach support to voluntary sector organisations will be strategically aligned in a manner that both supports those organisations and the Commercial organisations who will be able to see the value that auditable Social Value can create for their own marketing. Successful, strategically co-ordinated application of Social Value has huge potential to deliver social and financial benefits.  The methodology developed by Sonnet Advisory (within  Bates Wells Braithwaite) identified the measure of real value that are achievable through strategic application of Social Value[3]. Through working with Sonnet Advisory the intention is to develop the existing model that will provide an auditable assurance for demonstrating the social and financial benefits realisable through Social Value and enabling those benefits to be applied and monitored strategically, creating a methodology for evidencing that value. [1] https://www.crowncommercial.gov.uk/news/crown-commercial-service-and-microsoft-azure-agree-new-pricing-arrangement-for-public-sector [2] https://www.goquorum.com/ [3] https://www.ceci.org.uk/the-value-of-a-social-tenancy/
column14:
Hull has a unique digital infrastructure as the only UK city with Full Fibre to the Premises connectivity to every business and household in the city together with ubiquitous Fixed Wireless Access (Radio) and LoraWan (Low power) network connectivity.  The Council has a focus upon addressing digital exclusion through a separate project which has the aim of facilitating comprehensive customer enablement.

Governance for this project will be provided by the newly established Community Cohesion Project Board developed to ensure that the lessons learned through the Pandemic are not lost, however, there will be close engagement across the projects through the Director of Legal Services and Partnerships (Sponsor) who is engaged in both projects.
