column2: Mind the Gap
column4:
Oxfordshire County Council, in collaboration with digital agency Forty Two, has developed a proof of concept web based mapping and analysis tool. To date, it has been successfully applied within Adult Social Care to solve a number of challenges.

This project will bring the tool to production readiness, and apply it to post COVID recovery through looking at:

1. Changes to care home capacity and utilisation
2. Build-up of health conditions left un-addressed during COVID representing increased future demand for health and care
3. Changes in behaviours during COVID leading to changes in the profile and types of prevailing health conditions, including mental health

This will allow Local Authorities to more effectively apply their limited resource to ensure optimal delivery of services post COVID.
column7:
Oxfordshire County Council has made initial investments to develop a digital decision support tool for commissioners in Social Care. The tool is a web app to easily map and visualise data to produce actionable insights. This allows decision makers and commissioners to have richer, more accessible insights at their fingertips to make more informed, data driven, decisions.

The tool has been co-developed with digital agency Forty Two. Forty Two work with local authorities to bring a design thinking approach to developing digital solutions to real world challenges.

The tool has so far been used in Oxfordshire to:

• Map current and projected capacity and demand for home care and reablement care
• Remodelling of delivery zones for home care and reablement care
• Financial modelling including pricing sensitivities of home care
• Travel times within zones for key workers,
• Urban and rural split of demand for home care
• Proximity of residents to other health and care services

The tool has been developed to be sector agnostic and can therefore be used to analyse data and generate insights in any sector and for any challenge that would benefit from geographic data analysis and visualisation.
column8:
The primary users of this tool will be local authority officers and analysts within adult social care commissioning and planning teams. Two objectives are proposed:
  1. Take the tool from proof of concept to production ready
  2. Build a transferable use case looking at identifying geographic and demographic areas of need post-COVID. Outcomes will include:
    • Actionable insights to support capacity planning for health and care services based on changes in demographics and needs driven by COVID. These changes could include care home capacity and utilisation, build-up of health conditions left un-addressed during COVID, and changes in behaviours during COVID leading to changes in the profile and types of prevailing health conditions, including relating to mental health.
    • Based on the success of the use case, pre-populating the tool with nationally available open data (an extensive volume of open data is already available via public APIs) to ensure the tool is ready for deployment in any Local Authority.
3.5 FTE resources will be secured across the organisations involved for the delivery of this project and does not require involvement from the broader population. The programme will be run using Agile across 4 sprints:
Sprint Dates Milestones Success Criteria
Sprint 1 3rd – 14th Aug
  • Users can upload data, select open data and save analysis securely
  • Development and Production environment creation
  • Private and Open data discovery complete
  • Feature delivery
  • Data identification
Sprint 2 17th – 28th Aug
  • Open and Private data integration
  • Use case agreement
  • Feature delivery
  • Local Authority sign off
Sprint 3 31st Aug – 11th Sep
  • Use case development and analysis
  • Oxford Brookes evaluation
  • Local Authority sign off
  • Independent verification
Sprint 4 14th – 25th Sep
  • Complete analysis incorporating Local Authority feedback
  • Unit and acceptance testing and deployment
  • Local Authority sign off
  • Testing passed
  • Deployment of tool
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The outcome of the analysis is expected to show changes in both the demand and capacity for health and social care needs due to COVID 19. These could include changes in demand for care at home, and changes to the profile of health conditions in the population, including mental health conditions. Based on analysing a combination of openly available data, as well as data held privately within Local Authorities, a successful outcome will be as follows:
  • Local Authorities gain insights and clarity about the changes in demand and capacity within health and social care
  • The insights produced from this project are able to drive actions that address these changes and make better use of existing resources and improve delivery
  • The value of the approach in combining open and private data is demonstrated and then able to be replicated to other challenges Local Authorities face
  • Successful delivery of a production ready tool with nationwide open data integrated ready for use by Local Authorities
column13:
To ensure the outputs of this project are easily reused by councils across England, the following will be ensured:
  • The product will be a fully browser-based web application eliminating installation and licensing overhead for Local Authorities
  • The product will be easy to use, with drag and drop functionality to ingest local data
  • The product will have relevant UK wide open data integrated to minimise the time it will take to integrate the tool within other Local Authorities
  • Compliance with GDPR
  • Hosting in UK data centres to UK Government standards
  • Easily consumed documentation to allow maximum utility of the product by Local Authorities which will include a video overview and product onboarding tour
  • The availability of this product will be communicated and disseminated through multiple channels to ensure Local Authorities are aware they can benefit from this project at no extra cost
column14:
The project team will follow accessibility guidelines for websites as laid out by UK Government and good practice within the UI/UX industry to ensure equal usability across all groups of users.

Given that the tool being built is for use within Local Authorities and is not going to be used by members of the public, digital exclusion does not apply.
column16: Help with nationwide communication and publicity to ensure maximum coverage with Local Authorities as rapidly as possible.

column2: Financial Inclusion Analytical Dashboard
column4:
Brent is one of the most diverse boroughs in the UK and has one of the highest numbers of workers furloughed nationally. It is anticipated there will be an increase in the numbers of residents facing financial hardship when current government COVID-19 support schemes end. 

The Council aims to enable early identification and targeted intervention for those residents facing financial hardship through the development of a Financial Inclusion Dashboard, which will bring together multiple internal and external datasets to give a holistic view of Brent residents. In this 8-week period, we will test a number of hypotheses (see 5.5) to better understand our analytical tool.
column7:
Sep’19 - Feb’20:
  • Design thinking with Adult Social Care (ASC): Design thinking sessions to define problem statements and potential solutions with service managers in ASC and Housing
Apr’20:
  • COVID Response Management App: Track and assist residents who were shielding during COVID-19
Jun’20:
  • Rental Arrears Management Solution: Streamline and increase rent collection for Brent’s Housing Management
  • COVID-19 Grants Management App: Streamline the grants process and implement logic by which grants to small businesses are allocated
  • Child & Young People (CYP) PoC:
    • Single view of the child using varied data from different sources
Jul’20:
  • Financial Inclusion:
    • Inform a holistic and cost-effective financial inclusion strategy in response to COVID-19 using data analytics
    • Intel and insight on policy design
    • Early interventions and prevention for customers facing financial hardship
    • Identifying value datasets
These streams of projects have led to the ideation of an approach that would enable Members and Officers from the Council to effectively identify and support residents facing financial hardship and provide support in a proactive manner.
column8:

column14:
Data will be incorporated from various sources to identify residents who may be digitally excluded and as such, at risk of experiencing financial hardship.

The Council is currently capturing data on digital exclusion as part of the Civic Centre sign-in process and applications for the Council's COVID-19 Support Fund.

Policy measures and interventions will consider digital exclusion. In turn, this will inform future design principles on how the Council can improve our payment making systems making them more accessible for those that are digitally excluded.

Any solutions designed throughout this project will incorporate the GDS accessibility standards.

This would enable a broader understanding of the impact of COVID-19 on Brent residents and allow for national and regional benchmarking.

Support from MHCLG officers could be given to validate data analysis and enable further understanding and identification of trends.

column2: Introduction of hybrid Public Meetings
column4:
Our project will improve community engagement with Public Meetings whilst limiting people’s exposure to Covid-19.

We wish to live stream all Public Meetings to our website to ensure that our residents and the press have full access to the democratic process without jeopardising their wellbeing. 

We would like to enhance this by introducing a hybrid style of meeting that offers meeting participants (Councilors, Officers and guests) the choice of appearing in person at our Council Chamber, Offices or virtually from home. 

Offering opportunities for those able to attend digitally will make meetings safer for anyone needing to attend in person.
column7: During the pandemic, we have been delivering exclusively remote Public Meetings using Microsoft Teams to conduct the meetings and OBS Studios to stream them to YouTube. Examples of our meetings are available at: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UC1YJ8D2hTTd2fFQFuBnTyGA/ As restrictions ease, we remain committed to delivering digital access to Public Meetings once they resume being held in our Chamber. This project will install the necessary equipment into our Chamber and provide the necessary software for us to live stream meetings held in person. Continuing to provide a live stream would negate the need for those able to watch online to attend in person. The project will also integrate our Chamber with Microsoft Teams so that meeting participants have the option to appear in person or remotely. We have been working with Public-I who are the supplier of our existing equipment to establish what additional equipment and software will be required to facilitate a hybrid style of meeting. We have seen a demo of the working solution and have been given provisional costs. Exact costs cannot be ascertained until a site survey of our Chamber can be undertaken although it is not expected that costs will vary by more than £2,000 in either direction.
column8: August Instruct Public-I to undertake the site survey to define requirements and costings. This will include how many cameras need to be purchased, how long engineers will be required onsite and how the integration between the Chamber and Microsoft Teams will be achieved Once final costings are established, a purchase order will be raised, and Public-I have advised that implementation typically takes 6-8 weeks upon receipt of a purchase order September We would expect that equipment will be installed in the Chamber in September. The work will be undertaken by Public-I’s engineers although our Infrastructure Team will be required to be present if needed. Training will then be provided by Public-I. The Infrastructure Team will be trained on the AV rack and the Democratic Services Team will be trained on operating the new meeting controls Integration will also be set up between Public-I and Modern.Gov so that the webcasts sit on our website. The majority of the work will be undertaken by Public-I but assistance will be required from our Web Developers. October We expect our first hybrid Public Meeting to take place in October. It is not expected that Councillors will require training as the equipment in the Chamber will be automated and those appearing remotely will continue to use Microsoft Teams with which they are already familiar.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success within 8 weeks will be demonstrated by the implementation of a solution that enables all residents, regardless of age or ability to be able to fully participate in Public Meetings without putting themselves at risk.

During the pandemic we have provided digital access to our exclusively remote meetings and it is essential that this level of access is not withdrawn as restrictions ease.

This project will ensure that residents continue to be able to participate in meetings from home. We strive to continually engage residents who might otherwise feel disenfranchised from meetings; perhaps interested in local issues but unwilling or unable to travel to our offices. This could be due to disability, age, ill health, lack of transport, working patterns or caring commitments.

Once our new facilities are in place, we intend to use social media to continually promote meetings to improve community engagement. We will experiment with different methods and continually monitor viewing figures.

Success will also be measured by the number of Councillors who express willingness to continue to attend remotely. We believe that providing more flexibility for Councillors to honor their meeting commitments will help us attract a more diverse range of Councillors in the future.
column13:
We are willing to share experiences with other authorities. This would include how people respond to this new way of delivering meetings and any good practices we discover that make meetings run seamlessly.

We will be particularly happy to share the level of engagement achieved with older people. Rother has a high percentage of elderly residents and we continue to look for opportunities to engage them with digital services.

During the application process we have actively sought to find another authority interested in submitting a joint bid to work in partnership with. Although we have not been able to find a willing partner, we continue to liaise with other authorities regarding opportunities for joint projects and knowledge sharing on this project and any other Covid-19 initiatives.

If the MHCLG is aware of other authority attempting a similar project we would gladly make contact with them and share knowledge and experiences.
column14:
Rother is committed to delivering accessibility with regard to digital services.

At meetings, our speakers are reminded to speak clearly so that meetings can be well followed by partially sighted people. The software we hope to purchase from Public-I includes subtitles and transcripts which will improve accessibility for those with hearing impairments.

By negating the need for people to attend meetings in person, this project will improve access to meetings for anyone with a physical accessibility issue. It will also ensure that those needing to shield due to age or underlying health condition are not excluded from our meetings.

Provision will continue to be made for the digitally excluded to attend meetings in person once it is safe to do so; either by sitting at a distance in our Chamber or in a separate, sanitised room where we will assist them in observing the meeting online using our own technology.
column15: £40,000.  The project is expected to cost £87,000 in total over a 5 year lifecycle. We are therefore asking for a partial contribution to help us establish a new way of delivering meetings which we are committed to maintaining ourselves in the long term.
column16: If MHCLG are aware of any other authorities delivering hybrid Committee Meetings we would welcome any expertise in this area.
column17:
Neighbouring authority Wealden District Council already deliver live streams of meetings from their Council Chamber. We have a good working relationship with this authority and deliver many shared services in partnership with them. These include Building Control, Environmental Health, Legal Services and our contract for Waste Collection is also shared with them.

Wealden have offered to share their experiences of live streaming meetings with us. Wealden are yet to establish a solution for hybrid meetings so we would of course be willing to share our experiences of this unique element of the project with them.

column2: COVID Impact on Place - data dasboard
column4:
To show visually in a centralised way the impact of Covid-19 on the economy and environment of the Isle of Wight.
Produce a place based dashboard from a variety of internal databases and publicly available sources. Use a data warehouse or repository to facilitate this. 
The dashboards will demonstrate the economic and environmental impact of the Covid-19 pandemic and help inform the recovery and progress toward a new normal. 
The aspiration is that this will start to allow comparison and identification of relationships between seemingly disparate data sources (e.g. between economic/housing data and school attainment).
column7: Since the start of the covid-19 crisis, analysts from across the Hampshire and Isle of Wight Local Resilience Forum have been working collaboratively to bring together a wealth of information into one compendium spreadsheet. This has formed the basis of reporting to various agencies and partners. This work demonstrated the value in collating together disparate data sources into one place, to then be utilized by different audiences resulting in efficiencies and more value added. This work was led by local public health intelligence teams and as a result focused primarily on the clinical impact of covid eg infections, hospital admissions, and deaths. There has also been a significant economic and environmental impact to our populations, but with less dedicated analytical resource in those service areas it hasn’t been possible to produce a similar compendium for that economic and place impact eg residents on furlough, unemployment rates, calls to Citizens Advice Bureau etc. The Isle of Wight Council has migrated to the MS Office 365 platform. As part of this there has been experimental use of MS Power BI to create visual displays of data. To date this has been limited and relied heavily on manual data manipulation. There is an aspiration to develop this work further and to push the boundaries of what is possible using this technology, in particular with regards to automation, but capacity and expertise is limited at present within the organization. By working with MHCLG to develop a place dashboard we would develop in-house skills which could be used to continue this transformation in future
column8: Recruitment of specialist resource from Agency – liaise with Agencies pre-project and engage contractors– week 1 (informal enquiries with recruiters have begun so that we can engage quickly in the event of a successful bid). Internal resources – scoped and redeployed in order to support external contractors      to deliver effectively on project tasks – pre project and week 1  Project scoping – weeks 1-2   IWC Covid-19 Recovery cell will identify areas of the IW Wider Economy subject to greater long-term risk. We will work with the Cell to propose and assess what data will be included in the dashboards Establish data sharing agreements – weeks 2-5 Help from MHCLG with guidance for fast tracking this work will be welcome. column2: 100% Digital Leeds toolkit
column4:
To ensure that the most disadvantaged are not hardest-hit over the next few years, people and communities need to have the best chance to sustain themselves.  Digital inclusion provides the opportunity for people to do that and gives the Council and partners the ability to facilitate better outcomes for people.  This can be achieved by convening people and community based assets to co-produce solutions rather than being reliant on services traditionally provided by Councils. 

This toolkit will set out the 100% Digital Leeds approach to digital inclusion, focusing on our response to COVID and digital’s role in Leeds’s recovery plans.
column7: In 2015 Leeds City Council launched a Scrutiny Board inquiry into digital inclusion.  In the five years since then, 100% Digital Leeds has gone from an idea in a report to an award-winning digital inclusion programme.  Much of our public-facing work is outlined on our website:  www.digitalinclusionleeds.com Behind the scenes, we have written Business Cases, contributed to strategies, submitted applications for funding, defined specifications for procurement, carried out data collection and analysis exercises and taken annual reports to Scrutiny Board. We have hosted visits or shared some of this information with 25+ other Councils on request.  However, we have not proactively collected and collated all of this information for publication until now.  This toolkit will be a useful guide for all Councils that are considering any aspect of digital inclusion to improve outcomes for their citizens. We will include a narrative that discusses ‘lessons learned’ and links to the real-world impact of the different interventions: lending equipment, training digital champions, building networks of community partners, administering grant funding and more. Writing this additional narrative and structuring the toolkit is the main piece of work that still needs to be done.  Much of the information and documentation is already in place.
column8:
• Weeks 1 – 3: Information gathering to collect all existing 100% Digital Leeds documentation, reports, blogs, articles and analysis to include in the toolkit.
  • Weeks 2 – 4: Test and learn to identify the most effective online platform for publication of the toolkit.
  • Weeks 4 – 8: Structure the toolkit, write additional narrative, prepare toolkit for online platform and start publishing specific sections.
  • Weeks 7 – 9: Start uploading the toolkit to the online platform, carry out user testing and consultation, make changes as appropriate.
  • Weeks 9 – 12: Final checks, edits and amendments.  Launch and publicise the toolkit.
  • Weeks 12 – 13: After launch, plan and advertise at least one webinar for other Councils, respond to questions and requests for information.
N.B. Weeks 9 – 12 are fixed as the launch date but the other dates and activities are indicative and subject to change.  For example, depending on progress we may carry out user testing and consultation at an earlier stage or we may need longer to decide on the most appropriate online platform for publication.  Activities started in weeks 12 – 13 will continue beyond the life of this project as part of the ongoing role of 100% Digital Leeds as an innovative and nationally respected digital inclusion programme.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success would be a combination of Outputs and Outcomes.

Outputs:  At the most basic level we would have data from the online platform to tell us how many times the toolkit had been accessed, specific sections downloaded, links followed etc.  Depending on MHCLG’s desired Outputs we could make it a requirement to register with email to access some or all of the toolkit – this would give us data on which Councils had accessed the platform.  We would also record all subsequent contacts, questions, requests for more information and webinar attendance from specific Councils.

Outcomes:  The purpose of the toolkit is to enable/support more Councils to introduce, scale-up or adapt digital inclusion programmes in their localities.  We will continue to monitor and report these Outcomes beyond the life of this project.  We will also use these ongoing contacts to learn from other places and continue to reflect and improve on the 100% Digital Leeds programme.  The ultimate Outcome will be an increase in people and communities across the country becoming more digitally included.  Measuring this Outcome is outside of the scope of this project.
column13:
The toolkit will be published using an online platform to ensure maximum reach and reusability.  After publication we will continue to respond to requests for more information from individual Councils.  We will also deliver at least one webinar inviting Councils to discuss the toolkit in more detail, ask questions and share ideas for implementing aspects of the 100% Digital Leeds approach in their locality.  We will encourage ongoing conversations between Councils on the best ways to increase digital inclusion in their area.

Leeds has spoken to colleagues in Croydon and Greenwich about submitting a joint bid as they are working on similar ideas.  Deadlines meant such a bid could not be pulled together in time.  We would all be open to a discussion brokered by MHCLG on a more formal relationship and funding arrangement if a joint project was considered to be more cost effective and would have more impact.
column14: The audience for the toolkit is staff in other Councils.  Therefore, although the toolkit will be published online, we would expect that digital exclusion will not be a barrier for the target audience.  We will ensure that the online platform and the toolkit itself complies with web accessibility standards.
column16: None required to develop and write the toolkit.  Once the toolkit has been published, support to publicise it to other Councils and encourage use of the toolkit to develop digital inclusion strategies and activities around the country.

column2: Digital Resilience for Town Centres
column4:
Research shows that 32% of high street businesses in the District have a social media presence and only 29% are active on a daily basis. 

Covid 19 has resulted in a 50% drop in their social media output. Footfall and spend are down significantly. With the local retail and tourist economy striving for recovery, this project will test new ways to drive hyper-local community engagement and spend.

This scalable, replicable, digital service project compliments Cotswold District Council’s approach to town centre resilience. It will provide the Council and local businesses with tools to encourage community engagement and boost consumer confidence.
column7: The Council has a working relationship with Maybe*, who are a consortium partner of the High Street Task Force, to assist with a digital support programme. The Maybe* platform provides stakeholders with a dashboard that consolidates all local digital business activity across the key social media platforms.  FYI see UK Government’s High Street Task Force and an example of Maybe* data in the PRDG group that shows the drop off in businesses use of social media activity. Using Google Maps, Facebook, Twitter, Instagram data the platform links all of the businesses within the authority area together so they can digitally collaborate and engage with localised conversations.  It reveals what is already working locally, who is active on social media and it enables the Council to showcase and promote each place through the content that the businesses are already producing. The package will significantly extend the scope of existing digital service programmes and increase their impact by providing additional tools that will transform community engagement and encourage local spend.  This project will enable local social media metrics to be measured and published on a daily basis and will link digital engagement activity to physical high street spend.
column8:
1. The delivery of the technology and deployment of this project will be resourced by Maybe*. The project will be managed via Slack, Trello and Google docs.  2. Task groups will be created in Cirencester, Chipping Campden and Tetbury.  3. The core data will be collected by Maybe*. All survey and spend data will be displayed within the Maybe* dashboard and is GDPR complaint.   4. This activity will provide the Council and local businesses with the tools to encourage hyper-local community engagement with businesses and consumers and it will facilitate agile place making, service review and policy formulation, including Covid 19 related changes in:
  • Consumer confidence on demand;
  • Local sentiment and opinion; and
  • The impact of local social media engagement on local physical spend.
The following tools will be deployed through three work packages: Deployment Work (Weeks 1 – 3) Deploy:
  • The Maybe* platform
  • Town wide surveys using facebook
  • A contactless loyalty programme. This will link facebook, credit and debit card spending in a physical location.
  • A plug in for the council website / others that serve the consumer desire to communicate with retailers prior to visiting a town or to book an appointment.
 Insights Work (Weeks 1- 8)
  • Report on survey insights;
  • Measure uptake through local engagement, spend and booking data;
  • Define the most effective and efficient decision making process and drive local place shaping based on new local data;
  • Primary KPI’s to measure number/amount of:
    • local organisations with a digital presence;
    • daily businesses active on social media;
    • consumers engaged in this project;
    • local spend tracked;
    • appointments booked;
Training Work (Weeks 1- 8)
  • Using strength-based approach, work with businesses’ champions to create demonstrators of the technology;
  • Enable the digitally-able to inspire others to adapt faster; 
  • Training will be recorded in line with the requirement to work in the open.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The success of the project will be measured by its ability to scale to other authorities. Key success criteria are:
  1. The successful deployment of scalable tools that improve localised placemaking and decision making through greater local insights;
  2. The insights that the tools reveal during the project; 
  3. The number of businesses that these tools assist;
  4. The transferable learnings from the project;
  5. Local Business and Community engagement.
The Impact of the project will be measured through these metrics:
  • The number of Consumers engaged;
  • The number of Businesses engaged;
  • What the insights reveal; 
  • Local sentiment;
  • Insight and trends into local consumer spend.
The number of businesses that are active on social media has been measured in participating towns to provide a baseline. This project will establish a framework for measurement and reporting that will assist place shaping and agile decision making through access to real time local data.  This project will turn readily available social media channels to accelerate local community engagement in decision making. In a socially distanced world being able to listen to what is being said locally provides the Council with a much better ability to understand, react and plan.
column13: The platform that has been selected is scalable to any other UK council. It's already gathering the insights from Google Maps, Facebook, Twitter, Instagram across 1.5m UK high street business across 3,300 locations. This data is already being used by the High Streets Task Force to drive their dashboard and can be deployed anywhere at scale. There is an existing synergy between Cotswold District Council and Maybe*. Various local stakeholders across Cotswold towns are familiar with the Maybe* team through digital training projects that have already been undertaken. This is seen as vitally important as this familiarity will help to ensure the project can ‘hit the ground running’ and deliver results within the 8 week programme.
column14: Since lockdown businesses’ use of social media dropped by 50% in the Cotswolds.   Business activity has not recovered despite increased use of social media by consumers. The Cotswolds relies heavily on the visitor economy so has been hit hard by lockdown.  Our older demographic amongst residents and traders makes it even more important to support bridging the digital divide.  The output is twofold: 1)    encourage local businesses to leverage opportunities to connect with their customers through the channels that they are already in. 2) accelerate and increase businesses’ use of digital channels by increasing digital skills. There is a risk that local businesses become a digitally excluded segment, putting them at risk of not reaching the population that represents the new opportunity of “shop local.”

  • Local Chambers of Commerce – network of local businesses and promote project visibility;
  • Town and Parish Councils – to facilitate connections, promote project visibility and aid deployment.

column2: Sheltered Housing Smart Living Solution
column4: The Covid19 lockdown had an enormous impact on our sheltered housing tenants.  The usual visits by Sheltered Housing Officers were replaced with phone calls, and resident events within the schemes ended with residents largely confined to their flats.  We realised that better technology could have allowed us to adapt.  Face-to-face support could have continued, and community events become virtual.  The Apello Smart Living System will enable us to video link with our tenants and improve our overall communication.  We can provide vital day-to-day support and, perhaps more importantly, move the coffee mornings from the shared lounge to the digital realm.
column7:
We have identified two Sheltered Housing Schemes (one in Mid Suffolk District and one in Babergh District) that we feel are suitable as starting points for what we hope will be a roll-out of this technology. The enthusiasm and open-mindedness of staff and residents will, we hope, enable us to fully embrace this new technology and use it to develop and improve our services for older people.

We have engaged with Apello, providers of the Smart Living Solutions, and discussed with them at length the opportunities that their product could present. They have carried out some initial site assessments and provided approximate costings, and if we are successful with our bid, they are committed to delivering within the timescales required by the Local Digital Challenge.

We have brought together a project team ready and able to deliver this piece of work, as well as evaluate the outcomes and plan future roll-outs. We have gained the support of senior management and the political leadership at Babergh and Mid Suffolk Councils.

Neighbouring Councils within Suffolk are exploring alternative products that provide similar services. It is intended that we will review each project to compare and constraints and share learning.
column8:
Project commences when funding agreed and order provided, Appello has issued quotes in anticipation of instruction and will deliver the projects using specialist installation teams (successfully delivered c210 SLS installations to date). The works will be managed by: Andy Ramsey – contract manager, Dee Willis – Site supervisor, Mei Parsons H&S adviser. Mid Suffolk have responsibility to order broadband lines, and advise residents of works/access for works. Milestones indicated below:
  • Prestart meeting to confirm program of activity 3rd August
  • Resident demonstrations 7th August
  • Commence install of new system 10th August
  • Remove legacy control equipment 11th August
  • 1st fix wiring & containment 28th August
  • Removal of legacy & installation of new apartment and communal alarm equipment, video door entry etc. 9th September
  • Programming all devices 19th September
  • Commissioning & snagging 22nd September
  • Training 23rd September
  • Apello floor walking and Go Live 24th September

By further defining and measuring a number of projected benefits:
  • Improved Monitoring to Support Needs of a Vulnerable Client Group - a do or don't need support call
  • Increased Safety & Security of Tenants - Increased security to the Building e.g. video door entry system, improved  fire safety
  • Increased productivity and staff efficiency - higher staff morale, reduced unnecessary visits with target resources leading to reduced CO2 emissions and savings in fuel costs and contributing to our Climate Action Plan
  • Tenants feeling safer, less isolated and generally more satisfied with their home and the quality of their life
  • Tenant Satisfaction and Staff satisfaction
column13: We would look to extend the product to more residents within both Mid Suffolk and Babergh Council and would proactively share our knowledge with our neighbouring Councils e.g. West Suffolk, East Suffolk, Ipswich Borough Council and Suffolk County Council. As well as rolling Smart Living Solutions out across more of our sheltered schemes we would look to trial this by way of innovation in a sample general needs stock. In this way we can utilise this technology to support other residents and gain further staff efficiencies. We are also keen to explore the opportunities the technology might present for residents to engage with other vital services such as health or social care. This would require joined-up working across key public sector bodies, particularly Suffolk County Council.
column14: This project aims to increase digital accessibility to our Sheltered Schemes where currently there is little digital access. Throughout the lockdown, our tenants have been left feeling isolated and without a sense of community. This solution enables access to platforms such as Skype and MS Teams and will enable community engagement events to take place once again e.g. bingo and virtual coffee mornings. It will also enable residents to talk to other residents via an in-scheme video connection. It will enable early identification of support needs with the functionality to raise an alarm in an emergency and the response times by digital are significantly faster than the current analogue technology. Tenants will have easy access to our customer service centre or their Sheltered Housing Officer in non-emergency situations. The product can be linked to the Council’s digital tenant portal ‘My Home’ which will allow access to additional services e.g. viewing rent accounts.

column2: Delivering Improved Services through Enhanced Systems, Data & Insight
column4:
NCC are seeking funding to accelerate the configuration of a shared system in line with partners’ and the wider sector’s individual organisational requirements. 

The immediate impact of this project will be the increased access to and usage of comprehensive data and analytics providing sector-wide, consistent, operational and strategic vulnerability data, enabling a faster integrated response and informing the re-opening, design and delivery of universal and targeted services that drive systemic change - minimizing the effects of the pandemic and leading to improved outcomes for vulnerable people, many of whom will not have previously needed support or been known to local councils.
column7: The Norfolk Vulnerability Hub was designed, configured, built & deployed across 8 Local Authorities plus Voluntary Organisations to coordinate the support for vulnerable residents, primarily those on the Government’s Shielding programme. This initial phase was built, tested & rolled-out to multiple organisations – in just a few weeks, illustrating the cross-sector commitment & agile approach taken. The system is currently used sector-wide to manage local authority and voluntary sector services including food & medicine deliveries, befriending services, domestic support and other non-clinical service-provision and has already produced improvements in cross-organisational communication, understanding and service-coordination for around 50,000 vulnerable residents. Due to the speed with which the system had to be delivered, it does not currently reflect differences in individual organisations’ operating models,  resident requirements etc. As such, under the current configuration, not all details relating to vulnerable individuals are held within the system, compromising the information available to front-line workers to inform their response to individual requests for support and aggregated data that informs service-planning. The focus of this project is to accelerate the system development to fully meet the needs of all partners, increasing & improving the quality & quantity of available data that will inform the design, scale and scope of the community response to the pandemic and associated societal issues.
column8:
1. Agreeing configuration of system for each individual organisation w/c 03/08/2020
Strategic & Operational working groups already established during the initial Covid19 response will be reconvened to agree partners’ system-requirements. Local data leads and operational managers are included to ensure systems and processes are designed in a way that ensures critical data is available at the required granularity. Members include representatives of all Local councils, VCSE organisations, Health colleagues and software suppliers.
  1. Agreeing data & reporting requirements for partners by end of w/c 10/08/2020
This ensures partners can access the data they need for day-to-day operations and reporting within their own organisations, and that the system can reliably inform partnership-board performance reporting and intelligence. Requires input from all partners as listed above, alongside Public Health colleagues, to ensure that test & trace requirements are captured.
  1. System Development in line with 1 by end of w/c 24/08/2020:
Product supplier to complete phase 1 technical development – estimated total build time of 12 working days to complete required changes with an additional 2 working days for UAT & feedback
  1. Developing an Enhanced Common Data Model – end of w/c 07/09/2020
This allows users without reporting experience to create dashboards & reports from within the system with no need for bespoke reporting solutions. To be completed by the supplier’s technical team & reviewed by analytical colleagues ensuring the model is fit-for-purpose. Anticipated this will require 8 days’ technical development with an additional 2 days’ UAT
  1. Pre-migration cleansing of partner data – end of w/c 14/09/2020
To ensure the data held within the system is as comprehensive as possible, council data relating to vulnerable residents will need to be included. As councils currently record in a variety of different systems, some cleansing will be required to ensure this data can be migrated successfully. Upgraded system live – w/c 28/09/2020
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success will be demonstrated by increases in organisations’ use of the system creating more consistent, high quality and wide-ranging data leading to better understanding of both the individual and the place resulting in increased equality of access to services.

Comprehensive client-level information available at-a-glance to front-line workers enables better-informed decisions to be made more quickly, improving both the efficiency & efficacy of service-provision and, more importantly, improving the quality-of-service for and, outcomes of, Norfolk residents.

Preventative services manage-down demand allowing redeployed staff to be returned to substantive roles, increasing the speed and capacity at which a broader range of services can reopen.

Improved customer experience as organisations access up-to-date information, ensuring service-offers compliment those already being provided by partners.

Partners report increased user-satisfaction with the system ,  measured through tracking the number of issues raised with system support and the number of systems escalations to the supplier.

Service-Users report increased customer-satisfaction. Speed of response, alongside existing customer satisfaction KPIs will allow us to monitor our impact through a “before & after” benchmarking exercise.

Progress will be recorded on the supplier’s wiki pages, highlighting progress made and achievements to-date, as well as on a partnership blog, which will be created in the project set-up.
column14: As a large and largely-rural county, with isolated areas with slow broadband speeds and an ageing population, some of whom may be less-willing to use digital means to contact us, we are keenly-aware of the need to ensure services are available to all who need our support, however they choose to engage with us.  A key component of the upgraded system will be to highlight & identify digitally-excluded residents and enable services to target communications via the most-appropriate means, using data collated via market-segmentation analysis, to ensure equality of access to vital services.  The system will enable those who are able to access public services remotely or digitally to do so, creating fewer choke-points within the system and creating faster response times for those who wish to access services via more traditional means.
column16:
We would welcome sharing data & information relating to vulnerability that may be available nationally or regionally that can be factored into the design & build of the technical solution & reporting to help us enhance our understanding of the increased vulnerabilities people are facing and act as early as possible to mitigate the detrimental effects.

We would also welcome detailed advice or guidance on current data-sharing guidelines and would especially welcome any approved data-sharing templates that may be in use by MHCLG.

column2: Homeworking Broadband Tracker
column4:
Covid 19 has meant that all office based staff are now home working for the foreseeable future. The success of long-term home working relies on the reliability of an individual’s personal broadband connection. Because broadband infrastructure is external to IT, understanding the causes of disconnections is frustrating for both staff and the IT engineer.   

With hundreds if not thousands connecting through different exchanges, using different internet service providers, we want to build a dynamically updating regional map showing the status of everyone connected. In an instant everyone can see whether the problem is confined to a local area or a specific ISP. If not then the problem is likely to be somewhere within the household itself.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
• Investigation into several methods of obtaining live user IP connection data has been largely completed.
  • Initial prototyping of how we anonymously map this user data to the location and ISP has been completed.
  • We are in the process of setting up a pilot to plot status markers on both the Google maps and iShare platforms.
  • Coding algorithms to spin out new checking processes when failed connections are detected is underway and will soon be tested.
  • Work on anonymizing data sets for security purposes is almost complete.
  • We are working on how to maximize the benefits of using a crowd-sourcing approach. The wider aim is to encourage other local organisations to participate.  Including connection data from multiple organisations in real-time will improve the value and accuracy of the regional picture for participating organisations.
  • We are still discussing how best to present mapping data to mobile device users so they can see the extent of the regional failure over their 3/4G connections.
  • Developing ideas for capturing a history of the quality of participants connections over time.
column8:
The output of this initiative will not be a turnkey application. This solution combines an organization’s existing systems and resources with freely available public domain mapping software to help solve a common problem.

Most of the development work can be completed by internal development teams. There will be the need to involve Google & Astun, for them to supply us with a Google Maps solutions Architect. The objective can be achieved in eight weeks and is broadly divided into the following key milestones.

1. Capture live connection status of all participating users.

2. Perform intelligent algorithmic update of connection status field when a persistent change of connection is detected.

3. Update a central mapping database and where appropriate programmatically merge any Google KML files provided by participating organisations.

4. Enable timer-based map updates

5. Produce broad documentation/guidelines to help other organisations develop their own solutions. The development of these guides will be undertaken by an outsourced professional and the working project group.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
This solution will help participating organisations and their officers dynamically identify the causes of internet outages while working from home during the Covid-19 pandemic. This data can also be used to identify patterns of failure within the region and fed back to ISPs and local telecoms providers.

Success will also be measured by less calls to the IT service desk when staff experience outages, because they will be able to self-diagnose using the new software.
column13:
Wherever possible we deploy methods and systems freely available in the public domain e.g. DNS and Google Maps. We will offer written guidance around the concepts and stages and provide examples of how we achieved it.

Whilst it is possible, even likely that some organisations may struggle to capture the dynamic status of their connected clients it should in most cases be possible to resolve because most VPN and RAS services will offer this capability.

All councils are subscribers to the Public Sector Geospatial Agreement (PSGA) which provides access to various data sets from Ordnance Survey.
column14: Not relevant
column16: Nothing other than item 7.4 (below)
column17: The benefits are amplified by increasing the number of participating organisations within the region. Help in promoting the service to others would be welcomed.

column2: Impact Based Decisions
column4:
COVID-19 has exposed some of the stark inequalities in Lambeth, both between and within our neighbourhoods. As we move into thinking about renewal and recovery, these inequalities need to be better understood so we can target our resource where it is most needed and ensure our decisions lead to positive local impacts.

We are developing a Neighbourhood Opportunity Profile that will give us a deep understanding of need in our local neighbourhoods alongside an Impact Based Decisions Tool that will enable decisions about investment to be made based on impact – positive and negative – within these neighbourhoods. 

Whilst we have seen examples of these individual products that we are developing in other local areas, we believe it is the combination of these elements that is truly innovative.
column7:
This project was launched in February 2020 with an ambition to move into a testing phase from May 2020. Despite the intended engagement activities being cancelled due to the pandemic, we have continued to make progress on this project. The discovery phase involved:
  • Desk review: gathering wider tools and approaches for impact measurement including from other governments (Oldham, Scotland, New Zealand, Santa Monica), influential thinkers and institutes (Happy City, Doughnut Economics, UN Sustainable Development Goals), alongside Lambeth’s Borough Plan and impact frameworks used within some of our most established programmes
  • 15 expert interviews: understanding impact measurement methodologies including social value, social network analysis, social return on investment, complexity theory, cumulative impact analysis, theory of change
  • 5 staff workshops: input from majority resident staff across service areas and roles to develop the impact framework
The output of this phase of work is the first iteration of a Societal Impact Framework that describes a good life for individuals and a thriving neighbourhood in Lambeth. This will be tested and refined through future phases of work including community engagement. As we move into the prototyping phase, we will be using the societal impact framework as the basis for two products:
  • Neighbourhood Opportunity Profile
  • Impact Based Decisions Tool
column8:
This project is currently in a prototyping phase and funding and support from MHCLG would:
  • accelerate progress by allowing prototypes in multiple use cases and we would be able to complete four rather than one in the time period
  • enable us to appoint external partners to validate assumptions alongside communities so the products can be scaled to other local authority areas
  • provide additional expertise and capacity to maximise the effectiveness from private sector partners including Methods Analytics
This will allow us to advance project timelines to meet the needs emerging from COVID-19, especially given projections of an Autumn resurgence of the virus. Stakeholders and users: Our key stakeholders are the people who live, work or run a business in the borough. This means the prototyping phase will involve local engagement work so we can ensure the data collected provides an accurate reflection of lived experience The products will have a range of users:
  • Decision makers including the Management Team and Political Cabinet will use them to have a view of impact across major decision and spend areas
  • Commissioners and service delivery leads will use them to develop new services based on local need and impact, and to evaluate these services
  • Residents, voluntary sector organisations and businesses will be able to have a shared view of challenges in their local area through the website, and develop local solutions to tackle these
Project plan
Timeframe Activity Deliverables
January Project inception Project endorsed by Informal Cabinet
February – March Discovery (see question 5.2) Societal Impact Framework
April – May Pause due to COVID-19 response effort NA
June – July Agree 4 prototype use cases with a cross section of service areas and scope data requirements 4+ prototypes agreed and scoped
August – September (funding period) Engagement session with local government partners to share and gather feedback on the approach. Run 4+ prototypes using the societal impact framework, measures and tool. These need to be confirmed but are likely to include: -       Neighborhood Opportunity Profile in one ward and then across the borough -       Health and Wellbeing hubs benefits case that quantifies indirect benefits within the model -       Developing options for a community safety approach -       Evaluation of business grants given through the crisis 4+ prototypes delivered   Development of tools and guidance          
October onwards Launch of tools and approach internally and externally   Continuous development of new applications for the tools and embedding Societal Impact Framework within day to day running of organisation Launch webinar/ round table with other local authorities   Internal workshops explaining tool and first applications
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Over the project period we will create and start to embed the following products within the organisation:  
  1. Societal impact framework – a codesigned framework that presents the ingredients for a good life and thriving neighbourhood (now in draft)
  2. Neighbourhood opportunity profile - real time picture of challenges and strengths in different neighbourhoods based the societal impact framework
  3. Impact based decisions tool – a tool that enables decisions about investment to be made based on a holistic understanding impact – both positive and negative – within local neighbourhoods
These products will be immediately useful to services and will feed into renewal and recovery planning for COVID-19. At the end of the funded period we will be able to demonstrate where the products have been used as part of decision-making processes and, therefore, where the Council has been able to take a data and impact driven approach. We may be able to start to identify the impact of some of these decisions and gather feedback on the use of the tool. It is anticipated that these products will find new applications in the Council as they become more embedded in the way in which officers think about impact for individuals and within neighbourhoods.
column13:
We have the ambition to share our progress and learning with other local areas and have already started to build these relationships nationally and internationally through the discovery phase of work. Both our process and our products will be open source, transparent and shareable. This will enable scalability of the approach and support us to build a strong network of likeminded collaborators for ongoing support and feedback. In order to share the outcomes and outputs of this project we will:
  • Host a public launch event in October on the theme of Impact Based Decisions where we will share our progress and invite others to share work in this area
  • Publish blogs about our experience of developing the products, for example, in relation to how we work with residents to bring qualitative and quantitative insights together
  • Publish guidance for our development process
  • Publish code and data via Lambeth’s Azure DevOps portal
column14:
There are several ways we will ensure our project takes into account accessibility, including for those who are digitally excluded:
  • Inequalities including digital inclusion and accessibility are embedded within our societal impact framework, this means they are issues we will track within neighbourhoods and will be assessed in future decision making.
  • For the purpose of the product development, community engagement will play a big role. This means there will be an active effort to engage people in qualitative research, where possible face to face, but where not we will use other methods such as online and telephone interviews. We will work with partners such as Age UK Lambeth to identify residents who may not usually be able to reach.
  • We will conduct an equalities impact analysis for the project and engagement process to ensure a wide range of voices are represented.
column16: In addition to the funding we would value input and feedback on the project from experts within MHCLG.
column17: We will be continuing to work with a range of partners for this work in both a formal and informal capacity. Building on the early engagement through the discovery phase we recognise the value that working with private sector partners, other local authorities, academic partners or think tanks and local initiatives, can bring to this multi-dimensional work. Some of our existing partners have been listed in 4.2.

column2: Future Work Design - Using staff experiences of newly adopted working practices to inform future work design and supportive digital services that maximise productivity, efficiency and wellbeing
column4: Covid-19 created an unprecedented situation, and Local Authorities (LAs) were forced to rapidly develop emergency working practices to maintain service delivery. Recovery task-groups now recognise the need to understand the experiences of staff in adapting to these new ways of working so future work can be properly ‘designed’. This should consider productivity, motivation, and wellbeing, incorporating staff perspectives and innovations so that digital services can sustain good future working practices. This proposal is a collaboration across four regional LAs and a team of psychologists and researchers from the University of Hull (UoH).
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7:
The four participating LAs have implemented technical systems to enable thousands of staff to work remotely. Across the four LAs, the following digital services have been implemented:
  • Enabled home working on 20,000+ council laptops and desktops
  • Doubled remote access infrastructure and outbound phone call capabilities
  • Deployed call centre solutions
  • Provided: soft phone capability on laptops, 4000 headsets and several miles of network cables
  • Rolled out Microsoft Teams to 20,000+ staff
Uptake figures for these provisions demonstrate the efforts of staff to embrace these new ways of working: Data suggests that on the average working day, circa 10,000 Users are logged onto the network from home, 200+ conference calls are made using loop-up and there are 10,000+ active Microsoft Teams users, 4000+ Teams calls and 100,000+ chats. The LAs are now keen to collaboratively reflect and understand the impact on staff, particularly in relation to health and wellbeing. The team at UoH has a strong track record in applied psychological research exploring staff perceptions to inform future working in public sector organisations. They recently undertook a study of experiences of employees working at home due to COVID-19, the findings of which have informed this project design.
column8:
The aim of this project is to provide the recovery task-groups across the four LAs with timely information for proactive interventions. This will inform refinement of strategies and actions/recommendations implemented immediately that enable positive and constructive working practices. This will be achieved by a) Undertaking virtual focus groups and interviews with LA representative staff groups about what works well and what does not; b) Developing a series of ‘Working Practice Profiles’(WPP)* for staff groups and an explanatory White Paper; c) Delivery of consultation sessions with the wider project team d) The creation of an evidence-based questionnaire that enables collection of quantitative data relating to the WPPs immediately after the project.  *WPPs will be a 2-page proforma completed for each staff group summarising the consensus about those working practices they want to sustain and protect. This requires analysis of what has and has not worked well and how a balance of choice can be achieved.
Key Week Activities Milestones
W0: Project set up within LA and UoH settings M0: UoH Ethical process completed (1 week)
W1: Preparation for data collection – arranging focus groups & interviews M1: Interview & focus group framework agreed; Participants contacted.
W2 – 6 Qualitative data collection & analysis: c32 digital focus groups & interviews conducted with representative staff groups across 4 LAs (8 each). M2: Data collection and analysis complete; WPPs generated.
W7:  Consultation with collaborative LA project team to discuss findings in relation to digital service delivery, and refinement of WPPs. M3: Finalisation of informative WPPs.
W8: Design of a survey to test the generalisability of the WPPs. M4: Delivery of: a) A pack of WPPs, accompanied by White Paper for national LAs b) Finished survey tool ready for dissemination
Post-project dissemination activities Webinar to discuss findings Local Authority conference involvement Further collaboration to extend impact Academic paper
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The speed with which new working practices had to be deployed meant there was limited opportunity to mitigate against potential risks to staff. In the wake of COVID-19, issues such as recruitment, retention, staff morale and mental health are emerging as concerns related to these new ways of working.

The UoH team draw on past experience of delivering similarly time-bound projects relating to work design and wellbeing. They will facilitate the collaborative process through which the four local authorities can compare and contrast challenges, explore lessons learned, understand required improvements, and share best practice. Through having this information, the LAs can implement immediate interventions that mitigate risks and realise benefits swiftly, also reducing further risks posed by a potential 2<sup>nd</sup> wave of COVID-19.

Based on the findings of this work, success will be demonstrated through thoughtful design of working practices and support arrangements that respond to staff views and needs, increase staff morale and welfare, enable choice around remote working, and facilitate positive work/life balance. This work will inform decision-making impacts, staff engagement, productivity, efficiency, recruitment, retention, and sickness absence, and will feed into positive culture change in health behaviours and sustainability for all four LAs and beyond.
column13:
The three main outputs are:

1) WPPs,

2) survey tool, and

3) White Paper.

These will be developed with organisational and individual confidentiality in mind, for dissemination widely across LAs thus sharing learning and best practice. The dissemination and analysis of the local and national survey is beyond the scope of this eight-week project, but the survey tool can form the basis of future data collection across national LAs. After the project, a webinar will be designed and broadcast by the project team collaboratively to enable maximum dissemination across LAs. As such, it is hoped this work will support and enable good work design for LA employees at a national level. The project team will seek opportunities for future collaboration across the four LAs, with possible opportunities to involve other LAs.
column14: The survey tool will be designed for use in a range of different formats such as electronic, hard copy and usable on different devices to ensure the widest reach possible across the country for Local Authorities, Partners and all interested parties; ensuring adherence to the accessibility legislation.
column16: No further support is required from MHCLG. The project will be delivered solely by the 4 collaborating LAs and the UoH research team.
column17: The Local Government Association (LGA) will be a very effective organisation to communicate and distribute the study/tool through. As well as sharing it with Health partners through Clinical Commissioning Groups, Regional Sustainability & Transformation Partnerships and Local Digital Roadmap Boards to facilitate the effective communication of the project and although not specific to health, their office workers are likely to experience the same challenges.

column2: Rochford Town Footfall Measurement Pilot
column4:
We are working closely with stakeholders in our community to not only reverse the negative consequences Covid-19 had on our already declining High Streets but to use this disruption to drive positive change and make our High Streets vibrant and attractive places of the future.

This Pilot will fully test the viability of measuring and analyzing footfall data.  It will allow us to make fact-based decisions regarding wider scale implementation with the aim of understanding both footfall trends and the effect of explicit improvement actions.

Pilot results will inform and guide our subsequent actions and also be publicly available.
column7: Link to background paper: https://drive.google.com/file/d/16w-Sw_yMbi6Ag8_NnfpWrPmTG5xRRX8K/view?usp=sharing   To date: Vendors have been consulted A Pilot Proposal has been documented (please see attached)   Involvement of private sector organisations: Taylormade Business Consultants – documentation of pilot proposal Several vendors have been consulted, the two with whom we continue to explore options are Springboard and Retail Sensing (R-Com Consulting)
column8:
The high-level schedule is as follows, this is detailed in the Pilot Proposal (attached) Proposal Phase: 18-Jun to 15-Jul Milestone: Grant application submission: 15-Jul Prepare Phase: 15-Jul to 3-Aug Milestone: Go/No Go based on success of grant application – w/c 3-Aug Plan & Place Phase (if grant application is successful):
  • Equipment order confirmed – w/c 3-Aug
  • Equipment delivered, installed and calibrated – 10-Aug to 14-Aug
  • Integration test – 14-Aug to 23-Aug
Milestone: Go/No Go to start pilot – 31-Aug Pilot Phase: Data collection and analysis – 31-Aug to 25-Sep   After the pilot: A presentation of the project will be prepared. We will quickly assess the pilot and roll-out options   Throughout the pilot key stakeholders will be involved, these include: Primary: Rochford Parish council, Rochford Chamber of Trade, Rochford Town Team, Rochford retail business owners. Other: Hockley Parish and Rayleigh Town Councils, Hockley and Rayleigh Chambers of Trade.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The purpose of the pilot is to demonstrate the ability to collect usable footfall data.  We will consider the pilot a successful if it meets the following criteria:
  • Counts are accurate to > 95%
  • Direction of travel can be determined with 95% accuracy
  • Operating costs are as expected
  • SLA for access to analytics is met
  • The process to analysis and publish the data is scalable and not resource intensive
  • The data provides a means to: determine daily and weekly patterns; obtain trends over time; distinguish between normal and special variation in order to isolate cause and effect.
If successful, the pilot will have demonstrated this specific technology.  This, in turn, will impact the ability to deploy fact-based management of initiatives to improve our High Streets across the area and also provide input to any national initiatives with similar goals.   Further information can be found in the attached Pilot Proposal document.
column13:
The project will provide a blueprint for other councils or BIDs to rapidly implement a “perimeter” model for footfall measurement. This model is ideally suited to small market towns.

Our aim is to feed data into any specified national data base, we need further information to validate and enable this. All data and analysis will be made publicly available, both from the pilot and from subsequent phases.

Lessons learned will be openly shared.
column14: This pilot is aligned to our wider programme of initiatives to transform our high streets. This broader programme takes the requirement of the digitally excluded segments of the population fully in to account.
column16:
Advice on aligning data and analysis to any national programme

Advice on grants which will help further deployment of the pilot
column17:
High Street Task Force by
  • as above, advice on aligning data and analysis to any national programme
  • providing any collated lessons learned from similar initiatives.

column2: Knowsley Better Together: Strengthening Community Volunteering
column4:
The Knowsley Volunteer Hub was established to support the borough’s vulnerable population throughout the COVID-19 crisis.  The Hub was set up at pace and responded well to all requirements set out by Government. 

This Project will develop a ‘best in class’ technology platform that integrates and evolves existing volunteer management platforms. 

The platform will support Knowsley’s ambition for volunteers to play a key role in COVID-19 Recovery, Knowsley’s 2030 strategic plan, and to enable a much more agile community response to any future crisis - aligning with Knowsley’s Better Together Principles.

Learnings will be shared across the Liverpool City Region.
column8:
Week 1-3 - Discovery and staging site set up. Initial discovery phase and system set-up is to establish the basic workflows, system settings and further development where needed to meet project requirements. Staging system developed. This will form the basis of the final system, Admin users to test different scenarios during the build. Admin users will customise elements of the site to ensure its compliance with the branding guidelines. Week 4-5 – Volunteer Staff – Operational Training
  • Training sessions for operational staff on how the system has been configured and the basic process that volunteers and staff will follow.
  • Developing Knowsley polices for safe and effective volunteering,
  • Admin users will develop reporting and insight options
  • Real-world scenarios tests.
  • Test scenarios using both web and mobile applications, Operational staff know how to assist any volunteers who are having difficulties on different devices, fringe case scenarios such as how to process volunteers who do not have access to the internet.
  • Post-training, TeamKinetic will continue to support Knowsley in the ongoing development of the site.
  Weeks 5-6 – Initial User testing
  • Initial test of opportunities with a trusted set of volunteers.
  • Follow the volunteers through life cycle of an opportunity, including logging hours and leaving feedback.
  • User testing in a supervised environment
  Week 6-8 – Populate the site with Opportunity Content
  • Add Providers and opportunities.
  • Email all existing volunteers asking them to register on your new website and start to publicise your new volunteer gateway.
  • Promote the new volunteer gateway to providers and volunteers.
  First year:
  • Launch of volunteer strategy and ambition.
  • Creation of a compelling 12 month campaign to recruit, train and deploy cohort of ‘super’ volunteers.
  • Recruit volunteers and providers.
  • Share practice across Liverpool City Region partners.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The Knowsley Volunteer Hub has supported the borough’s vulnerable population throughout the COVID-19 crisis. The Hub recruited 188 volunteers, supported 1,200 households with essential provisions, and issued 5,800 food parcels.   However, as result of the time it took to create and operationalise the Volunteer Hub, the peak in demand came before the Hub model was fully operational.   The development of a Council managed volunteer database has resulted in a bespoke volunteer management platform that is not linked to any existing networks / databases. One Knowsley (CVS) also has a volunteer database of 583 volunteers that pre-dates the COVID-19 pandemic.   Our June 2020 Lessons Learned review identified the need for a ‘best in class’ technology solution to support Knowsley’s ambition for volunteers to play a key role in COVID-19 recovery, Knowsley’s 2030 strategic plan, and to enable a much more agile community response to any future crisis.   We would demonstrate success by these measures (across first 8-12 weeks):
  1. Creation of single ‘best in class’ volunteer strategy and platform to support the borough’s volunteer strategy.
  2. Increase in volunteer recruitment and deployment. Target 350-500 volunteers.
  3. Transfer of volunteers from existing systems. Target 60% activate new accounts.
  4. Increase in volunteer opportunities (from Council and non-profit partners across the borough). Target 30 new providers.
  5. Model to be shared across the Liverpool City Region.
column16: The Council would welcome further support from MHCLG to learn more about reusing existing user research on engaging and encouraging local communities to participate through digital platforms. The Council would be specifically interested in understanding innovative ways in which digital innovations for public meetings that have already been tested during the COVID-19 crisis can offer lessons for how digital technology can be used to take participative democracy one step further, from involvement in public meeting, to true participation in designing services around the needs of the community.

column2: Lancashire Covid 19 Business Support Collaboration
column4:
This project is to establish a single, Lancashire wide database of companies assisted through funded Covid 19 support to enable support to be coordinated and companies to be directed to the best sources of support.

Over recent years considerable progress has been made in enabling local authorities in Lancashire to share business data via a common database and Data Sharing Agreement but some authorities have not had the resources to be able share information with the wider group.
column7:
We are currently at inception stage (pre-project) however we already have the common CRM system and Data Sharing Agreement in place amongst our local authority partners.

The economic development database system is established across Lancashire and Lancashire County Council are the database administrators. We support district councils with their use of the system, offer helpdesk assistance and hold regular user group meetings.

At present, some Covid 19 enquiries have been logged in to the system (currently 3,535 10th July) although data from some local authorities is incomplete. We created a specific Covid 19 enquiry form for the district councils to use which they could add to their website or use in-house to log enquiries. Although a number of districts have utilised the form, many Economic Development departments either haven't had the resources to participate or have had the funding administered in another department away from their processes.

Head of Service Business Growth Andy Walker has informed the local authority's Heads of Service of this project and a meeting has been organised with the Economic Development Officers on 20th July.

Feedback so far has been very positive as Economic Development sections would welcome the support this project would offer in informing them not only of their own client interactions but have the ability to compare and contrast responses with their neighbouring authorities.
column8:
2nd-15th July
Pre-Project - At this pre-project stage, Lancashire County Council (LCC) have been working with the Lancashire local authority partners to gauge support for the Local Digital Covid 19 Challenge. LCC Head of Service Business Growth, Andy Walker, has informed the heads of service within the local authorities about the fund and encouraged their participation. We have spoken with our Data Protection Officer to confirm the current Data Sharing Agreement is sufficient for this exercise.

16th July – 2nd August
Feasibility – The feasibility stage is around gathering knowledge on the data format held in each local authority and planning for the project start. We will work with our software developers to communicate preferred data formats and discuss what data the group wish to appear in the design stage of the interactive charts and forms. The final designs will be agreed upon prior to project start.

3rd – 30th August
Engineering – The project will officially start on 3rd August with the designs for the form and interactive charts being finalised through the Feasibility process. The software developers will then have 4 weeks in which to design the form and charts and begin to collate and sort the data from the various organisations ready for the Deployment stage.

31st August – 20th September
Deployment – Once designs have been completed and signed off by the lead council in collaboration with partners, the software developers will have 3 weeks to deploy the designs and data into the database. Data would be deployed into a test environment before going live to check for any issues.

21st – 28th September
