Post project - Once all data is imported and the interactive charts are live, we will have a training and follow up session with all parties to gain their feedback on the process and the outcomes gained.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
This project would deliver a number of successful outcomes;
  • The ability to see the all relevant information on businesses and previous interventions whilst handling live enquiries
  • Inform decision making for officers around future business support
  • Allow cross border information sharing so officers in separate councils can view data on clients contacting them for business support
  • Show a true Lancashire wide picture of support at local level
  • Ability to analyse and disseminate the information by various themes, such as sector, age of business and employee sizes
  • Inform higher level decision making on the Lancashire economy based on the business intelligence gathered
  • Improved client experience due to greater awareness
column13:
This is a collaborative project amongst the 13 district councils and the County Council of Lancashire. At deployment stage, the data will be accessible across the County to each of the local authority database users together with access to interactive charts. The charts will also be made available to local authority officers such as higher level managers, via an online link.

Individual business support officers will be able to look up the enquiry history of any companies on their patch and to see which organisations have previously provided support, and to add any interventions they make. They will also be able to use the data for analytical purposes and to inform local economic strategies.
column14: This is not particularly relevant as very few businesses or individuals seeking support would be regarded as digitally excluded, and those with less developed digital skills are already supported through the Boost project.
column16: This project is based around our local authority partners releasing the potential of this fund, assisting them to enhance their knowledge and support to their local business community.
column17: Support from the Lancashire Enterprise Partnership and/or emerging combined authority grouping may encourage all local authorities for fully participate.

column2: Assistive technology pilots to promote independence and reduce isolation during and after covid
column4:
The Covid period has severely impacted residents living in sheltered accommodation and isolated adults living alone. To protect staff, councils have had to reduce callouts to homes for all but severe need, leaving residents feeling more isolated and vulnerable. This has reduced councils’ ability to use their judgement to make health-based recommendations for residents and their families.

Our hypothesis is that assistive technologies can improve customers’ physical and mental health, promote independence, reduce anxiety and diminish the cost of care. We propose running a pilot with 30 older adults in Royal Greenwich, supported by London Office of Technology and Innovation.
column7:
We have engaged with suppliers and seen demonstrations of several relevant technologies available. From these demonstrations, we have identified technologies that we would like to pilot within Royal Greenwich. We intend to use Amazon Echo - a voice recognition device - and miiCare, which is a wearable and enables health monitoring, in addition to allowing the client to raise the alarm if they are in an emergency situation.   Additionally, we have engaged teams within the organisation to identify potential clients to be part of the pilot and we will be splitting this group of people into 3 cohorts:-
  1. Control group
  2. Group using voice recognition
  3. Group using wearable and health monitoring 
The project is able to build on LOTI’s methodology for designing and evaluating pilots of Assistive Technologies, This helps ensure that pilots of new assistive technologies focus on achieving clear outcomes for users, and are evaluated in a way that is comparable across local authorities. We have identified the internal staffing resources required and the need for an Assistive Technology Manager to support the project.
column8:
Timelines We will create three groups to test our hypothesis using a control group and two technology products: one a voice recognition tool with help-based AI (Amazon Echo), the other a service which includes a wearable element (miiCare). We will begin with a pre-mobilisation stage, to prepare for the 3rd August commencement. This will include:
  • define evaluation criteria
  • Identify resident participants
  • Arrange delivery of devices (we are already in touch with relevant suppliers)
Weeks 1+2: 
  • Recruit Assistive Technology Project Manager (bid will fund this person’s salary for 2 months. (We plan to use an agency posting to ensure rapid recruitment.)
  • Survey clients to get a baseline to measure outcomes against
  • Fit technology
Weekly
  • Review project, including check-ins with residents or family and monitoring KPIs
  • Weeknotes or blogpost to interested authorities
  End of month 1
  • Retro and pivot if needed (for example, swapping devices or adding additional software
End of month 2
  • Begin formal evaluation, including projecting potential savings
  • Recommend next steps (e.g. further pilots)
  • Write up and share findings
  • Write new service pattern in partnership with Health colleagues, including conclusions on evaluation outcomes
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Our target groups for demonstrable success are:
  • MHCLG: perception that pilot has delivered
  • Residents: did they feel less anxious/more resilient?
  • Operational staff: are these devices viable as part of a care package?
  • Finance staff: do these devices reduce the cost of care?
Our evaluation criteria will be based on our desired outcomes: 
  • Reduce customer anxiety
  • Promote independence
  • Reduce the cost of care
This will be evaluated by the following measurables:
  • Cohort survey before and after
  • Family survey before and after
  • Telecare and out of hours call out numbers
  • Telecare and out of hours reassurance-only calls
  • Projected savings figures from healthcare operational experts
  • Quality of data coming from devices
The design of the pilot will be 3 groups, each with 10 people in: Group 1 will be a control group, whose technology and support will not change. Group 2 will be provided with an Amazon voice recognition tool.  Group 3 will receive the voice recognition tool and also a wearable device. We will endeavour to make sure that groups 2 and 3 are divided between primarily physical and primarily mental health needs, so that we can test each device with two broad sets of needs.
column13:
All the outputs from this pilot will be published and actively shared. This will include:
  • The new service pattern
  • Testimonials
  • Potential savings analysis
  • Technical information 
  • Evaluation of the effectiveness of specific devices, software and intervention patterns
  • Recommendations and lessons learned
We would be happy to partner with other councils who are looking to implement similar technology.
column14:
This pilot is specifically targeting groups who are likely to be digitally excluded or underconfident: older adults. To compensate for this we will:
  • Give full guidance to the resident to make sure they are as confident as possible through the pilot
  • Train our staff to help them by phone/their usual communication method where they need technical help
  • Use on-site staff and family members for support
We will make sure participants meet a basic level of ability to operate devices. Both devices are designed to be highly usable by less confident technology users.
column16: None except to help share learnings afterwards
column17: LOTI are already helping us to design and evaluate this pilot. Amazon and MiiCare will be engaged throughout. We are exploring whether Behavioural Insights Team could help to evaluate the pilot, and we have put in prospective costs for this.

column2: Non contact framework for delivering council services using video consultations
column4:
The pandemic creates barriers to delivering or receiving information and/or services in person.  We can help control the virus if customers stay at home and we work from home to limit contact with other people.  
Customers need 'safe' access to the information and services provided by Councils  and the delivery of services that would benefit from being “face-to-face” safely is a concern for us.
Our ambition is to minimise the need for physical presence by staff or customers at council offices by providing scheduled video meetings with customers, enabling both parties to view and discuss documentation either by screen sharing or video camera.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Whilst many of our services are enabled for digital self service, some interactions are far better with a face-to-face conversation. Being able to sit side by side to look at documents greatly enhances service delivery, therefore we feel that enabling customers to have a video consultation with back-office staff would provide the equivalent service level to meeting in person.
To date we have performed assessments on what types of face-to-face contact we envisaged video meetings could be applicable to and have captured and categorised all visits to our receptions since opening up again in a limited capacity on 6th July. We are currently analysing this data to target the right teams and services for this intervention.
Currently we are testing a prototype of this process utilising Microsoft Bookings. This provides an interface for a customer to self schedule a Microsoft Teams meeting with back-office staff and utilises email notifications to inform the customer of meeting details. We are documenting this configuration process and any configuration choices or caveats that we come across in order to produce and share a best-practices guide with other authorities.
We are considering how we could provide access to this facility for customers who are digitally excluded by providing a space which has dedicated conferencing equipment, we are expecting to be able to do this using a Teams Rooms device.
We have started discussions with our Digital Platform provider to see whether they could build a solution that fulfils the desired features that are lacking from the existing prototype.
column8:
Our timeline for the project will be as follows.

Delivery Phase:
August 3rd 2020
- Refine and confirm system requirements (Internal Business Analyst and Customer Services Manager along with Partner Organisations). Estimated 1 week.

August 10th 2020
- Engage with external suppliers to procure for development based on system requirements (Internal project manager). Estimated 1 week.

August 17th 2020
- External supplier develops solution using an iterative agile approach. Delivering a software solution and relevant implementation documentation. To be project managed by our internal Project Manager to ensure that delivery is progressing according to schedule. Estimated 4 weeks

- Analyse and capture requirements for measuring success - specify and design staff and customer follow up surveys. Re-design current reception and telephone customer services data capture to capture redirection to video consultation. Business Analyst and Customer Service manager in consultation with Partner Organisations. Estimated 2 weeks.

- Plan signposting and advertising of new service to customers, both digital and physical. Customer Services Manager and Communications Manager to be involved with this. Estimated 2 weeks.

September 14th 2020
- Implementation and deployment with internal customer services departments, update signposting and advertising, both digital and physical. Customer Services Manager and Communications Manager to be involved with this. Start capturing data to measure success. At this stage we will be interacting with the public and capturing their feedback including successes and difficulties. Estimated 1 week.

September 21st 2020
– Analysis of feedback from internal Customer Services in order to report to MHCLG and wider Local Digital group. Estimated 1 week .

Post delivery phase – dissemination with Partner organisation:
September 28th 2020
– Implementation and deployment with Partner organisations. Project Manager and Project Coordinator to be involved with Lead at each Partner Organisation. Capture data to measure success and begin analysis. Ongoing.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Data analysis would be key to demonstrating the success and impact of this solution.
Historically we have captured the face-to-face demand levels in our reception areas and as it stands, we are capturing more detailed analysis of services required for face-to-face visits. We propose that we would extend the data capture to include redirection to the video consultation.
Additionally we propose to survey all participants in the proof of concept to capture their satisfaction and the effectiveness of the service. We would analyse this feedback and use it to steer the specification of the custom solution. The survey could be performed either with the customer at the tail end of the call, or as a follow-up via email.
We would also ensure that all staff provide their feedback on the video consultation after each session to ensure that we capture the value that is being provided and also identify any barriers or improvements that could be made.
column13:
We will package our guidance around using Microsoft Bookings for dissemination to any other organisation that could make use of this product to deliver video consultations.

If we are successful in our application for funding we will work with a supplier to deliver a reusable open source digital service. Our current line of thinking is that we will work with our Digital Platform supplier, Placecube, to deliver the product as a pre-configured Cube which can be consumed by any of their other customers at no additional cost. Given that their platform is built on Open Source software they would publish the source code and some associated guidance around how a non-customer organisation could consume and deliver this product.

Our intention is to design the integration architectures so that implementation details are abstracted via an API enabling other video conferencing or calendaring solutions to be plugged in to the product. As it stands we are intending to integrate with Office 365 for calendaring and MS Teams for conferencing which would meet the needs of many councils (and certainly all the organisations we have consulted with so far).
column14:
There is significant risk that this could be inaccessible for digitally excluded customers, but we are investigating whether we could utilise a dedicated video conferencing terminal within one or more of our offices to allow access for customers who do not have access to their own equipment. Specifically in our prototype we are looking to utilise a Microsoft Teams device, but would investigate alternative possibilities as part of the full solution build.

Whilst in the current climate many customers are familiar with and will no doubt already have used video calling equipment, ideally the solution could be delivered from the customer perspective without needing complex setup of video conferencing tools. We will investigate whether we could provide a solution that – from a customer perspective- doesn’t require the installation of a desktop or mobile application and could be delivered fully within the browser.
column16:
We feel that MHCLG could support us by guiding us towards best practices outcomes that they have seen during other funding applications. We suppose that we could be provided exemplar project documentation from previous funding rounds that we could use to develop a clear definition of project outcomes, and perhaps reporting templates etc.
We would have liked to have taken up some of the Local Digital Fund training opportunities from GDS but we were not LDD signatories at the time, and there seem to be no dates scheduled now.
Additionally, we would appreciate some support with publicising our project and keeping other councils and organisations updated with our progress.
column17:
We have 6 other local authorities that are interested in our project proposal. We feel that their input into requirements will be crucial and that having other organisations implement the project will give us faster feedback for improvements and or success.
In addition, Rugby Borough Council are active in the Microsoft Local and Regional Government Champions group so would intend to advertise and share our guidance around Microsoft Bookings there.
We also are members of a Warwickshire regional IT technical managers forum comprising of 5 Districts and 1 County authority. We have previously collaborated on digital projects together and would look to do the same here.

column2: Shaping a directory of services around resident need in Norfolk
column4:
We will develop the Norfolk Community Directory around resident need in Norfolk, making it easier for residents to independently find information about every day services and support in their local area. The project enables people to find information in one place by taking and sharing information from other databases and directories, across health, housing and the voluntary sector.

We will simplify the process for grass roots organisations to advertise their offer by only having to create their record once in any directory in Norfolk, by proactively replicating information across all platforms, creating a “no wrong door approach” to information provision.
column7: We will develop the Norfolk Community Directory around resident need in Norfolk, making it easier for residents to independently find information about every day services and support in their local area. The project enables people to find information in one place by taking and sharing information from other databases and directories, across health, housing and the voluntary sector. We will simplify the process for grass roots organisations to advertise their offer by only having to create their record once in any directory in Norfolk, by proactively replicating information across all platforms, creating a “no wrong door approach” to information provision.
column8: A shared governance and development model (inc district, police, housing health etc) going forward for the NCD will be implemented, this will create a shared sense of ownership of the directory 31/08/2020  A developed comms plan with three key impacts 1) Norfolk Residents understand where to go for information on services 2) grass roots organisations will know to add information about their service to the NCD 3) staff across local councils, health, housing and the police will know to access the NCD to find information that will help them support residents they are working with find services that will help them in their day to day lives, further supporting community based support in the event of a second wave of coronavirus – 01/09/2020  Enhancements to search functionality to the NCD that enable customers to find offers based on need (i.e. any hardship issues they may be facing) 01/09/2020  A further sprint to create and offer automatic creation of records across all directories in Norfolk to reduce the administrative burden on small organisations 7/09/2020  Re-direct (and backfill) current resource to a role to provide advocacy and support across the 7 district partners to showcase the system to staff and ensure they can access the information on behalf of residents. This role will also act as a point of contact for any queries relating to the NCD on an ongoing basis to ensure that momentum is maintained 14/09/2020 A sprint for the creation of automated feeds in to the Norfolk County Council data warehouse – including those from the Lily database, Lumi, Brightmap and the Community Action Norfolk Database of voluntary sector organisations – this would be done by backfilling and re-directing developer resource from NCC as well as additional support from the software supplier – 25/09/2020
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success for the project would be measured by:
  •  Shared development and governance of the NCD across all public sector stakeholders to create a single source of information for residents. This will mean that all parties support the development and usage of the NCD creating a sense of collaborative working towards shared commissioning and development the system.
  • To support our wider ambition around the development of community hubs, working together with district councils to shape and commission our services collaboratively and develop a community first model of demand prevention and repository of information that is shared across all parties.
  • Services will be able to re-open more effectively as customers will be able to self-serve information on services that will help them re-engage in every day life, within their own communities, we would therefore see a reduction in phone calls asking for information and signposting in our contact centre, freeing up resource to support customers who cannot find information online
  • It will be easier for residents to understand where to find trusted sources of information and find services, local groups and clubs that will meet their needs. Initial research from the Norfolk and Waveney CCG is that those in the shielded population who accessed support in their community saw better outcomes during the lockdown period. Increased usage of the NCD, we will see this in page hits.
  • Increased number of records on the NCD.
column13: We will invite other councils to share in our development experience and working model through a series of webinars and blogs, the shared development model and governance structure would be easily replicated by other councils to support a community first approach to demand prevention.  The communications and engagement plan would also support other councils trying to roll out a similar approach in the future  In addition, the components and functionality of the development would be shared across the other users of the software platform as there is a collaborative development model in place, which includes several other local authorities.
column14: The main opportunity for accessibility and digital exclusion as part of this development is enabling our customer service and community teams to provide and assisted digital service to access the information on behalf of our residents, we have an active campaign planned to direct residents to shield list to contact us for support in a pre-emptive manner. Many of the people who run grass-roots organisations are not confident in using technology and part of the proposal is to provide support for them to put their services on-line. Our 47 Libraries are also open to the public and staff are trained to provide information and advice using the NCD, this project would support that approach further but increasing the scope and reach of the NCD
column16: We would welcome support from MHCLG around any research relating to those most likely to be disadvantaged by Covid 19 and guidance on where these populations may exist in order that we can target development on the NCD against these key cohorts (In addition to BAME and those experiencing in work poverty) It would be helpful to have access to any partner organisations who may have experience of implementing something similar.
column17: The incumbent software supplier, as well as strong partnerships with key stakeholders including the STP (sustainability transformation partnership, NHS) the new PCN;s, all 7 district councils in Norfolk, the police community policing teams, housing Associations in Norfolk who are also now working in partnership to take a housing first approach, as well as Community Action Norfolk, Voluntary Norfolk and the Norfolk Community Foundation (VCSE infrastructure organisations in Norfolk).

column2: Developing AI to help deliver business support
column4: During Covid 19 the demand for support from BMBC’s business support programme, Enterprising Barnsley, has increased hugely. This demand is predicted to continue and increase due to the economic impact from Covid-19. This project needs to enhance the use of the current website chat functionality, to integrate logic options to aid self service of clients, whilst capturing data to feed into our CRM system.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
When lockdown started due to Covid-19, the Enterprising Barnsley team switched to an online chat function as their primary point of contact for businesses needing support in the borough. This allowed the organisation to work more effectively and manage higher volumes of enquires than previously. Whilst a successful solution, this has meant a lot of time is spent manning the chat function, when 90% of the questions asked can be solved with script-based answers.

This project is building on the use of that chat functionality as the primary point of contact but with automation around the most asked questions. It will also work on linking the chat function to the CRM system used by Enterprising Barnsley, Evolutive, which is also used by multiple councils across the UK.

This will allow us to make sure we track every interaction with our customer base without a lengthy manually data entry step, that would slow down the process as well as increase the risk of errors in the data.
column8:
As project lead, BMBC will be putting in officers time to the project. This will be both from Enterprising Barnsley as well as our IT team to help implement the project. We expect this to come to approximately 0.5 FTE in total for the duration of the project. We will be using companies we already have a trusted relationship with to both implement the logic into the chat function on the website, as well as integrate the data captured during the chat into our CRM. This reduces the risk of loss of productivity with new organisations understanding the base systems. The extra data we will capture through the chat function will allow us to better see patterns, as well as understanding the use of this channel of communications compared to the more traditional ones of phone and email.

By integrating the automation of the chat function, it will allow even greater ‘always on’ experience for our clients, allowing them to access data and support any time day or night, rather than just during manned hours now. We ultimately hope to build on this data and interactions to develop AI learning that can also anticipate the client’s needs based on their initial questions. This research would be started as part of this project, but not completed due to the tight deadlines. We hope this research will give us a solid base to work from in establishing appropriate AI.

Whilst we would only collect a basic amount of data via the chat function, this will be compared to the data we already have in the CRM about those companies and that data can also be used to spot patterns and trends so we can adapt our support offer to match much more quickly and effectively.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
As we already have a platform for direct chat, we would see success as the roll out of the automated chat function to the website, and once live, a reduction of enquires needing to be passed to Business Advisors via the chat reduce by 75%. This will free their time up to proactively support their portfolio of businesses.

Success would also be the chat data being automatically integrated with our CRM for reduction of duplication of data, as well as accurate representation of where each client is based on their last point of contact from us, no matter what channel that was through. Colleting this data would also allow us to help predict future treads, meaning we can be proactive with the support avaialble making sure the right interventions happen at the right time

Data from both these stages would then be used to enable the test AI to start learning. We would also combine the data for this stage with other partner councils to have a wide yet accurate dataset as possible.
column13:
The solutions that we’ve outlined for this work would be based on the widely utilised Wordpress platform. This would allow easy integration with other councils using Wordpress. Due to the wide acceptance of Wordpress, this greatly increases the acceptance of it within other organisations.

Designing a way of extracting data from the chat function to pass directly through to the CRM will also be able to be re-used by other councils.

BMBC are the lead for the delivery of a business support project called the ‘Launchpad’. The four South Yorkshire partners (Barnsley, Sheffield, Doncaster and Rotherham) are keen to explore how these improvements could benefit their business support teams and are happy to work closely thorough our existing networks to trial the new system as they are all experiencing the same challenges of Barnsley i.e. trying to manage the large increase in businesses requiring support and guidance.
column14:
Our business support service is available to all and we are always looking at new ways to engage with those excluded segments of our population

We have found that by improving our online business support provision this has helped those marginalised groups who would like to start or grow a business be able to access support as transport and work pressures have meant that traveling to a physical location is time/cost-prohibitive.

We appreciate that not everyone will have the right level of computer literacy as others, therefore, will be working in collaboration with our Employment and Skills Colleagues to identify those individuals in our communities who need additional support and guidance to get online and improve their IT / digital skills.
column16: We don’t feel much input from MHCLG will be needed on this project as we have a clear plan and set outcomes. We also have the knowledge within the team to make this work, as well as good relationships with other councils that are involved, as well as the identified third party suppliers
column17:
The third-party organisations mentioned (Genius Division, DXW, & Alcium) all have excellent working relationships with the delivery group. Each one will have the following roles.

Genius Division – Integration of the selected chat system with our website.

Alcium Software – Creating the link between the web chat and the CRM to allow data to be passed over and captured.

DXW – Scoping the ability to take the services further with integration of true AI into the system to help build on the work delivered.

Launchpad – Lead contact for other councils in SCR to help rollout the findings and benefits from this project.

column2: Data and reporting moving forward into real-time vulnerability from council and partner systems
column4:
During the Covid-19 shielding process we have accessed/received datasets from multiple departments and partners in the form of one off, change only and full replacement datasets in many formats.
As it’s difficult to predict future situations,  flexibility is the key and having the ability to apply normalisation to datasets, maintaining full access to the original data and apply an index to this to test theories and apply operational resource quickly and apply rapid change to protect the vulnerable.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7:
Basics in use, rapidly prototyped and iterated on. Ready to progress, share and validate methods used.

Datasets have been drawn into a spatial data warehouse where we have then normalised the data and matched with the LLPG and NHS Number where relevant.

Basic indexes have been created to identify vulnerability for the provided shielding list.

Basic indexes have been used to identify vulnerability outside of the shielding list using local authority datasets and partner datasets e.g. Housing Association .

We have worked the project to a point where we can replace data at speed and make changes to the index to score vulnerability.

Dashboards/Mapping have been created to share data at appropriate levels.

Users have been engaged to improve data quality and supply.

Some validation of indexes has taken place using contact results to confirm the index is returning vulnerable records.
column8:
We would progress work on this project over 3 sprints within the 8 week delivery period.

Week 1 would be used to plan the sprints and week 8 used to share output and learning.

- Sprint 1 overview: Validate and document results from existing datasets and indexes. Plan and document additional indexes and normalisation standards. User research around operational dashboard/mapping standards.

- Sprint 2 overview: Create additional indexes and document with purpose, create reusable code for creation of normalized dataset examples. Creation and testing of reusable code for indexes.
Creation of dashboard/mapping to support indexes for service users to interact with and use operationally and strategically.
User interaction/testing around dashboarding/mapping

- Sprint 3: Review sprint 1/2 results and iterate where required to improve speed of replacement dataset and index changes.
User interaction/testing around dashboarding/mapping

Code and relationship diagrams shared along the way in a public space.
Creation of webinar to support other outputs.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Working closely with data owners and providing them with a return on provided data would enable us to show success based on the usage of the dashboarding and mapping at pace.

Take up and use of our outputs would be the key demonstration of success internally.

Over time we would then monitor the impact on the ground, operationally this would enable savings to be made due to targeting of resources where required, We will be able to demonstrate this output using existing shield datasets and contact responses to validate the indexes are targeting the correct points.
column12: The key intelligence specialist will be on leave the week of the 3rd, If we could push it a week to start on the 10th this would help with success.
column13:
Fully worked examples of code shared in a public repository.

Supplier independent guidance and methods shared publicly.

End to end webinar published.

Week notes publicly shared.

Open contact and working with interested councils to improve and progress reuse.
column14: N/R
column16: Continue dialog and sessions if any additional data is made available that we may use in the creation of indexes as per the shielded dataset.
column17: FME specialist to speed up the development of workspaces for testing allowing us to concentrate on reuse of the methods.

column2: Vulnerability Hub & Inclusion - Services Fit for the Future
column4:
Councils have stood up services at pace in our Covid-19 responses. At Barnet, our initial emergency response efforts focused on supporting the medically vulnerable and those self-isolating, through a digital Vulnerability Hub (also used by 15 other Local Authorities).

Our project will deliver a future Vulnerability Hub blueprint outlining lessons learnt, digital capabilities, data/reporting indicators, and service model to continue supporting our most vulnerable through inclusive, accessible and fit-for-purpose services.

We will work in partnership with government and community partners to develop recovery initiatives which will effectively reflect demographics and wider socio-economic vulnerabilities beyond clinically at-risk residents due to Covid-19.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7: We have a core working solution in the form of a digital Vulnerability Hub powered by Hitachi Solutions, through a free license support until September. This primarily served the purpose of supporting the medically vulnerable (based on the NHS shielded patients list) and those self-isolating. As we plan for recovery, there is a real need for us (and other Councils using similar solutions) to assess which digital, data and service operating model elements are required to continue supporting our vulnerable residents as shielding measures are relaxed. We have outlined priorities for our Borough Plan, which include supporting communities and businesses to thrive and developing prevention and intervention models to address vulnerabilities due to Covid-19. Current activities to date linked to the vulnerability agenda. Data and Digital lens: Our Customer and Digital Services are assessing options for the Vulnerability Hub powered by Hitachi Solutions. We have started analysis to provide insights into our vulnerable cohort since the start of the pandemic - e.g. what were the most prevalent vulnerable indicators? How does this differ between those identified as medically vulnerable (shielded list) vs. those self-identified as requiring support during the crisis? Strategic Commitment: The council is committed to draw on lessons learnt from its response to address wider socio-economic needs, particularly as it relates to disadvantaged and vulnerable residents. We have outlined strategic initiatives which this work will feed into, which will see us working with partners to collectively re-think how we operate individually and together to support residents. Community lens: We discussed this idea with two community groups who expressed support to represent and collect the views of communities: Inclusion Barnet, the council’s voluntary sector partner; and Barnet Multi-Faith Forum, our faith partner.
column8:
We will structure our project delivery into two phases: Phase 1: Discovery (4 weeks) We will undertake an accelerated, evidence-based assessment of best practices, lessons learnt and challenges by bringing together services, local government and community partners. We want to challenge ourselves to assess how our responses are/will be representative of vulnerability factors in recovery - e.g. digital accessibility, BAME impact, disabilities, debt, health inequalities. This will involve engagements with:
  • Digital Vulnerability Hub users (up to 70 staff across services)
  • Residents/broader population (through Community Groups supporting this project)
  • Barnet Customer and Digital Services
  • MHCLG – insights and trends in addition to locally available data in Barnet, input to assess data requirements for Vulnerability Indicators
  • Community and Voluntary Sector (CVS) partners
  • Partner Local Authorities
Key outcomes:
  • Insights into Covid-19 supported cohort and types of vulnerabilities
  • Evidence-based assessment of hypotheses/key lines of enquiries
  • Vulnerability Hub user journeys – what worked well, what requires improvement
  • Shared understanding of needs and opportunities for Vulnerability Hub services in recovery, including role of CVS partners
In week 4, we will set up Challenge Panels with broad representation to review joint findings, insights and priorities to address challenges. Phase 2: Co-design and Feasibility Test (4 weeks) We will work with stakeholders to develop a blueprint for how a future Vulnerability Hub service may look like. This will cover:
  • Digital capabilities
  • Data/reporting requirements based on suite of Vulnerability Indicators
  • Service operating model
  • Lessons learnt and recommendations of applicability for other Local Authorities
Main activity includes additional configuration of existing Vulnerability Hub solution – e.g. developing PowerBI analytics dashboard templates, additional applications/modules which will be used to test feasibility with pilot users, and to validate how the digital solution is scalable across other organisations.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
A successful outcome means having a clear service blueprint for the next phase of our Vulnerability Hub services which are representative of physical, socio-economic and emotional vulnerability factors for our residents, as the wider impact of Covid-19 continues to be felt within our communities. Success and impact we will demonstrate within the 8-week period:
  • Clear service blueprint which provides roadmap for us and community partners to work together in targeting support against a broad set of vulnerability indicators beyond medical vulnerabilities
  • Prototype of additional application/digital tools on existing Vulnerability Hub solution to address identified need/opportunity – this will be shared with partner Local Authorities currently using the same interim solution who will need to think about “what’s next” for Vulnerability Hub
  • Further strengthening partnership with community and voluntary sector – residents and community groups are heard, had opportunities to shape services and understanding of how they can play an active role in future Vulnerability Hub
  • Set of lessons learnt and recommendations applicable to other councils to ensure our sector recovery initiatives/services accurately address demographics and socio-economic vulnerability factors
During the project, we will also identify a set of longer term benefit measures through deploying and implementing recommendations.
column13:
Our output for a Vulnerability Hub service blueprint will be relevant to inform other Council’s recovery planning to design services that are inclusive, accessible and representative of the needs of those directly or indirectly impacted by Covid-19 due to a range of vulnerability factors. Our recommendations will also directly benefit councils inform their recovery planning, taking into account wider resultant economic impacts, second waves and local lockdowns. We have identified forums/networks where we will be sharing our findings for the benefit of other councils:
  • London Office of Technology and Innovation (LOTI)
  • North Central London (Health) network
  • Local Area Research & Intelligence Association (LARIA)
  • London Innovation & Improvement Alliance (LIIA)
  • Associations of Directors of Adults & Children’s Services (ADCS & ADASS)
column14:
Our project is centered around ensuring wider vulnerability factors are considered in tailoring our recovery responses, and will therefore consider factors which may impact on ability of vulnerable residents to effectively access our services due to reasons such as digital security, disabilities, isolation, faith/religious considerations.

We will ensure these form part of our Key Lines of Enquiries in the Discovery phase of the project, where we will draw on both data and community group feedback to provide an evidence-based assessment of the accessibility of Covid-19 support services to date.

We will also be working in partnership with our identified Community Network organisations to ensure broad representation and involvement in shaping this project.
column16:
In Discovery, we will value policy and research support to provide input, challenge and understanding of national landscape, trends and findings - e.g. relevant disparities review into Covid-19 risks and outcomes, regional and local BAME impact assessment.

In the Design & Test phase in developing a blueprint for Vulnerability Hub, we will require input to jointly assess and determine a relevant suite of “Vulnerability Indicators”, reporting or data structures for identifying vulnerabilities, for which we can collect data and regularly monitor.

We will value support to identify suitable forums and channels to collaborate with partners and to share findings.
column17:
Our Barnet Communities Together Network is a 1,500 membership strong network including the public, private, faith and community sectors. This covers groups such as residents associations, ethnic groups, faith groups, small businesses and GP-backed social prescribing link workers. This enables us to deliver community-related initiatives at pace. Specifically, two organisations have expressed support to be directly involved throughout the project to provide required input and challenges based on their understanding of demographics and vulnerabilities:
  • Inclusion Barnet – provide voluntary and community views, insights to people with disabilities and older residents.
  • Barnet Multi-Faith Forum – provide ethnic, BAME and faith views and representation

column2: Inclusive Recovery in West London
column4: This project is designed to generate new data insights about businesses in West London and their requirements to support a sustainable and inclusive economic recovery. This information will help boroughs design their business support and skills interventions and the development and implementation of the West London Build and Recovery Plan. It will specifically support growth businesses meet skill gaps, therefore accelerating their growth and supporting our communities to develop the skills to access opportunities with those businesses. It will also create a best practice and lessons learned guide for local authorities on using state of the art business data analytics in economic recovery projects and programmes which will be disseminated nationally.
column7: The strategic context for this project is the Build and Recover plan being developed by the West London boroughs in response to the impact of COVID-19 on the West London economy. Further information is available here: http://democracy.lbhf.gov.uk/documents/g6985/Public%20reports%20pack%2003rd-Jul-2020%2011.00%20West%20London%20Economic%20Prosperity%20Board.pdf?T=10 West London Alliance recently commissioned Oxford Economics to estimate the economic impact of COVID 19 on the West London economy. The findings of this work is informing West London’s plans for economic recovery (the emerging West London Build and Recover Plan). The Build and Recovery plan proposes a closer alignment between skills and business support programs and more responsive and flexible targeting of resources. Additional data analysis about the profile of business community in West London has been required and the WLA boroughs have engaged with suppliers such FAME (Moody’s Analytics) and Beauhurst to understand their current offer. Through the market engagement a compelling case has emerged for integrating borough level data on businesses (e.g. business rateable value) with data sets held by Beauhurst. Richer data will support improved targeting of engagement with the business community. This in turn will improve the targeting of business accelerator and skills initiatives and focus limited resources.
column8:
The process we have developed to solve this challenge in the timeframe is set out below. The timeline has been de-risked by the partnership. All partners have confirmed resources are in place to mobilise and deliver this work in the timescales set out. Key Milestones
  • Selection confirmed (03/08/2020)
  • Complete grant agreement between LB Ealing/WLA and MHCLG (24/08/2020)
  • Procurement started for supplier (02/09/2020)
  • Kick off Meeting with project team (02/09/2020)
  • User requirements workshop with borough leads (10/09/2020)
  • Supplier appointed (18/09/2020)
  • Development of solution and proof of concept (02/10/2020)
  • User testing and refinement of solution (Users: 7 WLA borough economic development teams and corporate strategy) (09/10/2020)
  • Adoption of solution into economic development teams in West London boroughs as part of business intelligence service. (23/10/2020)
  • Methodology and Best Practice document 1st draft completed (09/10/2020)
  • Methodology and Best Practice document final draft (30/10/2020)
  • Dissemination workshops with Local Digital and LOTI (November 2020)
Resources
  • Project management and administration (West London Alliance)
  • User research and development (private sector supplier and Borough officers in kind commitment of time)
  • Production of methodology and best practice document and dissemination (West London Alliance and private sector supplier)
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
This project will -
  • Provide the capacity needed for boroughs to receive tailored business intelligence reports on growth businesses in West London. This includes analysis such as business starts and closure by sector and ward, business growth, by sector, company size, skill needs, staff growth and turnover, sectoral breakdown of companies by size and ward and other detailed spatial and time series analysis essential for planning responsive economic recovery programmes.
  • These insights will allow West London boroughs to engage with companies in growth sectors to identify how they can accelerate their growth, identify their recruitment and skill needs and workspace requirements.
  • This project will generate granular insights into supply chains around Heathrow, Park Royal and other Opportunity Areas. It will identity which companies have received equity investments and made commitment to R+D and are progressing with apprenticeships. This information is essential to targeting business and skills support programmes more effectively.
  • Create a best practice and lessons learned guide for local authorities on using state of the art business data analytics (publishable output available for all local authorities and general public) setting out an overview of what datasets are available, how integrate with internal datasets, how to analyse business location data, how to analyse business financial data.
  • Use local government data in a new way with business intelligence suppliers potentially creating a new business model for sharing information.
  • Allow boroughs to send out targeted COVID response emails. For example, Officers could contact business involved in the green economy or growth sectors; or email council procurement opportunities to relevant suppliers.
  • Cross-reference the data with postcodes available through businesses rates data-base, to allow for new cutting edge geographical and spatial analysis.
column16:
Legal structures for data sharing between public and private sectors.

LOTI and MHCLG support to run 2 x borough workshops on state of the art business data analytics to ensure the findings are disseminated and value created by this project is captured and transferred to all local authority economic development teams.

UCL Govtech Lab and/or ODI with for data integration legal/techncial

LOTI support for dissemination

column2: Research project to identify ways to address digital exclusion
column4:
Covid-19 has revealed as stark divide in digital inclusion.  As more and more services moved to be offered digitally it was clear that there are groups excluded from accessing services digitally. 

Research undertaken by the ONS in 2019 identified those most likely to be excluded and the barriers for this. 

https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/householdcharacteristics/homeinternetandsocialmediausage/articles/exploringtheuksdigitaldivide/2019-03-04

This research project will take these findings to the next step of how we can address this issue benefiting both the users of digital service and local government efficiency in delivering them.
column7: Often the solution for these issues is to direct the individual to places where they can be supported, for example in Staffordshire our library service provides this support.  During the response to Covid-19 with these services not available, some people will have been supported by family and friends and it needs to be recognised that this potentially could make a vulnerable person even more vulnerable to inappropriate influences.  The solution of improving access by providing hardware and suitable infrastructure is well known and is something that we have provided in some instances.  However, in some cases it is too simplistic to believe that digital exclusion can be countered with the provision of a tablet and data package.  We have provided basic digital skills packages that can be accessed via libraries and have provided an online offer (which obviously requires the users to have a certain level of digital ability already to access them)  We have not dedicated any resources to date to investigate creative and innovative ways to address the digital divide and build the confidence of users previously excluded from accessing our digital services.
column8:
Produce specification for research partner   - July 2020  Commission Research partner – by 10 August  Research partner conducts research – 17 August to 18 September  Research partner produces paper with outcome and recommendation – by 25 September  Future actions  Share research paper with partner authorities, local digital partners – 2 October  Plan to implement recommendations – 9 October   Recommendations implemented – 9 October to 18 December  We will work with the key stakeholders in Staffordshire County Council, including Adult Health and Care, Children’s Heath and Care, Libraries, Skills & Employability, and partners including Health, Police and Fire & Rescue to facilitate contact for our research partner to reach excluded groups including: 
  • Vulnerable over 70’s 
  • Care Leavers 
  • Refugees 
  • Complex needs 
  • Sensory needs 
The specification for the research partner will request that they look beyond provision of hardware, data and training as possible solutions and be creative both around how we can increase digital inclusion for this groups and identify any other possible options to engage with these groups if traditional face to face engagement is not possible.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: An increase in users from the potential excluded groups accessing services digitally  Feedback directly sought from these groups about their experiences (that will be used to inform further developments and improvements)  We are beginning to return to services being available through traditional routes as well as the digital offering.  We anticipate that although there will be some return to traditional access methods many users will opt to continue accessing services digitally.   As we improve the digital offer we would expect to see the footfall from traditional access routes fall further. We expect the research outcomes to help us to think outside the box and be creative about how we can improve accessibility and inclusion  We will implement recommendations from research within 3 months wherever possible.  There is the potential for repeated waves of Covid-19 or further corona virus outbreaks in the future and we want to reduce the number of people who may be digitally excluded during those outbreaks
column13: Although the research commissioned will focus on circumstances in Staffordshire our potentially excluded groups will be shared across councils and it is certain that some of the options for increasing inclusion will be suitable for application in other areas.  Staffordshire would be open to the suggestion of joining up with other councils in commissioning this work to get the benefit of a larger research group as well as the implementation of the recommendations from the research paper.
column14: The aim of our proposed project is to identify the group that are digitally excluded and options for how inclusion can be improved    As part of the specification the research partner will be asked to consider how they can engage with a digitally excluded group whilst maintain the required social distancing guidance.  This may include personal visits with appropriate PPE or contacting groups using letter, telephone call.   The research partner will also be asked to consider how they can engage with groups using existing contact points, for instance by engaging the involvement of other organisation’s who engage with these groups.  The work to identify the possible routes to engage with groups will be started as part of developing the specification work that will take place in July.
column16: Identification of any other local authorities who are looking to conduct a similar research project so that a collaboration can be considered.  To support distribution of outcomes and recommendations
column17:
• Local Business 
  • Borough and District Councils in Staffordshire 
  • Staffordshire Police 
  • Staffordshire Fire and Rescue 
  • Midlands Foundation Partnership Trust 
  • Parish Councils 
  • Local Digital Partners if they wish to join and support this bid 
  • Local Housing Associations

column2: Identifying Individuals and Communities that have been negatively affected by COVID-19
column4:
This project will prove the concept that data can be joined across multiple services to add benefit and create automation. Initially targeting individuals and communities that are on the road to crisis or have found themselves in crisis as a direct result of COVID-19.
Outcomes to include:
•	Reduce data movement and replication
•	Increase the benefit of data reuse
•	Support business operations and the front line with real-time insights
•	Utilise BI & Analytics to avoid the risks of missing critical information & opportunity 
•	Tackle COVID-19 with data
•	Future proof data to meet new demands
•	Ensure governance, compliance and security
column7: Medway Council has been enhancing its intelligence led approach, introducing a Business Intelligence Strategy and producing service business intelligence analysis for the past 18 months using Office 365 Power BI.  This work has been completed inhouse with no support from private sector organisations. This analysis has proven invaluable in supporting Medway Council’s business change programme, including our vulnerable residents using our children’s and adult social care services. Medway has also produced public ward profile dashboards (www.medway.gov.uk/wardprofiles), as part of its transparency agenda. These ward profiles have been used by officers to develop long term strategic plans for Medway Council and provided an invaluable data source for community organisations to develop services for the communities they serve.
column8:
To ensure the project is able to commence as soon as funding has been released.  Medway Council will take a three-pronged approach to kick start implementation.
  1. Medway Council Officers will be working with intelligent-i who will be providing a Data Engineering Consultant, Public Sector Analytics Consultant, Project management and Principal Consultant for the duration of the eight-week project. Intelligent-i have a proven track record of expertise in delivering projects at scale with other Local Authorities including Kent County Council with whom this project aims to develop data sharing capabilities to identify venerable residents and communities.
  2. Using expertise from data owners across Medway Council to prioritise and provide expert advice across the data sources identified below:
    • Housing – Academy/MPS Northgate
    • Revenues (Council Tax) & Benefits – Northgate
    • NHS Shielding Lists
    • Pillar 1/2 testing
    • Boundary data (converted from GIS)
  1. Working with Local Authority partners in Kent County Council and the twelve Kent Districts through the Kent Resilience Forum Task & Finish Group on Financial Hardship (supporting Recovery across Kent & Medway), to put in place mechanisms to support the post proof of concept benefits as referenced in 6.1.
Key Date and Milestones for the project:
  • Pre-Project: Contracts, planning, briefing               03/08/2020 – 21/08/2020
  • Infrastructure Design/Technical Pre-requisites     24/08/2020 – 02/09/2020
  • iDAP Configuration Completed                                  03/09/2020 – 04/09/2020
  • Data Classification & Load                                           03/09/2020 – 25/09/2020
  • Analytics Development                                                 21/09/2020 – 16/10/2020
  • User Acceptance Testing                                              05/10/2020 – 16/10/2020
  • Proof of Concept Completed                                        16/10/2020
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The analysis of datasets will identify individual and communities (demographic and geographic) that have been negatively affected by Covid-19, to the point of 'crisis', or may be affected in the future.  It will consider changes in circumstances of individuals and communities over time examples include:
  • Number of applications for homelessness, insecure tenancies and households with rent arrears
  • Number of households defaulting on Council Tax payments, applications for Council Tax reduction
  • Number of applications/claims for Housing Benefit and Universal Credit
  • Number of individuals on the Shielding List (including details of incoming registrations)
  • Cases of Covid-19 based on Pillar 1 and 2 testing
Analysis of 'high risk' individuals based on the presenting factors outlined above, and indications where they may exist across multiple datasets (where possible). This will provide insight into the changing economic and social circumstances of Medway's residents to understand recent impacts and draw inferences regarding medium and longer-term effects of Covid19 across the authority. Once individuals and communities have been identified through this analysis, work will commence on prioritising service delivery to them. Providing targeted support packages to reduce the impact of COVID-19 through homelessness prevention, debt advice, benefit assessment, health screening, employment and training opportunities.
column13:
Medway Council is currently involved and contributes to the Kent Resilience Forum Task & Finish Group on Financial Hardship (supporting Recovery across Kent & Medway).  After the eight week project, the outputs and outcomes will be shared with this Task & Finish Group, Kent County Council (KCC) and Kent’s twelve districts. Post proof of concept it is envisaged that the Medway platform will be developed to meet wider data and analytics requirements including (but not limited to):
  • The use of Master Data Management to link datasets for a single of view of the citizen and family unit to maximise visibility of needs and service provision and support decision making
  • The automated production of statutory returns from Local to Central Government
  • Automate council processed improving the customer experience and reducing costs to the council
  • Automate services through a customer account, allowing residents and businesses to self-serve
column14: N/A
column16:
This project aims to make the most of and utilise the data that Medway Council holds, this richness of data could be enhanced through the addition of centrally held data sources.

Medway Council would require support from MHCLG to act as a broker between Local Government and Central Government Agencies, such as, the Department of Work and Pensions (DWP) and Her Majesty’s Revenues and Customs (HMRC) to develop local information governance data sharing agreements.
column17: No other organisations have been identified that could help to deploy and/or realise the benefits at speed.

column2: Benefit Maximisation Service
column4:
There are £10 billion pounds of unclaimed benefits in the UK. 
Citizens who are most in need are missing out on essential benefits which could mean the difference between living in poverty with ever increasing pressures of debt, or becoming more resilient with increased wellbeing.
In 3 months our prototype Benefit Maximiser generated an approximate combined £2,708 additional income per month, for 55 residents
We will develop our Benefit Maximisation Service into an open-source product. It will be targeted at early intervention and will have an immediate positive impact on citizens and significantly increase income for local authorities.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
The Council engaged with Northgate Public Services (NPS), to deliver a pilot service, aimed at reducing poverty. Starting in April 2020, the partnership has developed a prototype that has been designed to target and review households living below the poverty line. The prototype utilises data from the Policy in Practice Benefit Calculator, LIFT’s dashboard and the DWP Searchlight database. We have also engaged with Thames Water to add additional insights.

Using the prototype NPS has reviewed 778 households within the borough. Of these households, 676 were eligible for support, whether that be in the form of advice on their energy bills, increasing income through additional benefits or accessing alternative Council funding. NPS has been able to confirm granted awards for 55 households totaling additional support of £2,708 per month.

Complementary to this work, Snook have created the “Benefit Identifier” with North Lanarkshire Council. The application combines Revenues & Benefits data with Education data to identify the pupils who are entitled to free school meals and school uniform grant. As a result of adopting this approach, the Council saw an increase of 1841 of children registered for free school meals and/or clothing grants in 1 year.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We already know we can demonstrate success in a short period of time, as the pilot has already seen 55 households benefit via £2,708 per month of additional income being sourced over a three month period. The pilot phase has enabled us, via NPS, to identify constraints and areas for improvement to increase outcomes for both the citizen and the Local Authority

For the next phase we will focus on increasing citizen engagement, to enable us to better support them and produce comprehensive information that will show the longer term benefits for citizens through accessing this service.

NPS have robust project governance processes in place which enable them to track and report on a range of variables including lessons identified and risks, financial information and recommendations. The ongoing collation of this information enables us to consider the implementation of changes to the service whilst minimising disruption to day-to-day operations.

Most importantly, because the pilot has been running for three months we have already identified the more successful components, meaning efforts can be focused on developing these areas to ensure a more citizen centric experience that produces better outcomes for improving the financial circumstances of our end users.
column13:
We understand the majority of local authorities do not have the resource to commit to collate and review the data analytics required to give the results we have demonstrated through the pilot phase in Tower Hamlets.

Our approach is a wrap around service, harnessing the analytics via the LIFT Dashboard and other key data sources relevant to the local area, making it accessible to all with minimum resource or investment required.

We will openly share the full details of our approach we are taking so Local Authorities could take this approach themselves, or partner with NPS to provide the service meaning Local Authorities wouldn’t require long term investment in data analytics and support staff which would significantly reduce the risk to adopting this approach.

24 Boroughs in London and 7 other LA’s are accessing the LIFT dashboard. These local authorities will be able to benefit from the Benefit Maximisation Service.
column14:
The support service that wraps around the software to provide the greatest impact for the Benefit Maximiser will remove the multiple barriers people face when attempting to access and apply for benefits they are entitled to, these include:
  • Lack of access to data or WiFi
  • Lack of access to suitable technology and/or low digital literacy
  • Lack of trust in existing credit calculators due to impersonal AI interfaces
By creating a support service that targets vulnerable households this will specifically reduce barriers to access for those who are digitally excluded. It also allows Local Authorities to connect with individuals/households, before they go into arrears or just at the point of crisis ( hardship-loan, debt-collection, housing-arrears) and build a “face-to-face” relationship of trust by offering increased income rather than demanding the payment of arrears, reducing pressure on the individual. A simple working solution, that creates synergy between LA’s and its citizens.
column16: We would ask support from MHCLG to engage with other Local Authorities so we could test the concepts and value of the service with a wider platform.
column17:
There would be a 3 step process to deployment:

1 - Connecting with our existing cohort of local authorities already using the LIFT Dashboard

2 - Widen or reach to local authorities in the in London area

3 - Connect to all local authorities and Unitary Authorities in England

column2: Cheshire East Council – Consultation and Participation portal
column4: As a result of the social challenges now being faced and with specific regard to public participation, particularly in consultation and service design arenas, Cheshire East Council is seeking to develop a mechanism for excellent two-way engagement and collaboration, with residents and stakeholders, which will provide a platform to securely connect local government, partners and citizens to generate and share high quality feedback and evidence. This improved collaborative and engagement space, will ensure that the recovery phases and deliverables following Covid-19, are joined up and focus upon the needs of communities first, and can embrace any innovation and address and resolve challenge and provide a blue-print to share across other councils.
column7:
A series of initial meetings have been undertaken within the Council to explore how a site to support engagement and participation might work within the Council with several options being proposed. A solution proposal document has been drafted which will act as a guide around the nature of solution, which is being considered, with the aim that the overall solution will be built on a number of different technologies. We want to offer a seamless experience to the user rather than just redirect them to other sites and software. The key design principles which have been identified for this project are to: -
  • Develop a mechanism for two-way engagement and collaboration, with residents and stakeholders;
  • Provide access to a platform to securely connect government with partners and citizens to generate and share high quality evidence;
  • Utilise current available technology and will ensure that return on investment can be leveraged from already implemented systems;
  • Provide a single identity provider to reduce the number of logins;
  • Provide a seamless experience to customers and link connected data, maps, apps and people, enabling informed and faster decisions, extending the reach of data to support;
  • Improve access to information and evidence aligned with opportunities to be involved in longitudinal dialogue;
  • Ensure an accessible and inclusive experience for all visitors regardless of channel and device;
Create a design and process which can be shared with other Councils with the key deliverables built with open source code.
column8:
The users of the portal will be stakeholders from the community who are seeking to maintain and increase their participation in local government despite the challenges now being faced as a consequence of Covid-19. Community engagement has been shifting towards an increasingly digital arena and stakeholders need to find that this experience is as connected and seamless as possible. The key milestones of this project will be  stage 1 - the finalisation of the scope of the project (week 1) - stage 2 is anticipated to be the development of a single site which will provide a secure link between citizen and local council, supporting a seamless experience to customers (week 2 to week 4) - stage 3 will support the development of connected data, maps, apps and people, enabling informed and faster decisions, extending the reach of data to support to stakeholders and will ensure that they can access data and information which supports engagement and decision making. Stage 3 will also include promotion and marketing to ensure uptake (week 5 to week 8).

Contensis - existing capability will be utilised to provide a unique website (or microsite), with the overall solution made up of a hybrid set of technologies. All technologies need to be seamlessly integrated. Dynamics365 will provide the common data store to support automation and crucially the single view of all consultations, surveys, polls and documents. Web accessibility will be handled by Contensis and additional web accessibility tool Browse-Aloud will be implemented across the microsite.

Stage 1 development will see the likely involvement of Smart Survey

Stage 3 development will see the likely involvement of ESRI

The users of the site are anticipated to all stakeholders within the Borough, with a focus upon a desire to involve and listen to our stakeholders, which will ensure that the Council is best placed to collaborate and consult on the direction and plans of the borough, involving and learning at all stages ensuring that the process empowers all and supports longitudinal engagement.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success will be measured through the numbers of residents and other stakeholders who feel that they are able to influence local decision making. This indicator has been captured at a local authority level through the Are you being Served LGA assessment and through the Cheshire East Residents Survey. There will also be the opportunity to measure the levels of use of the site and the number of and quality of responses to consultation and other engagement activities being undertaken by the Council. This is likely to include a consultation on the Corporate Plan, Customer Experience Strategy and Budget consultation.

Cheshire East Council work closely with a Digital Influence panel who act as a group of stakeholders which can be engaged with about digital developments. This group will be used to assess the benefits that the development of a engagement hub provides and will also be used to ensure that further development supports specific needs within the community and does not exclude any residents or stakeholders.
column13:
The design of the system will utilise the Microsoft stack to manage integration, data storage and Power/Business applications platform to develop integrated apps and services, systems which are ubiquitous across the public sector. We will look to use the digital marketplace to seek solutions where Contensis and Microsoft and existing solutions cannot fulfil a requirement or wider need, again a process which will provide other councils with an opportunity to follow the development principles being developed by Cheshire East. Existing technology is to be utilised wherever requirements are met. Going forward the business will manage the website, content and content types within Contensis using a no code mantra, this culture of Low and no code supports the notion that the site will be managed and administered by those involved in engagement activity. Flexibility and scalability to on-board other solutions and strategies such as Marketing and communications and stakeholder management systems will ensure that the site provides opportunities to start small and grow.

Due to the additional costs for systems and sites supporting engagement and participation, which are available from the private sector, many Council’s are not able to consider the use of these platforms. It is anticipated that this system will provide a low-cost alternative which will enable the integration of currently procured tools.
column14: The design will ensure that the new accessibility regulations which have been designed to ensure public sector websites and mobile apps are accessible to all users, especially those with disabilities, sit at the heart of the site. Inclusion of the Equality, Diversity and Inclusion officer and the Web Team Manager will ensure that accessibility and inclusion are considered at each stage of the design and delivery activity.
column16: Promotion of digital engagement across the public sector will support the notion that the relationship between stakeholders and Councils is becoming more digital and as such local Councils which are seeking to develop systems and solutions which support this move towards a more enabled digital narrative and dialogue will be better placed to ensure that they can follow general central guidance. Guidance around pitfalls and best practice will be valuable to ensure that design and development can avoid any know issues. Knowledge transfer around the nature of Council systems within other local authorities will allow the development of code to ensure that it can be undertaken in such a way as to ensure that the majority of applications can be incorporated into any scalable design.
column17:
Support from ESRI and Smart Survey will enable the Council to provide greater integration in a shorter time and will ensure that functionality can be leveraged from existing products.

A number of local authorities currently hold contracts with both ESRI and Smart Survey and collaboration with these providers will ensure that any requirements or specifications can be delivered to other councils interested in the site development.

column2: Unified View of Community Need
column4:
Many children/young people, parents/carers require support from public, voluntary and charity organisations. With a multitude of services available, it can be difficult to navigate and access the right service at the right time. Since Covid, complexity of needs and service demand has grown.
 A countywide digital tool will enable us to connect individuals with services more quickly and accurately. Risks can be identified earlier and escalation prevented more easily. The solution will utiise the best of our Covid response, build on digital tools put in place in other councils, in order to match needs with capability and capacity.
column7: The need to match users with capacity in children/young people support services was identified through user research across the public, voluntary and charity sectors in Kent and Medway. This was done as part of a multi-agency Covid-19 impact assessment, which assessed our response to the pandemic, experiences of families and young people and expectation of increased and ongoing demand through the period of recovery and restoration. Our research also identified open source products, which have applicable functionality to meet our requirements. Specifically, the Beacon solution developed by FutureGov/Camden/Hackney councils (https://beacon.support/) which enables councils to record the needs of their citizens, and keep track of need fulfilment through council, voluntary and community services.
