Conceptually similar to a CRM/case management tool, its data model has contacts (referred to as people in need), and contacts can have needs associated with them (referred to as support actions but are treated a bit like tasks), which are assigned to users, who can mark them as completed and leave notes on the needs. Beacon is an open soure solution and can be modified to meet our local needs, allowing us to deploy it quickly as a solution for children, young people and their families.
column8: We will work with FutureGov as our digital partner to understand user needs in Kent/Medway and customise an implementation of Beacon to deploy as a minimum viable product (MVP). We will work in an agile way as a blended team: collaborating closely with service users; this includes children/young people and their parents/carers as well as service providers within the council and the wider public sector, plus charitable and voluntary groups. We will work iteratively and flexibly, so that we can respond to our changing understanding of user needs. The project will run for eight weeks, broken into four sprints: Sprint 1: 3 Aug - 14 Aug We will build on work done to-date and refine our hypotheses which we can then validate through research with users in future sprints. This will help us understand whether our initial proposed approach will help solve the problem. We will also start engaging service users and groups as participants for our research. Sprint 2: 17 Aug - 28 Aug We will start to customise the Beacon software and tailor it based on our understanding of user needs in Kent, along with building a companion service directory. Sprint 3 and 4: 31 Aug - 10 Sept We will start quick weekly iterative rounds of usability testing with all our engaged research participants, improving the software with each round. We will also start planning an open beta to begin at the end of sprint four, which will include discussion of how to host the digital service and support the ongoing development of it. We will map how users should be signposted toward the new service we are building. By the end of the project we will have a well-tested implementation that is ready to be made more generally available to a wider group of residents.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The project focusses on iteration of a product and as such will capture regular feedback which will assist the team in understanding the users need and developing a solution that is fit for purpose. Success in the short term will be demonstrated through satisfaction of users of the product and adoption of its use. Lead indicators for success will be measured for the 2 quarters following development to include;
  • Increase in satisfaction of people accessing services
  • People are more engaged in local support and services
  • Increase in service provider satisfaction
  • A % improvement in service waiting times
  • A % increase in use of localized services
  • A % improvement in repeat contacts to Council services
  • Effective use of resources increased
column13: We would seek to leverage our own network as well as that of FutureGov and MHCLG to disseminate the outputs/outcomes of our work to be used by other councils. We will work transparently, sharing our experiences during the 8-week project through published weeknotes. We would also leverage the support of FutureGov to develop case studies and blogs on the work, with the hope that these would also be shared positively by the MHCLG. Most importantly, any product developed will be open source and open to reuse. The foundation of the solution is Beacon, the code of which is already openly published: https://github.com/wearefuturegov/beacon. We would append all work done to the Beacon solution via github whilst also making it available through government portals (Knowledge Hub) and other local government knowledge sharing resources, both non-Covid and Covid specific such as https://www.coronaviruslocalresponse.co.uk/.
column14: The solution will meet government accessibility requirements including Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. The Beacon solution assumes service provider digital access, but the approach supports digitally excluded/vulnerable citizens. From our research we understand that many of our service users are not digitally excluded but do rely on smartphones as their primary source of digital access; therefore, we will take a mobile first approach to solution development. We will also ensure the solution can be accessed through traditional service delivery methods both in person and via telephone. Contact Centres and service providers, such as Children’s Centre’s and Job Centre Plus will have access to the digital solution during any assessment, contact or ongoing support being provided to a family, which can be used to support these touchpoints and capture actions.
column16: We expect the solution will have relevance for children’s services across the country. Therefore, we would welcome support from MHCLG in supporting communication to other councils as well as government departments who may feel the solution has relevance for their Covid response efforts, such as the Department for Health and Social Care. We would also look to MHCLG to support us in validating our approach to evaluation, ensuring we are capturing the right metrics to demonstrate value in embedding the solution within Kent/Medway and the case for other councils looking to adopt similar solutions.
column17: Medway Council Kent and Medway Clinical Commissioning Group – NHS 12x District Councils of Kent Kent and Medway Charity and Voluntary sector FutureGov

column2: Carer App
column4: The Carer App will provide a digital platform, engaging carers remotely, reducing crises through peer support networks, e-prescribing relevant local social supports, and improving carer well-being/social connectedness during the pandemic and beyond. The app will be designed for carers (specifically Dementia Carers) and professionals who encounter carers, offering resources/information about services 24/7, catering to the out-of-hours nature of caring and the additional pressure of reduced operating hours and delivery of support services during Covid-19. After successful launch in Hounslow, the app will be scaled to other London boroughs, and eventually across England (as successfully done with Hestia’s award-winning BrightSky domestic abuse app).
column7: This proposal has been developed with Hounslow carers/key stakeholders, all experienced in providing care/carer support in Hounslow (including carer rep, Hestia, LBH, Hounslow CCG, etc.) Joint working with carers/other stakeholders (and informed by Hounslow’s JSNA and Carer’s Strategy, e.g. est. 9% of total Hounslow population is a carer – over 24,000) has determined the precise need for carer support in Hounslow, particularly in the wake of Covid-19. Initial discussions/recommendations with stakeholders/national carer feedback (e.g. technology is being under-utilised in combating loneliness), suggests there is an identified need for a single tool which consolidates all resources/services available to carers and promotes carer’s well-being. We therefore have a detailed concept but want to do more to understand user needs, map the journey, and research technical delivery options. Hestia is an experienced support provider (in Hounslow: provide ~230 hours respite p.w. for carers; 200 carers on their co-production mailing list; host 2 regular Carer Groups). They are also experienced in creating support apps, having developed BrightSky in partnership with the Vodafone Foundation. BrightSky has since been scaled across the UK/internationally and is regularly used by individuals/professionals (e.g. Met Police). BrightSky is downloaded ~400 times p.w. and is free to download for Apple and Android users.
column8:
We anticipate the following milestones in the development of the Carer app: Identification of Key Users/Stakeholders for app co-development:
  • Area mapping exercise and user research with Hounslow Carers/LBH/Hounslow CCG, identifying priority areas for app promotion; capitalise on Hounslow’s abundant networks/communities to identify users to feed into app design:
    • Hestia's Dementia Carer Meeting; Parent Carer Forum/Mental Health Carer Forum/Brentford FC (young carers);
    • Heathrow Community Network;
    • LB Hounslow ASC News page;
    • Pop-ups at Chiswick Sainsbury’s and other big local supermarkets;
    • Social Prescribing Link Workers;
    • Carer Mailing Lists.
App Co-development:
  • Agree design approach; agree/develop app content and aesthetics with user groups; showcase on Adobe XD; engage in continuous improvement exercise through feedback sessions;
Review/Evaluation of User Testing:
  • Review initial app response (identified need of app and its features; app feedback obtained through focus groups, e.g. Dementia Carer group and surveys); summarise findings to inform and detail how this will impact further app developments;
  • Analyse/understand timescales of developing content into live app and scaling to other London boroughs/nationally long-term.
Deliverable Target Completion Date
Funding awarded 3rd August
Promotional Plan/Comms. to identify/recruit further carers/professionals for user testing/research 10th August
Identify and Confirm Tech Partner(s) 21st August
Co-develop app content with users, showcasing through Adobe XD; continuous improvement exercise 28th August
Finalise content and content sign-off; 4th September
Carer Product Testing; Feedback gathering; Journey mapping 23rd October
Analysis/outputs submitted 30th October
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success/impact will be measured via number of carers reporting:
Outcome How App Meets Outcome
Feeling better recognised/connected ·       For carer/professional use; ·       Self-Identification ‘Autonomy’ tool - more carers identified/aware of services available; ·       Directory - increased connection with support services.
Improved health/well-being ·       Directory - e-prescribing to local support services; ·       Diary function - therapeutic outlet; ·       Well-being Platform - tips for healthier living/planning for future etc.
Increased ability to balance work/caring responsibilities; increased breaks from caring role ·       Chat/Diary functions; ·       Autonomy - more individuals benefit from carer services.
Reduced loneliness/increased social connectedness ·       Chat forum - sharing experiences/meeting new people; ·       Directory - access to new groups/local opportunities.
The app will support in minimising/containing COVID-19 infection, supporting carers to access resources remotely and avoiding face-to-face contact. Success against these outcomes will be measured through carer surveys/Hestia’s bi-weekly carer groups/digital focus groups and:
  • Data monitoring:
    • App downloads (target: 300 within 3 months);
    • Individual clicks through to each app feature; number of feature uses;
    • Engagement times (identifying peak use times);
  • App/Play Store reviews;
  • Case-studies, documenting journey with app interventions;
  • User feedback.
Google Analytics will be used to monitor user behaviour; there may be scope to develop a key analytics dashboard.
column13: We propose the app acts as an interface to signpost carers to existing support resources (local/national), consolidating all available support into one place. Whilst the app pilot in Hounslow will signpost to local support (e.g. LBH’s Family Service Directory, One You website, Coordinate My Care, London-wide Care Place etc.), where other Councils have similar existing resources, these can be included to tailor content locally. Additionally, and based on Hestia’s existing experience elaborating the BrightSky app (servicing the UK/Ireland), it will be possible to allow users to use their current location/input a particular postcode/town to locate local support (no matter their location), without the need to download multiple local apps. All other app features can be used without the need for local tailoring. Hestia commits to writing a case study evaluation for promotion via our existing relationships with Adult Social Care commissioners in every other London borough. If successful, LBH will canvas existing LOTI partners to identify other London Boroughs to work with.
column14:
With smartphone use up in the last 6 years/a recent increase in use of online platforms (Hestia’s bi-weekly carers group meets on Zoom), the Carer App will offer flexible support without relying on traditional working hours, in line with all four pillars of Hounslow's most recent Digital Strategy. To ensure accessibility for all:
  • App will be free to download;
  • Designed for use by carers and professionals;
  • Hestia will continue building on existing partnerships with HSBC/Barclays to facilitate digital workshops featuring the app;
  • WaveLength in Hounslow provide free tablets for those lonely and unable to buy technology; the Carer App will be feature on the tablets as standard;
  • Available in multiple languages (BrightSky is available in 5 languages); BrowseAloud; large print;
  • Integrated with Hestia’s Floating Support service, supporting to build digital confidence (well-being sessions);
  • In long-term, launch a web-based app/website to accompany the phone-based product, increasing search capabilities (e.g. discoverable via Google) and allowing access for individuals without mobiles/smartphones.
column16: We would find it beneficial to engage in skills sharing with MHCLG’s Digital Team in order to share best practice and support in continuous development. We would also appreciate MHCLG’s support in showcasing any work and developments of the app in order to increase user reach, e.g. promotion via Zoom seminars, publication of our case study, peer support via Teams group for funding recipients etc.
column17: We will need the support of local partners and organisations for promotion of the app within Hounslow. These will include stakeholders within the Council (to promote within carer newsletters etc.), Hounslow CCG, local support providers (e.g. Hestia, Mulberry Centre, Alzheimer’s Society, Harlington Care, Carers4Carers etc.), local GPs, Hestia’s Hounslow Resource Centre and Hestia’s Enhanced Dementia Care Service. We anticipate being able to reach 300+ carers within the first week via these channels. We will also liaise with Carers UK for permission to signpost to their national chat forum.

column2: Virtual Day Service for Older People
column4: The aim of the project is to create a virtual day service to support older people and to see how technology can be used to enable people to participate in their communities, maintain social networks and prevent people from experiencing social isolation and contribute to maintaining people’s physical and mental wellbeing.  The project fits within the community engagement theme of the challenge.
column7:
Lockdown led to the closure of all non-specialised day service provision for older people in Hartlepool.  There were 91 older people who had been assessed by a social worker as needing a level of support via a day service and were accessing a building based day service provision at least one day a week.  In all cases, the reason for accessing a building based day service was to reduce social isolation.

During lockdown the service provider did keep in touch with the people who had previously accessed the service and whilst this was appreciated by the service users, a regular phone call did not fulfil their needs in relation to keeping in touch with their friends and maintaining physical and mental wellbeing.

To help reduce social isolation for this group and increase participation and contact with friends we introduced a weekly Zoom bingo session.  Supplementing our own supply of tablets with those donated by a Community Interest Company (CIC) led project, we have identified 15 people who previously attended the day service and who were willing to try out our bingo idea.

A member of staff visits the person’s home taking with them a tablet, dabber and bingo cards, sets up the tablet and signs in to a Zoom bingo session.  A staff member acts as caller and the participants play 2 cards - 2 lines on each and one full house.  The bingo session lasts approximately 45 minutes and feedback from the participants has been excellent with all enjoying the activity and all requesting more sessions and the opportunity for more friends to take part.

Building on this success, we want to take the offer further by extending the online participatory offer and developing a volunteer base to help us support more people to take part on a regular basis.
column8:
Week 1         Project engagement and set up

We anticipate 3 members of local authority staff working on the project.  The first week will involve them meeting with the bingo group, recruiting them for the project and starting to look at activities they are interested in so developing a menu of activities.

Week 2 – 8    -roll out activities

This will be the labour intensive part of the work.  Working with the participants staff from the local authority will test activities and get feedback from participants to inform ongoing design.  The menu of activities will be developed and then each individual will select their options and a rolling programme of tablet distribution and participation in activities will take place.  It is anticipated that as participants broaden their activities they will start to explore other ways of using the technology so beginning to suggest different things they would like to do that staff can support.  This might include contacting family and friends.

Week 3-8       -the local authority development officer will work with Incontrol-able to recruit volunteers to ensure the longer term sustainability of the project.  Whilst waiting for DBS clearance, volunteers will receive training and will work with the local authority workers to meet individuals.

Week 4-8       -recruit more participants - project staff will work with our commissioners and social workers to recruit more participants.  The development officer and Incontrol-able will start to explore the potential of engaging other partners to ‘add-on’ options for people such as health and/or social care virtual appointments. We will also work with the wider voluntary and community services to look at how we can add their activities to the menu and link people virtually to their services
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Impact and success would be demonstrated by people being engaged in the project, increasing their digital activities over the project, thinking about new ideas that are relevant to them and reporting that the project has helped them stay active, well and connected.

Case studies would be produced by the project team that would demonstrate the impact.
column13: We would share the development and outcomes of the project via social media, case studies and feeding in to any appropriate forums.
column14: Accessibility and digital inclusion are the building blocks of our project.  Evidence from the lockdown period demonstrated to us the lack of access to technology and data with our older and vulnerable population.  Whilst all of the participants in our Zoom bingo sessions had experience of technology it was always via their children or grandchildren and only 1 had her own tablet and wifi but she did not use the tablet.  The bingo sessions has demonstrated to the participants that using technology can really help them reconnect with their friends whilst having a bit of fun.  We would like to extend this opportunity and think that our project will then link to Incontrol-able's Project 65 project which loans technology to older people.
column16: Support to promote the project.
column17: Local members of the voluntary and community sector.

column2: Business Support Data Infrastructure Roadmap
column4: To review business data held across the WMCA, Local Authorities, chambers and LEPs and make recommendations on how this can be improved in order to monitor the impacts on businesses and how they are being affected by Covid-19. A significant issue in the region has been a detailed understanding of business data. This was previously identified in the region by the Office for Data Analytics as an issue but the impact of Covid-19  has accelerated the need for comprehensive and consistent data for administering programmes, monitoring delivery and evaluating impact. This work would provide, proof of concepts, benefits realisation, prototype data and guidance in order to demonstrate the wider benefits and need to invest in collaborative approaches to business data.
column7:
The Office for Data Analytics carried out an extensive review of data needs in partnership with PAConsulting and requirements for the West Midlands, with over 40 interviews and 10 workshops looking at gaps in data and analytics. These gaps were prioritised and the need to bring together business data across the region to supplement national datasets and provide a comprehensive view of business activity in the region. Enabling standardised engagement and data collection from businesses is a key component of building a consistent and rich long-term view of the region’s business activity. Since the outbreak of covid-19 actors have come together to share information and data this project would allow us to build on that and identify a route map for better business data.

This project would enable us to develop a consistent approach to business data  and identify ways forward on bringing data together across multiple organisations. This would enable business support interventions to be better targeted, monitored and evaluated, as well as providing an up to date data system for policy makers and advisors to use in delivering business support. It would support the delivery of interventions in the recovery plans alongside providing better data for performance management across organisations. There is also a need to upskill analysts and sharing of best practice on the use of business data which will help deliver consistency of analysis across the region and ensure robust evidence-based decision making. Many agencies across the region analyse data on businesses to gain an understanding of how businesses in the region are performing and how investment and interventions should be targeted and prioritised to support them. This investment in the providing guidance and options on better data and data capability in the region will enable new analytical techniques to be applied and utilised in the development and evaluating of investments and delivery of covid-19 interventions.
column8:
Our approach will be to use the established West Midlands Regional Economic development Partnership, which already has an established board and research programme. We would bring together academics in data, evaluation and impacts of covid-19 with private sector partners who have been working for the Office for Data Analytics, along with staff within the partner organisations to develop a road map for developing our business data in the region. Our users would be the various economic recovery groups established regionally including the Mayors Economic Impact Group, the regional Recovery Working Group and the partners themselves. This will include:
  • wk 1 to 4 - Compiling a business data asset register to ensure consistent analysis in the region regarding the meaning and use of data and to enable analysts to know; Where data is; What it contains; Whom to contact; What the data means; What the data can be used for. This will help us understand what can be utilised within any further system development such as matching to official sources for performance monitoring, and matching to other systems such as business rates. this should icnlude Requirements gathering for shared data system to ensure needs of primary users (WMCA, WMGC and LEPs/growth hubs) and secondary users (Local Authorities, Chambers of Commerce, Universities) are understood and scoped appropriately. This should include stakeholder mapping, roles and resource requirements. This would also include data ethics and sharing requirements and agreements. This should identify the benefits and uses of the data for partners including cost saving and improvements of services.
  • Wk 4 - 6 Review of current systems and capabilities to look at how systems can be merged and enhanced, what data is already owned and available to be utilised and shared. Review skills and capacity around data base development (ODA work would suggest this is currently very limited in this area and work is often contracted out rather than building an asset for the region and the partners). Review of data development, purchase and licensing this will allow us to identify any gaps in data identified in earlier stages, and set out options for filling them.
  • WK 6 - 8 - Recommendations for system build and or system enhancement costs the aim will be to identify the requirements for any regional data team that can manage and develop the required system and maintain it for all partners. This may include data extraction from other systems and feeds across systems so organisations can maintain current systems if required. This will include skills in developing the data architecture, the user interface that partners can access, performance dashboards, activity monitoring and reporting tools. Management and delivery costs identifying options on the build and delivery of options and solutions.
  • Wk 6 - 8 Analytics and research programme identify what a research programme would look like to build capacity and strength in the region for carrying out cutting edge analytics on the data. This programme should explore utilising cutting edge analytics to understand what works in terms of interventions, and development of a greater understanding of the businesses in the region to design successful business support
The project would be steered by the team identified and would include staff from across the partners of WMREDI, including student internships, ODA staff and apprentice and private sector secondees (PAConsulting) from the team who have already worked on the ODA work.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: The main outcome would be a roadmap to developing better business data in the region and establishing the partnership to develop this longer term. Currently partners operate systems largely working on data about businesses they know, there are gaps in information which include micro companies, larger companies and supply chains. There are also multiple systems or ones which lack resilience for future development and also a lack of data analytics capability looking at business data specifically. Bringing together this partnership will enable us to think through the issues Covid-19 has raised in identifying businesses who need support, what kind of support they need and how we ensure they access it. It will look at options and ways forward which would overcome the challenges of understanding the impacts of such an economic shock in the future and understand how we continue to monitor impact now. The main impact would be getting partners to come together to develop a plan for the region which gives us a way forward and identifies potential funding routes and cost savings. This work will feed into the recovery planning and build on the work already established in monitoring the impacts of the virus. It will also enable the academic research on data and businesses to be plugged into the development of any approaches so they are based on sound evidence and evaluation.
column13:
We will make the roadmap and the tools developed to create the data asset register available to share across partners and other councils nationally. We would also share any suggested research programme and any baseline work carried out. We would seek to present the work at any suitable conferences, events etc which can highlight the work and the approach. We would look to ensure the benefits assessment is accessible to politicians and officers wishing to understand what investment in business data can achieve. The main outputs will be:
  • An assessment of the benefits and impacts of improving business data for the local authorities, growth hubs, LEPs and CAS
  • Indicative roadmap the region could pursue to put a business data base in place
  • An outline research programme which could utilise that data for the benefit of the the regional partners, including better development of interventions, monitor and evaluation approaches
The outcomes would be better understanding of which businesses need support, what type of support and how we monitor their growth. This would deliver a better long term evidence base for future economic recovery and identification of investment opportunities.
column14: A key issue identified in monitoring the impacts of covid-19 has been digital exclusion for some businesses, this includes not having emails and up to date contact details when LAs have been delivering grants. This has highlighted the issue that many businesses are not technology switched on or utilising the full range of technology and innovation. This project would specifically address how do we identify those companies to ensure they are not left behind and how do we ensure any databases and company details are accessible. We would seek to identify companies who could benefit from other support to enable them to become more digitally enabled so they are not left behind in the wake of the technology transformation happening as a result of covid-19.
column16:
Additional support would be needed to:
  • Legal advice on data sharing (especially where the data is owned by MHCLG and BEIS)
  • Help steer and provide advice and guidance
  • Support approaches to data sharing across government bodies and support form central legal and digital teams for facilitate this
  • Identification of business data assets at a national level which could be shared to regional and local, and future opportunities for national data purchases which can be accessed locally
  • Access to data and research in this area on benefits and projects
  • identification of opportunities to scale up and fund the development of the road map
column17:
Potential other stakeholders:
  • It would be useful to understand how government departments are utilising business data in the development of policy and what benefits they realise through the process. This would help us get a better picture of the potential impact of business data application and usage from other areas, such as HMRC, Land Registry or BEIS
  • Other local authorities, LEPs or combined authorities who are looking at better business data applications
  • Opportunities to work with novel business data suppliers, such as credit reference agencies, banks, digital companies that utilise web scraping technology

column2: Data and Standards for a Scalable Approach to Vulnerability
column4:
A better understanding of social, economic and health vulnerability is a cross-cutting key feature of Covid-19. Building on councils’ experiences of Covid-19, and using homelessness prevention as a comparison, we will demonstrate how ready access to joined-up data drawn from many local and national sources, can create a scalable approach to vulnerability.

We will create scalable models to show that applying data standards, and secure shared access to some key datasets, can speed up how we identify vulnerable households, coordinate a multi-agency response, and track outcomes.  Smarter data makes us more prepared for further outbreaks, and tackling vulnerability in general.
column7:
This project builds on the following foundations: Research by MHCLG, CRISIS and DWP has identified the policy interventions required to tackle homelessness. This project will clarify and specify the data and digital standards needed to enable this work at scale in the context of wider social, economic and health vulnerability exposed by Covid-19. Our project also builds on recent LGA led work on tackling loneliness.  LGA’s review of councils’ recovery plans to inform Recovery and Renewal Panels, and drive out common data opportunities.  iStandUK’s collaboration with a number of organisations and projects, whose approach to data standards will be incorporated,  including:
  • MHCLG: Homelessness Data Pilots.
  • DWP:  Data to prevent homelessness.
  • Social Finance: A methodology to define standards for vulnerability; applied to ‘safeguarding’ and ‘school-exclusions’.
  • Government Digital Service to prioritise the work of the Data Standards Authority
  • GM Fire and Rescue: Data Quality, Provenance, Factor Analysis leading to the Integrated Risk Management Plan.
iStandUK is working with the LGA to build on the OpenCommunity project and develop the OpenReferralUK standard and APIs. This work has brought together councils, software suppliers, app developers, and community support professionals to deploy a standard to improve access to local services. See http://e-sd.org/VdVca/
column8:
At the end of the project we will have:
  • defined risk indicators for vulnerable people, and mapped them to data requirements considering both local and wider systemic challenges.
  • illustrated the draft standards, and design patterns that enable collaborative working that reduces hardship
  • validated the standards and improved journeys with Local Authorities, and industry
  The following are ready to work with us as soon as the project is mobilised:
  • Standards expertise
    • iStandUK
    • Government Digital Service
    • NHS Digital
    • Local Government Association
 
  • Data Sharing expertise
    • DWP
    • DCMS
 
  • Requirements, Policy Context, and Proving 
    • Partner Local Authorities
    • CRISIS and other voluntary sector
    • GMCA.
    • LGA 
    • MHCLG Analytics
    • iNetwork and Invited Local Authorities
    • TechUK
  As detailed in the Support section, we will procure capacity for:
  • Project Management
  • Researcher
  • Standards Expertise
  • Business Analysis
  Week 1-2 - Understand the Problems
  • Workshops/Surveys with Local Authorities and stakeholders to listen to their data stories for COVID-19 and Homelessness.
  • Capture ‘asks’ where data has not been shared, or has quality issues.
  • Clarify policy context
  Week 3-4 - Scope
  • Identify vulnerability risk indicators and how they are recorded and tracked;
  • Undertake stakeholder mapping, and list the data sources involved;
  • Undertake journey and process mapping to illustrate how data is prepared and shared
  • Generate problem statements’, and user-stories, from the research
  Week 5-6 - Solve It
  • Workshops with Local Authorities, TechUK, and other participants, to propose a data ecosystem of data, standards, patterns and platforms.
  • Propose the ‘digital capabilities’ required to support secure access to real-time data
  • Create ‘To Be’ journey
  • Assessment of data availability and suitability 
  Week 7-8 - Prove It
  • Validate journeys, standards, and patterns with GMCA and other stakeholders
  • Workshops to propose practical next steps to support Local Authorities implementation
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: The project will produce draft standards and design patterns.  The final ‘prove it’ phase will provide an independent assessment that the proposals are deployable and will bring about improved journeys that reduce hardship.   The proposals will be compared to the policy context and evidence such as the common data opportunities from the LGA’s COVID-19 Recovery and Renewal Panels, and the user stories from the LGA/DCMS Loneliness project.   GMCA expects to make immediate use of the resulting models in their data sharing, transformations, warehousing, and Business Intelligence.   TechUK will work with their members to assess the proposals against the tools that can be brought forward to deploy them.   One of the immediate impacts of the project is to influence the work and priorities of the Government Digital Service’s (GDS) Data Standards Authority. GDS have asked iStandUK to bring a local public services focus to their standards agenda, to be relevant to prevention, collaboration, and tackling complex needs.   After this eight week project, iStandUK will continue to champion the use of standards and platforms, at a place and community level, by engaging with the National Data Strategy via our DCMS involvement.
column13:
The initial phase, where we listen to councils’ COVID-19 and Homelessness stories, and their data ‘asks’, will ensure that we are focussed on common problems of vulnerability, not just those experienced by individual councils. Similarly, the pattern is generic so that it can be scaled to other vulnerability settings.   The recent DCMS/LGA project to apply standards as a part of combating loneliness, is an example that we would follow to put a community of local authorities, suppliers, and data  owners, around a set of standards.  This provides an ongoing design authority to ensure that standards are applicable to all.   With a focus on ‘open standards’, our goal is to drive improved outcomes that can be deployed irrespective of products or infrastructure.  iStandUK scope standards as:  
  • semantics
  • syntax
  • quality
  • rights
  • trust
  • transport
  • governance
  Standards proposals will conform to open standards for government and GDS API Guidance
column14:
Digital Exclusion is a factor that can give rise to vulnerability, and can be a barrier to discovering and accessing available support.  We will work with CRISIS and other front-line organisations to ensure that new journeys do not disadvantage the digitally excluded.  We expect those journeys to offer a choice of digital self-service, and mediated support; both powered by up-to-date joined up data.   We expect that the public will also take an interest in 
  • aggregated open-data that tracks the trends and responses to vulnerability;
  • how their personal data has been used
  Once data is in a standard form, or can be accessed over an API, a market for tools that can consume that data will arise, where those tools can reach their audience by incorporating accessibility standards.
column16: Procurement  - although Tameside will procure the services, we would like advice and support.  We believe that these services can be contracted rapidly via the Digital Marketplace - we are already building the specifications. Promotion - we hope to reach many Local Authorities as we scope the problem, and test the proposed solutions.  Given the short timescales, we would like to use MHCLG channels to encourage councils to take part. We plan 4 show-and-tells; one at the end of each phase. Collaboration - we would like regular and frequent involvement from the MHCLG Collaboration Manager for support and guidance.
column17:
iStandUK is committed to continuing to build on the outputs, working with: 
  • GMCA - for deployment into their BI function
  • GDS - to set the Data Standards Authority (DSA) agenda.
  • DCMS ( National Data Strategy ), and NHS Digital, for real-time secure data sharing.
  • TechUK, within their Local Government Committee, for their members’ offers to Local Authorities.
  • MHCLG Local Digital Collaboration Unit to champion the outputs to drive through data and standards adoption.
  • LGA to integrate into the Standards and APIs that are being established via OpenReferralUK and the Loneliness Agenda
  • CRISIS as a champion of homelessness prevention

column2: Rapidly Building Digital Skills for Employment in a Post COVID Economy.
column4:
Covid-19 has exacerbated a chronic challenge for many communities across Sheffield; to take advantage of employment, training and engagement opportunities in the city our citizens need to have relevant digital skills and tech-enabled approaches to working. 

Furthermore, our businesses recognise that they need to be more reflective of the communities they serve, and a workforce from more diverse backgrounds would improve relevance and engagement with wider markets.

We will consolidate and extend learning activity in socio-economically disadvantaged communities in Sheffield. 

This project will engage a range of partners to promote Key Digital Skills for Employability amongst adults.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Our proposal is a new development which builds upon learning from a range of projects that have sought to build Digital Skills for Employability.   The most recent of these was supported by the Digital Skills Innovation Fund managed by the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sports (DCMS) During this activity (June 2019- March 2020) our consortium of partners found amongst other things:  
  • people getting jobs with tech sector businesses who would not have considered this an option.
  • remarkable progress with people seeking out, learning and using multiple coding languages.
  • that our tech sector business employer partners have been positive and encouraging.
  • a new network of supportive contacts across the country.
  Part of Sheffield’s response to COVID-19 has been to quickly enact and empower a Local Economic recovery plan. Skills development plays a pivotal role in this strategic response.   The Private sector partners involved in this work include:  
  • Sheffield Digital - a representative body for Tech Sector Businesses based across the city region
  • Sero Consulting Ltd – an established consultancy business working in Sheffield’s Ed Tech Sector
  • The Web Developer Academy – a innovative start-up teaching people how to code
  • Brightbox Makerspaces – an innovative start-up delivering learning around ‘physical computing’.
column8:
We are able to undertake this work immediately as we have a Local Consortium of providers in place, following a competitive tender process which took place within the last year.   Our users are individuals (particularly women) who live in areas that are socio-economically disadvantaged. We know that Family Learning Workshops work very well as a method of engaging parents. We will then target these parents with specific and tailored careers education, information, advice and guidance about the tech sector.   Strong business engagement is a key element of our success and we are utilising providers who have clear links into Sheffield’s Tech Sector Businesses. Our consortium is comprised of the partners mentioned in 5.2 with the following additions:
  • The Higher Education Progression Partnership
  • The Game Academy – An innovative Tech Startup
  Within 10 working days of acceptance of our bid we will:
  • Identify specific socio-economic areas that activity will take place in
  • Report into the Economic group’s response to CoVID-19
  • Liaise with our partners to ‘tweak’ existing plans and confirm our new approach
  • Undertake online and word-of-mouth campaigns to raise awareness and recruit for the courses
  Week commencing 24th August we will:
  • Undertake 10 family learning/employability sessions which will be completed by mid-October. These learning events will take place in local schools, libraries and community centres.
  • Undertake delivery of a programme of follow up support sessions focussing on the opportunities for skills development in a tech-enabled economy.
  Week commencing 7th September we will:
  • Begin a Web Developer Academy cohort online and in person that will support an identified group becoming junior software developers
  All learning activities undertaken will be:
  • Engaging and interesting to the target group
  • Practical in nature
Promote other available opportunities for example the Essential Digital Skills Qualification and DfE Skills Toolkit
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
For each of the key actors we would outline the following:   Participants
  • Increased confidence in their own learning skills and employability prospects
  • Increase understanding of their talent and how to navigate the labour market
  • Increase their readiness for employment in an environment that has been changed by COVID
  • Increase awareness and understanding of requirements for tech-enabled working world
  • Give a step change in the adoption and utilisation of new technologies
  • Increase access to and take up of micro-qualifications
  • Understand how Further and Higher Education can contribute to their employability journey
  • Become ambassadors for their family, friends and community
    Partnering Businesses
  • Improved connection with employment-ready individuals
  • Ability to quickly influence the learning and skills agenda and experience of potential recruits
  • Access a group of people who are currently outside of their usual networks, this in turn will inform their user experience and user interface thinking.
  • Increased demonstration and understanding of Tech-enabled methods of working
  • Profile raising for businesses by being part of this project
    Sheffield City Council
  • Growing intellectual capital for what work in this area
  • Access to network of similar projects in order to develop and improve our understanding
  • Specific example of linking business intelligence to employer need
column14:
Our approach to ensuring accessibility will be informed by the following, we will:  
  • Think about accessibility from the start:
  • Take a whole consortium approach whereby all partners will be encouraged to consider involving the previously excluded
  • Incorporate research with users
  • Develop a clear understanding of what accessibility means.
  • Test for accessibility:
  • Consider the non-digital parts of our initiative:
Through our learning thus far in this area, we have made significant learning gains in understanding the accessibility of our projects.   The liaison with local community representatives has already informed our approach especially in approaching those towards the digital excluded end of the continuum.   We are developing innovative working practices and responsive methods that take into account the constraints of a post-covid world.  For example, although our Q&A sessions will be over a bespoke video platform, they will also be live-streamed into community settings where participants can safely engage.

column2: Better Ways of Working
column4: Response to Covid-19 has highlighted the potential for different work practices linked to our Better Ways of Working programme.  This means researching the tech solutions that enable home working whilst managing reduced office sites for teams to continue with collaborate, provide mutual support and in person contact with the public.  The solution would be a software system that manages flexible working that is easily usable by individuals and teams.  As part of the research we are looking for a solution to create a dashboard for Covid related information to interpret trends to help with track and trace.
column7:
The council has been running the Better Ways of Working (BWOW) programme for a number of years to create a flexible working environment to consolidate office locations. Whilst we offered home working this was adhoc but with the majority of the workforce working from home due to Covid, and it proves to be possible, the plan is for this to be the “forever” plan.

Pre-Covid the council had invested in laptops for each member of staff, a conference calling facility and a secure VPN - staff and services have generally adapted well and proved the potential of the workforce working from home. We have made our main office site Covid secure and some services need to return but this needs a management system. In addition we are collecting Covid information to inform our planning but needs this on a stronger footing and co-ordinated (rather than on email where information can be lost). With a recent outbreak on a farm it is essential to capture information to support track and trace.

We work with a company called Hoople to provide IT services. We would like to commission advice to review the home working and site management tech options.
column8:
The problem we have is that due to lock down staff have been working from home and the council have reduced the number of sites to operate from. This will be the plan in the long term but with the plan for teams to come into the office at least once a week for collaborative working or more often for some teams that need supervision or mutual support (e.g. social workers). This is a massive balancing act and want to explore how technology can support management of the process including easy self serve for managers, teams and individuals. Therefore we need some resource to investigate what is possible – off the shelf systems, build our own or purchase. At the same time we want to explore the best way of creating a tech solution for creating a Covid dashboard for reporting – we think the research and presentation of the options can be one piece of work. Milestones:
  1. Commission tech research on options for solutions
  2. Research and report produced and test of potential solutions
  3. Business case prepared and tender or purchase
Resource whilst we would see research and consultancy for 20 days, but also project management that would conduct the delivery, solution purchase costs (software / hardware) - there is a low budget for that as not clear of the solution at this stage. The lead will be the council working with Hoople as project managing. We work very closely with Wye Valley Trust (as also a client for Hoople and we share the network) so we would consider sharing a systems solutions with them. This is along with our other category 1 responders in terms of dash-boarding information.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: We are looking for options paper that we can progress through the council’s decision making process, with the purchase of any software programme and associated hardware. This is a very clear outcome in that research would include a full options appraisal to the management system and dashboard. We think this would be incredibly useful for other authorities who we would look to share with. This is a very short time scale considering August will be holiday time – with a longer period it could have included other outputs in terms of the deliverables.
column13: The research work would be freely available for other councils to use as this is going to be a common area of interest as many local authorities move to homeworking and the continued requirement for track and trace.
column14: The work is targeted at staff rather than the public. However, what is clear is that the IT skills and lack of IT confidence are holding staff back from fully using home working. Whilst in the office it would be easy to ask a colleague to show people how to do something or a member of IT, people are tending to work things out for themselves (or not). As part of the research work an element would be around the best ways of training staff as well as cultural change requirements of working at home e.g. how to use the tech to keep in touch beyond the obvious tools available.
column16: Any similar research conducted to review if relevant to consider in the Herefordshire Council context. Any contact for relevant consultants.
column17: Other organisations involved in deployment are Hoople Ltd who operate the council’s ICT. They also support Wye Valley Trust (the hospital trust) who would be interested in sharing learning. Because of the 2 month turn around it would be difficult to involve other parties – especially over August.

column2: Helping Communities Recover – Place-shaping for the isolated
column4:
The project will survey the impact of COVID on isolated older people who face isolation and digital exclusion, map this data and identify areas of greatest need. 

Building on health and wellbeing planning with Dr Anne Brice (PHE), Sir Muir Gray (Optimal Ageing), and Age UK, a tailored pilot programme will then be developed to improve wellbeing, engaging isolated people with physical and virtual communities through libraries. 

By targeting shared problems, we will foster social engagement, communication and wellbeing, both during the recovery phase and for future demographic changes, to create a knowledge service for living longer better.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column8:
Key milestones:
  • Development and delivery of survey - 03-16 August
  • Analysis of results and mapping of data - 17-30 August
  • Initiation of targeted activities - 31 August-27 September
  • Assessment of impact - 14-27 September
  Our users will be shielded older people in Oxfordshire, surveyed for the impact of isolation and digital exclusion. They will be assessed for location in Oxfordshire to provide data to be mapped and analysed. The previously-identified programme of activities will be prepared during this phase in conjunction with Oxfordshire County Council’s Public Health team, community groups, and volunteers, and then delivered once the pilot libraries and locations have been determined. The results from this will form a targeted roll out with results which can be applied to other councils and areas.

column13: A toolkit of advice and evidence of impact on communities will be produced, with a shared methodology and evidence of impact to allow other councils to implement similar surveys and programs through their library services, and a model for working with community health and wellbeing providers.
column16: We do not envisage the need for additional support from MHCLG to advance this project. We would value the opportunities available in highlighting the project’s deliverables through the MHCLG.
column17: Further collaboration with the University of Oxford could help us deploy our survey and gather data more quickly and efficiently through collaboration with healthcare providers.

column2: Supporting vulnerable residents to access housing and homelessness support in a COVID environment
column4: COVID-19 has redefined what it means to be vulnerable. Having access to a home or somewhere safe has never been more important. With services moving online due to building closures there is a small but incredibly vulnerable group of people who have difficulty accessing critical housing services. Our project will reduce the number of vulnerable residents who reach crisis and increase the number of vulnerable residents we support. To achieve this, we will co-create a new pathway for vulnerable residents, defining new working practices to ensure those who are most vulnerable increase resilience and can access essential support.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Over the past year LBE Housing services have been completely redesigned, using human-centered design and agile methods we designed a service blueprint and operating model to transform the Council to a new digital and preventive model. We have focused on how we meet residents’ needs earlier. 96% of our homeless applicants come through our housing office (John Wilkes House), and of the assessments 96% require support, often resulting in crisis for the resident and a challenging problem for the council. Our new service model is dealing with issues upstream with our specialist Outreach service working with households prior to crisis point. This new service is now delivered entirely through phone and online channels, a transition that was accelerated due to COVID-19. This has highlighted a need to address areas where channel shift and digital forums are not suitable, given our housing office is unable to reopen with social distancing measures. Therefore, we need to review our service design and blueprint to ensure those who are vulnerable and would often meet face-to-face can access the services they need.

To deliver our co-creative resident driven design we have worked with several partners; Parent Engagement Network (PEN), Advice Aid and EY-Seren
column8:
LBE will lead the PoC, working with stakeholder groups below (and in 4.2) Sprint 1: Align (1 week)
  • Kick-off meeting: define objectives, deliverables, milestones
  • Develop service proposition for vulnerable groups (below)
  • Agree on research questions and methods
  • Setup interviews
Sprint 2: Understand (1.5 weeks)
  • Understand user needs through 1:1 interviews
  • Develop research guides setting goals and format
Sprint 3: Define (1.5 weeks)
  • Create personas for vulnerable groups highlighting barriers they face - use cases, resident needs and problem statements
  • Design user journeys
Sprint 4: Co-create (3 weeks)
  • Develop prototypes and new journeys
  • Test personas against journeys
  • Validate journeys and prototypes with residents in co-creation sessions
  • Update service blueprint reflecting access for vulnerable groups
  • Create guidance
  • Test blueprint and guidance with staff/providers
Sprint 5: Iterate and deliver (1 week)
  • Finalise outputs
  • Brief staff who will be responsible for implementation
Methods:
  • This project will be run using agile methods
  • Use online collaboration tools MS teams and MURAL to document and share learnings
  • A range of research and co-creation tools – e.g. use elements of World Café, Purposeful Play and Lego to expand thinking and design tools e.g. scenario writing and role play
Stakeholder groups:
  1. Vulnerable residents:
  • Fleeing domestic abuse: 5685 domestic abuse incidents annually
  • English not a first language: 49% of school age children have a non-English first language
  • Deaf or hard of hearing: c.44,500 (13%) of residents with hearing loss
  • Mental or physical disability: c.52,101(15.61%) people have a disability
  • No access to internet or a phone
  1. Front line staff
  • Housing service
  • Domestic abuse
  • Learning disabilities
  • Mental Health
  1. VCS
  • Age UK

column10:
We will deliver at pace to maximise impact, as government support reduces, we anticipate an increase in potentially vulnerable residents requiring our services   The impact of this project will be:
  • Reducing number of vulnerable residents who reach crisis point and require LBE intervention
  • An overall increase in the number of vulnerable residents supported by the Housing service
  • An inclusive and accessible housing service for all residents who require it
We will measure this by:
  • Monitoring number of residents supported who are vulnerable, and following their journeys – using our knowledge and insight team
  • Survey front line staff who are trained in how to support and respond to vulnerable groups – through our quality team
  • Getting instant user feedback on the day – immediate feedback forms/questions at end of assessments
  • Requesting a panel of experts across teams (including Mental Health, Learning Disabilities, Domestic Abuse and Homelessness and immigration team) to review our new processes and approach in the style of a case audit/panel
Once the service is fully operational:
  • We will seek out quality audits from leading providers e.g. shelter
We will explore opportunities for accreditation for staff e.g. CIH certificates
column13:
1. North London Housing Partnership (NLHP): LBE are the chair of the NLHP, we have an established track record of delivering housing and homelessness projects across six London boroughs, we will actively support these boroughs with adopting our approach
  2. Use our pan-London networks: LBE are part of two pan-London housing initiatives from the London Ventures programme, an innovation partnership that fosters cross-sector collaboration and focuses on human-centered design. London Ventures can share outputs and findings through London Councils and their key issues newsletter with a readership of over 20,000. LBE are also part of the pan-London housing directors group where we will present our findings to all of the 33 London local authorities
  3. Outputs: we will share our personas, resident journeys and blueprint with other councils to help them understand how we tailored our services to user needs. Using our open collaboration tools MS team and MURAL
column14:
The focus of this project is to include the ‘excluded’; where exclusion is a result of their vulnerability. This group are at significantly greater risk than others and require a tailored service. Our approach needs a considered and empathetic approach to engaging these groups:
  • It will be important to create a safe and supportive environment for participants to share difficult and personal experiences
  • We need to include a variety of specialists to advocate or translate for some of our vulnerable groups
  • We will be flexible with our research users, engaging with those who are now in stable environments as oppose to those who are currently experiencing crisis
  • Use partnerships with voluntary sector organisations who can advocate for vulnerable groups (where appropriate)
Key groups we will work with to ensure this:

  • Resident group – Housing advisory service
Customer service standards

column2: Building a Wave 2 Shielding Support Model
column4:
LBE had one of the largest ‘Clinically Extremely Vulnerable’ (CEV) cohorts in London, with over 23,000 residents identified. LBE successfully contacted and triaged 21,737 residents and delivered nearly 13,000 food parcels through our own system, using a mix of software solutions to identify, prioritise and contact CEV residents. 

This project will enable LBE to further develop and automate supporting CEV residents in the event of 2nd Wave. Using common software solutions adopted by other local authorities we can create a playbook for Shielding triaging to be adopted by other local authorities to enable a more effective 2nd Wave response.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7:
LBE ran a very successful Shielding Support service (see section 3.2), having designed a set of processes to meet this need rapidly and at scale.  LBE has designed and implemented;
  • Dynamics 365 CRM module to case manage the contact and triage process using telephone and SMS, with referrals to relevant support services (food delivery, safeguarding, social contact etc.).
  • MDM Model that incorporates the CEV data held on MS Dynamics with over 1 million records held by LBE, including commonly deployed systems such MOSAIC and Synergy, to prioritise residents using a weighted vulnerability index and to update social care records with a client’s Shielding status.
  • Power BI dashboards that enable the vulnerability indexing and export of data for action.
  • Excel based Performance dashboards, MHCLG reporting and delivery processes that can be used as a basis for automating these functions using PowerApps and Dynamics 365.
  • Successful incorporation and use of Gov.UK Notify platform for SMS texting of CEV residents.
  • A separate process for vulnerable residents not identified as CEV, based on a webform and spreadsheet referral tracker (also for automation).
These processes have already been mapped and presented as an example good practice via the LOTI data analyst forum (7th July 2020).
column8:
Project Milestones:  
  1. Procurement of additional development resources – section see 7.2 (weeks 1 and 2)
  2. Agree process modeling and design specification (week 1 to 4)
  3. Implement development and user acceptance testing (weeks 2 to 6)

Local government sector:

  • Findings will be presented via the LOTI data analysts’ network and LBE hosted webinar
Data sources:
  • MHCLG / NHS Shielding data
  • Council-held data including information on Social Care, Housing, Benefits status etc to assess vulnerability
  involvement from the broader population
  • All CEV and other vulnerable residents will benefit by an enhanced Shielding support offer by LAs applying this model.
Outcomes: Development of an end to end Shielding process that enables LAs to effectively;
  • Match, process and priorities vulnerable residents for contact using the most effective channel (Phone, SMS, Visit etc.).
  • Improved triage process and automate referrals to support services.
  • Enhanced matching and data validation to prevent duplication and handle multiple resident referrals from different referral sources using key identifiers (UPRN, NHS ID, DOB etc.), to create a single view.
  • Incorporate broader support offer to non-CEV vulnerable residents into process.
  • Fulfill MHCLG reporting requirements and manage CEV list updates.
  • Automate updating of social care clients shielding status.
Daily reporting dashboards to assess service performance and forecast future demand (e.g. food supplies).

column13:
A portable model will be developed using software solutions commonly used and applied by other Local Authorities (LAs) such MS Dynamics 36, Power Apps, Power BI and service solutions such as MOSAIC (Adults & Children Social services) and Synergy (Schools / Education). The model will enable LAs to match, prioritise, contact and triage CEV residents as well as follow the required steps set by MHCLG, by applying a more automated process to reduce the impact on resource such as;
  • Flagging cases on Social services systems and;
  • Completing weekly reporting returns for MHCLG.
The project learning, final data model and schemas will be collated in the last 4 weeks of the project to create a playbook that other Local authorities can utilise to prepare for supporting Shielding residents in a 2nd Wave scenario. LBE will also present its findings at LOTI’s data analyst network and host a webinar for interested LAs.
column14:
This project will result in better access to services for digitally excluded CEV households and those with accessibility issues. The process will look to link Shielding NHS data with a range of Council held datasets to better identify and prioritise those most vulnerable residents – many of which maybe digitally excluded (and can’t register for the Government register),  don’t speak English as a first language or maybe at risk of exclusion due to other characteristics.

By mining data already processed by the Council, LBE’s model will enable Council to profile vulnerability with accessibility issues as a factor and identify the most appropriate channel for engagement e.g. phone call with an interpreter, referral to specialist service to make contact, visit to home address etc.)

column2: Building a Post-COVID Early Help Offer
column4:
LBN’s research deep-dives into vulnerable children and NRPF families impacted by C19 have found increased need and incomplete information, particularly data from schools and children attending school out of borough, families from other boroughs in temporary accommodation in Newham, and asylum seeking families. 

This project will enable us to find relevant data to identify vulnerable children and provide a data sharing agreement and data sharing schema and future data standardisation between London borough councils and schools, complementary to Camden and Central Bedfordshire’s data sharing bid with VCS. This will enable more targeted C19 recovery for vulnerable children and families.
column7:
C19 work:
  1. HELP Newham targeted C19 response for families, including family boxes and youth befriending service
  2. Vulnerable children deep-dive research on children at increased vulnerability as a result of C19 and the lockdown, with data from the council and schools focusing on those not identified by the council previously. Data gaps were NRPF families, children in school out of borough and families in Newham temporary accommodation from other boroughs
  3. NRPF deep-dive worked with VCS identifying NRPF groups and tailoring policy responses to C19 recovery for them
Early Help service redesign
  1. Early Help Needs Assessment, completed in July 2019, is being updated to reflect C19 and improved data sources
  The support of the following has been confirmed:
  • London Office of Technology and Innovation (LOTI), partner organisation, with experience in data sharing between boroughs
  • London Borough of Brent
  • London Borough of Ealing
  • London Borough of Tower Hamlets
  • Greater London Authority (GLA), the London Datastore can display data relevant to specific boroughs, and would serve as a future data sharing platform
The project will build on existing data sharing principles developed by LOTI and London Datastore functioning as a use case for this work.
column8:
Two concurrent processes will be involved: 1) borough-level coordination to identify and gather critical datasets; and 2) creation of a data sharing agreement with partner boroughs. Within Newham:
  1. Procurement of data coordinator (weeks 1 and 2)

  3. Data collection, creating an initial data schema and sharing (weeks 2 to 6)
  4. Value assessment of data for service (weeks 6 to 8)
With inter-borough partners:
  1. LOTI-coordinated data sharing agreement discussion between partner borough councils and Information Governance Group for London (weeks 1 to 2)
  2. Workshop to finalise data sharing agreement and data sets to be shared (week 2 to 3)
  3. Sharing agreed upon datasets (weeks 4 to 6)
  4. Follow up workshop between partners (week 6)
  1. Development of data sharing playbook (weeks 4 to 8)
  Challenges and potential solutions: Data sources: We will mostly focus on council-held data. However, existing relationships with VCS organisations are being fostered. For education data, the thrice yearly pupil census returns. Data collection and coordination: the services of a data coordinator will be commissioned immediately following the start of the project. Inter-borough coordination: LOTI have agreed to partner on this project, and provide their experience in data sharing agreements between local authorities. Asylum seeking data: we will be looking to work with MHCLG to gain access to HO held data on asylum seeking children and families. C-19 and lockdown restrictions: all meetings and the workshop will make allowances to be held virtually. Sustainable reporting schedule: for educational data, the pupil census is collected thrice yearly. The data sharing agreement will contain the remaining terms.

  1. Access to data sharing platform (London Datastore)

The data sharing agreement early in the project will be expandable to include more partners as necessary, and will include the following to ensure reusability and sustainability:

Learnings will be collected throughout the project, and will form part of a playbook created to inform similar efforts or for further partners to join the current initiative. The inclusion of three other boroughs in this initial process ensures that learnings will reflect this reusable requirement from the start.
