column2: Planning Notification and Consultation Plaform
column4:
The Prime Minister has made it clear that keeping the building industry going is key to the supporting the UK's economy.  The planning system is central to this.

Some of the barriers faced by the planning system during COVID have been challenges about how to consult community on development proposals and keep residents informed about planning applications being considered so that they can help shape the places they live in.

Using the new live stream of development data achieved through the Planning London Datahub, we would like to build an notification tool for Londoners to self select the types of schemes they would like to be consulted on regardless of whether they are a resident in the borough, ensuring they can be fully involved in shaping their local environment regardless of whether they are shielding or receive official consultation letters.
column7:
The GLA, working with 35 planning authorities across London, has built a database that receives data relating to all planning applications in London.  This includes planning applications, prior approvals as well as other notifications.

This data set is in the process of going live, and currently holds all of the data for 9 LPAs, with the remainder scheduled to come on line in the 2/3 weeks, with then further changes to how built environment data is collected at the point of submission and decision coming on line later in the year. See

Part of the business case for centralising the data set, and putting it into a single format (schema) was to enable it to become useful for Londoners as a data set, and help Londoners and planners to access and understand how the city is changing.  Over half a million Londoners live within 50 meters of the boundary of a borough (excluding the Thames) and won't ever be consulted on schemes that might impact them.

During COVID the planning authorities in London have worked hard to keep the industry moving by issuing decisions and supporting developers.  Communities have struggled to keep up to date, as neighbour notification systems have relied on site notices and neighbour notification letters which have been delayed or lost in the postal system.  As such a working system would have the greatest impact on the lives of Londoners.
column8:
Discovery (2 Weeks) There are a number of elements of discovery that would be needed at the outset to address the problem including:
  1. Users - what are their preferences to access information
  2. LPAs - what information would they want and need consulted on
  3. Responses - How best to have information fed back
  4. Information - What format should it be presented in
  5. GDPR and Personal Information implications
  6. Existing Tools with similar functionality and elements that can be replicated
It is proposed to do this using a number of workshops with users to get their insights including :
  1. Planning Officers
  2. Residents (using Talk London as a framework to access volunteers)
  3. Heads of Planning Services
  4. Planning Lawyers
  Build (4 Weeks) There are numerous examples and tools that already have functionality that may be useful, including accessing and monitoring geospatial information, alert systems and notification systems that can be replicated without substantial reconstruction.   These will be reviewed and replicated as part of the build process together with identifying gaps where additional coding is required. The build will be carried out Atkins who have constructed the London Planning DataHub so have a full understanding of the data format and schema, and will work on how it can be distributed and presented to Londoners in the most effective way. Through the discovery work we will have a team users engaged in the project so at the appropriate moments during the build they can be involved in testing and commenting on the functionality. Testing Core to ensuring that it meets user need will be testing with both the original groups and other residents and users of the products. The outcomes of the project will be:
  1. Discovery Report with Business Case
  2.  Code for a product (in Alpha)
  3.  Recommendations for Next Steps - including further business case
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The procurement could be managed through a contract variation to the current contract in place to deliver the LDD, enabling a speedy mobilisation of the project. Successes:
  1. 2 week discovery completed with a discovery report issued
  2.  2 x 2 week development sprints with the outcome at the end being a prototype
  3.  2 week testing period with a full report and business case for the tool
Key Outcomes A discovery report detailing information on consultation in London A prototype consultation tool Code for the prototype capable of being uploaded onto Github
column13:
The product would be set up to use the GLA schema as the framework for notification.  This is a published scheme and all bar two back office systems in the UK have been set up to issue data in that format (or have their data issued in that format) meaning that any tool could be replicated or set up to issue notifications anywhere in the UK. The key outputs of the project once we are satisfied are robust are:
  1. Improvement for resident awareness and involvement in the planning system
  2. potential savings in cost to LPAs for doing neighbour notification letters (for a single borough in London this currently exceeds £20,000 pa)
  3. Reduced risk of COVID transmission due to less need for resident interaction
  4. Speeds up the planning process, and so delivery of the development industry through making more robust and resilient notification and consultation systems.
column14:
Accessibility

As part of both the discovery and testing, accessibility will be reviewed, initially to understand requirements and then subsequently to ensure those requirements are addressed fully in the product.  Discovery and testing would be carried out both with users, and with the testing lab that the GLA subscribe to.

Digital Exclusion

The product would not initially replace neighbour notification letters and site notices, but instead would test the waters of whether this is the appropriate way forwards, as such users would have an enhanced experience rather than a replacement experience.

That being said this would trigger an area of work around how best to keep the digitally excluded involved in the development process moving forwards, and enable the best possible solutions for them.
column16:
There are a number of areas:
  1. Oversight and guidance on the work the team at MHCLG have been doing in this space, including digital site notices and resident involvement
  2. Guidance and support on how politicians at Westminster want to gain from resident involve in planning moving forwards
  3. Support in delivering this as an agile project
  4. Support in maintaining the appropriate profile for the project
column17:
A number of boroughs in London have volunteered to be involved in this project and 'go first'.  If successful we will pull together a group of Boroughs in London to provide oversight to the project not just to feed into functionality (and ensure it is fit for purpose) but steer the project.

This would also be the case with a number of private sector partners.

Boroughs that have already expressed an interest include:

Southwark

Camden

Westminster

Hammersmith and Fulham

column2: Digital Wellbeing Platform Access For Sheltered Housing Teams
column4:
Covid-19 has had a significant impact on the wellbeing of older adults, isolating them from loved ones and preventing them from engaging in their usual pastimes. This means that varied, interesting activities are more important than ever for their wellbeing.

We propose access to a new digital platform for all Thurrock sheltered housing schemes and the local community. This will enable staff to flexibly access e-learning training and content to enhance the wellbeing of service users. 

The platform will inspire schemes to deliver varied activities, stimulating service users mentally, physically and emotionally, aiming to prevent isolation, reduce risk of depression and increase physical mobility.

The aim of the service is to link the local community to the project, which in turn will reduce social isolation and mental health for many more people.
column7:
Oomph! Wellness, a social enterprise that provides exercise and activity solutions to the care sector and local community, are developing the “Oomph! On Demand” platform, which is due to go live in September 2020.

Sheltered housing have completed a digital audit with our tenants and have details of their digital connectivity and training requirements. Tenants will be supported to train to connect to the activities which will provide them with a varied options for their specific needs. People from the local community who are socially isolated will be encouraged to log on and join the activities. Various partners will be instrumental in promoting and linking the vulnerable into the project and its resources.
column8:
Immediately, training will be able to provided to those identified from the digital audit, plus publicity for tenants, partners and community will produced with a media campaign promoting the activities.

The Oomph! On Demand Platform is due to go live in September 2020. Users of the platform will be numerous staff and tenants at each Thurrock sheltered housing scheme. As soon as the platform is live, users will be supplied with a log-in which provides instant access to exercise and activity content, including hundreds of activity ideas and exercise moves, printable planners and newspapers, and live and recorded videos. Filters can be used to find content directly relevant to different service user interests and ability (for example, activities relating to nature or arts and culture, and exercise moves that are seated or standing). This content can be implemented into schemes instantly for immediate service user benefit.

The platform can be used directly with the service user, with videos that are created for different levels of ability, to support inactivity and isolation. To make it as accessible as possible, the platform can be accessed on desktop, tablet and mobile phone.

The staff at the schemes will also be supplied with log-in details and a deadline to complete their online e-learning courses. Upon completion of each course, users will be supplied with a certificate. They will then be able to deliver fun and varied exercise and activity sessions within schemes immediately after completion. These fully adaptable sessions will stimulate service users mentally, physically and / or emotionally, and provide opportunities for social interaction.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Data will be provided throughout the project to demonstrate usage of the platform.

Initial data will be provided from Oomph! to Thurrock following the e-learning training completion, including the number of staff and tenants who have completed the training and their feedback. This will provide quantitative and qualitative feedback on how useful they have found the e-learning aspect of the project.

Ongoing data on platform usage from a content perspective will be provided from Oomph! to Thurrock. This will demonstrate if users are utilising the exercise and activity content that is available to them, and equally which content types they are finding most useful – for example, whether they are browsing activity ideas, watching videos, printing exercise programs or downloading monthly activity planners.

Other data can be collected on the exercise and activity sessions run in schemes and the community as a result of the project, through periodic user surveys. These surveys will collect quantitative and qualitative data on number of sessions run, as well as service user case studies and feedback.
column13:
The project and resources will be made available for any other Councils and details of project will be displayed on the Council website.

Links with other Councils will be encouraged via our partners such as Public Health and the voluntary sector.

Information will also be shared with the South Essex Networking group which meet and shares ideas and good practise.
column14:
The platform enables content to be delivered to tenants and the community in 2 ways.
1) By the individual service user if they are comfortable at home
2) By one of the team visiting the service user or in a communal hall following social distancing guidance.

All sessions and activities on the platform can be downloaded as a PDF which then can be used individually or with a team member, either online or in a printed format.
column16: Guidance for promotion and delivery as needed/where possible and any additional support to promote to other local authorities.
column17: Thurrock Council work closely with the local community groups who use our facilities to connect the local vulnerable community. Housing work closely with Adult Social Care and Public Health to improve the quality of life for all of our residents.  We link into the voluntary sector and also local mental health groups such as Reach Out for Mental Health.  We have many partners who refer the vulnerable to our  service.

column2: Workplace Analytics
column4:
The Covid19 emergency has changed the way we work. It has meant wholesale remote working and the application of new collaborative tools. It has also brought new demands upon our staff to manage and work within these constraints as well as new opportunities. We believe we need to understand what has happened so we can optimise working methods and make the most of new opportunities in the future.
Wiltshire Council will apply the insight tools from Microsoft Workplace Analytics to understand the new, post Covid19 ways of working. The hours and patterns of working, the way people are collaborating and meeting, the tools they are using and how this links to levels of productivity. The intention is to optimise support for our staff to enable them to be more effective, efficient and supportive across Wiltshire communities during the next few years as we recover from the Covid emergency and emerge as a likely smaller but more focussed and effective organisation with even stronger links with partners. This project can be a pathfinder for local authorities providing an example and tools for their organisations.
column7:
During the past week, working with Microsoft, we have completed discovery of the tool and requirements to apply it. We have high level initial results from a test application of the tool which compared Jan/Feb 2020 with Mar/Apr 2020. This shows:
  • The working day span has increased – there is more working outside normal hours and 27% of employees have a working span of more than 50 hrs a week
  • Increased use of Teams and E-mail
  • The average meeting time has decreased and an average of 6.3 attendees per meeting
  • Of employee collaboration time 31% is external and 69% is internal
We know the highest external collaboration ‘tunnels’ The project discovery has also triggered consideration of how we can use the information to develop our organisation into an efficient ‘post covid’ public service. How might we be smaller but equally or more effective? Can we free up employees time to deliver more but also manage the stresses of greater independent working? How can collaborative work across Wiltshire communities be best achieved using technology tools or other means? How can we link insights in this area to developing our staff and focussing on our key organisational priorities? The use of workplace analytics in this project will look to identify data to address these questions and, almost certainly, it will trigger more questions.
column8:
The following is a high-level delivery approach to role out Workplace Analytics to achieve the desired outputs. Timescales are in weeks from receiving the funding:   Phase 1 – Prepare for the change – Set up and scoping – weeks 1-2
  • Receive the funding and purchase the workplace analytics tool/licences
  • Establish a project team with sponsors to focus on delivery
  • Confirm the questions/problems/issues that the organisation wishes to solve – for example, working time patterns (aligned to bid deliverables)
Phase 2 – Manage the change – Implementation – weeks 2-4
  • Build dashboards to answer the key questions established in phase 1
  • Establish a baseline of metrics to measure the benefits
  • Use the baseline to identify key areas or services to work with
  • Implement solutions to address areas of concern – this may include managers training on managing remote teams, implementing new technology such as M365 tools or providing skills and training where
Phase 3 – Cement & exploit the change – review of solutions – weeks 4 –8 and ongoing
  • Utilise the analytics package to re-run analytics and review solutions implemented against the baseline
  • Identify successes and areas for future development
  • Establish a long term roadmap for using analytics
  • Identify BAU support for tool
  • Pass over to support teams
Plus develop the support tools and examples of outside applicability so we can share these
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Data, insights and analytics can provide Wiltshire Council with metrics that can be captured to provide potential areas of focus or improvement. The tool can be applied within weeks and some historical data can be available immediately. Dependent upon that data we may start to focus on particular areas of work, perhaps such as managing those working excessive hours or at unusual times of the day to ensure they can be productive long term and are not prone to significant stress. Baseline information will also enable the project to focus on key themes, insights will drive the focus of the council’s people change team on specific services to implement solutions to address areas of concern. This will likely include:
  • Work/life balance – ensuring that staff are not working excessive hours which may lead to burnout, ill health or them leaving the organisation
  • Meeting frequency/attendance – ensuring that time in meetings is productive and outcomes are achieved
  • Collaboration (both internal and external) – ensuring that teams are working collaboratively to resolve customer issues. This has the additional benefit of increasing inclusion with staff working remotely
  • Service improvements – analytics could provide information that could be used to assist in services transforming. This is particularly important within a changing organisation and the impact COVID-19 recovery has on the organisation
  • Alignment of analytics with our workforce strategy and staff survey
column13:
Project Outputs (those specific to benefit for others)
  • Deployment of Workplace Analytics licences within a local authority – we can tell others how we configured the product and deployed it
  • Training of key council staff to use workplace analytics – we can share our training materials/approach
  • Reporting – we can share any dashboards we develop and tell others about key indicators we looked for to highlight, for instance patterns of working falling disproportionality across the organisation
  • Information Governance – we can show how we addressed any concerns or issues within the Wiltshire IG framework which is mature and effective
  • We are willing to share data sets with others who may apply the tool in the future and we are prepared to create and lead a community of interest amongst local authorities who utilise Workplace analytics or similar approaches
  • Bring some influence and pressure upon Microsoft GB to include this tool as part of standard licensing
column14: As this is deployed internally we do not anticipate any issues of digital exclusion.
column17: We have secured assurances from Microsoft to provide licences within budget and support for technical deployment.

column2: Utilising digital inclusion and engagement as catalyst for COVID-19 recovery
column4:
We will work with community partners to identify, understand and address the barriers to digital inclusion and engagement, in the context of a community devastated by the coronavirus pandemic. 

This will include engaging local people and supporting them to participate in an online festival, designed to address the impact of Covid-19 on their community and help promote its recovery. 

We will use these interactions to enable digital participation and stimulate creative outputs for inclusion in the festival. These artistic expressions, including inspiring stories and eulogies for local people, will form a virtual tribute and help galvanise an emergent online community.
column7: This is a new project but it aligns with a number of other projects and priorities in Brent, including areas focused on Covid 19 recovery and our London Borough of Culture 2020 programme. Pending decision by Brent’s Cabinet (20 July 2020), Brent will launch a Covid-19 support fund aimed at supporting residents impacted by Covid-19. This is intended to include support for digital inclusion comprising grant awards of devices, digital skills training and broadband connectivity for residents. Prior to lockdown, plans for a multi-cultural festival celebrating the Church End neighbourhood were near complete. It was expected to be week long program in May, taking place in the High Street, designed to explore the area’s history, people, culture and experiences. It would have included workshops, seminars, art displays and much more. Brent, including the Church End neighbourhood, has been particularly impacted by C-19 (Example1 & Example2).The need for a festival to support and bring the community together is more important than ever. This project includes work to help re-imagine elements of the festival online, alongside a core focus on developing and delivering a community engagement approach to ensure all residents have the digital skills, tools and confidence to be able to participate in the festival online.
column8:
Stage 1, w/c 03/08/2020, planning and procurement Finalise detailed plan for delivery of the project, including identifying and bringing on board consultants / practitioners to deliver:
  • Programme of sessions / workshops aimed at understanding the local digital landscape and developing an approach to up-skill and engage the digitally excluded
  • Online festival programme development, including securing offers from local partners and understanding their digital support needs. Expressions of interest from local partners include:
    • Video memorials, tributes and inspiring stories
    • Podcasting offer to local businesses and residents, including tools and skills to help promote their business / tell their stories
    • Workshops exploring and articulating the history of the area and cultural roots of the diverse communities living there
    • Online cooking classes highlighting signature dishes from local restaurants and local shops to source ingredients
  • Digital training for community partners and local people, including:
    • help to facilitate conversations about death, mourning and grief
    • help to tell stories about their community response to Covid-19
  • An exhibition brief, incorporating the commission of art by local artists and contributions by members of the community through artworks, photographs, videos, stories and eulogies; including plan for its curation
  • Content creation, through online workshops for local groups to help give them the artistic tools and inspiration to create outputs for the exhibition
  • Digital support, to create a dedicated online space for the exhibition to be accessed
Stage 2(a), 17/08/2020 - 27/09/20 Deliver programme of targeted support to up-skill and engage the digitally excluded to enable them to participate online (using participation at Church End online festival as an incentive). Stage 2 (b), w/c 17/08/2020, community work and content creation Schedule, market and support training, conversations and workshops in the community to lay the ground work for the creation of the exhibition. Stage 3, w/c 14/09/2020, curation Curation of exhibition, collaboration between curator and digital technician. Marketing of opening of exhibition, and planning of launch events with online ‘artist talks’, where people who submitted work can talk about their process. Stage 4, w/c 28/09/2020, opening of exhibition Exhibition shown online with accompanying events including artists talks, community discussions and consultation about how to use the exhibition to create a lasting memorial.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
A key measure of success will be a greater understanding of the barriers and solutions for enabling people to participate online. This will be achieved through testing targeted engagement approaches, gathering feedback, adapting and providing support. (In terms of KPIs success measures would include no. of local people engaged / digitally supported / participating at online festival.)

Some success indicators may be linked to take-up of digital inclusion elements of Brent’s Covid-19 Support Fund (Note: this is pending Cabinet decision, 20 July) by local people engaged by the project, which will be captured alongside qualitative data to gain richer insights about other barriers to, and potential incentives for, online participation.

Regular feedback from local people involved in the community will be captured throughout the project to help measure success and understand changes in feelings of community spirit, openness for difficult discussions, hopes for the future of the community and increased willingness to engage digitally.
column13:
In the context of lockdown, recovery and the emerging post-Covid landscape, it is clear that digital inclusion, participation and engagement will become an even greater priority for all local authorities.

To support this, we will produce a guide that will help other councils identify, understand and address the barriers to digital inclusion, participation and engagement. This will include the key learning points from the project, including suggested building blocks, tools and approaches for other authorities to use with their residents.

Brent has one of the most diverse populations in the UK - and it is anticipated that this strength will help ensure that the outputs from this project will be applicable to and reusable by all authorities.

We will also create a guide based on this project entitled: ‘supporting a grieving community’, that can be used by other councils to help support communities affected by the pandemic.
column14:
Identifying and supporting digitally excluded segments of the population to improve their online access capability is a core part of the project. The Church End neighbourhood is located on the border of two of Brent’s most digitally excluded Wards (Harlesden and Willesden Green: based on ongoing research by Brent’s commissioned partner ‘Citizens Online’); and it is the aim of the project to engage and digitally include these residents and community groups to help generate the shareable outputs detailed above.

As key stakeholders in this project, these cohorts are also anticipated to benefit from outputs from a linked project (the Brent Covid-19 Support Fund) as likely recipients of digital support including devices, digital skills training and broadband connectivity under that scheme.
column16: No additional MHCLG support is required
column17:
Strong links to the local community are essential to the success of this project. Our core project team (listed at 4.2) consist of Brent and VCS leads with well-developed relationships with key stakeholders and ties to the local area. This includes networks and forums of local businesses, residents and community groups that will support this project and realise its benefits at speed.

Brent is also a member of LOTI and, if successful in this funding application, could utilise this link and relationships with other London authorities to rapidly deploy the learning and outputs regionally.

column2: Digital exploration of the new UNESCO Black Country Global Geopark
column4:
The Black Country’s internationally significant geology has just been given UNESCO Global Geopark status recognising the unique role of these natural and cultural features in the industrial revolution and the growth of the region. Our project uses digital technology to share and interpret this world class heritage and to get more people active outdoors following long periods of Covid restrictions. 
The App will be developed to inspire people to explore and learn about these sites in an interactive way. Using augmented reality, mapping and trails, it engages people with nature, their health and the heritage they see around them. It’s free, scalable and easily update-able.
column7: Walsall Council (WMBC) has worked with the App Developer Sprytar/Love Exploring to scope the potential sites and trial themes for App roll out in Walsall.    WMBC has partnered with Dudley, Wolverhampton and Sandwell Councils to create the Black Country Geopark for which UNESCO Global Geopark status was announced on 10/7/2020. Each council plans to create Love Exploring App trails for 2 sites (2 x 4 councils = 8 sites) to promote the UNESCO status. Covid-19 restrictions have prevented the usual promotional events associated with this announcement, but the innovative, family-friendly nature of the App will provide a hook for publicity.   Using residents’ and visitors’ own smartphones to convey information in a fun and relevant way is ideal to interpret the international geological importance while reducing risks of Covid-19 transmission. Being free it will attract families and individuals facing financial pressures. During the pandemic Local Authorities have had to cease all events and activities to promote built and natural heritage and access to healthy activity.  Spytar https://loveexploring.co.uk/ has developed the App and has run successful trials in 3 local authorities.  The platform is stable and they have the capacity to roll this out further.    A lack of funding prevents us from commissioning the work needed to create the games and trails for our 8 Black Country Geopark sites.
column8:
The project will be delivered by the four Black Country Boroughs with input from app developers who have already trialled the delivery in Walsall. Data for mapping and drawing content on maps is already available through published media such as leaflets and web pages. The app users will be young people and families who are interested in augmented reality, school groups and teachers who want to learn more about their local area and its importance to regional development. It will also be of interest to elderly residents who may not have experienced many of the natural parks and green spaces across the Black country due to lack of information or lack of confidence. Elements of the app will also focus on the importance of green spaces for health, so people wanting to improve strength and fitness will also be targeted. The app will have multiple users and many different functions appealing to a wide audience. Local Authorities have had to put many plans on hold during the pandemic including summer health and fitness programme, environmental education programmes and the regional launch of the geopark. Part of the app also focuses on promoting events and activities and encourages schools’ involvement through inter-schools competition.  Key milestones: 
  • 3/8/2020 Funding confirmed / Press Release  
  • 4/8/2020 Procurement processes.  
  • 10/8/2020 Design and content developed and agreed. 
  • 24/8/2020 Augmented reality packages developed for x4 Local Authorities across the Geopark 
  • 7/9/2020 Develop Heritage/ health trails for x4 Local Authorities 
  • 21/9/2020 Launch programme, roll out of social media campaign and press.  
  • 30/9/2020 Review evaluation processes.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The Love Exploring App generates considerable data which can be used for measuring success and impact.  These will be demonstrated by: 
  • Completion of 8 App trails at Black Country geopark sites within the timescale, supports individuals/families to access nature / heritage, increase footfall to visitor sites and supports post-Covid economic growth.  
  • Number of downloads of the App trails, and demographic data, demonstrates increased engagement in health and well-being activity following long periods of social restrictions.  
  • Delivery and analytics of the social media campaign. A marketing tool kit, bespoke YouTube video and resources are provided by Spytar.  
  • A SurveyMonkey report provides qualitative feedback data to assess how much users knew about the geology before and after having used the trail.  
  • Measuring time spent doing the trail and link this to physical activity goals, physical and mental health benefits.  
  • Offer a webinar to other local authorities considering use of this technology and measure attendance at that.  
  • Longer term, measure uptake of the App or competitor technology by local authorities.
column13: The outputs are directly applicable and replicable by others outside of the current partnership. The developed app, which assembles information on 8 sites in 4 regions in the Black Country, will be easily accessible and can be used by interested parties thus widening its scope, appeal and impact. Promotion will be via our various Local Authority Networks, in which End Users and other Stakeholders will be kept up to date. Heritage / tourism organisations and Schools will be encouraged to use the App as a tool in creating new tours, access and interest in green spaces and environmental education. Beyond the project the App will be continuously updated with renewed information and data from existing and new partners.  These solutions will be used to further promote the Black Country and other Regions (as they join) as visitor/tourist destinations driving health outcomes and economic growth in a Covid reset environment.
column14: Mobile technology can play a central role in closing the digital divide. The development of the app will meet relevant disability standards and will be designed to appeal to all age groups and languages. The digitally excluded will be able to access the app through hardware available through community organisations and schools who have access to tablets and smart phones. Sharing smartphones within organisations will be possible to encourage more skills development in those residents who have not got access to appropriate hardware. This type of technology has a massive amount of interest and will spark greater interest from others not digitally engaged. We will be able to take a number of devices to any location and run pop-up sessions, for example, in local parks, libraries and community associations.
column16: We have a dedicated and multi–skilled team in house to project manage and existing arrangements in place to ensure procurement meets public contract regulations across the partnering LAs.  We would benefit from any support that MHCLG could provide in terms of additional marketing activity e.g. via the digital marketplace/Slack, to help us reach a wider audience and scale up the project for national replication. We would welcome the opportunity to engage with MHCLG social media and bulletin channels.
column17: The external supplier will provide the capacity and expertise to develop the app/software at speed. Alongside the support set out in 7.3, we will also make use of our existing Local Authority networks to reach a wider audience, our relationship with UNESCO for publicity and communication on an international scale and locally, our existing partnerships with schools, housing associations and community groups to enable access for the digitally excluded.

column2: Remote Inspection Toolkit
column4: This project offers the ability to create inspections, surveys, forms, and documents which include video or images from residents’ and landlords’ mobile devices. Local Authorities need to inspect properties internally and capture images: from HMO licenses, to the standard of temporary accommodation, repairs, maintenance, ASB etc. COVID-19 has made it difficult and unsafe for officers to access properties and gather evidence. This project will allow inspections to be completed remotely, simplify officer workflow, and ensure the safety of tenants, landlords, and inspection officers during a period of unknown certainty of infection rate. It will also help to reduce travel to multiple properties reducing infection risk and cost.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
The London Borough of Camden provided the initial support for the development of a low-code application platform to open and integrate a number of legacy IT systems. This now supports the creation of web-based systems, forms, offline mobile apps, and web service APIs

The open-source platform allows developers, and the less-technical, to quickly create and integrate user interfaces with drag and drop, all in the browser. Advanced users can also develop their own components, should it be required.

Ealing have partnered with Rapid Information Systems to create a property survey and Case Management System for Temporary Accommodation, and are looking to expand the platform to include an easy to use facility to add images and video from remote mobile devices, which could be added to any inspection, survey, app, or form made with the platform.

Some positive proof of concept has been done but more work is required to support as large a number of devices, operating systems, and video-conferencing clients as possible. Currently video from MS Teams, Zoom, Google Meet and other web-based video-calling can be incorporated. As well as recording the video and taking snapshots in the app, we want to add the ability to zoom and annotate them.
column8:
3 August to – 21 August: Development of new remote video and image capture component for the low-code platform (Managed by Ealing, work done by Rapid Information systems, testing by Ealing inspectors and broader LA community).

24 August to – 11 September: Development of integrated zoom and annotation for image capture component for the low-code platform (Managed by Ealing, work done by Rapid Information systems, testing by Ealing inspectors and broader LA community).

31 August to – 18 September: System testing (Managed by Ealing, work done by Rapid Information systems, Ealing inspectors, and broader LA community).

7 September – 30 September: Development of documentation including guides to create inspections, surveys, and forms and incorporate remote video and images, as well as operational guides for officers and IT departments, and guides for residents. (Ealing, Ealing inspectors, Rapid Information systems, broader LA community).
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Initial success would be for an officer to remotely conduct a temporary accommodation standards inspection, and gather satisfactory visual evidence.

To see impact of the project a Local Authority would expect to see engagement from tenants and landlords in the use of the system. If landlords are using the system on a regular basis it would suggest they are transparent about the quality of their properties and open to have support improving them. If a tenant is open to use the system to help complete an inspection it would help to build a relationship with the Local Authority during a time which would be harder due to restrictions in place.

Secondary success would be if other local authorities could use the toolkit to create their own inspections that could be done remotely.
column13:
The source code and distributable of the low-code platform, and remote video and image components will be made available, with documentation, for other councils to make use of.

To ensure future embedding of the outcomes by other councils Ealing Council and Rapid would engage with remote workshops to help re-users get the best outcomes from the product.
column14: These segments must not be left behind. Accessibility is handled in the usual fashion with WCAG via CSS, etc. and devices with accessibility options like screen readers, zoom, etc. Cheap devices with large screens are available, and user guides can be provided in an accessible format. The holder of the remote device is using existing software with its own accessibility.
column16: Would benefit from additional testing, documentation, and communication.
column17:
Councils (Private and Temporary Accommodation) that currently may face restrictions due to Covid and needs to complete inspections on a Property or Establishment that needs controls in place.

The Home Office – Need to inspect Temporary Accommodation and again may face restrictions due to Covid,

This will be deployed across 31 London LAs for testing which will test the robustness of all features and durability.

column2: Digital Youth Hub Solution
column4:
An online HUB for young people aged 14-25,  to address the complex challenges of employment, education and training emerging as a result of COVID-19.  

The resource will combine information and advice with career development, study skills and wellbeing. The hub will be a gateway for IAG services, education, training and employment in the travel to learn/work area.

The hub form part of a wider community engagement strategy to tackle the challenge of youth unemployment.  A distinctive feature will be provision of information and resources for parents and professionals working with young people, to ensure individuals have access to necessary support.
column8: We will meet the needs of the challenge by fusing some well-tested delivery with innovation including a digital careers advisor with a  particular focus on the post-C19 world.  Our timetable to deliver is: August 2020 Development server deployment - create a server environment to host a development version; install framework and applications; test Design brief buildout - work with client and designer to create website stylesheet to conform to new brand guidelines. September 2020 Populate site - commence site content build; determine access controls and user functions Client testing Comms plan to launch site Project officer in post Oct 2020 Shared information available Targeted participants  Targeted participants will fall within current statutory obligations held by the Children, Families and Education Directorate, and additionally supported by the unemployment support delivered within the Regeneration Directorate. IAG to support parents will be a key area as parents are recognised influencers.
