column2: Post COVID19 Community Engagement using Digital Wellbeing Assessment
column4:
A post COVID19 world we will see increased negative societal pressures such as poor mental health, domestic abuse, suicidal thoughts, homelessness, addiction and catastrophic financial hardship such as increased arrears as debtors recover from events like redundancy.

Using the TellJO digital wellbeing assessment means we can quickly identify and support people experiencing vulnerability in a cost-effective way and scalable way.

The outcome of using TellJO will see increased community engagement, better support and reduced cost in managing crisis such as homelessness, financial hardship and suicidal thoughts

Mid-Sussex and West Sussex Councils proposed use of TellJO is both innovative and ambitious.
column7: As proof of concept we conducted a free pilot with TellJO (www.telljo.org) the results highlighted how our residents are struggling with life difficulties, and gave us the opportunity to support them. Indications are that: 76% of respondents are highly vulnerable 47% used TellJO to write more information about their difficult circumstances 26% used TellJO to self-help via digital sign posting 97% of respondents were experiencing financial hardship 52% of respondents were experiencing suicidal thoughts The proof of concept test demonstrated that TellJO offered an opportunity to identify, engage and support our most vulnerable citizens. This is key as we recover from COVID19.  A report by the Citizens Advice in June 2020 suggests 1.3 million households have fallen behind on their council tax because of coronavirus.  It indicates that people caring for older relatives, key workers, those who have had to shield or are vulnerable to coronavirus (such as those with mental health issues), and disabled people have been hit particularly hard. The ability to contact those struggling with life difficulties before additional fees are added to their outstanding debts, such as Council Tax, is crucial so we can identify help and ensure that our collection remains ethical.
column8: Project start on 3rd August – Prior to start date: Procure TellJO services Assign vulnerability champions and conduct training Assign community engagement targets and milestones from those with Council Tax arrears: How many people with domestic abuse can we identify? How many people with suicidal thoughts can we identify? How many people with financial hardship can we identify? How many people with poor mental health can we identify? How many people with addictions can we identify? Increased collection tax collection. Create process for Vulnerability Champion’s to receive data, complete updates, blogs, workshares, signposting and safeguarding. Final brief with stakeholders, ensure knowledge of the project is shared to county partners, and other interested parties. 4Th August  Start digital wellbeing assessments to potentially vulnerable citizens (users). Give additional financial advice to help the person/family. Weekly digital meetings to iterate and evidence successes and failures. 30th August Target Milestone 1,000 outbound messages and 150 completed assessments Review community engagement targets and performance. Project review with Mid-Sussex, West Sussex and TellJO, assess potential further opportunities for use of wellbeing assessment, review results to date Review by how much TellJO has increased community engagement as well as reducing the arrears. 30th September Project debrief, review end target milestone of 3000 outbound messages and 450 completed assessments from those with Council Tax arrears. Review direct impact on community engagement: How many additional people with domestic abuse have been supported? How many additional people with suicidal thoughts have been supported? How many additional people with financial hardship have been supported? How many additional people with poor mental health have been supported? How many additional people with addictions have been supported? Increased council tax collection. Create project debrief pack and distribute to stakeholders Create project debrief webinar and advertise to stakeholders October: Completion of webinars
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
We will complete 450 post COVID19 digital wellbeing assessments, as well as reduce outstanding Council Tax for identified vulnerable residents.  By early identification of issues we can identify available help, without adding to the stress of the vulnerable person, and without the need we believe to pass the case to enforcement agents, so ensuring no additional unnecessary fees are added.  We also can signpost vulnerable residents to independent financial advice in advance of the introduction of Breathing Space in May 2021.

We will directly support an additional 450 vulnerable residents digitally, by safeguarding, or by direct intervention, who would not have otherwise been supported without the digital assessment.  This will also help the Council identify further assistance including financial help towards vulnerable residents owing Council Tax.

Also in the Money and Pensions Service ‘The Economic Impact of Debt Advice’ publication they stated that avoiding a mental health state saves £600 to £800 per person.  By identifying early we can bring additional savings.  Telljo has a clinical psychologist on the team, who has helped design the assessment to catch people before worsening debt and by association deteriating mental health occurs.
column13:
By creating a full debrief pack, we will highlight the positives and the learnings of the project.  We will also give outcomes on reducing Council Tax arrears by vulnerable residents.  There are over 300 Local Authorities in England collecting Council Tax.

We are aware that it is TellJO’s long term desire is to provide its digital assessments and dashboard to councils free of charge.

By increasing awareness and providing an evidence base, we want to showcase how all councils can use digital for community engagement and as society recovers from COVID19 we want to help vulnerable residents stay up to date with their Council Tax.

We will create a cost benefit analysis including the impact on Council Tax collection and make available to all councils via our debrief pack and webinars.
column14:
TellJO is for digitally included citizens, whoever, many people experiencing vulnerable circumstances prefer a digital approach as it removes the fear and judgement from communication by telephone or face to face.  This is particularly key as we all recover from COVID19, as residents recover from a once in a lifetime challenge and may face additional help with key issues such as paying key commitments like Council Tax.

If there are citizens that we are concerned about that have not responded to a TellJO wellbeing assessment, it could mean they are digitally excluded and we can make a different approach to them.

By using digital in the first instance, we can focus our attention on the responders to bespoke support, this will save costs and time.  We can then use the resources to signpost them to any available help, such as financial help as we recover from COVID19.
column16:
We would like MHCLG to help us with our cost benefit analysis, including reducing the outstanding Council Tax arrears, as we build the data set.

We would also like guidance on how best to communicate the project debrief at the end, with a list of the best stakeholders to engage at a national level, including with other Government Agencies such as the Department for Work and Pensions, other Local Authorities and Voluntary Agencies.
column17:
We would like to share the results with other stakeholders within Westminster, such as the Cabinet Office, DWP, HMRC, voluntary agencies, other Local Authority teams, Housing teams dealing with homelessness and minister dealing with safeguarding and vulnerability.

We believe by sharing the evidence base and data, we can encourage government departments and other Councils to quickly roll out and reduce costs, whilst supporting a greater number of people.

column1: London Borough of Barking and Dagenham
column2: A digital front door to access debt-related services, resources and post-covid support: This will encompass an improved information and support offer, self-serve and self-diagnosis tools and processes, the digitization of the Homes and Money (HAM) hub and a campaign to outreach to our most vulnerable residents.
column4:
Due to the pandemic, over 500,000 people nationwide have lost their jobs with a further 7.6 million jobs at risk. Households are building up dangerous levels of debt, with Barking & Dagenham, seeing a 136% increase in new claims including 20,000 more for universal credit and unemployment benefit in May alone.

Most Local Authorities provide support schemes but residents don't always know how to access them.

We’d like to create a centralised digital offer that through specially designed workflows funnels those at risk though to a clear set of digital tools and support to help reduce debt, improving financial well-being.
column8:
Currently, residents seeking help from the HAM hub require a face-to-face booked via phone or email. The actual web pages themselves include only generic information.   By the end of the 8 week project, LBBD will create a self-serve digital front door for the hub, encouraging residents to proactively improve their financial well-being by accessing a range of digital tools and resources that help identify risks and provide fast access to the varying levels of support needed.   We’ll refine the process so that instead of lengthy and complicated paper-based forms we provide digital tools such as:
  • Digital forms and case management
  • Digital Money Diary
  • Self-Diagnosis tools and decision trees
  • Improved information and guidance content clusters
  • Digital Balance Checkers
  Using these digital tools, residents will have quick and clear access to the support best suited to them, receiving additional relevant advice via automated email/text messaging tools. Complicated cases could then be allocated via a digital case-management system where each case can be triaged before being allocated to the relevant support team, ensuring that the most complex cases receive the correct interventions in a timely manner.   We will endeavour to repurpose existing tools and systems in new ways, delivering this project in an agile framework over four 2-week sprints.   Milestones:
  • Sprint 1 - Project mobilisation, discovery & research
  • Sprint 2 – User insight interviews/ service workshops, proof of concept
  • Sprint 3 - Real user testing, prototype refinement and iterations
  • Sprint 4 – Complete service pattern and MVPs (including working digital content clusters, process maps, tools and content live and ready to share)
  We’ll design and test the digital hub with residents, holding workshops with our partner local authorities Greenwich and Lambeth at the end of each week to share progress, interrogate outputs, code and prototypes for scale/iteration, consumption and reuse within their respective local authorities.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
As we define the hub’s new digital front door, we will do so with data insight and measurement in mind. There are a number of ways to demonstrate the success and impact of this project and one of these will be to develop a PowerBi report which monitors key data streams and indicators including;  
  • Engagement - Number of residents accessing debt and money support, information, guidance and tools digitally
  • Number (and complexity) of form submissions
  • A reduction in physical appointments requested
  • Number of new unemployment or universal credit cases
  • Number of residents with debt indicators e.g. falling into Council Tax or Housing rents arrears pre/post project
  • Number and duration of debt/arrears-related contact centre calls presented pre/post project, including enquiries diverted to online hub
  Resident surveys and online feedback will also be invaluable as will understanding the cumulative residents savings (i.e. additional income from benefits they didn't realise they were eligible for) alongside the cost/impact mitigation from the councils’ perspective (i.e. if debt increased at current levels, how much more staff would be needed).   Beyond the project we will also work with residents and community organizations to develop peer evaluations and engage in a conversation about money management within the borough.
column16: The cross-council team and plan are solid with all subject matter experts across 3 councils ready to contribute, so if we are able to secure this funding we do not anticipate needing any additional support from MHCLG to deliver the project. However, we are always happy to get in touch if we need to.

column2: Wigan Wallet (Community Assets Digital Wallet)
column4: This technology allows residents to rapidly prove entitlement to access council services without exchanging physical documents – both critical to Covid-19 management and safety. Councils can issue fully-auditable, digital, re-useable credentials, without entrusting data to third parties. Residents store them on their phones, and can use them in multiple contexts, presenting them to service providers, staff, retailers etc.; retaining full control over when, and with whom, they share. Only the minimum data required for a transaction is disclosed; no data flows to a central store. It requires only light-touch integration with existing IT and requires no changes to data safeguarding mechanisms.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7: The technology has already been deployed elsewhere as a working solution, and this project represents scaling. However, its use in the specific context of UK Local Government is still at POC stage (albeit with an instantly deployable technology ready to go). The project builds on the principles of the Wigan Deal (about which much has been written), and seeks to deploy a secure, privacy-enhancing mobile technology to further enhance service delivery, build local capacity and reduce bureaucracy for staff and residents. It is designed to digitize local assets and then to keep those new data assets in local hands. These principles apply to every council of course, and there is nothing about the technology itself which restricts its use to the Wigan context. Groundwork to establish that the technology would not interfere with other/existing data infrastructure projects has taken place, and a working prototype of the wallet is ready to be deployed immediately. In fact, there is immediate demand in the Covid-19 context: existing mechanisms of proving residence/entitlements to access services currently requires lengthy face-to-face contact and paper-document sharing (which risks Covid-19 transmission), in each scenario. The proposed solution builds on powerful privacy and encryption technology that is already being used by the US Department of Homeland Security. Details here: https://www.dhs.gov/sites/default/files/publications/CSD%202018%20Tech_Guide_Web%20Version_508.pdf . This formative work and proven use-case also involved user-testing and evaluation. The creation of an asset wallet in Wigan would be a unique, and homegrown, use of the technology.
column8:
The project is ready to go.   Phase 1: A prototype involving a small number of social workers (3/4) and a small number of staff at local foodbanks (3/4) in order to test the technology with the provision of foodbank vouchers to local residents. Residents who receive these digital foodbank vouchers will also need to download the app on to their phones. With foodbank demand rising during Covid-19, the system should reduce paperwork and face-to-face contact time, making interactions safer and more efficient.  
  • 10-20 users in total
  • Immediate verbal feedback
  • Timeframe - 2 weeks – beginning 3rd August
  Phase 2: The relatively small roll-out in phase 1 would be immediately followed by a roll-out of a digital ‘proof of residency’ credential to participating Wigan residents who have downloaded the app. This would allow residents to store a strong and re-useable proof of residence credential on their phones. Use cases have already arisen during Covid-19 as services have been re-opened to local residents but not others. With local lockdowns also likely, use-cases are likely to increase. In the longer term, there are countless efficiency savings to be made, as well as giving residents direct control over their council-issued entitlements.  
  • Build out to include NFC technology alongside QR codes
  • 100+ users (dependent on number who download the app)
  • Light integration with existing databases
  • Formal feedback process initiated with staff and users
  • Timeframe - 6 weeks (13th August integration with existing IT system, 7th September resident credentials issued)
  No changes to existing data infrastructures are required. The technology provider itself will never have sight of the contents of any data being shared between council, residents or service providers.. Audit trails of credential presentations are kept within the residents’ mobile phones.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success criteria:
  • Relative ease of integrating the technology with existing IT infrastructure and planned future data projects.
  • Satisfaction from frontline professionals and administrators in terms of ease of use
  • Proven reduction in time taken over bureaucratic processes for both staff and residents e.g. participating residents no longer need to fill out certain forms, go through lengthy credential checking processes, or collate paper documents
  • Level of interest and uptake by local residents
  • Extent of positive impact on the council’s ability to plan responses to Covid-19 in both the short and long-term
  • Initial sense of the value to local residents of providing the ability to build their own set of data assets without worrying about data-exploitation by third parties
  • Level of interest in neighbouring councils in adopting the technology
  A rapid-evaluation tool will be created to collect feedback from the three relevant user-groups:
  • System administrators
  • Service provider staff
  • Residents
  This tool will involve the collection of data that provides a meaningful measure of success in a very short time frame. In practice this will likely include both soft (qualitative) and hard data (e.g. number of users/transactions).
column13: If success criteria are met there are few barriers to scaling the technology beyond Wigan. Once the idea has been proven in the Wigan context it can be deployed elsewhere with ease. For example, the Greater Manchester Combined Authority have already been made aware of the planned project and have given backing in principle to these initial phases. We are aware that other similar-sounding technologies are available. The key difference here is that our proposed solution does not require whole-sale changes to existing data infrastructures, leaves data-assets in the hands of councils and residents, and can be deployed almost immediately to help address the challenges presented by Covid-19.
column16: No specific direct support has been identified beyond support with providing assurance to research and evaluation and opportunities to share learning and scaling potential.
column17: The key organisations needed to ensure that the project can be rapidly put into action are all already outlined in this proposal itself, and initial discussions around deployment have already, and are already, taking place.

column2: Beach Check Application
column4: “Beach Check” is a free to download smart phone application (Android and IOS) enabling visitors to beaches along the Bournemouth, Poole & Christchurch coast to make informed decisions. Using real time and up to date information, this application will allow users to avoid crowded sections of the coast; ensuring safe, socially distanced visits to award winning beaches and mitigating risks seen recently in the declaration of a major incident (June 25th 2020).
column7:
A smartphone application for Android and IOS operating systems has been developed so far. This frontend generates the visuals for the beach status check by gathering data from either the users accessing or being on the beach or rangers and lifeguards in their daily role. A predictive model is generated daily according to previous episodes with a coincidence in several considered aspects such as weather forecast, holidays, proximity to weekend or other relevant inputs added by rangers. This prediction is created manually through a .csv file generation. Several frontend updates rights have been created depending on who is sending them. To enable it, two apps have been developed: Beach Check in its version for beach visitors and Beach Check Pro for “admin” users. First ones can send their updates to the backend so the “super admin” will check them and decide whether to apply them. Second ones are “admin” users capable to generate an immediate update on the beach info shown by both apps. However, these processes are completely manually conducted. This project advocates to create an automatic process by which info can be real time updated and warnings and predictive models more accurate by using artificial intelligence algorithms. The second phase of this application, which funding is being sought for through this bid proposes to create an intelligent backend that will:
  1. Compare the current hourly situation to past historic daily patterns, to correlate a high number of characteristics and inform an accurate prediction.
  2. Gather data from several systems deployed throughout the seafront, detecting current beach occupation in real time, alerting visitors and enabling rangers and lifeguards to respond quickly to overcrowding.
  3. Colour accesses to the beach and inform users and Council’s employees about their situation and the need to close them or regulate their usage.
Using AI predictive functionality is built into the application; colouring the areas every day, taking the information from the historic data about similar day patterns, considering weather conditions, bank holidays or weekend days, or proximity to holidays. By comparing the future date to these patterns, the system can predict the status and colour the areas accordingly. By having a smart backend over the current manual configuration, the app will bring highly reliable and updated information to users and emergency responders, to help mitigate situations similar to those reported on Bournemouth beach in the national media on 25th June which led to the declaration of a Major Incident. To date, the following private sector organisations have had had involvement in this project: 1. X-tention 2. Xpertnest
column8:
Milestones and timescale:
  1. Technical plan generation. W1
  2. Project Kick off. W1
  3. Algorithm development W1-W2
  4. Current App API generation. W1-W2
  5. Backend Data bus set up. W2
  6. Beach camera interfacing W3
  7. CCTV systems checking – architecture and video streaming. W1-W3
  8. Footfall sensors planning – complementary to CCTV systems. W2 – W5
  9. Footfall sensors installation and setup. W5
  10. API generation for Footfall sensors and CCTV system. W4-W6
  11. Video analysis integration. W4
  12. Data gathering to Data bus. W6
  13. Integration with the frontend. W7
  14. First tests. W7
  15. UAT_ User acceptance Test. Iteration.W7-W8
  16. W8
To solve the challenge in this period, several work packages will be executed simultaneously. Concretely, the project can be divided into three main blocks that can be conducted in overlapping periods:
  1. Backend systems generation.
  2. Predictive modelling.
  3. CCTV and footfall analytics and integration.
To work on the three work packages, four separate companies will be hired. One to create the backend solution and the predictive model, the second one to supply footfall sensors, third one to create the video analysis process, and the fourth one to provide the connectivity to all the equipment scattered throughout the area. This way, timescale risks can be assessed and minimised.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Project success will be demonstrated through:
  1. An accurate prediction on expected beach density, based on historic data.
  2. A double-checked successful colour-coded front end thanks to the data gathering from the several interconnected subsystems.
  3. An accurate visitor detection by CCTV analytic and footfall detection.
  4. An increase on the app downloads and use.
  5. Successful management of social distancing
column13:
The backend solution, predictive algorithms, APIs generated to interoperate with CCTV and footfall sensors and ML algorithms can be reused by any Council not just in a sea front/beach density scenario, but in any other environment such as city centres, high streets, parking venues, etc.

Once the solution bundle will be delivered and hosted, tests by other councils can be generated to check its suitability to their use cases. As the algorithms are technology agnostic, other sensors can be added to the footfall detection systems, such as pollution, traffic, parking sensors, etc. so the technology can be fully adapted to any use case.

The BCP Council’s city brain is one of the key assets provided to the solution. However, agreements can be reached to share parts of this backend architecture or other council’s proprietary solutions can be used to the same purpose. Other companies supplying technologies and subsystems can be added to the bundle just by connecting to their APIs.
column14:
Warnings obtained by either the predictive models or the real time information will be available for seafront rangers and lifeguards to be able to advise beach users not using the smartphone application. Information panels can be placed on beaches accesses points e.g  digital signs on the Bournemouth Pier.

this predictive information can also be supplied to local media and be used by BCP Council communications teams on a daily basis to pre-inform potential beach users.
column16: No additional support will be needed from MHCLG.
column17: We have the necessary resource to deploy the initial project, but additional benefits could be realised by working with other organisations and charities to reduce traffic congestion and to ensure that the vulnerable residents are aware of a potential influx of tourists and are given the advice to stay safe.

column2: Emotional Wellbeing in the Community
column4:
The Suffolk COVID-19 Emotional Wellbeing Partnership will promote; evidenced, established and proven models as a primary prevention approach to emotional wellbeing.

By building on existing tried and tested support, ‘Emotional Wellbeing in the Community’ aims to increase the take up, and frequency of use, of both physical and online resources; appealing to all ages and increasing both reach and scale. Our programme of community engagement will start with schools and care homes, but then widen so that it is universal and accessible to all.
column7: The Emotional Wellbeing in the Community Task and Finish Group was set up in response to concerns over the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on emotional wellbeing. The group represents VCSE, and the Public and Private sectors. The group have extensively mapped all the available wellbeing offers and services across Suffolk, for people of all ages and backgrounds. Two existing emotional wellbeing programmes (one for workplaces across Suffolk, and the other schools in Ipswich) have been further developed by our group representatives. These offers have been effective in reducing workplace sickness and absenteeism, and emotional wellbeing in primary school children. The group plan to scale-up this approach and have already engaged with over 30 schools and 27 care homes within Suffolk who wish to be involved. In response to local emotional needs, a campaign has been developed and launched to engage and connect the wider population in the giving and receiving of gratitude. ‘Suffolk Says Thanks’ involves people of all ages developing and sharing messages, pictures and videos through a central hub, as well as working with traditional media such as radio and newspapers to encourage people to engage in wellbeing activities. https://www.suffolksaysthanks.com/
column8:
Outcome of call (w/c 3rd August 2020)
  • Project start (w/c 3rd August 2020)
  • Develop evaluation materials including questionnaire pack, semi-structured interview design and case study design.
  • Wellbeing resources based on the 5 ways to wellbeing designed for schools and care homes (w/c 3rd August 2020)
  • Video content designed for young people, based Suffolk’s Emotional Needs & Resources model (w/c17 August)
  • Develop communications plan for the Suffolk COVID-19 Communication Team Leads (county council, district and borough councils, VCSE, Local Economic Partnership, CCGs, schools).
  • Submit project to the University of Leeds Ethics Board for rapid review (usually 5-10 days; w/c 3rd August 2020)
  • Resources uploaded, packs for schools and care homes printed (24th August 2020)
  • Resources distributed across to schools and care homes, daily wellbeing planner sent to county wide newspapers to be distributed (w/c 31st August 2020)
  • Schools and care homes to be using the wellbeing resources and packs (w/c 31st August 2020).
  • Questionnaires and semi-structured interviews conducted with school children and teaching staff, care home residents and workers (w/c 21st & 28th September 2020).
  • Data will be collated to inform case studies about the engagement and impact of the resources in 2 care homes and 2 schools. Case studies will collate information from school children, parents and teachers for schools, and care home residents, their families, and staff (4 in total; 21st & 28th September 2020)
  • Data analysis (w/c 5th October 2020)
  • Project delivery finish (8th October 2020)
  • Project write up which will include the evaluation and lessons learnt from the project team (w/c 12th October 2020)
  • Project report submission (31st October 2020)
  • Throughout the project, weekly team meetings
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success and impact will be demonstrated by engagement and reach across Suffolk, including:
  • Number of children and young people across school year groups
  • Impact on wellbeing as identified through the questionnaires and semi-structured interviews, including Emotional Needs Audits
  • Case study examples of the engagement and use in schools and care homes
  • Intention to continue using the resources in the future (continued uptake will be assessed outside of this project at 3 months).
  • Findings and learnings to be shared with Local Government Association, Public Health England, Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, Department for Education, Department of Health and Social Care and national and regional mental health charities (e.g. Mind, Rethink Mental Illness).
  • Publication of project report and submission to a high impact peer reviewed journal.
column13:
The 5 Ways to Wellbeing programme is a national initiative, and the Suffolk’s Emotional Needs & Resources model is based on the internationally recognised Human Givens approach to mental health. Both resources will be unbranded and available for use by any council and partner organisations.

We are utilising existing partnerships such as Covid-19 workgroups, mental health networks and wellbeing partnerships to identify available resources and deliver this project. Other councils will also be regularly engaged in similar networks and will be able to use these resources to deliver similar activity in their area.

By sharing experiences, learning, outcomes, case studies and the project report with other councils through Local Government Association, Public Health England, Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government, Department for Education, Department of Health and Social Care and mental health charities.

We would seek to publish our work in a relevant journal.
column14:
The project information and resources will be provided in both paper and online formats to ensure packs can be accessed and support all people, including children and adults experiencing tech poverty. Printed material will also support people who have neurological conditions such as dyslexia in our community.

We will seek to ensure our information considers literacy reading age. In Suffolk, our minimum literacy reading age is 8 years. All of our resources will be tested by the Hemingway application and proofread by Suffolk County Council’s plain English expert.

Delivering this project in school and care home settings enables additional support to be accessed by pupils and care home residents from their teachers and carers.

Other local services such as the Suffolk Library Service will provide additional support to people who need, for instance, to access resources via computers and use of internet.
column16: The project team will not require support from MHCLG in the delivery of the project but would value its assistance in sharing the learning and outcomes of the project with other local government organisations, as mentioned previously in this application.
column17:
Head teacher associations, school’s coordination group and adult community services will support the promotion, marketing, and distribution of resources to schools and care homes.

District and Borough councils’ communities’ teams by engaging with local communities via newsletters and word of mouth.

Social media platforms, local print press and radio stations to promote the projects launch and to promote the work of those taking part with the aim of increasing engagement in the project.

VCSE sector will promote the project through their groups and communication routes.

column2: Utilising technology to maintain joint working in the community care professions during C-19
column4:
Traditionally, the delivery of social care has involved face to face contact between customers and professionals in the home. Sometimes, this contact involves more than one visit and more than one professional.

Covid-19 has required social care professionals to apply the principles of social distancing to their business processes in order to reduce the risk of transmission of the virus. Customers are also less willing to allow practitioners to enter their home.

This proposal seeks to utilise video technology to perform screen to screen assessments to manage these challenges.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
Some initial scoping of requirements to fulfil the project has been undertaken. This includes:
• Reviewing current BAU methods of working and identifying issues as a result of C-19.
• Assessing potential hardware requirements for both professionals and patients
• Identifying the project’s human resources, including a dedicated resource to assist in tasks such as process mapping and training.
• Identifying audio visual companies to procure, should the project progress.
• Seeking advice from ICT security on the most appropriate video conferencing software.
• Consideration of the social care pathways to be used in the project and also the most suitable patient group.
• Data protection advice regarding our proposal
• Development of materials to support customers in engaging in the process
• Engagement with social care professionals to scope viability and test concept

The challenge is to deliver a business process that traditionally would involve significant face to face contact, remotely, efficiently and safely that achieves the same quality outcomes as a face to face intervention would have done, by using technology instead. As well as reviewing our current BAU processes, we have begun early redesign of new ones. In the hope of getting a head start on the project, given the timescales
column8:
The team are confident that the project can be completed within timescale and in addition to the resources outlined in section 4.2 the following resources are also available.
• Social care practitioners skilled in delivery of the traditional process, who already have high levels of IT competency
• Customers who are willing to participate in a process that utilise technology
• A workforce who are flexible and quick to adapt to change
• Expert advice on information governance, communications and technical knowledge

The services that we would like to put in scope are

• Community Occupational Therapy assessment, leading to the provision of equipment that is handed over remotely.
• Community Occupational Therapy assessment, leading to DFG application and provision of structural alterations.
• Farmborough Court placement, leading to Community Rehab Team intervention and subsequent intervention from Reablement

Key Dates & Milestones
• Bid submission by 15 July 2020
• Project team meeting to review business processes and confirm processes in scope by 17 July 2020
• Panel virtual interview 23/24 July 2020
• Confirmation of final hardware requirements by 24 July 2020
• Selection notification w/c 3 August 2020
• Appointment of project support officer w/c 10 August 2020
• Finalize Information Governance requirements 31 July 2020
• Appointment of an AV company w/c 17 August 2020
• Receipt of hardware w/c 24 August 2020
• Hardware asset tagging & configuration w/c 31 August 2020
• Completion of evaluation plan 31 August 2020
• Sign off new business processes & working protocols w/c 17 August 2020
• Staff training for video conferencing w/c 24 August 2020
• Go Live with phase one video conferencing w/c 31 August 2020
• Completion of scripting for video training 31 August 2020
• AV training video completion w/c 21 September 2020
• Go live with full roll out video conferencing 1 October 2020
• Go live with full roll out of video training 14 September 2020
• Completion of evaluation exercise 30 October 2020
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The project will identify

• The number of customers enabled to engage in distanced assessment through access to loaned equipment
• The number of customers enabled to engage in distanced assessment in total
• The number of customers supported to access remote equipment handover
• The number of customers supported to access remote rehabilitation
• Improved timeliness of average length of time taken to complete an assessment by comparison to pre-covid timescales
• User satisfaction survey in respect of distanced assessment
• User satisfaction survey in respect of remote equipment handover
• Reduction in unit cost for assessment process
• Readiness to roll-out further beyond October 20 and transfer to other user groups etc.
column13:
Fiona Brown, Executive Director of Neighbourhoods, Sunderland City Council is a passionate advocate and influencer regionally and nationally with regards technological development and change. Fiona is Regional Chair of the North East branch of ADASS and the lead for Digital Solutions in Adult Social Care, our established networks across Councils will be utilised such as. Online support videos will be produced and available for professionals and customers. A targeted series of short webinars with regards our journey and lessons learned is planned to allow Councils direct access to our project team and importantly talk through implementation.
The project anticipates that if the pilot is successful, the principles of this new way of working can be applied to various council processes involving face to face contact between customers of the Council and Council officers, for example the Youth Offending Service. We of course would share our outcomes, like new processes and the use of the white label tutorial videos, with all councils and services.
column14:
Digital exclusion may relate to poverty, age and culture or skills and knowledge.

One of the project’s main aims is to loan hardware to customers who do not have it within their personal means to access this, whether this is a result of poverty or culture.

In addition the project is able to harness support from Sunderland Care and Support (SCAS), the Council’s long arm trading company and provider of a range of social care services including the provision of assistive technology, to teach new or existing technology users how to navigate around mobile devices such as mobile phones, tablet devices etc.

The use of video affords opportunities for sub-titling to ensure language is not a barrier.

Simply by engaging in the project, customers who are able to access equipment in the longer term, are more likely to become digitally engaged in order to support their overall health and wellbeing.
column16: Any opportunities for networking with other similar projects or useful organisations would be welcomed, in addition to objective scrutiny of the project proposals and expected outcomes.
column17:
Sunderland Care and Support (SCAS) will be a vital partner, supporting with procurement and distribution of equipment to customers, as well as providing learning opportunities for new technology users.

SCAS will also be able to support with on line promotion of the offer to customers.

Altogether Better Sunderland and local health partners will also be able to support in the promotion of the offer through on line and day to day working with patients.

The contracted provider to support the development of video materials is an essential partner.

column2: Post COVID-19 Open Shops Application
column4: We propose to create a mobile application that gives residents and visitors to the BCP Council area the confidence to use the conurbation’s retail centres in a considered and safe manner, by providing them with agile and up to date data regarding shop opening times and restrictions. This will enable successful and enjoyable shopping trips and avoid disappointing visits and a lack of trust in returning to the high street.
column7:
This potential project has already been embedded into COVID19 recovery working groups within the Council and external partners (mentioned in section 7.4) and has an established internal project working group with tourism, communications, economic development and smart place teams respectively. Internal programme management processes have been established and quotations have been received by three companies. Requirements have been described and written so there is the chance to benchmark different proposals. Essentially those requirements are describing:
  • How commerce and hospitality venues owners will describe their opening conditions, easily, digitally and flexible enough to inform different characteristics.
  • Frontend by which users and visitors will be able to check them.
  • Online real time location to suggest open venues to shop locally.
  • Search tools to find a commerce or hospitality venue filtering by sector, area, schedule, etc.
column8:
We have been conducting several meetings with the company potentially developing the app (subjected to the procurement process) to make sure the requirements can be met within the expected period. There will be three main work packages that will be developed at the same time 1) Android APP 2) IOS app 3) Backend platform. By splitting the project there will be three different teams working on the three WP to ensure the work is done. Follow up working group meetings will be done weekly to check that the GANTT timescales are met properly. End users are mainly commerce and hospitality venues owners. However, as they might be part of an aggregation stakeholders, several entities have been listed in 7.4 with which we will create partnership agreements to promote the APP usage among the local businesses. A communications campaign will create engagement and buy to the application for use by the public. The delivery road map and timescale is designed as it follows:
  1. Kick off meeting. Consortium agreements and Project Management briefing. W1
  2. Basic Requirements gathering from local commerce and venues. Meetings with clusters and associations to gather their thoughts and suggestions.
  3. Communication look and feel alignment with BCP Council. Communications meeting. W3-4
  4. APP coding and development. W2-W5
  5. Wireframes check out: W5
  6. UAT (User Acceptance Test): W6
  7. Local commerce and Venues engagement process: W2-W8 and beyond the app launch.
  8. Comms campaign launch: W7
  9. First user tests evaluation W7
  10. Final commercial launch: W8
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
There are several KPIs showing success and impact on the app usage.
  1. Engagement with local businesses. Updated information by a large number of them will be critical to measure the app success and its usability.
  2. Users usage: number of downloads and logins from local users and visitors.
  3. Number of searches: number of searched businesses per user and period of time.
  4. An overall figure of increase in sales and consumption's locally.
column13:
The app can be extended geographically. Initially just BCP area businesses will be entitled to be part of the application, but there is no prevention to set up a wider area (e.g. cross-Dorset area) in which commerce would be allowed to upload and update their information.

The backend information payload is light enough to allow a geographical extension on the app usage. Additionally, the SDK (Software Development Kit) of the application can be transferred to another Council so that it can deploy it in another region, enabling a further implementation and usage in its jurisdiction.
column14:
Accessibility guidance will be observed in the app development. That means that upper sized fonts, colouring and maps zoom will be implemented.

In a future evolution, voice recognition for users and voice directions can be implemented.
column16: The explicit backing from MHCLG would be needed to get a major support from local businesses. The application and the communications campaign would show the MHCLG logo and a disclosure stating that the project is possible thanks to its support.
column17: BCP Council has close links with the conurbations three Business Improvement Districts and the Bournemouth Chamber of Trade and Commerce, all whom are representing and supporting businesses to grow and thrive during these difficult times. These organisations will be able to quickly engage, gain support and create buy in to the Open Shops platform on our behalf and alongside our own BCP communications on the project. As a Council, we are closely linked with traders’ associations within the BCP area, hospitality associations and tourism boards. Through internal officer involvement on the Open Shops project on these additional boards; we are able to directly engage with a broad range of businesses to create a vibrant business user ecosystem on our  proposed app.

column2: COVID-19 Community Support Network
column4:
At Stoke-on-Trent City council this would be delivered as an improvement project through our new internal A.D.A.P.T Continuous Improvement Network.  
We propose to link the vulnerable persons database built in house to the Communities ICT platform, roll out and setup of the MyStoke mobile app and investigate and pilot technologies such as a Chatbot and Alexa Skill
When a vulnerable person calls and is in need of assistance, we can use technology to take the call, manage the process and workflow of the request, build in the functionality of the mobile app to send a push notification to the person to reassure them that we have received the request and are dealing with their enquiry with minimal human intervention.
This introduces new ways of working with vulnerable people and reduces operational demand.

The following elements are built

Prototype in place

In Development

column2: Data and Reporting Test and Trace
column4: Staffordshire County Council are currently collating COVID-19 Test and Trace data from various sources and stored within a spread sheet which is then being processed by by relevant teams.  There is a need to ensure a real time dashboard view is available to senior leaders responsible for our COVID response.  We aim to develop a database which will streamline processes and negate the need for manual intervention.  The solution will be used by all districts and boroughs within Staffordshire and can be written is such a way to ensure it can be shared with other LAs
column7: Local authorities have had to respond quickly to the challenge of managing the Test and Trace program.  At Staffordshire we have coordinated different cells depending on the location of the incident and each cell has developed their own processes.  More recently, we have pulled all these processes together to ensure consistency and have all information recorded in one excel spreadsheet.  There is a manual process to pull information necessary for a dashboard view for management reporting.  Due to the nature of the project this needs to be done at regular intervals and is currently time consuming for key personnel.
column8:
SCC would like to develop a database to manage this program which will automate many of the functions, freeing up staff to respond to the incidents in a timely manner.  This can be achieved by building in workflow and the automatic generation of notifications to LA colleagues and also the districts and boroughs within Staffordshire who we are working closely with.

Our in house ICT developers are familiar with current processes, the data and the scope of what we want to achieve and are ready to build a bespoke database that could be transferred to other local authorities for their own use.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
Success can be evidenced by the production of a streamlined database that can be transported to other local authorities for their own use and further development.

It will have robust data capture and timely workflows to ensure the relevant authorised individuals have real time access to new and on-going COVID incidents.  It will also self generate comprehensive dashboards for management reporting in real time.
column13:
The database and workflows will be written in such a way to ensure the code can be used by other LAs

We will be working with Stoke-on-Trent City Council on this project who will be able to test portability and will be able to provide input from a unitary authority point of view.

We are also sharing practices with our district and borough councils.

The application will be written with standard technologies, utilising a SQL backend and .Net Web application front end. This would be portable, and avoids the use of third party libraries that would generate license implications.
column14:
We have developed a web online form and have built into our process an alternative route for sites to provide the same information via a phone call.

The application will be available via a URL. Individuals who need to utilise the service will be contacting the council via phone, email or letter, and the Council and partners would then use the system to track and record the relevant information. The system will be written to be as accessible compliant as possible to take into account staff members with specific needs, and will be responsive.
column16: We would appreciate any assistance in ensuring that third parties, and other government bodies are aware of the package, and know that it is available for them along with support.
column17: Third parties may be brought online to facilitate the process based on specific skill sets. However the utilisation of these resources would be specific to the demands at the time, and would be carefully monitored to ensure that the best return on investment was being achieved.

column2: Businesses and Community – Services Impact Assessment Tool
column4: Businesses and community assets have been impacted by COVID-19. We aim to gather data on what services were present, who was using them and how busy they were before the pandemic and compare this with now. We will combine automatic data harvesting from online sources, potentially including crowd sourcing with surveys and publicly available data from companies house into a single location. This will then help inform our economic recovery work. Additionally we will look to publish this information to the community to provide a single home for data on local businesses to help the community shop locally. This data will then feed our ongoing efforts to support business by helping us to target those that could have a digital offer but don’t.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7:
No specific work has been done, we will use elements of other projects to bootstrap this project. 
  • Development of interactive maps to display data, currently this can draw data from flat files and our streets management system.
  • Business data, we have reference data from FAME, our business rates system and business grants schemes.
  • Business surveys, we have already conducted some surveys of businesses and the impact COVID has had on them. 
  • Dashboarding, we have previously developed interactive dashboards on PowerBI that display geo-spatial information. 
  • Data warehouse, we have linked our online portal to a data warehouse which allows us to take forms data and automatically processes it. 
  • https://filaindiana.it/, we would look to use this as a learning example of how crowdsourcing and service scaling can be done quickly.
  • We have previously worked with Unboxed to provide service design and user research support around citizen engagement and have an open support contract with them.
column8:
This project would combine an agile build process for the data collection tool, occurring over 3 sprints and intelligence gathering and usage of the tool to create a high quality data environment and perform analysis.   Week 1  - Agree the goals of the development and technical standards and data formats. Finalise development contract and ways of working.  Output: Product roadmap and standards document.   Week 2
  • Sprint 1: Company verification, find the company online, on social media and in companies house.
  • Internal data collation to create an input data set for use with testing the tool and agreeing on output data specifications
  Week 3
  • Sprint 1 output: Tool that can verify company details and pull back basic public information.
  Week 4 
  • Sprint 2: General data retrieval, retrieve basic information such as opening times, services offered and presence of an ecommerce option from public information.
  • Working with recovery leads and town highstreets projects to find out key questions they would like to have analysed. Assess the role of crowdsourcing vs company managed updates getting high quality data. 
  Week 5
  • Sprint 2 output: Deploy and test tool within our environment to pull back enriched data.
  Week 6
  • Sprint 3: Historic and COVID data retrieval, use entity extraction to confirm that COVID measures in place in the business. Usage of internet cache and archives to obtain pre-COVID versions of the same general information.
  • Conduct a survey of either business owners or crowdsourcing approach to provide a method to verify tool gathered data.
  Week 7
  • Sprint 3 output: Final version of the tool run in HDC and partner data.
  Week 8
  • Intelligence reporting and deep dive sharing sessions. 
  Week notes and other sharing would occur during the project as well. Code would be published to a public repo at the end of the project.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: The project's success would be in allowing us to show on a map different businesses and community assets present in an area and let us filter and manipulate what is displayed based on a set of parameters. This could then be overlaid with population density data that allows us to understand how the services that are available to our residents have changed pre and post COVID and continue to change.    Ideally it will also allow us to understand how different risk reduction working practices are being implemented and hence see the links with COVID related data from Public Health England.   The impact of the project will be felt by allowing us to tailor our economic recovery strategy to better ensure that all of our community continues to be served by local businesses and community services that will deliver what they need to thrive in a post COVID landscape. We will also be able to better target our support to local businesses in helping them become digitally enabled and hence survive in a post COVID landscape which will protect local jobs and help communities thrive.
column12: I am on leave the week prior to 3rd of August so would likely start the week afterwards in order to give me time to finish setting up the project.
column13:
As part of the project we would publish 4 different outputs which could be re-used.
  • Week notes / show & tells – These would cover the lessons we are learning as we go along, specifically looking at the issues with combining the different data sets and our findings around data accuracy and completeness. This would help other councils understand how to assess their current data holdings.
  • Intelligence papers – Based on the data gathered we would create intelligence papers that present our conclusions and analyze of the data, we would also where useful provide anonymized data sets so others can perform their own analysis. We would include web-ex based presentations and discussion sessions on this where we could help others understand the lessons learnt and final intelligence product. This would allow other councils to directly apply our impact analysis.
  • Code – We would release our developed code and infrastructure config scripts in a public repo so that others can use it as the basis for their own solutions. We would run a technical deep dive session to brief anyone interested in this when we had a stable live release.
  • Data - During the project we would ask our partners to create initial lists of businesses and community organisations in their area. We would combine this with our own master data for the initial usage of the data harvesting tool and pass the resulting enriched data sets back to our partners.
