column16: Access to economic models developed within central government that can predict job losses / business viability in a post COVID world when fed with usage data we are gathering.  Communication support to help tell other LAs what we are doing and support them in adopting it.
column17: Someone with experience developing economic impact models, so able to take the data we have will obtain and develop forecasts from it showing future trends including analysis of an impact of a second wave. Someone able to take CCTV feed, run them through video analysis to generate a rough people count in order to provide real time data on how busy locations are. Someone with AWS -> Azure experience who could take the https://filaindiana.it/ solution and move it to a hardware platform we support.

column2: Establish a collaborative playbook for digital inclusion
column4:
The lockdown has highlighted the chronic lack of digital capability in certain communities across the UK. Individuals lacking access to kit, connectivity and skills are isolated, while many SMEs lack the know-how to trade successfully. We intend to tackle real world challenges faced by users in the London Borough of Croydon and the coastal town of Eastbourne, and publish a comprehensive playbook that others can contribute to.

This project is different from past digital literacy projects in 3 crucial ways: 1) we’re addressing the needs of multiple audiences in one go, so our guidance will have greater uses 2) we will test and iterate as we go 3) the lockdown has introduced complex social distancing challenges, and it’s vital that a publicly funded project addresses them now, so the learnings can be widely shared.
column6: Other (please specify in your answer to the next question)
column7:
Our project’s workstreams are at different stages - laptop and device loans are ready to scale, learning sessions have working solutions, and connectivity is at inception. Aspects of this project have been previously prototyped in Croydon and Eastbourne.

Age UK Croydon

  • Age UK Croydon is a charity enabling older people in Croydon to live well, healthily and independently.
  • As lockdown started we moved almost all our services online. We're now offering Healthier Lifestyles Exercise and Nutrition classes via the internet, advice appointments via Zoom and Whatsapp, and starting to connect our clients to other services.  We've also worked with a large number of new clients on food poverty, connecting them with online shopping, food banks and other solutions online. Many of our clients have some kind of digital capability, but  their confidence is low. Other clients need support getting the basic technology and connectivity to enable them to access basic services.

TechResort

  • TechResort is a Community Interest Company founded in 2013 in Eastbourne, East Sussex. Its CIC objectives are to share tech skills as widely as possible in the town, with an emphasis on tackling inequality and diversifying the local economy.
  • Laptop loan scheme. TechResort’s 20 existing laptops were loaned during lockdown to those most in need. The work involved assessing the suitability of the machines for use, clearing down and equipping with necessary software and virus protections, and establishing easy to follow terms of use
  • Socially distanced learning station. Using inexpensive and readily available kit (standard laptops, monitors, peripherals and long cables), TechResort has created the computer equivalent of a dual control car, allowing an educator to guide a user to complete web-based and locally installed tasks.  This is readily replicable without specialist skills, training, equipment or software
column8:
We intend to tackle the challenges of getting online in two significantly different locations with a different audience in each. This should result in a rich series of actionable findings that will have immediate relevance for local authorities across the UK. Our project will be led by Croydon Council. Day to day activities will be run by Age UK Croydon in Croydon, and TechResort CIC in Eastbourne. Age UK Croydon will be working with its existing users, elderly and isolated people across the borough. TechResort has worked with many different users over its 7 year history. For this project, it will focus efforts on offering digital skills to the town’s SMEs & sole traders who are struggling to use online tools effectively. Both Age UK Croydon and TechResort are rooted in their communities and respond directly to their needs. Since lockdown, they have received many urgent and desperate emails and calls, and we understand the same is happening in towns and cities across the UK. We propose an 8 week project and are ready to start immediately:
  • 4 distinct but related work streams, structured into 4 x 2 week sprints, following agile and iterative methods set out in the GDS service manual
  • Stream 1 considers the device and software needs of each audience. It will suggest the best setup for users with limited knowledge and budget, with a minimum standard and working practices for donated equipment
  • Stream 2 tackles connectivity issues, including affordability, maintenance and frequently confusing offers from broadband providers
  • Stream 3 will cover skills learning sessions with users, and we expect three models to be tried: 1) Face to face help in the homes and sheltered accommodation of vulnerable people 2) a “socially distanced dual control set up” in an easily accessible location 3) online tuition for groups using tools such as Zoom or Google Meet
  • Stream 4 considers the format of the Playbook itself, a comprehensive set of findings published on a collaborative platform. The budget does not provide for a bespoke solution, so we will be looking for something off the shelf where content can be easily exported to future products
We have a lot of ground to cover in 8 weeks, so will prioritise streams 3 and 4. Lockdown necessitates a complete rethink of how digital literacy skills are shared, so this is vital. A space for all comers to share valuable insights is also important.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: This project is ambitious, but necessary. To tackle digital inclusion issues effectively, we need to provide meaningful learning sessions with users, together with device, software and connectivity recommendations. Usefully, Age UK Croydon and TechResort already have established networks of users so we are able to hit the ground running. Our key metric is the number of meaningful sessions delivered to users. We’re looking to make a significant difference to their internet confidence and help them towards independent learning using other tools. The project will count and classify device, software and connectivity issues, and attempt solutions. Even if we’re not able to make very much progress, our data should be invaluable to future sessions and policy makers. We also intend to encourage learner led subject matter. Once a user understands the basics of internet browsing, we’ll help them to achieve their own goals rather than set a curriculum.  This is more likely to lead to them engaging with the subject matter more confidently. The fact that we’re doing this work in lockdown will have a meaningful impact. Both Age UK Croydon and TechResort have heard from countless individuals and SMEs who are desperate. Small and targeted efforts should make a significant difference to them, and the Playbook will mean our learnings will benefit other communities in the UK.
column13: As signatories of the Local Digital Declaration, Croydon Council embraces the need for open, transparent and collaborative working.  In its three successful MHCLG Local Digital Fund bids (Drupal code sharing Discovery and Alpha, Adult Social Care User Research Discovery) we have used many collaborative channels - the Croydon.digital blog, the social media accounts of our team, and Local Digital Slack. We intend to lever all of these outlets, and add the reach of our partners. Age UK Croydon has 130 “brand partners” across England with active ongoing conversations around these issues. TechResort is well established on social media, and has a strong digital education following. The Playbook we create will be open to all, and easy to join. We will focus on major easily replicable set ups, free and low cost software, and readily transferable skills. We believe the right mix of relevant and comprehensive content will be reused by councils and other bodies across the UK.
column14: The project is aimed squarely at digital excluded segments of the population. During lockdown Age UK Croydon and TechResort have received significant numbers of enquiries from isolated individuals, and SMEs in need of help, so will be able to offer targeted support throughout. Much of the available learning content is, itself, online and this can put it out of the reach of people with a lack of confidence with digital skills. Our learning formats will seek to address this by giving excluded people the training and confidence they need to approach additional materials.
column16:
As with other Croydon Local Digital Fund projects, we would benefit from:
  • Publicity for the project to encourage collaborators from other councils
  • Help and guidance from an MHCLG contact
column17:
We intend to seek the help of two types of organisations:
  • On affordability of devices and connectivity, we believe local Credit Unions have a part to play. We will contact East Sussex Credit Union and the Croydon, Merton and Sutton Credit Union early in the project. Citizens Advice may also be able to offer guidance 
  • Local connectivity providers. We’re unlikely to make very much headway with UK wide broadband providers in such a short timeframe, but local providers such as CloudConnx in Eastbourne may be able to help. TechResort is already supported by CloudConnx, and we will aim to find similar providers in Croydon

column2: Digital Collaboration Platform with Parish Councils
column4: Working in a three tier environment can have its challenges.  However for South Staffordshire these challenges are turned into opportunities.  By focusing on the best possible outcomes for our communities we work closely with county and our parishes, and these relationships were born out during the lockdown where we worked seamlessly together to ensure our residents were safe.  South Staffordshire’s proposal is to further enhance our communication mechanisms through a shared digital platform.
column7: Initial scoping of the project has been undertaken to establish the Vision, Objectives and potential Benefits from establishing a Digital Collaboration Platform for the District. To further enhance three tier working South Staffordshire Council is working with Staffordshire County Council, and the 27 parishes in the District, to develop our approach to Locality working.  A key aspect of this work are locality member forums.  These forums are held in each of the five localities where the three tier of members meet twice annually to collaborate on relevant community issues. The onset of Covid 19 has not halted our progress in strengthening our three tier working in fact it has aided its development, and we will continue to host these forums virtually.  However the District Council recognises that whilst at County, and District level we have had the appropriate digital tools to continue delivering our services to our communities, the access to digital tools has presented more of a challenge to our parishes, who have managed to stay connected to their communities despite the lack of digital tools and support.  This proposal aims to provide a common platform for communication across the three tiers, bringing specifically parish accessibility to the same level as District and County. The Council's Corporate Leadership team, including Dave Heywood the Chief Executive and Cabinet, including Councillor Brian Edwards MBE the Leader of the Council, have discussed and considered the benefits of a Digital Collaboration Platform for the District and are fully supportive of the initiative. Potential Implementation Partners have been engaged and are extremely keen to support the District of South Staffordshire with this highly innovative idea, including discussing potential technical delivery options. The project, including the submission of this application, has been considered at the Council's COVID-19 Recovery Programme Board gaining full support. A project board is in the process of being established for the delivery of the Digital Collaboration Platform, reporting to the COVID-19 Recovery Programme Board on a weekly basis.
column8:
Critical to the success of this project will be the engagement and commitment of the key stakeholders, the Parish Councils. This project will focus on establishing a robust set of principles that promote collaboration and sharing of information across the District. Parish Council contributions are essential so the engagement on this activity has already started and will run for the duration of the project. These discussions will establish a common set of data sharing, data retention and data disposal rules which will be reflected in the building of the Digital Collaboration Platform. An Implementation Partner will be engaged through the Project Team to provide a digital solution that enables the information sharing and collaboration requirements agreed by the Parish Councils to be met. The approach will be iterative, with a proof of concept with a couple of Parishes, a Pilot with a few more Parishes, followed by rollout across all Parish Councils in the District. All phases of delivery will be supported by adoption activities to maximise the potential for success, with regular lessons learnt and feedback opportunities to continually improve and refine the delivery approach as well as the final version of the Digital Collaboration Platform. The key milestones for the delivery of the project are:
  • Mobilisation (activity to September due to Parish Council availability during August)
  • Design (September to November due to Parish Council meeting schedules to gain agreement to the information sharing and collaboration principles)
  • Proof of Concept (including build, 2 weeks)
  • Pilot (2 weeks)
  • Rollout (4 weeks)
From Proof of Concept to completing the Rollout, the project is 8 weeks. The engagement with Parish Councils and their contribution to the information sharing and collaboration principles are key to the success of this project. Those aspects of the project cannot be shortcut and must be respectful of Parish Council governance and meeting cycles.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
The real benefits of this project will be seen in the longer term and will be reflected in the quality, timeliness and effectiveness of the services delivered by the councils to the residents and businesses in the South Staffordshire district. A district of local Parish and District council's acting in unison, delivering a joined up seamless service, irrespective of where the request originated.

In the short to medium term the benefits of this project will be experienced by both the Parish and District Councils with regards our response to COVID-19; the modernisation and standardisation of district digital infrastructure; enhancing business continuity arrangements; and the fostering of sharing good practice, knowledge and expertise between all South Staffordshire councils.

Initially the Digital Collaboration Platform will witness the forming of stronger bonds between the South Staffordshire District councils with a common goal of delivering this project. This will strengthen the very healthy locality and 3 tier working that already exists in South Staffordshire and which lies at the heart of the Council Plan.
column13: Sharing our experiences and working in partnership is at the heart of what we do.  Following initial piloting we would utilise the opportunity to share our work through various Local Government networks and undertake webinar/demonstration with teams both in terms of the application of the project and the outcomes/benefits on the ground.
column14: South Staffordshire is a rural district with an ageing population.  We recognise that our 27 parishes present the closest touch point to our communities and that their village halls offer a variety of local services to our communities. We also recognise that having digital access / or being digitally proficient and confident, presents challenges for some sectors of our communities.  For example Covid 19 has made us aware of the challenges faced for some in being able to do online food shops, and our proposal aims to work with these sectors of our communities through providing support at parish level.
column16:
The critical activity for this project is the definition of the information sharing and collaboration principles. Not only do each Parish Council have to be in agreement with the principles, but they also have to be compliant with GDPR regulations.

Guidance from MHCLG on the best approach on how to establish a common set of agreed information sharing and collaboration principles would help reduce a significant project risk. Likewise, access to Data Protection and GDPR expertise to assist in resolving any queries that may arise in the area.

Access to legal advice and services to critically assess the information sharing agreements that are likely to be developed as part of this project.
column17:
Initial discussions with the potential Implementation Partner has indicated that Microsoft technologies may form a part of the technical solution for the Digital Collaboration Platform. Access to Microsoft expertise to assist with options appraisals, solution designs and product capabilities would potentially speed up the technical delivery.

Access to expertise at the National Cyber Security Centre to support with the design and assessment of the Digital Collaboration Platform from a cyber risk perspective would provide confidence to Parish Councils when requested to sign off the solution and to sign up to using.

column2: Maintaining a Street Champion Network.
column4: Communities have come together during COVID-19 to support each other, as a result more people have joined existing networks or created new ones to provide a community response and keep neighbours safe. To ensure these networks are maintained and data is held safely and securely we seek to work with Groop, a community managment platform to provide the infrastucture for groups across Gloucester to remain in touch and connected in the event of further outbreaks or local emergencies.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7: During Covid19 an extensive network of Community and Street Champions have stepped forward to serve neighbours in their street with tasks such as collecting prescriptions and delivering food parcels. This includes over 100 people in Barton and Tredworth ward, and approximately 200 in Longlevens and Elmbridge. These people have played a critical response role throughout the pandemic. We are aware of the possibility of a second wave of COVID19 is highly possible in the winter months, when activities move back inside. With this in mind we are seeking to retain the networks that have evolved and create a online communication groups using a secure data management platform call Groop https://groop.com/ We have entered a discovery phase, testing the platform with local community groups and newly formed mutual aid networks. This has been received well, however, leaders have expressed a desire for a 2 year commitment to make use of the platform sustainable. Introductory sessions have been held and subject to funding approval onboarding with community groups would commence over the summer. Gloucester Community Building collective would administer the platform over the duration and provide further onboarding for new groups once the system has been established.
column8: Discovery Phase. (June and July 2020) The partnership has already entered a discovery phase, as it has been seeking to provide a digital solution to communication and data management in response to supporting existing and newly formed groups that have been involved in the Covid19 response. A limited number of community groups and associations have seen demonstrations of the Groop Community Management software. Groop offers a secure online platform that enables groups and volunteers to communicate with each other, stay compliant and meet safeguarding standards. The platform provides facilities to arrange physical and virtual meeting through a secure video conferencing facility. The platform is accompanies with a mobile app that allows instance communication between group members. Trial phase and set up (July and August 2020) A small number of groups are trailing the platform. Feedback has been positive, although a longer term commitment is required in order to embed use of platform with residents and group leaders. The intention is to trial the system during July and August to secure advocates. We then see to tailor the system to meet the needs of groups based on feedback On Boarding (September and October 2020) After the initial set up phase (8 weeks) onboarding will commence with community groups across the city. An introductory video will explain how users can register and access the system, allowing them to establish their own group on the platform. The Gloucester Community Building Collective will take the lead for onboarding and implementation, support groups to use the platform. Implementation (October 2020) Any community group or association within Gloucester will be able to access the platform for free from September onwards for 2 years. The Gloucester Community Building Collective will provide support for the duration.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10:
• We aim to establish a minimum of 15 groups established on the system, each hosting an average of 25 participants. This enables these independent groups to manage data correctly and efficiently to prevent data breaches and more time to focus on all the good work that they do
  • Better connected communities, with an efficient system in place, supporting better connections being made with local people in their own community and the benefits that brings. At present over 750 residents made offers of help through the Help Hub. This is in addition to those who supported neighbours independently in their own community.
  • Engagement on this digital platform enable's the councils vision of supporting people to become more active in their community and it provides an efficent response to a localised lockdown should one occur.
column13:
Other Councils' in Gloucestershire are already interested in accessing this platform, so we would seek to produce a case study, which would identify the successes and areas requiring further attention. This would be shared, so they could assess its appropriateness for their communities.

Project outcomes would be shared through the Enabling Active Communities Group, which all tier 2 councils in Gloucestershire attend. Gloucester City Council also lead the Stronger Safer Gloucester  partnership which supports and enables community groups to share best practice.  Further dissemination would occur through Gloucester City’s ongoing involvement in Nesta’s Upstream Collaborative initiative, which is seeking to better understand successful approaches to community driven change.
column14:
Whilst the platform is available on standard internet browsers. It is accompanied by a mobile app, which can be used on both apple and android app devices for free. The app is exceptionally easy to use with simple on screen instructions and colours that enable easy read for those with a visual impairment. Notifications appear in screen in the form of an alert bubble, when a message is received. The app reflects functionality of similar communication apps such as facebook and whats app, however it protects users from service providers using data for advertising purposes.

On accessing the platform users can establish their communication preferences, restricting unwanted communication.

Groop admins can also use the platform to send regular group emails and record contact telephone numbers.

The Platform also offers a Guardian service to support members under 13 and or those who may have a cognitive impairment.
column16: Support in communicating the positive impact of digital engagement on local residents, demonstrating the role of digital platforms in addition to physical connections within a community.
column17: Gloucester Community Building Collective CIC - A local social enterprise that seeks to grow communtiy capacity and improve neighbourliness across the city. It has relationships with key VCS organisations and a network of community and street champions, enabling them to promote and implement this project at speed. https://www.facebook.com/gloucestercommunitybuildingcollective Groop - is a socially focused business that provides community and communication solutions to local groups. They have a strong reputation working with local government and community groups nationally.  https://groop.com/ They are experts in supporting community groups providing risk management for volunteering, 24/7; taking care of the compliance, communications and data.

column2: Developing New Working Practises Through Digital Transformation
column4: Supporting local businesses to transition to a new digital delivery in a cyber safe way. Engaging with the business community that does not have access to super-fast broadband to enable the capacity to introduce a new digital capability to their current business. Ensuring the businesses understand the support and help that is available from the NCSC to introduce a secure by design additional capability to help with Covid-19 recovery work.
column7: For Lincolnshire the County does not have a 100% super-fast broadband capacity yet and SME' s are suffering from the impact of Covid-19 restrictions. The Councils have identified areas of the County that do not have broadband connectivity but do have businesses located and working in this geographic area. The Council's will engage with the businesses and provide the capacity and capability to enable them where appropriate to move to or enhance the current offering with a digital delivery. The Councils will also ensure the businesses are provided with details around the NCSC cyber security offering that is available to help and support there growth.
column8:
The Council will undertake the following work and deliver the milestones:
  • Week 1 - 3 agree on number and schedule for supported businesses
  • Week 1       complete smart device procurement and setup
  • Week 2 - 3 prepare supporting documentation
  • Week 3 - 6 deliver support, training and awareness raising within community
  • Week 6 community deliver digital outcomes and improvements

The steps on spatial analysis will including broadband capacity will be defined and managed in a open file format available to all councils. The logic for the analysis will be captured and shared.
column14: This project is specifically aimed at supporting the digitally excluded business community within Lincolnshire. We will actively seek out the community that does not have access to broadband, does not have a digital channel and equip and support them on a cyber secure change delivering digital outcomes.

column2: Volunteer Hub
column4: The project intends to develop a system which will allow residents or organisations to volunteer their time and expertise to complete support requests for other residents.  Potential volunteers will be able to register online, specifying what help they can give and when they are available.  Applicants will be screened for suitability and entered onto the system.  Support requests from the community will then be matched within the system to suitable volunteers and tasks will be tracked to completion via the system. On completion of tasks feedback from residents will be gathered by the system.
column7: Initial requirements gathering has been completed by the Council.  Hitachi Solutions have been engaged to assess viability of implementing the solution and confirm this is possible within the required timescales. Other councils, specifically the London Borough of Haringey, the London Borough of Brent, the London Borough of Islington, the London Borough of Havering and the London Borough of Lewisham, have expressed an interest in the bid and are interested in making use of the resulting functionality.
column8: Project will be started on 3rd August 2020. It will take 1 month for Hitachi to develop and produce the initial system in Power Apps. This will be completed with continuous feedback from H&F Council. H&F BI Service will start developing a series of Power BI dashboards which will be connected to the Power App allowing users to monitor the service at the end of August. This will be completed by the 3rd week of September. Both the Volunteer Hub and BI dashboards will be made available to any Council who wants to deploy the solution. All developments will be completed within a 2 month period.
column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column13: The solution will be built using Microsoft’s Power Platform and could be deployed for any other council with an Office 365 tenant.  More specifically the solution is designed to work in concert with the ‘Shield and CAN CRM’ which H&F and 17 other councils have already deployed. The ‘Shield and CAN CRM’ is a CRM system developed by Hitachi to record residents in the borough and their needs (e.g. loneliness support, food packages).  This project will produce a complimentary app that will allow those needs to be matched to volunteers and organisations that can satisfy them. The Volunteers Hub can also be used as a standalone product for Councils who do not have the CAN/Shield CRM All Power BI Dashboards will be shared with any Council deploying the Volunteers Hub and can be used if they have an Office 365 tenant.
column14: The Volunteers Hub will record details about support requests and volunteers to understand whether there are any excluded segments of the population by comparing to demographic statistics. There are a number of different routes for residents to request support from the Council and these take into account accessibility. The mechanism for volunteers to register with the Volunteer Hub will be accessible online but also volunteers can register via phone
column16: There will be no need for further support.
column17: Hitachi Solutions have already developed and deployed a CRM which H&F (and 17 others) have used to assist in supporting shielding and other residents.  They will also be able to rapidly build and deploy the functionality proposed here.

column2: Sentiment Analysis
column4: During and post COVID-19 conditions; at a time of rapid and fundamental change, Sentiment Analysis can provide Local Authorities with a shared and real-time understanding of the impact on and feeling of, local citizens and local communities.  It enables sight into ‘blind spot’ areas allowing us to take another view on how citizen priorities and expectations may have shifted, this can be incorporated into our return to services planning; giving local residents a greater voice in local decisions.  We can monitor changes in opinion and feeling following decisions to support continuous improvement, make use of language detection and translation to better support our diverse communities and apply enhanced emotional intelligence in the way we work and communicate having identified and understood what acts as emotive triggers.
column6: Proof of concept or prototype testing
column7:
(this work was undertaken on a part-time basis over a few weeks by internal SBC staff only - same staff as outlined in the previous section)
  • Internal team discussion covering the potential Sentiment Analysis benefits for Local Government during COVID.
  • High-level technical review of AWS Comprehend, Microsoft’s Azure Text Analytics and Python.
  • Light-touch engagement with three Heads of Service stakeholders to establish high-level potential use cases.
  • Took a decision on a quick development achievability trial utilising free-tier to explore this quickly at no cost.
  • Scope agreed to explore technical achievability, compare the strengths of solutions and understand the art of the possible
  • We have technically trialled development for consumption and analysis of social media (twitter), online news (one local news website), emails (members hotline) and Jadu (SBC website/citizen case management) data, applying some control data sets (compliments and complaints) for assurance and assessing volume and confidence scoring.
  • We’ve just scheduled (not yet taken place) a brief ‘show and tell’ update for the three Heads of Service stakeholders on what we’ve consumed, qualified and visualised over the previous few weeks, along with sample measure results.
  • We’ve done some really valuable light-touch technical work (cheaply), have it established it works, we can see where it’ll need additional scope and some refinement and we can see the potential value/business benefit to LA's.

column9: We (the lead council) run the project and if required run the procurement as well
column10: How would you demonstrate success and impact in such a short project? Success will be demonstrated by: ·         alignment to user stories that have been value-determined and supported by the business ·         evidencing against agreed key measures ·         demonstration of Sentiment Analysis outcomes providing, the often ‘silent’ citizen voice, a part in informing business actions and decisions relating to continued COVID service response and COVID reset activities   Although my team are small, they are high-performing and familiar with blended lean/agile working.  This coupled with our proof of concept work to-date, a good level of support that’s established with Heads of Service, Councillors, our COO and the wider business, along with the ability to co-produce alongside an established supplier who can provide additional development breadth and data science expertise, sets this us up for achievability and success.  The co-production model between my team and Methods has been used successfully in a piece of previous development, we work effectively together, side-by-side as a single team and ensure skills transfer forms part of that work model for sustainability post initial delivery and sharing with others.
column13: We will ensure this in a number of ways. 1)      By using open architecture; we believe not only is this is a really good fit for the Emerging Technologies space and rapid development and delivery but it importantly supports highly cohesive design, that’s loosely coupled, and can be in severable modules which can be acquired easily, quickly and at low cost through best of breed free tier in the cloud and open source.  Making it perfect for sharing with others in an adaptable, interoperable way, that fully supports flexibility, customisability, and extensibility. 2)      The above approach coupled with weekly updates, blogs and documentation of our work including releasing the code through GitHub and via MHCLG through our commitment to the Local Digital Declaration will reduce development time and cost for others and enable replicable quality without compromising achievement of their or our solution long-term goals; it enables everyone to easily adapt the work and address dynamic changes and requirements. 3)      We will actively seek opportunities to promote the use of the work by others, be available for contact to provide as-needed advice and setup methods to support skills and knowledge transfer to help embed the work widely, in a sustainable way for all.
column14:
As at 2017, 92% of our population was digitally able, where citizens do write in or call the Council, this is digitally captured by staff on our CRM system; we already digitally assist these residents.

Therefore, the project has taken this into account and it is focussed on a digital proposal; however, we could incorporate additional technical methods which would enable the reading of letters and/or voice to text conversion for the very small proportion if needed.
